title: Specialize Roles, Mix Deployments: Pushing the Cost-Accuracy Frontier of LLM Agent Teams
summary: LLM agents are increasingly deployed as multi-role teams, where tasks are divided across specialized roles such as planner, executor, and verifier. In these systems, cost and accuracy are no longer properties of a single model: they depend on which model fills each role and where it is hosted, including API, self-hosted, and hybrid deployment. Existing agentic benchmarks typically evaluate fixed models or fixed agent configurations, and therefore offer limited guidance for cost-accuracy-optimal deployment. We introduce AgentCARD, a role-aware benchmark suite for evaluating LLM agent teams across role assignment and deployment mode. AgentCARD combines a role-decomposed evaluation harness, a unified API/self-hosted cost model, Pareto-frontier analysis, and a Shapley-based diagnostic for identifying role bottlenecks. Our evaluation shows that heterogeneous teams consistently occupy the cost-accuracy frontier. They improve accuracy by up to $44\%$ over cost-equivalent homogeneous teams, or match the strongest homogeneous team at up to $12\times$ lower per-task cost through hybrid deployment. We further find that the best role assignment is domain-dependent: some domains are planner-bottlenecked, while others are executor-bottlenecked. Finally, AgentCARD extends beyond planner--executor teams to workflows with additional roles such as verification, and supports continual evaluation as new domains and team structures emerge. Our code is released at: https://github.com/Auto-CAP/AgentCAP

AI.tldr: AgentCARD is a benchmark suite for evaluating LLM agent teams across role assignments and deployment modes, showing heterogeneous teams outperform homogeneous ones on cost-accuracy frontiers.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agent benchmarks typically evaluate fixed models or fixed agent configurations, offering limited guidance for cost-accuracy-optimal deployment in multi-role agent teams.
AI.method: AgentCARD combines a role-decomposed evaluation harness, unified API/self-hosted cost model, Pareto-frontier analysis, and Shapley-based diagnostic for identifying role bottlenecks.
AI.result: Heterogeneous teams improve accuracy by up to 44% over cost-equivalent homogeneous teams, or match strongest homogeneous teams at up to 12× lower cost through hybrid deployment. Best role assignment is domain-dependent.
AI.conclusion: AgentCARD provides a comprehensive framework for evaluating LLM agent teams, revealing that heterogeneous teams with optimal role assignments consistently dominate cost-accuracy frontiers and supporting continual evaluation as domains evolve.

title: How Much Coordination Gain Is Real? A Paired Noise-Floor Protocol for Multi-Agent LLM Benchmarks
summary:
Multi-agent LLM coordination papers report small benchmark deltas as evidence that one architecture beats another. A prior question: how much paired trial-0 disagreement do two protocols produce on the same model and benchmark when their API inputs are configuration-equivalent (matched by code inspection plus a SHA-256 byte audit), short of full identity-replay? On Claude Haiku 4.5 against tau^2-bench retail, the clean configuration-equivalent contrast (no_coord vs. intercept, both inert at trial 0) gives signed paired gaps of +10pp and 0pp across two n=100 seeds; pooled across both, +5pp with Wilson CI [-2,+12], not significant. The largest single-seed contrast (+18pp pull-vs-intercept, p_corr=0.012) did not reproduce at the second seed (-3pp, p_corr=1.0); no trial-0 contrast is significant after Bonferroni at either seed or pooled. The envelope of observed paired gaps spans [-3,+18]pp across two seeds, with pooled upper Wilson CI ~15pp. Seven of ten recent multi-agent coordination architectures report headline effects below this local floor, and one more sits inside the envelope; whether they survive a same-model paired replication is, by construction, untested in their original settings.
  We define coordination-active pass^k, pass^k restricted to trials where the coordination mechanism is logically active, as the minimum reporting protocol, with sample-size targets and runtime hooks in the body. Measurements run on ET-MCP, a task-scoped negative-knowledge store conformant with MCP 2026-07-28, used as a substrate to isolate reader-side choices, not as a contribution. On Haiku 4.5 the candidate readers (pull, intercept) do not improve trial-1 recovery; we give a preliminary diagnosis of failure modes with refinements on existing production hook surfaces.

AI.tldr: A study finds that many reported improvements in multi-agent LLM coordination may be within the noise floor of paired trial-0 disagreement, as shown by configuration-equivalent contrasts on Claude Haiku 4.5, with no significant effects after correction.
AI.motivation: To evaluate the reliability of small benchmark deltas reported in multi-agent LLM coordination papers by measuring paired trial-0 disagreement between two protocols under configuration-equivalent conditions, questioning whether claimed architectural improvements exceed local noise floors.
AI.method: Conducted clean configuration-equivalent contrasts (no_coord vs. intercept) on Claude Haiku 4.5 using tau^2-bench retail with n=100 seeds, analyzing signed paired gaps, Wilson confidence intervals, and significance after Bonferroni correction. Defined coordination-active pass^k as the minimal reporting protocol and performed measurements on ET-MCP, a task-scoped negative-knowledge store.
AI.result: No trial-0 contrast was significant after Bonferroni correction. Pooled gap +5pp with Wilson CI [-2,+12]. Envelope of observed paired gaps across two seeds spans [-3,+18]pp. Seven of ten recent multi-agent coordination architectures report headline effects below this local floor, and one more sits inside the envelope.
AI.conclusion: Many claimed improvements in multi-agent LLM coordination architectures may be within the noise floor of paired trial-0 disagreement and might not survive rigorous same-model paired replication, underscoring the need for stricter configuration-equivalent testing and adoption of coordination-active pass^k as a minimal reporting protocol.

title: Structural Distinguishability of Static and Adaptive Policy Regimes in Agent-Based Regulatory Simulation
comment: 19 pages, 4 figures, 9 tables. Simulation-based methodological study
summary: Agent-based models are widely used to evaluate policy interventions in complex socio-technical systems, yet many policy-oriented ABMs represent regulation as a fixed scenario parameter. This limits their ability to distinguish whether regulatory conclusions depend on agent adaptation, policy adaptation, or the interaction between both. Building on a previously proposed four-regime architecture, this paper contributes a controlled simulation benchmark rather than a new general framework. Using a single configurable emissions-regulation ABM, we compare constant policy/constant agents, constant policy/adaptive agents, adaptive policy/constant agents, and adaptive policy/adaptive agents under matched simulation conditions. We evaluate naive fixed policies, tracking-aware calibrated fixed policies, and three adaptive controllers: setpoint, safety-margin, and one-sided control. The benchmark recovers expected controller archetypes: setpoint control tracks the cap but produces frequent boundary crossings, safety-margin control reduces violations through conservatism, and one-sided control can limit violations but may ratchet toward over-conservatism when combined with adaptive agents. The contribution is methodological: scalar indicators, cap-relative symbolic diagnostics, trajectory motifs, and visual inspection jointly reveal how regulatory conclusions can differ even when average outcomes appear similar. Adaptive policy-oriented ABMs should therefore be evaluated through regime distinguishability, not only through average performance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a simulation benchmark to compare constant vs adaptive policies and agents in emissions-regulation ABMs, showing that regulatory conclusions depend on regime distinguishability beyond average outcomes.
AI.motivation: Many policy-oriented agent-based models represent regulation as a fixed scenario parameter, which limits their ability to distinguish whether regulatory conclusions depend on agent adaptation, policy adaptation, or the interaction between both.
AI.method: Using a single configurable emissions-regulation ABM, the study compares four regimes: constant policy/constant agents, constant policy/adaptive agents, adaptive policy/constant agents, and adaptive policy/adaptive agents under matched simulation conditions. It evaluates naive fixed policies, tracking-aware calibrated fixed policies, and three adaptive controllers: setpoint, safety-margin, and one-sided control.
AI.result: The benchmark recovers expected controller archetypes: setpoint control tracks the cap but produces frequent boundary crossings, safety-margin control reduces violations through conservatism, and one-sided control can limit violations but may ratchet toward over-conservatism when combined with adaptive agents. Scalar indicators, cap-relative symbolic diagnostics, trajectory motifs, and visual inspection jointly reveal how regulatory conclusions can differ even when average outcomes appear similar.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive policy-oriented ABMs should be evaluated through regime distinguishability, not only through average performance, as regulatory conclusions can differ even when average outcomes appear similar.

title: Machine-Coached Policy Revision in Adaptive Agent-Based Regulatory Simulation: A Controller-Level Contestability Layer
comment: 26 pages, 2 figures, 14 tables. Methodological study of machine-coached policy-controller revision in adaptive agent-based regulatory simulation
summary: Policy-oriented agent-based models are increasingly used to study regulatory interventions in complex adaptive socio-technical systems. Recent adaptive ABM frameworks distinguish between static and adaptive agents, fixed and adaptive policies, and alternative controller designs. However, most diagnostic workflows remain ex post: trajectories are analysed after simulation, but the resulting evidence is not systematically fed back into the policy controller. This paper proposes a lightweight machine-coached policy-revision layer for adaptive agent-based regulation. The layer represents policy decisions as defeasible rules with explicit conflicts and priorities, generates explanations for controller actions, and allows diagnostic failures to be translated into rule additions, removals, or priority changes. The contribution is not a new optimal controller and does not claim formal guarantees for unrestricted machine coaching. Instead, it provides a simulation-compatible operationalization of controller-level contestability: policy decisions can be explained, challenged, revised, and re-evaluated in held-out simulation runs. A stylized emissions-regulation ABM is used as the experimental component. A controlled simulation experiment focuses on an over-conservatism failure in the VPVA regime. The predefined coaching template adds a relaxation rule to the symbolic controller, reducing over-conservatism recurrence under held-out seeds while preserving violation, overshoot, and volatility guardrails. The paper argues that machine coaching is best understood as a controller-level extension of explainable adaptive ABM, complementary to causal, information-theoretic, and trajectory-based diagnostics.

AI.tldr: A lightweight machine-coached policy-revision layer for adaptive agent-based models that allows policy rules to be explained, challenged, and revised based on diagnostic feedback from simulation runs.
AI.motivation: Most diagnostic workflows in adaptive agent-based models (ABMs) are ex post—trajectories are analysed after simulation but evidence isn't systematically fed back into the policy controller. This creates a gap between diagnosis and policy improvement.
AI.method: Proposes a machine-coached policy-revision layer that represents policy decisions as defeasible rules with explicit conflicts and priorities. It generates explanations for controller actions and translates diagnostic failures into rule additions, removals, or priority changes. Tested using a stylized emissions-regulation ABM with a controlled experiment focusing on over-conservatism failure.
AI.result: The coaching template successfully adds a relaxation rule to the symbolic controller, reducing over-conservatism recurrence under held-out seeds while preserving violation, overshoot, and volatility guardrails.
AI.conclusion: Machine coaching is best understood as a controller-level extension of explainable adaptive ABM, complementary to other diagnostic approaches. It provides simulation-compatible operationalization of controller-level contestability where policy decisions can be explained, challenged, revised, and re-evaluated.

title: On the Identifiability of User Adaptation in Co-Adaptive Neural Interfaces
summary: We analyze identifiability in co-adaptive human-machine systems. We show that closed-loop encoder estimates do not uniquely identify user adaptation, but instead reflect properties of the joint system. We discuss implications for interpreting behavioral adaptation and propose conditions for identification.

AI.tldr: Identifiability analysis in co-adaptive human-machine systems shows that closed-loop encoder estimates don't uniquely identify user adaptation, but reflect joint system properties, with implications for interpreting behavioral adaptation.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of interpreting adaptation in co-adaptive human-machine systems, particularly the difficulty in distinguishing between user adaptation versus joint system properties when analyzing closed-loop encoder estimates.
AI.method: The authors conduct identifiability analysis of co-adaptive systems, examining how closed-loop encoder estimates relate to user adaptation versus joint system characteristics, and establishing theoretical conditions for proper identification.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that closed-loop encoder estimates do not uniquely identify user adaptation; instead, they reflect properties of the joint human-machine system. The paper establishes conditions necessary for proper identification in such systems.
AI.conclusion: Interpretation of behavioral adaptation in co-adaptive systems must account for the joint nature of human-machine interaction rather than attributing observed changes solely to user adaptation. The proposed identification conditions provide guidance for more accurate analysis in future research and applications.

title: Towards CSI-Native Foundation Models: A Channel-Adaptive Roadmap for 6G
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures, submmited to IEEE WCM
summary: Wireless foundation models offer a path toward reusable channel state information (CSI) intelligence for sixth-generation (6G) systems. However, existing generic-backbone adaptation and CSI pretraining methods often treat CSI as task tensors rather than propagation-conditioned channel responses, thereby failing to capture the intrinsic time-frequency-spatial geometry of wireless environments. This paper presents a channel-adaptive roadmap toward CSI-native foundation models, proposing a unified framework that aligns pretraining, positional modeling, and attention control with three channel requirements: scale-aware heterogeneous exposure, physical time-frequency-antenna coordinates, and correlation-bounded token interaction. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of the proposed framework across three dimensions: zero-shot generalization, reducing NMSE by more than 4 dB across spatial-temporal-frequency tasks; scale extrapolation, yielding up to a 5.4 dB gain under 8 times unseen antenna scaling; and inference efficiency, accelerating mobility-aware processing by up to 18.8%. A system-level evaluation with Sionna SYS further shows that the proposed framework uses only 7.01% of dense-pilot overhead, reaches -18.64 dB average NMSE, and improves average net spectral efficiency by 36.6% over dense LMMSE and 15.5% over WiFo, indicating that CSI-native representation learning can support pilot-efficient radio access.

AI.tldr: A CSI-native foundation model framework that aligns pretraining with wireless channel geometry for better zero-shot generalization, scale extrapolation, and efficiency in 6G systems.
AI.motivation: Existing foundation models treat CSI as task tensors rather than propagation-conditioned channel responses, failing to capture the intrinsic time-frequency-spatial geometry of wireless environments, which limits their effectiveness for 6G systems.
AI.method: Proposes a channel-adaptive roadmap with a unified framework that aligns pretraining, positional modeling, and attention control with three channel requirements: scale-aware heterogeneous exposure, physical time-frequency-antenna coordinates, and correlation-bounded token interaction.
AI.result: The framework achieves: 1) zero-shot generalization with >4 dB NMSE reduction across spatial-temporal-frequency tasks; 2) scale extrapolation with up to 5.4 dB gain under 8× antenna scaling; 3) inference efficiency with up to 18.8% acceleration for mobility-aware processing; and 4) system-level improvements using only 7.01% dense-pilot overhead, reaching -18.64 dB average NMSE and 36.6% higher spectral efficiency over baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: CSI-native representation learning can support pilot-efficient radio access in 6G systems by properly capturing wireless channel geometry, offering superior generalization, scalability, and efficiency compared to existing approaches.

title: BARD-MARL: Byzantine-Agent Detection for Learned Communication in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures; arXiv preprint; ancillary reproducibility/code bundle included
summary: Learned communication improves coordination in cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning, but it also creates a trust problem: a trained policy may route information through agents that have become faulty or adversarial. This paper studies Byzantine-agent detection for learned-communication MARL in adaptive traffic signal control. We propose BARD-MARL, a post-hoc diagnostic layer on top of BayesG, which is used as an attributed communication substrate rather than as a contribution of this paper. BARD-MARL combines two agent-level evidence streams: policy-graph features extracted from state-action trajectories and Bayesian trust statistics computed from BayesG latent mask probabilities. Across fixed-action, observation-flip, random-noise, and coordinated attacks in SUMO traffic grids, the results show that these signals are complementary rather than universally dominant. On a 25-agent grid, BARD-MARL reaches 0.843 AUC-ROC under a 10% observation-flip attack, while policy-graph-only detection reaches 0.917 AUC-ROC under a 10% coordinated attack. On a 100-agent grid, the unified BARD-MARL variant reaches 0.982 AUC-ROC for both 10% fixed-action and 10% coordinated attacks. The study shows that learned communication policies expose useful diagnostic evidence, but credible resilience claims require attack-specific ablations and explicit separation between coordination, detection, and mitigation.

AI.tldr: BARD-MARL is a post-hoc diagnostic layer for detecting Byzantine agents in learned-communication MARL systems, combining policy-graph features and Bayesian trust statistics to identify faulty or adversarial agents in adaptive traffic signal control.
AI.motivation: Learned communication in cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning creates a trust problem where trained policies may route information through agents that become faulty or adversarial, necessitating detection mechanisms for Byzantine agents in such systems.
AI.method: Proposes BARD-MARL, a post-hoc diagnostic layer built on top of BayesG communication substrate. It combines two evidence streams: 1) policy-graph features extracted from state-action trajectories, and 2) Bayesian trust statistics computed from BayesG latent mask probabilities. Evaluates across multiple attack types (fixed-action, observation-flip, random-noise, coordinated attacks) in SUMO traffic grids.
AI.result: Shows complementary nature of the two detection signals. On 25-agent grid: BARD-MARL reaches 0.843 AUC-ROC under 10% observation-flip attack, policy-graph-only detection reaches 0.917 AUC-ROC under 10% coordinated attack. On 100-agent grid: unified BARD-MARL reaches 0.982 AUC-ROC for both 10% fixed-action and 10% coordinated attacks.
AI.conclusion: Learned communication policies expose useful diagnostic evidence for Byzantine detection, but credible resilience claims require attack-specific ablations and explicit separation between coordination, detection, and mitigation components in MARL systems.

title: Beyond Fixed Budgets: Characterizing the Inelasticity and Limitations of Tree-of-Thought Reasoning Strategies
summary: Tree of Thought (ToT) search has become a promising direction for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models, but deploying these methods in practice raises a question that has received little systematic attention: how do different search strategies behave under varying compute budgets, model sizes, and problem difficulties? In this work, we evaluate two representative ToT methods; DPTS, a Monte Carlo tree search based approach, and SSDP, a semantic deduplication based approach, across two mathematical reasoning benchmarks (Math500 and GSM8K), two model scales (Llama-3B and Llama-8B), and four token budgets (3k--10k). Our analysis reveals that the two methods exhibit limitations that pull in opposite directions. DPTS suffers from a cold-start bottleneck at low budgets: it requires sufficient exploration before its value estimates become reliable, making it a poor fit for resource-constrained settings despite strong scaling behavior at higher budgets. SSDP, on the other hand, reaches candidate solutions efficiently but is prone to frontier depletion; its aggressive node merging permanently discards unexplored paths, leaving it unable to improve regardless of how much budget remains. Together, these findings suggest that neither a fixed exploration strategy nor a fixed pruning strategy is sufficient across compute continuum. We argue that effective search for scientific reasoning agents requires strategies that can adapt their behavior based on search progress and available resources.

AI.tldr: Evaluation of two Tree of Thought search methods (DPTS and SSDP) across varying compute budgets, model sizes, and problem difficulties reveals complementary limitations, suggesting the need for adaptive search strategies.
AI.motivation: To systematically investigate how different Tree of Thought search strategies behave under varying compute budgets, model sizes, and problem difficulties—a question that has received little attention despite the practical deployment of such methods.
AI.method: Evaluate two representative ToT methods (DPTS, a Monte Carlo tree search based approach, and SSDP, a semantic deduplication based approach) across two mathematical reasoning benchmarks (Math500 and GSM8K), two model scales (Llama-3B and Llama-8B), and four token budgets (3k–10k).
AI.result: DPTS suffers from a cold-start bottleneck at low budgets, requiring sufficient exploration before value estimates become reliable, making it poorly suited for resource-constrained settings. SSDP reaches candidate solutions efficiently but is prone to frontier depletion due to aggressive node merging, leaving it unable to improve regardless of remaining budget. The two methods exhibit complementary limitations.
AI.conclusion: Effective search for scientific reasoning agents requires strategies that can adapt their behavior based on search progress and available resources, as neither fixed exploration nor fixed pruning strategies are sufficient across the compute continuum.

title: NeuroShield: A Device-Agnostic Foundation Model for EEG Authentication
summary: A central challenge in EEG authentication is that models are typically tied to the acquisition settings in which they are trained. In particular, variations in headset hardware, channel layout, and signal duration create heterogeneous recordings that existing models are not designed to handle, causing each new headset or dataset to be treated as a separate model-development problem. This fragmentation limits multi-dataset learning, hinders knowledge transfer, and reduces model reusability. To address this limitation, we present NeuroShield, a reusable foundation model for EEG authentication that learns identity-discriminative embeddings from variable-channel and variable-length EEG recordings through a dual-stage transformer architecture. We pretrain NeuroShield on three public EEG datasets comprising 15{,}762 subjects and 28{,}116 sessions, and evaluate transfer on two unseen downstream datasets. Our evaluations show that, after fine-tuning, NeuroShield reduces equal error rate by 0.44--8.06 percentage points relative to the state of the art. NeuroShield further generalizes to segments longer than those seen during training and operates across channel layouts not encountered during pretraining. These results establish NeuroShield as a reusable and adaptable EEG identity encoder across heterogeneous recording settings. We release NeuroShield as open source to support reproducibility and community adoption.

AI.tldr: NeuroShield is a reusable foundation model for EEG authentication that handles variable-channel and variable-length EEG recordings through a dual-stage transformer architecture, outperforming state-of-the-art methods and generalizing to unseen hardware and recording settings.
AI.motivation: EEG authentication models are typically tied to specific acquisition settings (headset hardware, channel layout, signal duration), creating heterogeneous recordings that existing models can't handle. This fragmentation limits multi-dataset learning, hinders knowledge transfer, and reduces model reusability, requiring separate model development for each new headset or dataset.
AI.method: NeuroShield is a reusable foundation model for EEG authentication that learns identity-discriminative embeddings from variable-channel and variable-length EEG recordings using a dual-stage transformer architecture. The model is pretrained on three public EEG datasets (15,762 subjects, 28,116 sessions) and evaluated on transfer to two unseen downstream datasets.
AI.result: After fine-tuning, NeuroShield reduces equal error rate by 0.44-8.06 percentage points relative to state-of-the-art methods. It generalizes to segments longer than those seen during training and operates across channel layouts not encountered during pretraining.
AI.conclusion: NeuroShield establishes itself as a reusable and adaptable EEG identity encoder across heterogeneous recording settings. The model is released as open source to support reproducibility and community adoption, addressing the fragmentation problem in EEG authentication.

title: Empowering Economic Simulation Through Situation-Aware Llm-Driven Generative System
summary: Traditional economic modeling typically follows a TOP-DOWN paradigm, neglecting individual diversity and the complexity of social interactions. To better capture the complexity of societal structure, Agent-Based Modeling (ABM) employs a BOTTOM-UP solution by incorporating micro-level dynamics to generate macroeconomic phenomena. Reinforcement Learning further improves its decision-making ability through tailored reward signals. However, existing ABM systems struggle to generalize beyond predefined scenarios. Recognizing the potential of LLM-driven role-playing in perception and human-like decision-making, we propose SAMAS, which models individual agents with rich macroeconomic understanding embedded in LLMs and economic trajectories experienced in the passing simulation steps. By jointly modeling both macro-level structural patterns and micro-level dynamic behaviors, SAMAS achieves superior performance in volatility realism and turning point prediction.

AI.tldr: SAMAS combines LLM-driven agents with economic trajectories to model both macro patterns and micro behaviors, improving volatility realism and turning point prediction.
AI.motivation: Traditional top-down economic models neglect individual diversity and complex social interactions. While ABM offers bottom-up solutions and RL improves decision-making, existing ABM systems lack generalization beyond predefined scenarios. LLMs offer potential for human-like perception and decision-making in role-playing agents.
AI.method: Proposes SAMAS, which models individual agents using LLMs with rich macroeconomic understanding and economic trajectories from past simulation steps. Jointly models both macro-level structural patterns and micro-level dynamic behaviors through LLM-driven agents.
AI.result: SAMAS achieves superior performance in volatility realism and turning point prediction compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: SAMAS effectively combines LLM-driven agents with economic trajectories to better capture economic complexity through joint modeling of macro patterns and micro behaviors, addressing limitations of traditional economic modeling and existing ABM systems.

title: The New Associationism: Lessons from Deep Learning
summary: What can the success of modern AI tell us about how humans learn? This paper argues that taking AI seriously as a model of human learning supports a modest but genuine associationism. The central finding is that supervised learning -- learning driven by evaluative feedback -- underlies a surprisingly wide range of contemporary AI systems, from large language models to game-playing agents, differing primarily in how much work is required to generate the relevant feedback signal. This vindicates associationist ideals of a uniform, gradual, error-driven learning mechanism operating across domains, and defuses the once-influential argument that associationist mechanisms are too limited to account for human cognitive capacities. At the same time, the successes of deep learning depend on computational architectures that go well beyond anything classical associationists envisaged, and supervised learning operates within these as one component rather than a complete account of learning.

AI.tldr: Modern AI's success, driven by supervised learning, supports a modest associationism for human learning, though it requires advanced architectures beyond classical associationist ideas.
AI.motivation: To understand what the success of modern artificial intelligence can tell us about human learning, particularly evaluating associationist theories of learning.
AI.method: Analyzing contemporary AI systems (e.g., large language models, game-playing agents) to identify supervised learning as a common underlying mechanism across domains.
AI.result: Supervised learning underlies a wide range of AI systems, vindicating associationist ideals of uniform, gradual, error-driven learning, while deep learning architectures go beyond classical associationism.
AI.conclusion: Taking AI seriously as a model of human learning supports a modest associationism, but supervised learning is only one component within more complex computational architectures.

title: Massive Activations Are Architecturally Robust: A Controlled Scratch/Commitment Residual Stream Test
summary: Trained transformers reliably develop massive activations, a small number of hidden dimensions whose magnitude is far above the median and which concentrate on the sequence-start token. Whether these outliers are a removable artifact of the residual stream's overloaded read and write role, or instead a functional necessity, is actively debated. We test the artifact hypothesis directly, with an architectural intervention. Our architecture, Ledger Residuals, splits the residual stream into a mutable scratch stream (Deliberation) that intermediate computation may freely overwrite and a protected, decode-only accumulator (Commitment) that holds the representation the model reads out. If massive activations exist only because one stream is forced to be both scratchpad and answer, then a dedicated answer channel should remove the need for them. We find that it does not. In matched-loss language models at the 160M and 290M scales, the model rebuilds the canonical fixed-dimension, start-token outlier inside the protected channel. The rebuilt feature is smaller in magnitude than in a standard transformer but more sharply concentrated on the start token, and a stronger sparsity penalty makes it more persistent and more concentrated still, rather than removing it. Massive activations therefore look architecturally robust: they re-emerge in whichever representation the model decodes from, which is what we would expect if they are functional rather than incidental. We release our architecture and measurement code.

AI.tldr: Ledger Residuals architecture with separate streams for computation (Deliberation) and final representation (Commitment) still develops massive activations on start tokens, suggesting they're functional rather than artifacts.
AI.motivation: To test whether massive activations (outlier dimensions concentrated on sequence-start tokens) in transformers are removable artifacts of the residual stream's dual role as both scratchpad and final representation, or if they're functionally necessary.
AI.method: Proposed Ledger Residuals architecture that splits the residual stream into two: a mutable Deliberation stream for intermediate computation and a protected, decode-only Commitment stream for final representation readout. Tested with matched-loss language models at 160M and 290M scales.
AI.result: The model rebuilt canonical fixed-dimension, start-token outliers inside the protected Commitment channel. The rebuilt feature was smaller in magnitude but more sharply concentrated on start tokens. Stronger sparsity penalty made it more persistent and concentrated rather than removing it.
AI.conclusion: Massive activations are architecturally robust and re-emerge in whichever representation the model decodes from, suggesting they're functional rather than incidental artifacts of architectural constraints.

title: Artificial collectives of specialists and generalists excel at different tasks
summary: Collective artificial intelligence, where multiple agents work on shared tasks, holds potential to solve expansive problems in fields from medicine to collective governance. But while prescriptive engineering solutions abound, we lack descriptive scientific understanding of artificial collectives, and therefore principles for how to design resource efficient multi-agent systems. Through systematic experiments with optimizing agents, we characterize how agent interpretive abilities, rationality bounds, and task qualities interact to shape collective performance. Agents range from specialists, with narrow interpretive abilities, to generalists, with broad ones. Collectives of specialists correspond to sparse, centralized networks, while collectives of generalists correspond to dense, decentralized ones. We show that interpretive network properties have small performance effects on average (0.07 standard deviations of performance). However, for specific task qualities, these effects are 4.5 times larger (0.33 sd) and can reach much higher for certain task qualities (1.84 sd). This leads collectives of generalists to perform better on tasks that involve generating, choosing, and coordinating, while collectives of specialists with a few generalist mediators perform better on tasks that involve negotiating. Rationality bounds then moderate these relationships. At loose bounds, specialists outperform generalists through more effective sampling of high-dimensional decision spaces. At tight bounds, generalists outperform specialists through better gradient estimation. A fundamental trade-off between performance and convergence speed emerges at moderate bounds. These findings suggest that multi-agent design could benefit from matching interpretive networks to both task demands and agents' computational limits, with implications for the efficiency and energy costs of multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how different types of agents (specialists vs generalists) and their network structures affect collective AI performance, finding that optimal design depends on task demands and computational constraints.
AI.motivation: While collective AI shows promise for solving complex problems, there's a lack of scientific understanding about how artificial collectives work, leading to limited principles for designing efficient multi-agent systems.
AI.method: Systematic experiments with optimizing agents to characterize how agent interpretive abilities, rationality bounds, and task qualities interact to shape collective performance, comparing specialists vs generalists in different network structures.
AI.result: Specialists form sparse centralized networks, generalists form dense decentralized ones; interpretive network effects are small on average (0.07 sd) but 4.5x larger for specific tasks (0.33 sd) and can reach 1.84 sd. Generalists excel at generating/choosing/coordinating tasks while specialists with generalist mediators excel at negotiating. Rationality bounds moderate these relationships.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent design should match interpretive networks to both task demands and agents' computational limits, with implications for efficiency and energy costs of AI systems.

title: Specifying AI-SDLC Processes: A Protocol Language for Human-Agent Boundaries
comment: Position paper with formal specification, failure rate analysis, and feasibility demonstration. Companion empirical paper and open-source implementation forthcoming
summary:
AI agents now participate as first-class team members across the software development lifecycle, yet no specification language exists for expressing the human-agent responsibility boundaries, approval gates, and governance constraints this collaboration requires. Existing approaches encode process in agent prompts (subject to drift), target adjacent domains (workflow management, business processes), or address only fragments (access control, approval gates).
  We propose a domain-specific language for specifying AI-SDLC processes as protocols, with formal syntax, well-formedness conditions, operational semantics, and enforcement invariants. The language distinguishes policy (declared intent) from mechanism (structural enforcement), enabling implementations to bound process non-determinism through primitives such as validation tokens and capability boundaries.
  Three results follow. A failure rate analysis shows that structural enforcement bounds system failure rates at a weighted product of agent and validator rates, while behavioral compliance permits cumulative or near-saturating growth. The 2+N team pattern (two human-in-control roles plus N specialized agent members) formalizes classical Separation of Duties for AI-SDLC. Kleene closure of orchestration loops and reflexive protocol-adherence validation emerge as design properties rather than special-case constructs.
  We position the contribution against multi-agent frameworks (MetaGPT), workflow specification (FlowAgent, BPMN extensions), and capability-based security (SAGA): the novelty lies in the specific integration, not any single primitive. A working implementation demonstrates feasibility; empirical evaluation is future work.

AI.tldr: A domain-specific language for specifying AI-SDLC processes as protocols with formal syntax and enforcement mechanisms to manage human-agent collaboration boundaries in software development.
AI.motivation: AI agents now participate as first-class team members in software development, but there's no specification language for expressing human-agent responsibility boundaries, approval gates, and governance constraints. Existing approaches have limitations: encoding process in agent prompts (subject to drift), targeting adjacent domains, or addressing only fragments of the problem.
AI.method: Propose a domain-specific language with formal syntax, well-formedness conditions, operational semantics, and enforcement invariants. The language distinguishes policy (declared intent) from mechanism (structural enforcement), using primitives like validation tokens and capability boundaries to bound process non-determinism.
AI.result: Three key results: 1) Structural enforcement bounds system failure rates as weighted product of agent/validator rates, while behavioral compliance permits cumulative growth; 2) 2+N team pattern formalizes Separation of Duties; 3) Kleene closure of orchestration loops and reflexive protocol-adherence validation emerge as design properties rather than special-case constructs.
AI.conclusion: The novelty lies in the specific integration of formal protocol specification for AI-SDLC, not in individual primitives. A working implementation demonstrates feasibility, with empirical evaluation planned as future work.

title: CIExplainer++: Generating Causal and Interpretable Explanations for Graph Neural Networks
summary:
Explainable Artificial Intelligence aims to make black-box models more trustworthy by presenting, in a human-understandable manner, the elements that lead to the model's output. This involves both (i) identifying components and connections with genuine causal influence on outputs and (ii) translating such structures into an interpretable representation. For the former, we introduce CIExplainer, a novel perturbation-based method grounded in causal inference for explaining Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). CIExplainer identifies the subgraph with the highest causal effects on GNN predictions using the Potential Outcome Framework. We evaluate and compare CIExplainer on various GNN architectures (GCN, GraphSAGE, GAT, GIN) and datasets.
  To bridge subgraph explanations with human interpretability, we further propose G2TeXplainer, a method that transforms causal subgraphs into natural language explanations that capture both feature-level and relational information.

AI.tldr: CIExplainer is a causal inference method that identifies causal subgraphs in GNNs; G2TeXplainer converts those subgraphs into natural language explanations.
AI.motivation: Explainable AI aims to make black-box models more trustworthy by presenting human-understandable elements that lead to model outputs, requiring (i) identification of components with genuine causal influence and (ii) translation into an interpretable representation.
AI.method: CIExplainer: a perturbation-based method using causal inference (Potential Outcome Framework) to identify the subgraph with highest causal effect on GNN predictions. G2TeXplainer: transforms identified causal subgraphs into natural language explanations capturing feature-level and relational information.
AI.result: CIExplainer is evaluated and compared on various GNN architectures (GCN, GraphSAGE, GAT, GIN) and datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in identifying causal subgraphs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CIExplainer and G2TeXplainer together enhance the interpretability of Graph Neural Networks by providing causal subgraph explanations and translating them into human-readable natural language, advancing trustworthy AI for graph-based models.

title: Monitoring Diameters of Causal Communication Graph with Spatio-Temporal Logic
summary: Verification of multi-agent systems requires the ability to check meticulous topological properties when it comes to agents that can move through space in continuous time. This demands a logic with sufficient expressiveness to capture these dynamics. MuTGL logic has interesting properties for expressing entangled space-time properties. However, this logic lacks the expressivity needed to analyse reachability within specific distance bounds, or to track the length or the cost of communication chains: these are fundamental for decentralized monitoring, or graph-theoretic analysis of distributed protocols, where algorithmic complexities often relates with the system's communication graph diameter. We then introduce an extension of muTGL, including a new operator called the space horizon. This addition allows us to bound the distance of communication chains, hence enhancing the logic's expressiveness. We show that this operator allows to encode modalities from other logics, such as reachability or escaping which were not available in vanilla muTGL, while allowing a deeper entanglement of spatial and temporal properties. We provide a centralized offline monitoring algorithm for this logic and illustrate it on several examples on simulations of Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithms, distributed protocols for task allocation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an extension of muTGL logic with a "space horizon" operator to bound communication distances, enabling verification of decentralized protocols where algorithmic complexity relates to communication graph diameter.
AI.motivation: Verification of multi-agent systems requires checking meticulous topological properties for agents moving in continuous time. While muTGL logic can express entangled space-time properties, it lacks expressivity for analyzing reachability within specific distance bounds or tracking communication chain lengths/costs, which are fundamental for decentralized monitoring and graph-theoretic analysis of distributed protocols where algorithmic complexity relates to communication graph diameter.
AI.method: The authors introduce an extension of muTGL logic by adding a new operator called the "space horizon" that allows bounding the distance of communication chains. This enhances the logic's expressiveness to encode modalities from other logics (such as reachability or escaping) while enabling deeper entanglement of spatial and temporal properties.
AI.result: The space horizon operator enables encoding of previously unavailable modalities like reachability and escaping from other logics. The authors provide a centralized offline monitoring algorithm for this extended logic and demonstrate it on several examples using simulations of Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithms (distributed protocols for task allocation).
AI.conclusion: The extended muTGL logic with space horizon operator successfully addresses the expressiveness limitations of vanilla muTGL for analyzing communication chain distances, making it suitable for verifying distributed protocols where communication graph diameter affects algorithmic complexity, particularly in decentralized monitoring applications.

title: PEAR: Permutation-Equivariant Adaptive Routing Multi-Agent Debate
summary: Multi-agent debate improves the reliability of large language models (LLMs) through iterative peer critiques. However, fixed topologies often introduce persistent positional biases, amplify unreliable agents, and cause high sensitivity to role assignments. We introduce \textit{Permutation-Equivariant Adaptive Routing Multi-Agent Debate (PEAR)}, an inference-time protocol that dynamically reconfigures communication roles and sparse topologies across consecutive debate rounds. By strategically switching agent-to-role assignments based on evolving agent states, PEAR prevents any agent from permanently occupying a privileged network position or distributes influence more evenly across the debate. We theoretically characterize PEAR as an equivariant sparse router: it preserves accuracy under agent relabeling while reducing routing complexity and improving generalization. Comprehensive empirical evaluations across four reasoning benchmarks and six diverse LLM backbones demonstrate PEAR significantly improves average accuracy over the strongest debate baselines. The code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/PEAR.

AI.tldr: PEAR introduces a permutation-equivariant adaptive routing protocol for multi-agent debate that dynamically reconfigures communication roles and sparse topologies across rounds to reduce positional biases and improve reliability over fixed debate structures.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent debate approaches use fixed communication topologies which introduce persistent positional biases, amplify unreliable agents, and cause high sensitivity to role assignments, limiting their reliability improvements.
AI.method: PEAR is an inference-time protocol that dynamically reconfigures communication roles and sparse topologies across consecutive debate rounds by strategically switching agent-to-role assignments based on evolving agent states, preventing any agent from permanently occupying privileged network positions.
AI.result: Comprehensive empirical evaluations across four reasoning benchmarks and six diverse LLM backbones demonstrate PEAR significantly improves average accuracy over the strongest debate baselines.
AI.conclusion: PEAR provides a theoretically characterized equivariant sparse routing approach that preserves accuracy under agent relabeling while reducing routing complexity and improving generalization, making multi-agent debate more reliable and robust.

title: Evidential Fusion Network for Multimodal Survival Prediction under Missing Modalities
summary: Recent multimodal survival prediction models have demonstrated strong predictive performance by leveraging complementary information across modalities. However, such models generally assume data completeness and exhibit limited robustness toward missing modalities, which are frequently encountered in real-world clinical settings. We propose the Evidential Missing Modality Survival Fusion (EMMS) model for multimodal survival prediction under missing modalities. EMMS offers a straightforward, computationally effective approach to survival analysis without requiring a generative phase for missing data. By employing Dempster-Shafer theory and Gaussian Random Fuzzy Numbers for multimodal decision fusion, it considers both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty alongside modality reliability for fusion. Moreover, the model treats missing modalities as vacuous evidence, preventing interference with available inputs and naturally reflecting increased uncertainty and calibrated predictions. Extensive experiments on four cancer datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance while providing calibrated and interpretable uncertainty estimates under incomplete multimodal observations, without introducing additional computational overhead.

AI.tldr: EMMS is a multimodal survival prediction model that handles missing modalities effectively using evidential fusion, providing robust performance and uncertainty quantification without computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal survival models assume complete data and lack robustness for missing modalities, which are common in real-world clinical settings, creating a need for models that can handle incomplete multimodal data effectively.
AI.method: Uses Dempster-Shafer theory and Gaussian Random Fuzzy Numbers for multimodal decision fusion, treats missing modalities as vacuous evidence, and considers aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty and modality reliability without requiring generative imputation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on four cancer datasets with calibrated and interpretable uncertainty estimates under incomplete multimodal observations, with no additional computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: EMMS provides an effective, computationally efficient solution for multimodal survival prediction with missing modalities, offering robustness, uncertainty quantification, and interpretability for real-world clinical applications.

title: Cohort Organized Learning: Clustering Through Agreement
summary: In this article we describe Cohort Organized Learning (CoOL), a method for clustering data without explicit distance or similarity computations. Herein, we will describe CoOL, derive the gradients determined by expectation maximization to train the networks, show how to monitor convergence during training and evaluate the clusters after training, and discuss a series of examples and use cases. We also discuss CoOL's limitations and future prospects on related tasks. Because CoOL uses neural networks to estimate the clusters, it can be used to cluster any data that can be made compatible and we illustrate this on vector data and images.

AI.tldr: CoOL is a neural network-based clustering method that doesn't require explicit distance computations, using expectation maximization gradients for training.
AI.motivation: The need for clustering methods that can handle various data types without relying on explicit distance/similarity computations, leveraging neural networks' flexibility.
AI.method: CoOL uses neural networks to estimate clusters via expectation maximization gradients, includes convergence monitoring during training, and supports various data types by making them network-compatible.
AI.result: Method demonstrated on vector data and images, showing applicability to diverse data types, with discussion of limitations and future research directions.
AI.conclusion: CoOL provides a flexible neural network-based clustering approach that avoids explicit distance computations and can handle various data modalities, though with acknowledged limitations requiring future work.

title: Darwin Mobile Agent: A Roadmap for Self-Evolution
summary: The goal of artificial intelligence is to create agents capable of general, adaptive behaviour in open-ended environments. Guided by the "Bitter Lesson", we argue that the most effective path toward this goal is to systematically remove human priors and allow intelligence to naturally emerge through interaction with a "Big World" that is orders of magnitude more complex than the agent itself. We propose the mobile Graphical User Interface (GUI) as a practical proxy for such a world and introduce Darwin Mobile Agent, an open-source infrastructure designed as a foundation for autonomous reinforcement learning in this domain. This framework addresses the data-collection bottleneck in real-world mobile interactions by using an asynchronous agent-environment loop across parallel cloud-phone instances. We further propose a conceptual roadmap to systematically remove human priors from three fundamental pillars of a self-evolving agent: task curricula, outcome verification, and memory management. We validate that the Darwin infrastructure provides the stability and scalability required for the first stage of this roadmap: policy optimisation in the GUI domain. This work establishes the practical and theoretical foundation necessary to move toward truly autonomous, self-evolving GUI agents.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Darwin Mobile Agent, an open-source infrastructure for autonomous reinforcement learning in mobile GUI environments, aiming to create self-evolving AI agents by systematically removing human priors through interaction with complex "Big World" environments.
AI.motivation: The authors are motivated by the "Bitter Lesson" that suggests intelligence emerges more effectively by removing human priors and allowing agents to learn through interaction with complex environments. They aim to create agents capable of general, adaptive behavior in open-ended environments by using mobile GUIs as a practical proxy for complex worlds.
AI.method: The paper introduces Darwin Mobile Agent infrastructure featuring: 1) Mobile GUI as a proxy for complex environments, 2) Asynchronous agent-environment loop across parallel cloud-phone instances to address data collection bottlenecks, and 3) A roadmap to systematically remove human priors from task curricula, outcome verification, and memory management.
AI.result: The authors validate that the Darwin infrastructure provides the stability and scalability required for policy optimization in GUI domains, establishing the foundation for the first stage of their roadmap toward truly autonomous, self-evolving GUI agents.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes both practical infrastructure and theoretical foundations for moving toward autonomous, self-evolving GUI agents by embracing the "Bitter Lesson" approach of removing human priors and allowing intelligence to emerge through interaction with complex environments.

title: ELADO: Elliptic PDE Assessment Datasets for Operator Learning
summary: We introduce ELADO (Elliptic PDE Assessment Datasets for Operator Learning), a systematic benchmark suite constructed to show and quantify failure modes of neural operator architectures when learning solution operators of elliptic PDEs. While the benchmarks of existing datasets focus on average case performance, the ELADO datasets are constructed to highlight challenges that arise naturally in elliptic PDE problems. In particular, we construct several datasets built around Poisson's equation and the Helmholtz equation, each with non-constant coefficients. We define a controllable data-generating process to create datasets, that are designed to isolate a distinct source of difficulty. Specifically, these are (1) heavy-tailed solution distributions arising from light-tailed coefficient field distributions, (2) spectral distribution shift of the input data, (3) heavy-tailed distributions in the frequency domain of solutions, arising from light-tailed coefficient field distributions, (4) input sensitivity of learned operators, quantified by an empirical local Lipschitz analysis, and (5) the effect of input signal complexity on prediction accuracy under controlled amplitude normalization. We evaluate several neural operator architectures across all datasets and show that heavy-tailed targets, spectral shift, and input sensitivity each cause substantial degradation of the prediction accuracy that standard datasets and metrics (e.g., the mean relative $L^2$ error) may obscure.

AI.tldr: ELADO is a benchmark suite for neural operator architectures that exposes common failure modes in solving elliptic PDEs, focusing on challenges like heavy-tailed distributions, spectral shift, and input sensitivity.
AI.motivation: Existing operator learning benchmarks focus on average-case performance, but don't adequately expose specific failure modes that naturally arise when solving elliptic PDEs like Poisson and Helmholtz equations with non-constant coefficients.
AI.method: Created ELADO datasets through a controllable data-generating process that isolates specific difficulties: 1) heavy-tailed solutions from light-tailed coefficients, 2) spectral distribution shift, 3) heavy-tailed distributions in frequency domain, 4) input sensitivity via empirical Lipschitz analysis, and 5) effect of input signal complexity under controlled amplitude normalization.
AI.result: Evaluation of neural operator architectures showed that heavy-tailed targets, spectral shift, and input sensitivity each cause substantial prediction accuracy degradation that standard datasets and metrics (like mean relative L² error) may not reveal.
AI.conclusion: ELADO provides a systematic benchmark suite that better exposes and quantifies neural operator failure modes in elliptic PDE operator learning, highlighting limitations that traditional evaluation approaches overlook.

title: GARIP: A Running-Average Moving Reference for Last-Iterate Self-Play in Two-Player Zero-Sum Games
summary: Self-play with naive gradient ascent cycles in two-player zero-sum games: the last iterate orbits the equilibrium. Modern methods restore last-iterate convergence by regularizing toward a reference policy -- MMD a fixed one (reaching only the regularized equilibrium), R-NaD a periodic snapshot (the engine of DeepNash). We study GARIP, which anchors to the running average, and isolate what the choice of reference controls. Our central result is a mechanism: collapse tracks the peak lag of the reference, and among causal convex averages of a fixed mean lag the running average (flat profile, peak $=$ mean) uniquely minimizes that peak, while a snapshot's sawtooth has peak $= 2\times$ mean (a one-line theorem). Two consequences follow. Convergence: we prove local last-iterate convergence at constant anchor strength -- the anchor scales the base map's rotation by $1-β$, crossing the stability boundary and turning a recurrent base into a contraction (global convergence is conjectured at small $β$; we characterize a large-$β$ consensus failure). Robustness: GARIP matches R-NaD's peak performance -- on matrix games, the Coin Game, and the board games Connect Four/Othello, both moving references are far more robust than fixed-magnet and magnet-free baselines -- but is the better hyperparameter default; we report it both ways: over the full grid collapse rates are statistically indistinguishable, yet at conventional parameterizations a matched-mean-lag setting collapses in 0/40 vs 10/40 seeds (a snapshot matches it only by knowing to shorten $K$). The boundaries: an anticipatory (negative-weight) reference does better still on the stale side, and the advantage appears only where naive self-play cycles (five deep self-play loops). All experiments are pure JAX and reproducible.

AI.tldr: GARIP (Gradient Ascent Regularized to Iterate Past) anchors regularization to the running average policy, achieving robust last-iterate convergence in two-player zero-sum games by minimizing reference lag.
AI.motivation: Modern self-play methods for two-player zero-sum games struggle with last-iterate convergence - naive gradient ascent cycles around equilibrium, while existing regularized methods either anchor to fixed policies (only reaching regularized equilibrium) or periodic snapshots. There's a need for better regularization strategies that achieve robust last-iterate convergence.
AI.method: Proposes GARIP which anchors regularization to the running average policy rather than fixed policies or periodic snapshots. Analyzes how reference choice controls convergence via a mechanism where collapse tracks the peak lag of the reference. Shows among causal convex averages, the running average minimizes peak lag (flat profile with peak = mean) compared to snapshots' sawtooth profile (peak = 2× mean).
AI.result: Proves local last-iterate convergence at constant anchor strength - anchor scales base map's rotation by 1-β, turning recurrent base into contraction. GARIP matches R-NaD's peak performance across matrix games, Coin Game, Connect Four, and Othello, with better hyperparameter defaults. Experimental results show 0/40 collapse rate at conventional parameterizations vs 10/40 for snapshot approach. Demonstrates anticipatory (negative-weight) references perform even better on stale side.
AI.conclusion: Running average anchoring provides optimal lag profile for regularization in self-play, achieving robust last-iterate convergence. GARIP offers better hyperparameter defaults than snapshot-based methods while matching peak performance, making it a superior practical choice for training agents in competitive environments where naive self-play cycles.

title: Path-dependent program induction under resource constraints explains human sequence learning
summary: How do people build abstract, reusable knowledge from sequential experience under bounded cognitive resources? To answer this question, we integrate rate-distortion theory with recent advances in program induction to describe how prior knowledge shapes which future structures are cheap to encode and easy to discover. We formalize this in a hierarchical Adaptor Grammar (HAG) with distinct local (within-task) and global (across-task) libraries, governed jointly by constraints on memory and computation. In simulations, HAG achieves better rate-distortion trade-offs and stronger generalization than fixed grammars or shallow chunking methods. In an online melodic sequence-learning experiment, participants' recall errors reflected systematic simplifications and reaction times increased at inferred program boundaries. Trial-by-trial fits further showed that hierarchical libraries best explained individual differences in both recall and out-of-sample continuation choices, outperforming all alternative models. These findings cast structured learning as bounded program induction in which the order of experience shapes future abstractions a learner builds.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a hierarchical Adaptor Grammar (HAG) model that explains how people build abstract knowledge from sequential experiences under cognitive constraints, showing it outperforms alternatives in both simulations and human experiments.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the fundamental question of how people build abstract, reusable knowledge from sequential experience when faced with limited cognitive resources. It seeks to understand how prior knowledge shapes which future structures are easier to encode and discover.
AI.method: The researchers integrate rate-distortion theory with program induction approaches, formalizing their theory in a hierarchical Adaptor Grammar (HAG) with separate local (within-task) and global (across-task) libraries governed by memory and computation constraints. They test this through both simulations and an online melodic sequence-learning experiment with human participants.
AI.result: In simulations, HAG achieves superior rate-distortion trade-offs and stronger generalization compared to fixed grammars or shallow chunking methods. In human experiments, participants' recall errors showed systematic simplifications, reaction times increased at program boundaries, and trial-by-trial fits demonstrated that hierarchical libraries best explained individual differences in both recall and out-of-sample continuation choices, outperforming all alternative models.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest that structured learning can be understood as bounded program induction, where the sequence of experience influences the future abstractions that learners develop, with hierarchical libraries playing a crucial role in explaining individual cognitive differences.

title: B[FM]$^2$: Brain Foundation Model via Flow Matching with SplitUNet
summary: EEG foundation models can learn generalizable representations from large-scale EEG corpora to enable single-backbone transfer across diverse clinical and brain-computer interface tasks. Existing models typically discretize the continuous multi-channel EEG waveform into patches or codebook tokens and train a transformer with masked self-supervision. Recognizing that this discretization fragments continuous brain rhythms and obscures fine-grained temporal dynamics, we present B[FM]$^2$(Brain Foundation Model via Flow Matching), whose inductive bias aligns with the data by pretraining directly on the raw signal using continuous-time flow matching without patches, tokenization, or masking. However, multi-channel EEG signals pose an architectural challenge for flow matching: time is densely sampled and highly autocorrelated (thousands of timepoints), while the electrode axis is short (tens of channels) at distinct scalp positions. To address this time-electrode asymmetry, we introduce SplitUNet, a velocity network that factorizes each block into separate 1D temporal and 1D electrode convolutions and downsamples only along time, preserving electrode topology throughout the hierarchy. B[FM]$^2$ sets a new state of the art on 7 of 9 standard downstream EEG classification tasks, using a pretraining budget of only 36,895 segments ($\approx$ 307h), 1-2 orders of magnitude ($\approx$ 30x) less than required by existing EEG foundation models. Further, it generates synthetic EEGs that two board-certified neurologists cannot distinguish from brain data (Cohen's $κ=$ -0.096). https://jd730.github.io/projects/BFM2

AI.tldr: B[FM]² is a brain foundation model that uses continuous-time flow matching on raw EEG signals without discretization, achieving state-of-the-art performance on downstream tasks with significantly less pretraining data and generating highly realistic synthetic EEGs.
AI.motivation: Existing EEG foundation models discretize continuous EEG waveforms into patches or tokens, which fragments continuous brain rhythms and obscures fine-grained temporal dynamics. This approach fails to properly preserve the continuous nature of brain signals and introduces artifacts from artificial segmentation.
AI.method: B[FM]² pretrains directly on raw EEG signals using continuous-time flow matching without patches, tokenization, or masking. To handle the time-electrode asymmetry in EEG data (thousands of timepoints vs. tens of channels), they introduce SplitUNet - a velocity network that factorizes each block into separate 1D temporal and 1D electrode convolutions, downsampling only along time while preserving electrode topology throughout the hierarchy.
AI.result: The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on 7 out of 9 standard downstream EEG classification tasks. It uses only 36,895 segments (≈307 hours) for pretraining, which is 1-2 orders of magnitude (≈30x) less than existing EEG foundation models. Most remarkably, it generates synthetic EEGs that two board-certified neurologists cannot distinguish from real brain data (Cohen's κ = -0.096).
AI.conclusion: B[FM]² demonstrates that continuous-time flow matching on raw EEG signals is superior to discretization approaches, enabling more efficient pretraining while better preserving brain rhythm continuity. The method's ability to generate neurologist-indistinguishable synthetic EEG suggests it captures deep patterns of brain activity, offering promising directions for both clinical applications and brain-computer interfaces.

title: Decentralized Autonomous Traffic Management through Corridor Networks
comment: Presented at the Second US-Europe Air Transportation Research and Development Symposium (ATRDS2026)
summary:
As autonomous aircraft are introduced at scale and traffic density increases, centralized management becomes insufficient to coordinate the large numbers of crewed and uncrewed aircraft. Dedicated Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) corridors have therefore been proposed for organizing high-density autonomous traffic flows. The desire to scalably provide autonomous aircraft flexibility in trajectory planning motivates the development of decentralized approaches to traffic management in AAM corridors.
  In this work, we extend a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) approach to address the challenge of decentralized traffic flow management in air corridor networks. We test policies trained in a single-corridor setting on increasingly complex multi-corridor networks with combinations of merges and splits in a zero-shot manner. Experimental results demonstrate that learned behaviors transfer well to scenarios with varying traffic density, network geometry, and heterogeneous vehicle performance, without needing centralized coordination or model retraining. We evaluate system-level performance in terms of conformance to corridor boundaries, completion rates, average speeds, distance traveled, and maintenance of inter-aircraft separation. We find that although our policies require only locally coordinated entry, traversal, and exit behaviors, they collectively produce desirable traffic flows through the corridor network.

AI.tldr: Decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning enables scalable traffic flow management in Advanced Air Mobility corridors, with zero-shot transfer to complex network geometries.
AI.motivation: Centralized management becomes insufficient to coordinate large numbers of crewed and uncrewed aircraft as traffic density increases. Dedicated Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) corridors are proposed for organizing high-density autonomous traffic flows, motivating decentralized approaches for scalable and flexible trajectory planning.
AI.method: Extend a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) approach to decentralized traffic flow management in air corridor networks. Policies are trained in a single-corridor setting and then tested in a zero-shot manner on increasingly complex multi-corridor networks with merges and splits.
AI.result: Learned behaviors transfer well to scenarios with varying traffic density, network geometry, and heterogeneous vehicle performance, without needing centralized coordination or retraining. System-level performance (conformance to corridor boundaries, completion rates, average speeds, distance traveled, and inter-aircraft separation) demonstrates effective traffic flow management.
AI.conclusion: Locally coordinated entry, traversal, and exit behaviors learned by MARL agents collectively produce desirable traffic flows through the corridor network without needing centralized coordination or model retraining.

title: In LLM Reasoning, there is Irrationality on top of Value Misalignment
summary: Significant progress has been made in aligning LLMs with target value functions. We argue that, even when an LLM has been well aligned in (post-)training, it may still fail to maximise the aligned value in reasoning. We mathematically formalise this gap as rational value risk: the utility discrepancy between a model's deployed reasoning strategy and its rational counterpart, which is defined to be the responses that maximise expected utility in the steepest direction. The estimation error of rational value risk is further decomposed into three components from finite candidates, finite prompts, and imperfect verifiers. Extensive experiments are conducted, covering models Llama-3.1, Qwen-2.5, T{\"}ulu-3 families (7B-72B), GPT-5.2, GPT-5.5, and DeepSeek-V4, and benchmarks UltraFeedback, AlpacaEval, GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, and MathArena. The results validate that (1) rational value risk is widespread; (2) value alignment can reduce, but cannot eliminate, it; (3) the risk is highly sensitive to inference-time reasoning strategy; and (4) longer reasoning improves rationality with diminishing returns. The code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/LLM-Rationality.

AI.tldr: Even after alignment, LLMs may fail to maximize aligned values during reasoning; this gap is formalized as rational value risk, which is widespread and can be reduced but not eliminated by alignment, and is sensitive to inference-time reasoning strategies.
AI.motivation: Despite progress in aligning LLMs with value functions, there remains a gap between a model's deployed reasoning strategy and the rational strategy that maximizes expected utility, leading to suboptimal performance in reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The authors mathematically formalize rational value risk and decompose its estimation error into three components: finite candidates, finite prompts, and imperfect verifiers. They conduct extensive experiments on various LLM families and benchmarks.
AI.result: Experiments validate that rational value risk is widespread; value alignment can reduce but not eliminate it; the risk is highly sensitive to inference-time reasoning strategy; and longer reasoning improves rationality with diminishing returns.
AI.conclusion: Rational value risk is a significant issue in LLM reasoning that persists even after alignment, highlighting the need for better inference-time strategies to improve rationality.

title: CELEUS: Certifiable and Efficient LLM Evaluation via E-Processes
summary: Can we trust evaluation scores to capture an LLM's true real-world performance? Certifiable evaluation answers this question by providing guarantee for LLM evaluation. In particular, existing methods sequentially curate evaluation samples and keep updating confidence intervals (CIs) that cover the true performance with high probability (e.g., 95%) until some conditions are satisfied, e.g., the CI width reaches a target precision. However, existing methods are not generally anytime-valid: the claimed coverage (e.g., 95%) may fail when CIs are repeatedly updated and used to decide when to stop, leaving a gap between theoretical rigor and practice. This paper bridges this gap by proposing Celeus, a Certifiable framework for Efficient LLM evaluation, which leverages E-processes to build anytime-valid CIs. Concretely, we propose signals that combine two ingredients: (i) Uncertainty-guided sampling to select informative samples for evaluation, and (ii) Surrogate-assisted approximations for unevaluated samples. We prove that such signals remain unbiased for the evaluation score conditional on the past, enabling statistically-grounded and anytime-valid $e$-process CIs. More importantly, the two ingredients reduce estimation variance and help reach the target precision with fewer evaluated samples. We also prove that CIs obtained by Celeus can shrink at a near-parametric rate up to logarithmic factors and analyze the oracle variance-optimal sampling rule that motivates the empirical uncertainty-guided one. Experiments show that Celeus reaches the target precision using 54-62% fewer evaluated samples than baselines, while preserving anytime-valid coverage.

AI.tldr: Celeus is a certifiable framework for efficient LLM evaluation that uses e-processes to construct anytime-valid confidence intervals, combining uncertainty-guided sampling and surrogate-assisted approximations to reduce required evaluation samples by 54-62% while maintaining coverage guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing certifiable evaluation methods for LLMs are not anytime-valid: the claimed coverage (e.g., 95%) may fail when confidence intervals are repeatedly updated and used to decide when to stop, leaving a gap between theoretical rigor and practice.
AI.method: Celeus leverages e-processes to build anytime-valid confidence intervals. It proposes signals combining uncertainty-guided sampling to select informative evaluation samples and surrogate-assisted approximations for unevaluated samples, ensuring unbiased signals conditional on the past. The framework also proves CIs shrink at a near-parametric rate and analyzes the oracle variance-optimal sampling rule.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that Celeus reaches the target precision using 54-62% fewer evaluated samples than baselines, while preserving anytime-valid coverage.
AI.conclusion: Celeus bridges the gap between theoretical rigor and practice by providing a certifiable, efficient LLM evaluation framework with anytime-valid coverage guarantees and significant sample efficiency.

title: AlphaMemo: Structured Search-Process Memory for Self-Evolving Alpha Mining Agents
summary: LLM agents are promising for alpha mining via combining financial priors, symbolic reasoning, executable factor generation, and feedback-driven refinement. Yet, they face a combinatorial search space, noisy non-stationary feedback, redundant discoveries, and overfitting risks from naively reusing past successes. To address these challenges, we propose AlphaMemo, a self-evolving alpha mining agent with Structured Search-Process Memory. Rather than memorizing only final factors or full trajectories, AlphaMemo records reusable evidence about which edit motifs work or fail under specific parent-factor contexts. It extracts motifs from Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) differences, applies confidence-gated residual memory on top of a search-ledger prior, and uses asymmetric veto control to suppress high-confidence failure patterns. Experiments on CSI 500 and S\&P 500 show improved out-of-sample performance and fixed-budget discovery efficiency, with ablations validating the roles of residual learning, confidence gating, AST-diff motifs, and veto memory.
