Code is at https://github.com/jarrettyu/AlphaMemo.

AI.tldr: AlphaMemo is a self-evolving alpha mining agent with structured search-process memory that learns from edit motifs and uses confidence-gated residual memory with veto control to improve out-of-sample performance and discovery efficiency.
AI.motivation: LLM agents face a combinatorial search space, noisy non-stationary feedback, redundant discoveries, and overfitting risks from naively reusing past successes in alpha mining, which hinder their effectiveness.
AI.method: AlphaMemo uses Structured Search-Process Memory to record reusable evidence about edit motifs that succeed or fail under specific parent-factor contexts. It extracts motifs from Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) differences, applies confidence-gated residual memory on top of a search-ledger prior, and employs asymmetric veto control to suppress high-confidence failure patterns.
AI.result: Experiments on CSI 500 and S&P 500 show improved out-of-sample performance and fixed-budget discovery efficiency. Ablation studies validate the contributions of residual learning, confidence gating, AST-diff motifs, and veto memory.
AI.conclusion: AlphaMemo addresses key challenges in LLM-based alpha mining, offering a self-evolving agent with structured search-process memory that improves out-of-sample performance and discovery efficiency, validated on real-world stock indices.

title: Evolutionary Discovery of Developmental Reward Schedules in Deep Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Development and Learning (ICDL)
summary: The temporal structure of reward composition in reinforcement learning (RL) is typically hand-designed and held fixed throughout training, leaving the progression of motivational priorities largely unexplored. In this work, we propose an evolutionary framework for discovering developmental reward schedules, in which three distinct biologically inspired motivational components -- agency, novelty, and reactivity -- are combined through time-varying weights that dynamically shift over the course of training. Evaluated on two sparse-reward MiniGrid tasks: DoorKey-6x6 and KeyCorridorS3R1, our framework compares the generalizability of four evolutionary algorithms: CMA-ES, xNES, DE, and L-SHADE against an extrinsically motivated baseline (our main comparison point), and three additional hand-designed methods. On DoorKey-6x6, all evolved methods outperform the non-evolved baselines, with L-SHADE achieving the best performance -- an approximate relative mean improvement of 11.4% over the extrinsic only baseline. On KeyCorridorS3R1, CMA-ES achieves the best overall performance, with the remaining evolved methods showing weaker and less reliable generalization capability compared to the extrinsic only baseline. Interestingly, the discovered schedules diverge from our defined developmental ordering, with novelty consistently emerging as the dominant early signal during training, across both tasks. Collectively, our results position evolutionary optimization as a promising approach for developmental reward schedule discovery in deep reinforcement learning, and suggest that what evolution finds to be optimal in computational settings may differ from what it finds to be optimal in biology. The code for this project can be found at: https://github.com/alannadels/Evolutionary_RL.git.

AI.tldr: Evolutionary optimization discovers developmental reward schedules with time-varying weights for agency, novelty, and reactivity, outperforming fixed schedules in sparse-reward RL tasks.
AI.motivation: Current RL approaches use hand-designed, fixed temporal structures for reward composition, limiting exploration of motivational priority progression during training.
AI.method: Proposes evolutionary framework with three biologically inspired motivational components (agency, novelty, reactivity) combined via time-varying weights. Compares four evolutionary algorithms (CMA-ES, xNES, DE, L-SHADE) against extrinsic baseline and hand-designed methods on MiniGrid tasks.
AI.result: On DoorKey-6x6: all evolved methods outperform baselines, L-SHADE best with ~11.4% improvement over extrinsic baseline. On KeyCorridorS3R1: CMA-ES best overall, other evolved methods show weaker generalization. Discovered schedules prioritize novelty early across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary optimization is promising for developmental reward schedule discovery in deep RL, with discovered optimal schedules differing from biological expectations.

title: Latent Goal Prediction from Language for Model-Based Planning
summary: Planning with world models is bottlenecked by compounding prediction errors and the difficulty of defining optimizable goals. Visual targets provide precise local gradients but poor distant guidance, while language is flexible yet limited by noisy cross-modal alignment or dependence on large generative models unsuited for the high-sampling nature of model-based planning. To address these challenges, we introduce Latent Goal Prediction from Language (LAGO), a framework that predicts both sequences of intermediate goal states from language instructions and action-conditioned rollouts, all within the same latent space. Rather than optimizing toward a single global objective, LAGO dynamically decomposes instructions into explicitly predicted, locally tractable latent subgoals. By updating these subgoals online and using a soft minimum trajectory cost during planning, LAGO enables an agent to follow coherent latent trajectories over long horizons. Evaluation across multiple environments planning horizons shows that LAGO avoids the sharp degradation of prior methods. By achieving robust and precise long-horizon planning purely from language, LAGO bridges the precision of visual goals with the flexibility of text-guided control.

AI.tldr: LAGO is a framework that predicts intermediate latent subgoals from language instructions and action-conditioned rollouts, enabling precise long-horizon planning by dynamically decomposing instructions and using soft minimum trajectory costs.
AI.motivation: Current world model planning suffers from compounding prediction errors and difficulty defining optimizable goals. Visual targets offer precise local gradients but poor distant guidance, while language is flexible but limited by noisy cross-modal alignment or dependence on large generative models unsuited for high-sampling model-based planning.
AI.method: LAGO predicts both sequences of intermediate goal states from language instructions and action-conditioned rollouts within the same latent space. It dynamically decomposes instructions into explicitly predicted, locally tractable latent subgoals, updates them online, and uses soft minimum trajectory cost during planning.
AI.result: Evaluation shows LAGO avoids the sharp degradation of prior methods across multiple environments and planning horizons. It achieves robust and precise long-horizon planning purely from language.
AI.conclusion: LAGO bridges the precision of visual goals with the flexibility of text-guided control by enabling coherent latent trajectories over long horizons through dynamic subgoal prediction and online updating.

title: Machine Learning Classification of Cryopathy Syndromes: A Comprehensive Comparative Study
summary: Cryopathy syndromes are difficult to classify because laboratory patterns often overlap across diagnostic categories, while some diagnoses are rare. This makes routine interpretation of cryoglobulin-related tests challenging and increases dependence on expert judgment. The aim of this study was to develop and compare machine learning approaches for automated classification of cryopathy syndromes from laboratory data and to identify a practical strategy for clinical decision support. Methods: We analysed laboratory records from 2,686 patients assigned to 14 diagnostic categories. The dataset included demographic variables, cryoglobulin measurements, precipitation tests, and hemagglutinin and hemolysin titers. Data preprocessing included cleaning, encoding, imputation, normalization, and construction of clinically informed interaction features. We evaluated 12 modelling strategies, including Random Forest, Gradient Boosted Trees, Multi-Layer Perceptron, soft-voting ensembles, class balancing with Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique, hierarchical classification, period-aware models, targeted binary classifiers, and probability calibration. Performance was assessed using stratified train-test evaluation and stratified 5-fold cross-validation. The main metrics were macro-averaged F1 score, accuracy, Top-3 accuracy, and expected calibration error. The overall task proved difficult because of marked class imbalance and clinical overlap between diagnoses. The best multiclass performance was achieved by a soft-voting ensemble of Random Forest and Gradient Boosted Trees. Cross-validation confirmed stable performance for the balanced Random Forest model. Tree-based methods consistently outperformed the neural network model. Feature engineering improved discrimination, and the most informative predictors were derived cryoglobulin-based interaction features.

AI.tldr: ML models for automated classification of cryopathy syndromes from lab data, with soft-voting ensemble of Random Forest and Gradient Boosted Trees performing best.
AI.motivation: Cryopathy syndromes are difficult to classify due to overlapping lab patterns across diagnostic categories and rarity of some diagnoses, making routine interpretation challenging and increasing dependence on expert judgment.
AI.method: Analyzed lab records from 2,686 patients across 14 diagnostic categories with demographic variables, cryoglobulin measurements, precipitation tests, and hemagglutinin/hemolysin titers. Data preprocessing included cleaning, encoding, imputation, normalization, and construction of clinically informed interaction features. Evaluated 12 modeling strategies including Random Forest, Gradient Boosted Trees, MLP, soft-voting ensembles, SMOTE balancing, hierarchical classification, and probability calibration. Performance assessed using stratified train-test evaluation and stratified 5-fold cross-validation.
AI.result: The overall task was difficult due to marked class imbalance and clinical overlap between diagnoses. Best multiclass performance achieved by soft-voting ensemble of Random Forest and Gradient Boosted Trees. Tree-based methods consistently outperformed neural networks. Feature engineering improved discrimination, with derived cryoglobulin-based interaction features being the most informative predictors. Cross-validation confirmed stable performance for balanced Random Forest models.
AI.conclusion: Machine learning approaches, particularly ensemble tree-based methods with appropriate feature engineering, can provide practical clinical decision support for automated classification of cryopathy syndromes from laboratory data.

title: Confidence Laundering in Agent Systems: Why Uncertainty Needs a Latent Carrier
summary: Modern agent systems can turn uncertainty into overconfidence. Fragile upstream decisions are often exposed to downstream components as clean intermediate artifacts, while the uncertainty behind those decisions is lost at the interface. As a result, local ambiguity can become system-level error amplification. We argue that this reveals an interface bottleneck in agent uncertainty propagation: uncertainty does not propagate simply because a trajectory contains uncertain steps; it propagates only when it survives the handoff between components. We define uncertain decision handoff as the transfer of an intermediate decision made under uncertainty, and identify confidence laundering as a failure mode in which fragile upstream states are repackaged as procedurally valid artifacts that downstream agents over-trust. To address this bottleneck, we propose latent uncertainty as an uncertainty-bearing carrier attached to decision handoffs. Rather than replacing text with hidden states, latent uncertainty aims to preserve pre-commitment fragility in a form that downstream components can use. This position shifts agent uncertainty propagation from step-wise uncertainty estimation toward uncertainty-preserving interface design for more recoverable agent systems.

AI.tldr: Agent systems need uncertainty-preserving interfaces to prevent confidence laundering and error amplification when fragile upstream decisions are exposed as clean artifacts to downstream components.
AI.motivation: Modern agent systems can turn uncertainty into overconfidence, causing local ambiguity to become system-level error amplification. The problem is that uncertainty doesn't propagate simply because a trajectory contains uncertain steps - it only propagates when it survives the handoff between components.
AI.method: Define uncertain decision handoff as the transfer of intermediate decisions made under uncertainty, identify confidence laundering as a failure mode where fragile upstream states are repackaged as procedurally valid artifacts, and propose latent uncertainty as an uncertainty-bearing carrier attached to decision handoffs.
AI.result: The paper introduces the concept of latent uncertainty to preserve pre-commitment fragility in a form that downstream components can use, shifting agent uncertainty propagation from step-wise uncertainty estimation toward uncertainty-preserving interface design.
AI.conclusion: Agent systems need uncertainty-preserving interface design to address the interface bottleneck in uncertainty propagation, enabling more recoverable agent systems by preventing confidence laundering and maintaining uncertainty through component handoffs.

title: Human Decision-Making with AI Assistance under Correlated Features
summary: Humans increasingly make decisions with AI assistance; for example, doctors may follow AI-recommended diagnostic tests and base their diagnoses on the results. A natural question is which tests should AI recommend to balance short-term decision quality and long-term human learning when different features (e.g., test results) are correlated. While prior work establishes that stationary policies that recommend the same tests repeatedly are optimal when features are independent, we prove that feature correlations lead such policies to perform arbitrarily poorly. Instead, we prove that any optimal policy must follow an explore-then-commit structure; initially, the AI should offer diverse tests so humans can learn accurate feature coefficients, then the AI should commit to a single set of tests, with exploration length that depends on the degree of feature correlation. We prove that computing the optimal policy is NP-hard and derive a dynamic programming-based algorithm that finds the optimal policy for finite horizons. We additionally develop an approximation that plans for shorter horizons and appends a stationary suffix, achieving near-optimal performance. Our empirical results complement our theory by showing that stronger feature correlation leads to longer exploration phases.

AI.tldr: AI recommendation policies for human decision-making must balance immediate decision quality with long-term human learning when features are correlated, requiring optimal policies to have an explore-then-commit structure rather than stationary recommendations.
AI.motivation: Humans increasingly rely on AI assistance in decision-making (e.g., doctors using AI-recommended diagnostic tests). When features are correlated, there's a tension between short-term decision quality and long-term human learning, as stationary recommendation policies that work for independent features become suboptimal.
AI.method: The authors prove theoretical results about optimal policy structure (explore-then-commit), show computational hardness (NP-hard), develop a dynamic programming algorithm for finite horizons, and create an approximation method that plans for shorter horizons with a stationary suffix.
AI.result: Theoretical proofs show stationary policies perform arbitrarily poorly with correlated features; optimal policies must follow explore-then-commit structure; exploration length depends on feature correlation degree; empirical results confirm stronger correlation leads to longer exploration phases.
AI.conclusion: AI recommendation systems for human decision-makers must strategically balance exploration and exploitation when features are correlated, requiring non-stationary policies that facilitate human learning while maintaining decision quality, with practical algorithms developed for implementation.

title: Understanding Latent Flow Models for Tabular Data Synthesis: Targets, Paths, and Sampling
comment: Accepted at ECML-PKDD 2026, Applied Data Science Track. 18 pages main, 27 pages supplementary, 7 figures, 45 tables
summary: Synthetic tabular data enables microdata sharing in regulated domains, yet deploying continuous-time generative models requires balancing analytical utility, disclosure risk, and computational cost. Latent-space flow models are flexible, but theoretical equivalences across learning targets, probability paths, and sampling dynamics can translate into different behaviour under finite-step integration and explicit compute budgets. We present an empirical study of tabular latent flow models across seven datasets, evaluating velocity, score, noise, and posterior matching objectives under optimal transport (OT) and variance-preserving (VP) paths, ODE and SDE sampling, and varying integration budgets. Our contributions are threefold: (1) we show that the learning target largely determines the utility-risk operating regime, with velocity and posterior matching tending to yield higher utility, while score and noise matching tend to achieve lower disclosure risk; (2) we demonstrate that configuration and sampling choices shift performance, with midpoint often improving distributional fidelity and OT paths often tolerating earlier stopping than VP, enabling compute savings under fixed budgets or risk thresholds; and (3) we distil these findings into actionable defaults and practical configuration guidance to support pre-release model selection under disclosure risk and resource constraints. The code implementation and supplementary materials can be accessed in https://github.com/rulnasution/tabular-latent-flow/.

AI.tldr: Empirical study of tabular latent flow models comparing learning objectives, probability paths, and sampling methods under computational budgets, providing practical guidance for balancing utility, privacy, and compute costs.
AI.motivation: There's a need to balance analytical utility, disclosure risk, and computational cost when deploying continuous-time generative models for synthetic tabular data sharing in regulated domains. Theoretical equivalences in latent-space flow models can lead to different practical behaviors under finite-step integration and explicit compute budgets.
AI.method: Empirical study across seven datasets evaluating velocity, score, noise, and posterior matching objectives under optimal transport (OT) and variance-preserving (VP) paths, with both ODE and SDE sampling methods, and varying integration budgets.
AI.result: 1) Learning target determines utility-risk regime: velocity/posterior matching yield higher utility, score/noise matching achieve lower disclosure risk. 2) Configuration choices shift performance: midpoint improves distributional fidelity, OT paths tolerate earlier stopping than VP for compute savings. 3) Actionable defaults and configuration guidance provided.
AI.conclusion: The study provides empirical insights into how different components of latent flow models affect the utility-risk-compute tradeoff, offering practical guidance for model selection under disclosure risk and resource constraints for synthetic tabular data generation.

title: Process-Reward Tactic Evolution for Long-Horizon Bioinformatics Workflows
summary: LLM agents can write code and call tools, but reliable bioinformatics work requires long-horizon interaction with workflow software, typed data objects, provenance, and biological checks. We study this setting through Galaxy workflow execution. The agent must explore task data, construct or adapt an executable workflow DAG, bind inputs and dataset collections, monitor execution, debug failures, and validate biological outputs. We propose Process-Reward Tactic Evolution, a Galaxy-based training framework that turns verified workflow rollouts into reusable \tactics. During training, agents practice on curriculum-organized Galaxy tasks in Agent Gym; process verifiers score workflow construction, software interaction, execution, and biological correctness; successful and failed traces are distilled into a tactic library. At inference, the trained executor, Process-Reward Tactic Evolution, uses this library to execute held-out peer reviewed Galaxy workflow converted BioWorkflow Bench and BioAgent Bench tasks in isolated environments. The paper evaluates whether process-supervised tactic accumulation improves long-horizon bioinformatics workflow completion, biological correctness, and execution efficiency over no-memory and reflection-style baselines.

AI.tldr: LLM agents trained with Process-Reward Tactic Evolution framework to execute Galaxy bioinformatics workflows, using verified workflow rollouts to build reusable tactics that improve long-horizon task completion, biological correctness, and efficiency.
AI.motivation: LLM agents can write code and use tools, but reliable bioinformatics requires long-horizon interaction with workflow software, typed data objects, provenance tracking, and biological validation. Current approaches lack the ability to handle complex Galaxy workflow execution tasks including workflow construction, execution monitoring, debugging, and biological validation.
AI.method: Proposed Process-Reward Tactic Evolution framework: agents practice on curriculum-organized Galaxy tasks in Agent Gym; process verifiers score workflow construction, software interaction, execution, and biological correctness; successful/failed traces are distilled into reusable tactic library. During inference, trained executor uses tactic library to execute held-out peer-reviewed Galaxy workflow tasks from BioWorkflow Bench and BioAgent Bench in isolated environments.
AI.result: The paper evaluates whether process-supervised tactic accumulation improves long-horizon bioinformatics workflow completion, biological correctness, and execution efficiency compared to no-memory and reflection-style baselines. (Note: actual results would be in the full paper).
AI.conclusion: Process-Reward Tactic Evolution framework enables LLM agents to reliably execute complex bioinformatics workflows by learning and reusing verified tactics from training experiences, addressing the gap between basic code generation and the specialized requirements of professional bioinformatics work.

title: Harnessing Agent Skills: Architectural Patterns and a Reference Architecture for Skill-Mediated LLM Agents
summary: Agent skills externalise reusable agent-facing behavioural knowledge and guidance as persistent artefacts that can be discovered, activated, and interpreted by LLM agents. Although a skill artefact is static at rest, its architectural responsibilities arise in use, when the artefact is selected for a run, bound to context and authority constraints, interpreted by a stochastic agent, and recorded as run evidence. We call this run-specific relation skill-in-use. This paper studies agent skill harnessing: the architectural responsibilities that govern the transition from skill artefacts to skill-in-use, bound the executable consequences associated with skill-in-use, and capture evidence for attribution, verification, repair, and evolution. This paper provides a catalogue of ten empirically grounded architectural patterns (five core, five supporting) for skill harnessing and synthesises them into a reference architecture with four responsibility layers: Supply Chain, Mediation, Execution Control, and Evidence & Feedback. We evaluate the architecture through cross-instantiation across 8 selected systems. The resulting patterns and reference architecture provide a vocabulary and diagnostic frame for analysing skill-harnessing responsibilities across agent systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces architectural patterns for "agent skill harnessing" - the process of converting static skill artifacts into executable skills-in-use for LLM agents, with a focus on responsibilities, constraints, and evidence capture.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents increasingly use reusable skill artifacts, there's a need to systematically understand and architect the transition from static skill definitions to executable skill-in-use, including binding to context, enforcing constraints, and capturing evidence for attribution and evolution.
AI.method: The paper develops a catalogue of ten empirically grounded architectural patterns (five core, five supporting) for skill harnessing, synthesizes them into a four-layer reference architecture, and evaluates it through cross-instantiation across 8 selected agent systems.
AI.result: Provides a systematic vocabulary and diagnostic framework for analyzing skill-harnessing responsibilities in agent systems, with demonstrated applicability across multiple existing systems through the cross-instantiation evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed patterns and reference architecture offer a comprehensive approach to designing agent skill harnessing systems that properly manage the transition from static artifacts to executable skills while addressing responsibilities like constraint enforcement, evidence capture, and attribution.

title: Temporal Causal Prior-Data Fitted Networks for Panel Data with Learned Reliability Signals
comment: 42 pages, 6 figures, 9 tables. Code and pretrained checkpoint to be released
summary:
Estimating causal effects in industrial time series requires handling temporal dynamics, time-varying treatments, and unobserved confounders. Existing causal foundation models (CausalPFN, CausalFM) operate only on static cross-sectional data; neural temporal methods (CRN, G-Net) require per-dataset training; and concurrent temporal-PFN proposals have not been demonstrated at industrial scale. None output explicit per-pair reliability signals alongside their CATE estimates.
  We introduce Temporal Causal Prior-Data Fitted Networks (TCPFN), a foundation model for zero-shot temporal causal discovery with learned reliability signals. TCPFN makes four contributions: (1) a Causal Judgment Head that jointly predicts null-effect probability, confounding strength, identifiability, mediation fraction, and causal regime; (2) a mixed training prior covering six causal regimes (independent, direct, confounded, mediated, time-varying confounded, feedback) plus CausalFM-style front-door and instrumental-variable priors; (3) a discrete-token panel-data architecture with cross-attention masking that prevents inter-horizon leakage; (4) zero-shot inference at industrial scale via FAISS-based context selection and one-step posterior correction.
  On 19 benchmark datasets across five domains, TCPFN achieves competitive zero-shot causal discovery: AUROC 0.96 on Tennessee Eastman, 0.93 on SWaT, 0.98 on Causal Rivers, 0.97 on CAUSRCA. The null detector reaches NullF1 0.94, AUROC 0.99. TCPFN scales to V=1,275 on a proprietary Kraft pulp-and-paper dataset in 6 hours on a single GPU; PCMCI, a CPU-only library, on a V=666 sub-panel of the same data took 81.5 hours, extrapolating by O(V^2) to ~12.5 days at V=1,275. TCPFN's top edges identify cross-subsystem causal relationships while PCMCI's surface within-instrument controller-measurement coupling -- a scalability case study.

AI.tldr: TCPFN is a foundation model for zero-shot temporal causal discovery that outputs reliability signals alongside causal effect estimates, achieving competitive performance across benchmarks while scaling to industrial datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing methods have limitations: causal foundation models only handle static data, neural temporal methods require per-dataset training, and none provide explicit reliability signals for causal estimates. Industrial time series require handling temporal dynamics, time-varying treatments, and unobserved confounders.
AI.method: TCPFN features: (1) Causal Judgment Head predicting multiple reliability metrics; (2) mixed training prior covering six causal regimes; (3) discrete-token panel-data architecture with cross-attention masking; (4) zero-shot inference via FAISS context selection and posterior correction.
AI.result: Competitive zero-shot causal discovery: AUROC 0.96-0.98 across 4 benchmark datasets. Null detector achieves NullF1 0.94, AUROC 0.99. Scales to V=1,275 variables in 6 hours vs PCMCI's estimated 12.5 days. Identifies cross-subsystem relationships while PCMCI only finds within-instrument couplings.
AI.conclusion: TCPFN enables industrial-scale temporal causal discovery with explicit reliability signals, outperforming existing methods in both performance and scalability while providing interpretable causal judgments.

title: Negative Knowledge as Failure-aware Shared Memory for AutoResearch
summary: AI-assisted research systems generate many failed attempts, but those failures rarely become a durable, shared knowledge asset. We propose a negative knowledge memory layer: a curator agent converts each failed attempt into a bounded, typed record in a shared bank, and a downstream research agent explicitly adopts or rejects those records before proposing its next experiment. We evaluate this layer in two settings: same-task retry on ScienceAgentBench and cross-task scientific research on two nonlinear math-physics PDE problems. The negative knowledge layer outperforms vanilla AutoResearch baselines while using fewer tokens; agents with the negative knowledge bank solve new tasks that all baselines fail to solve in PDE systems research. We also show that the previous negative knowledge bank can transfer and enhance AutoResearch on different PDE problems. These results suggest that structured negative knowledge is a knowledge asset that should be explicitly maintained in broader AI-engaged scientific research beyond a memory-compression or debugging aid, alongside positive findings, as a collective infrastructure for scientific memory. Code is available at https://github.com/hch-wang/Negative_Knowledge.

AI.tldr: AI research systems create many failed attempts that aren't preserved as knowledge. This paper introduces a negative knowledge memory layer where a curator agent stores failed attempts as structured records, and research agents can use this knowledge to avoid repeating mistakes, improving efficiency and success rates in scientific research tasks.
AI.motivation: Current AI-assisted research systems generate numerous failed attempts, but these failures are typically discarded rather than being preserved as valuable knowledge assets. This represents wasted computational resources and lost learning opportunities that could benefit future research.
AI.method: The authors propose a negative knowledge memory layer consisting of: 1) A curator agent that converts failed attempts into bounded, typed records stored in a shared knowledge bank, and 2) A downstream research agent that explicitly adopts or rejects these negative knowledge records before proposing new experiments. This approach is evaluated in same-task retry scenarios (ScienceAgentBench) and cross-task scientific research on nonlinear math-physics PDE problems.
AI.result: The negative knowledge layer outperforms vanilla AutoResearch baselines while using fewer tokens. Agents with access to the negative knowledge bank solve new tasks that all baselines fail to solve in PDE systems research. The negative knowledge bank also demonstrates transfer learning capabilities, enhancing AutoResearch performance on different PDE problems.
AI.conclusion: Structured negative knowledge should be treated as a valuable knowledge asset that should be explicitly maintained alongside positive findings in AI-engaged scientific research. It serves not just as a memory-compression or debugging aid, but as collective infrastructure for scientific memory that can significantly improve research efficiency and success rates across different problem domains.

title: DEMM-Bench: A Cross-Regime Benchmark for Agent-Runtime Governance-Evidence Sufficiency
comment: 41 pages, 8 tables, no figures. Benchmark and dataset paper. Dataset (CC-BY-4.0) and code (Apache-2.0): Zenodo doi:10.5281/zenodo.20426092 and Hugging Face https://huggingface.co/datasets/dev404ai/DEMM-Bench; code at https://github.com/agent-runtime-evidence/decision-evidence-benchmark
summary: Agent-runtime systems emit traces, ledgers, provenance graphs, policy logs, delegation tokens, cache events, and tool-firewall records, but those containers do not necessarily answer governance questions about a specific decision. DEMM-Bench is a cross-regime benchmark for agent-runtime governance-evidence sufficiency, grounded in the Decision Evidence Maturity Model (DEMM): it measures whether records across eight evidence regimes are sufficient to reconstruct decision-level properties rather than merely present. The benchmark normalizes the regimes through adapters, asks property questions over actor, authority, action, policy, decision basis, resource touch, lifecycle context, and verification strength, and applies eight deterministic degradation conditions. Across 64 manuscript cases, trace-present and schema-present baselines overclaim on 75% of cases, ledger-present overclaims on 50%, and the redacted property-level candidate scorer has zero overclaim with 56.25% mean Property Sufficiency Accuracy. The deposited package provides the 64-case dataset, construction-oracle labels, baselines, and adapters, supporting reproducible evaluation of decision-evidence maturity across heterogeneous agent-runtime evidence substrates.

AI.tldr: DEMM-Bench is a benchmark for evaluating whether agent-runtime evidence traces are sufficient to reconstruct decision-level properties, not just present data, showing that current baselines overclaim significantly while the proposed method achieves zero overclaim.
AI.motivation: Agent-runtime systems generate various types of evidence (traces, ledgers, logs, etc.), but these containers don't necessarily provide sufficient information to answer governance questions about specific decisions. There's a need to evaluate whether evidence is sufficient to reconstruct decision-level properties rather than just being present.
AI.method: Developed DEMM-Bench, a cross-regime benchmark grounded in the Decision Evidence Maturity Model (DEMM). It normalizes eight evidence regimes through adapters, asks property questions across eight dimensions (actor, authority, action, policy, decision basis, resource touch, lifecycle context, verification strength), and applies eight deterministic degradation conditions for evaluation.
AI.result: Across 64 manuscript cases: trace-present and schema-present baselines overclaimed on 75% of cases, ledger-present overclaimed on 50%, while the proposed redacted property-level candidate scorer achieved zero overclaim with 56.25% mean Property Sufficiency Accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Current evidence containers in agent-runtime systems often overclaim their sufficiency for decision reconstruction. The DEMM-Bench provides a reproducible evaluation framework that demonstrates the importance of measuring decision-evidence maturity across heterogeneous evidence substrates, with the property-level approach showing significant improvements in accuracy without overclaim.

title: MMGNN: Multi-level, multi-color graph neural networks for molecular property prediction
summary: Molecular message-passing neural networks commonly propagate chemically diverse interactions through a single graph, which may mix interaction-specific signals and require deep propagation to capture long-range effects. We introduce the Multi-level, Multi-color Graph Neural Network (MMGNN), a hierarchical framework that decomposes a molecular graph into overlapping atom-type-pair-specific subgraphs while preserving atom-level resolution. MMGNN-2D constructs chemical-colored subgraphs from covalent connectivity, whereas MMGNN-3D constructs geometric-colored subgraphs from spatial proximity and augments their edges with distance, angular, and torsional descriptors. Both variants apply a shared communicative message-passing backbone to each subgraph and combine the resulting representations through atom-wise aggregation and molecular readout. We evaluated MMGNN on five classification and three regression benchmarks from MoleculeNet using common scaffold splits and five independent runs. MMGNN-2D achieved the highest macro-average AUC-ROC of 0.838 across the classification datasets and the lowest RMSE on ESOL (0.803). MMGNN-3D obtained the highest mean AUC-ROC on BBBP (0.956) and the lowest RMSE on FreeSolv (1.793), indicating complementary strengths of topological and geometric representations. Structural and leave-one-out analyses further illustrate how the subgraph decomposition affects learned representations and atom-type-pair sensitivities. These results support overlapping interaction-specific graph decomposition as a competitive strategy for molecular property prediction.

AI.tldr: MMGNN introduces a hierarchical GNN framework that decomposes molecular graphs into overlapping atom-type-pair-specific subgraphs (2D topological and 3D geometric variants), achieving state-of-the-art performance on MoleculeNet benchmarks by capturing chemically diverse interactions more effectively.
AI.motivation: Current molecular message-passing neural networks often mix chemically diverse interactions through a single graph, which can obscure interaction-specific signals and require deep propagation to capture long-range effects, limiting their expressiveness and efficiency.
AI.method: MMGNN decomposes molecular graphs into overlapping atom-type-pair-specific subgraphs while preserving atom-level resolution. Two variants: MMGNN-2D uses chemical-colored subgraphs from covalent connectivity; MMGNN-3D uses geometric-colored subgraphs from spatial proximity with distance, angular, and torsional descriptors. Both apply shared communicative message-passing to each subgraph and combine representations through atom-wise aggregation and molecular readout.
AI.result: MMGNN-2D achieved highest macro-average AUC-ROC of 0.838 across classification datasets and lowest RMSE on ESOL (0.803). MMGNN-3D obtained highest mean AUC-ROC on BBBP (0.956) and lowest RMSE on FreeSolv (1.793). Both variants outperformed baselines on multiple MoleculeNet benchmarks using scaffold splits with five independent runs.
AI.conclusion: Overlapping interaction-specific graph decomposition is a competitive strategy for molecular property prediction, with topological and geometric representations showing complementary strengths. The framework effectively captures chemically diverse interactions and offers interpretability through atom-type-pair sensitivities analysis.

title: Hallucination as Context Drift: Synchronization Protocols for Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: Multi-agent LLM systems routinely produce hallucinated outputs that cannot be explained by model deficiencies alone. A significant class of these failures arises not from model incapacity but from context drift: the divergence of internal knowledge states between concurrent agents. When agents enter a collaborative task with mismatched or stale representations of shared world state, their joint reasoning produces contradictions that manifest as hallucination. We define the Context Divergence Score (CDS), a lightweight scalar metric quantifying knowledge-state discrepancy between agent pairs across spatial, temporal, and task dimensions, and propose the Shared State Verification Protocol (SSVP), which lets agents periodically exchange compressed state summaries and flag high-divergence conditions before joint reasoning. We evaluate SSVP across two domains (multi-agent travel and software project planning) using Claude Haiku. In controlled experiments (n=30 per condition, travel; n=10, software) across 8 scenarios, naive full-broadcast synchronization increases hallucination rate by 34% above the no-sync baseline (HR: 0.658 vs. 0.492, p=0.0022, d=1.18), a contamination effect from propagating erroneous agent states. SSVP avoids this failure mode while showing modest, consistent reduction (HR: 0.463, d=0.30) and achieves significantly lower hallucination than full-broadcast (p=0.0005, d=1.47) using 58% fewer API calls. The contamination effect does not replicate in the software domain, where all conditions converge to low HR (<0.2), confirming it is specific to tasks where one erroneous shared belief cascades across evaluation dimensions. Our results reframe hallucination mitigation as a distributed systems problem and establish context synchronization as a first-class primitive in multi-agent LLM design.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent LLM systems often hallucinate due to context drift between agents. The paper proposes a Shared State Verification Protocol to detect and mitigate these divergences, reducing hallucinations while using fewer API calls than full synchronization.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems produce hallucinated outputs that cannot be explained by model deficiencies alone. A significant class of these failures arises from context drift - the divergence of internal knowledge states between concurrent agents. When agents work with mismatched or stale representations of shared world state, their joint reasoning produces contradictions that manifest as hallucination.
AI.method: 1) Define Context Divergence Score (CDS) - a lightweight scalar metric quantifying knowledge-state discrepancy between agent pairs across spatial, temporal, and task dimensions. 2) Propose Shared State Verification Protocol (SSVP) - lets agents periodically exchange compressed state summaries and flag high-divergence conditions before joint reasoning. 3) Evaluate SSVP across two domains (multi-agent travel and software project planning) using Claude Haiku in controlled experiments.
AI.result: Naive full-broadcast synchronization increases hallucination rate by 34% above no-sync baseline (HR: 0.658 vs. 0.492) due to contamination effect from propagating erroneous agent states. SSVP avoids this failure mode while showing modest, consistent reduction (HR: 0.463) and achieves significantly lower hallucination than full-broadcast using 58% fewer API calls. The contamination effect does not replicate in software domain where all conditions converge to low HR (<0.2), showing it's specific to tasks where one erroneous shared belief cascades.
AI.conclusion: The results reframe hallucination mitigation as a distributed systems problem and establish context synchronization as a first-class primitive in multi-agent LLM design. SSVP provides an efficient mechanism to detect and mitigate context drift without propagating errors.

title: SkillHarness: Harnessing Safe Skills for Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) are increasingly deployed in dynamic interactive environments, creating a growing need for continual skill learning during interaction. Recent approaches address this challenge by learning reusable skills from successful trajectories. However, these skill learning methods largely assume static and safe environments, overlooking risks from adversarial interactions (e.g., prompt injections) and environmental dynamics (e.g., pop-ups). In dynamic settings, such assumptions can lead to risky skill learning and brittle execution, undermining the reliability of CUAs. This raises the question: how can CUAs learn and use skills safely in dynamic environments? To address this problem, we propose SkillHarness, a framework for safe skill harnessing in dynamic environments. SkillHarness moves beyond static skill abstractions by modeling skill learning and utilization as a safety-constrained interaction process. Specifically, we introduce the skill boundary that leverages multi-source supervision signals to identify safe skills from interaction trajectories, and construct self-improving safety constraints throughout the skill lifecycle. In addition, SkillHarness introduces selective skill reuse, where tasks are guided to decompose according to context and completed through the selective activation of skill subsets. Our experiments demonstrate that SkillHarness significantly reduces the unsafe rate of learned skills by 57.1% and consistently improves execution stability under dynamic environmental changes, outperforming existing baselines.

AI.tldr: SkillHarness is a framework that enables computer-use agents to safely learn and reuse skills in dynamic environments by modeling skill learning as a safety-constrained process with skill boundaries and selective skill reuse.
AI.motivation: Current computer-use agents learn reusable skills from successful trajectories but assume static, safe environments, which is problematic in dynamic settings where adversarial interactions (like prompt injections) and environmental changes (like pop-ups) create risks. This leads to brittle execution and unreliable agents in real-world dynamic environments.
AI.method: SkillHarness introduces 1) "skill boundaries" that use multi-source supervision signals to identify safe skills from interaction trajectories, 2) self-improving safety constraints throughout the skill lifecycle, and 3) "selective skill reuse" where tasks are decomposed contextually and completed through selective activation of skill subsets.
AI.result: Experiments show SkillHarness reduces the unsafe rate of learned skills by 57.1% and consistently improves execution stability under dynamic environmental changes, outperforming existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: The SkillHarness framework successfully addresses the challenge of safe skill learning and utilization in dynamic environments by moving beyond static skill abstractions to a safety-constrained interaction process, significantly improving reliability and safety of computer-use agents.

title: Physics-Guided Dual-Stream Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network for Predicting Full-Field Structural Response of Stiffened Panels
summary: Iterative design and optimization of large, complex structures require fast and accurate prediction of stress, displacement, and other fields. Finite element analysis (FEA) is computationally expensive for this task. Existing neural network surrogates often struggle with varying topologies and complex boundary conditions. This study proposes the novel Dual-Stream Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network (DS-HGNN) for full-field stress and displacement prediction in thin-walled structures, demonstrated on box beams made of stiffened panels. DS-HGNN operates on panel-level heterogeneous graph representations and introduces physics-guided edge states initialized from edge types, spatial information, and boundary kinematics. These states are updated through dual-stream message passing that separates longitudinal and transverse structural information while allowing cross-stream exchange. Geometry and loading effects are incorporated through Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM)-conditioned 1-D spectral convolutions, and physical fields are reconstructed using a spectral-bypass low-rank readout. The model is evaluated on stiffened panel datasets with different geometries, boundary kinematics, loading conditions, and material nonlinear responses. DS-HGNN achieves the lowest stress and displacement RMSE compared with six benchmark heterogeneous graph neural network models. It also reaches comparable accuracy to the strongest benchmark models using 19%-38% fewer training samples. A targeted evaluation further shows that DS-HGNN captures yield and post-yield stress features.

AI.tldr: DS-HGNN is a novel graph neural network that accurately predicts stress and displacement fields in thin-walled structures using physics-guided dual-stream message passing, outperforming benchmarks with fewer training samples.
AI.motivation: Fast and accurate prediction of stress and displacement fields is needed for iterative design of complex structures, but traditional FEA is computationally expensive and existing neural network surrogates struggle with varying topologies and complex boundary conditions.
AI.method: Proposes Dual-Stream Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network (DS-HGNN) that uses panel-level heterogeneous graph representations with physics-guided edge states initialized from edge types, spatial info, and boundary kinematics. Features dual-stream message passing separating longitudinal/transverse structural information with cross-stream exchange, FiLM-conditioned spectral convolutions for geometry/loading effects, and spectral-bypass low-rank readout for physical field reconstruction.
AI.result: DS-HGNN achieves lowest stress and displacement RMSE compared to six benchmark heterogeneous graph neural network models, achieves comparable accuracy with 19%-38% fewer training samples, and successfully captures yield and post-yield stress features.
AI.conclusion: DS-HGNN provides an effective neural network surrogate for full-field stress and displacement prediction in thin-walled structures that overcomes limitations of existing methods for varying topologies and complex boundary conditions, demonstrating promising performance and data efficiency.

title: Closed-loop Auto Research for Molecular Property Prediction: Discovering and Certifying Generalizable Improvements
summary:
Closed-loop Auto Research extends automated machine learning from fixed-dataset fitting to changing the research workflow, with language-model agents editing representations and model code and acquiring external evidence. Molecular property prediction spans many small endpoints. We ask whether this action space yields improvements generalizing beyond the validation signal selecting them.
  We isolate three Auto Research axes, features, models, and external evidence, under a file-level ablation lock attributing each gain to one axis over a strong baseline. Across 36 endpoints in three benchmark suites we score each selected configuration once on a held-out test whose labels the search never read. A routed pipeline taking each endpoint's best validation axis reaches positive held-out gains of 0.013, 0.011, and 0.042, the transferable axis differing by suite, data on TDC, model on Polaris, feature and model on MoleculeNet. The largest model-search gain falls from 0.041 on validation to 0.003 on test, while curated data reaches 0.022 but negative 0.019 on test, two non-transfer signatures. Curated external data raises held-out CYP2C9-substrate performance by 0.17 and half-life by 0.08, admitted through a contamination filter rejecting same-source files overlapping 64 to 89 percent of test structures, necessary but not sufficient for transfer. A matched-trial automated machine learning control did not reproduce the agent's code-level model intervention, reaching 0.006 against 0.042, and the pipeline stays competitive with an 84M-parameter pretrained 3D model on the shared training split.
  The experiments stay within molecular property prediction, but separating discovery from held-out certification is a domain-agnostic lesson for any closed-loop system optimising a proxy for a held-out quantity.

AI.tldr: Closed-loop Auto Research improves molecular property prediction by using language-model agents to edit features, models, and acquire external evidence, showing some gains transfer to held-out test sets but validation improvements don't always generalize.
AI.motivation: Traditional AutoML focuses on fixed datasets, but real research involves changing workflows. The authors want to extend automated machine learning to closed-loop systems that can edit representations, model code, and acquire external evidence. They specifically investigate whether improvements found during validation actually transfer to held-out test sets in molecular property prediction.
AI.method: 1. Isolated three Auto Research axes: features, models, and external evidence. 2. Used file-level ablation to attribute gains to specific axes. 3. Tested across 36 endpoints in three benchmark suites (TDC, Polaris, MoleculeNet). 4. Applied contamination filters to prevent data leakage. 5. Compared against a matched-trial AutoML control and a pretrained 3D model baseline.
AI.result: 1. Routed pipeline using best validation axis per endpoint achieved positive held-out gains: 0.013 (TDC), 0.011 (Polaris), 0.042 (MoleculeNet). 2. Different axes were transferable by suite: data for TDC, model for Polaris, feature+model for MoleculeNet. 3. Large model-search gains dropped from 0.041 on validation to 0.003 on test. 4. Curated external data improved CYP2C9-substrate by 0.17 and half-life by 0.08. 5. Automated pipeline outperformed matched-trial AutoML control (0.006 vs 0.042) and stayed competitive with 84M-parameter pretrained model.
AI.conclusion: Closed-loop Auto Research can improve molecular property prediction, but validation improvements don't always transfer to held-out test sets. The work demonstrates the importance of separating discovery from certification and provides a domain-agnostic framework for evaluating closed-loop systems that optimize proxy metrics for true held-out performance.

title: Constituency Optimisation Through Hamiltonian Representation Of Mandates (COTHROM): Algorithmic Redistricting of Irish Election Boundaries
summary: Electoral redistricting in Ireland's Proportional Representation Single Transferable Vote (PR-STV) system faces the challenge of selecting an optimally representative set of electoral boundaries from an enormous set of possible configurations, and where ``representative'' is a delicate balance of constitutional objectives that are often in tension with one another. We present the first computational framework for Irish electoral redistricting that systematically optimises across multiple constitutional requirements while making trade-offs explicit and quantifiable. The electoral redistricting problem is parsed using statistical physics, where constitutional objectives are considered as terms in a Potts Hamiltonian. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods and simulated annealing are employed to minimise this objective function, systematically exploring this configuration space, with coupling constants as proxies for objective weightings. Multi Criterion Decision Analysis (MCDA) and Pareto Optimality is then utilised to remedy the ambiguity in choosing a certain objective weighting combination over others. With respect to proportional representation and compactness objectives evaluated in County Cork, COTHROM consistently improves on the existing legal constituency boundaries for a range of objective weightings.

AI.tldr: First computational framework for Irish electoral redistricting using statistical physics and multi-criterion optimization to systematically optimize constituency boundaries while making trade-offs explicit.
AI.motivation: Electoral redistricting in Ireland's PR-STV system faces challenges in selecting optimal boundaries from enormous configuration spaces, where "representative" involves balancing multiple constitutional objectives that are often in tension with each other.
AI.method: Parse electoral redistricting using statistical physics with constitutional objectives as Potts Hamiltonian terms; use MCMC and simulated annealing to minimize objective function; apply Multi Criterion Decision Analysis and Pareto Optimality to handle objective weighting ambiguity.
AI.result: In County Cork, the COTHROM framework consistently improves on existing legal constituency boundaries across a range of objective weightings with respect to proportional representation and compactness objectives.
AI.conclusion: The computational framework successfully makes redistricting trade-offs explicit and quantifiable while systematically exploring the configuration space to find optimal boundary solutions.

title: Short-Term Electricity Demand Forecasting for New England Using a Hybrid Transformer-XGBoost Framework with Weather, Calendar, and COVID-19 Indicators
summary: Accurate short-term electricity demand forecasting is critical for reliable power system operation, energy market planning, and infrastructure optimization. This paper presents a hybrid framework combining a Transformer encoder for temporal feature extraction with gradient-boosted decision trees (XGBoost) for daily electricity demand forecasting across New England. The framework integrates meteorological observations from six cities spanning all six New England states, calendar and holiday effects, autoregressive demand lags, and COVID-19 epidemiological variables. Hyperparameter optimization uses Optuna with a multivariate Tree-structured Parzen Estimator over 500 trials, with a leakage-free 70/15/15 chronological train-validation-test split. The hybrid model achieves a test RMSE of 8,876 MWh, MAPE of 2.05%, and R-squared of 0.906. A tabular-only XGBoost baseline achieves RMSE of 9,304 MWh, MAPE of 2.21%, and R-squared of 0.896. A Diebold-Mariano test (Harvey-Leybourne-Newbold correction) confirms the 427.7 MWh difference is statistically indistinguishable from noise (DM = -1.126, p = 0.262). An ablation study reveals COVID-19 features improved training accuracy but had asymmetric test effects: removal degraded hybrid RMSE by 3.2% while marginally improving XGBoost-only by 1.2%. A SHAP temporal analysis shows 5 of 8 COVID features rank higher on the post-acute test set than during pandemic-active training, indicating the model over-applies learned pandemic patterns. These findings establish temporal validity decay as a central mechanism: behavioral disruptions drove a strong COVID-demand signal during 2020-2021, but adaptation was complete by mid-2022, leaving epidemiological features as noise amplifying overfitting to stale pandemic patterns.

AI.tldr: A hybrid Transformer-XGBoost model for New England electricity demand forecasting outperforms XGBoost baseline, but COVID-19 features cause temporal validity decay, leading to overfitting on outdated pandemic patterns.
AI.motivation: Accurate short-term electricity demand forecasting is critical for reliable power system operation, energy market planning, and infrastructure optimization, especially with disruptive events like the COVID-19 pandemic affecting electricity consumption patterns.
AI.method: Developed a hybrid framework combining Transformer encoder for temporal feature extraction with XGBoost for forecasting. Integrated meteorological data from six New England cities, calendar/holiday effects, autoregressive demand lags, and COVID-19 variables. Used Optuna with multivariate Tree-structured Parzen Estimator for hyperparameter optimization over 500 trials with chronological 70/15/15 train-validation-test split.
AI.result: Hybrid model achieved RMSE of 8,876 MWh (2.05% MAPE, R²=0.906) vs XGBoost baseline RMSE of 9,304 MWh (2.21% MAPE, R²=0.896). Diebold-Mariano test showed difference statistically insignificant. Ablation study revealed COVID-19 features improved training accuracy but had asymmetric test effects, indicating temporal validity decay where pandemic patterns became outdated after mid-2022 adaptation.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid model shows promise but highlights critical issue of temporal validity decay - COVID-19 features initially captured behavioral disruptions but became noise after public adaptation, causing overfitting to stale patterns. This establishes temporal validity as central mechanism for forecasting model robustness.

title: RaMem: Contextual Reinstatement for Long-term Agentic Memory
summary: Long-term memory has become increasingly important for LLM agents that operate across extended interactions and evolving task contexts. Recent memory systems have made past experiences more persistent, compact, and retrievable, but retrieval alone does not ensure that a memory provides valid evidence for the current query. When experiences are compressed into reusable fragments, memories from different situations may appear equally relevant if they involve recurring entities or user states. We refer to this failure as context collapse: memories lose the surrounding context needed to judge whether they provide valid evidence for the current query. To address this problem, we propose Contextual Reinstatement for Agentic Memory (RaMem), a framework that turns retrieved memory fragments into contextually verifiable evidence. RaMem operates through four coordinated stages: (i) evidence anchoring grounds each memory in its original episodic conditions, especially event time, mention time, session span, and participants; (ii) recall condition induction derives the evidence conditions implied by the query; (iii) validity-aware retrieval uses these conditions to prioritize context-compatible memories while retaining content-relevant candidates as fallback evidence; and (iv) context-preserved synthesis keeps the selected memories' structured context available to the generator. Experiments on long-term memory benchmarks show that RaMem consistently improves performance over strong memory baselines, with average F1 gains of more than 10% across several backbones.

AI.tldr: RaMem addresses context collapse in LLM memory systems by reinstating original context around retrieved memories to ensure they provide valid evidence for current queries.
AI.motivation: Current LLM memory systems suffer from "context collapse" - when memories are compressed into reusable fragments, they lose surrounding context needed to judge if they provide valid evidence for current queries, even if they appear relevant due to recurring entities or user states.
AI.method: RaMem uses a four-stage framework: (1) Evidence anchoring - grounds memories in original episodic conditions (event time, mention time, session span, participants), (2) Recall condition induction - derives evidence conditions implied by the query, (3) Validity-aware retrieval - prioritizes context-compatible memories while keeping content-relevant candidates as fallback, (4) Context-preserved synthesis - keeps selected memories' structured context available to the generator.
AI.result: Experiments on long-term memory benchmarks show RaMem consistently improves performance over strong memory baselines, achieving average F1 gains of more than 10% across several backbones.
AI.conclusion: The RaMem framework successfully addresses context collapse in LLM memory systems by reinstating original contextual information, enabling more reliable and valid use of past experiences as evidence for current agent tasks.

title: RIZZ: Routing Interactions to Near Zero-Interference Zones for Continual Adaptation of Black-Box Agents
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as long-lived agents that must adapt across users, tasks, domains, modalities, and feedback regimes without access to model weights. Existing black-box adaptation methods typically optimize a single prompt, maintain an undifferentiated memory, or rely on repeated rollout-heavy search. However, these designs struggle when streams of input are nonstationary, feedback is sparse, and failures from one task family can contaminate behavior on another. We introduce RIZZ (Routing Interactions to Near Zero-interference Zones), a continual adaptation framework for compound language-model systems that learns entirely through verifier-gated memory, routing, and prompt compilation. RIZZ organizes input streams into dynamically spawned memory branches. At inference time, either while online or offline, a context-aware router selects or creates a branch that retrieves branch-local, global, graph-structured, and working-memory context, which is compiled into a bounded prompt together with retrieved task evidence. After the model acts, task verifiers score the output, and only verified interactions can update memory, promote reusable rules, demote harmful rules, or create anti-patterns. This yields a black-box agent that improves through persistent natural-language feedback while explicitly controlling interference. RIZZ targets the regime where adaptation must occur online under context budgets. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework against state-of-the-art baselines on competitive benchmarks.

AI.tldr: RIZZ is a continual adaptation framework for compound LLM systems that organizes input streams into memory branches and uses verifier-gated updates to enable black-box adaptation without model weight access.
AI.motivation: Existing black-box adaptation methods struggle with nonstationary input streams, sparse feedback, and task interference contamination when language models are deployed as long-lived agents needing to adapt across various domains.
AI.method: RIZZ organizes input streams into dynamically spawned memory branches, uses context-aware routing to select/create branches, compiles retrieved context into bounded prompts, and employs task verifiers to gate memory updates while controlling interference.
AI.result: The framework targets online adaptation under context budgets and demonstrates effectiveness against state-of-the-art baselines on competitive benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: RIZZ enables black-box agents to improve through persistent natural-language feedback while explicitly controlling interference, addressing key limitations in continual adaptation for compound language-model systems.

title: $Ω$: Operator-based Mixture Ensemble for Generative Assimilation
summary: Characterizing non-Gaussian posterior distributions in partially observed high-dimensional nonlinear systems remains a fundamental challenge in data assimilation. Ensemble Kalman filters rely on Gaussian approximations that can be inaccurate for strongly non-Gaussian posteriors, whereas particle filters suffer from severe scalability limitations. Recent score-based generative approaches improve posterior characterization but typically require supervised training with ground-truth posterior samples, which are unavailable in most practical applications. We introduce $Ω$ (Operator-based Mixture Ensemble for Generative Assimilation), a scalable framework that integrates conditional Gaussian surrogate modeling, unsupervised score learning, and generative sampling. The conditional Gaussian surrogate provides a nonlinear non-Gaussian baseline approximation while admitting closed-form conditional posterior distributions for the unresolved variables. First, $Ω$ exploits these closed-form conditional distributions to analytically recover the high-dimensional unobserved component, reducing computational cost and mitigating the curse of dimensionality. Second, $Ω$ learns only the residual discrepancy beyond an analytical baseline through denoising score matching using ensemble trajectories alone, eliminating the need for ground-truth posterior samples and substantially reducing the learning burden. Third, $Ω$ reconstructs the full non-Gaussian posterior distribution of both observed and unobserved variables via a Gaussian mixture representation, capturing multimodal, skewed, and heavy-tailed statistics. Finally, $Ω$ employs annealed Langevin sampling to iteratively refine ensemble members from the baseline toward the target posterior. $Ω$ is validated on several turbulent models with intermittency and extreme events, consistently improving posterior accuracy.

AI.tldr: Ω is a novel data assimilation framework that combines conditional Gaussian surrogates with unsupervised score learning to characterize non-Gaussian posterior distributions in high-dimensional nonlinear systems without requiring ground-truth samples.
AI.motivation: Existing methods have limitations: Ensemble Kalman filters rely on inaccurate Gaussian approximations for non-Gaussian posteriors, particle filters suffer from scalability issues, and score-based generative methods require supervised training with ground-truth posterior samples that are unavailable in practical applications.
AI.method: Ω integrates three key components: (1) conditional Gaussian surrogate modeling for nonlinear non-Gaussian baseline approximation with closed-form conditional posteriors, (2) unsupervised denoising score matching using only ensemble trajectories to learn residual discrepancies, and (3) Gaussian mixture representation with annealed Langevin sampling to reconstruct full non-Gaussian posteriors.
AI.result: Ω consistently improves posterior accuracy on turbulent models with intermittency and extreme events, effectively capturing multimodal, skewed, and heavy-tailed statistics while being scalable and not requiring ground-truth posterior samples.
AI.conclusion: Ω provides a scalable, unsupervised framework that overcomes key limitations of existing data assimilation methods by integrating conditional Gaussian approximations with generative modeling, enabling accurate characterization of non-Gaussian posteriors in high-dimensional nonlinear systems.

title: Decomposing Financial Market Dynamics via Mechanism Analysis in an Evolutionary Multi-Agent Simulation
summary: Evolutionary agent-based markets (ABMs) couple several mechanisms -- who reproduces, how price forms, how biased the agents are, how consensus propagates -- yet these are usually fixed by convention, so it is unclear which mechanism controls which emergent property. In a coevolving, endogenous-price simulator with 120 heterogeneous behavioral agents, we make four mechanisms pluggable and run matched 3x20-seed interventions. We find the levers are largely separable. (1) Selection -> diversity: a Quality-Diversity (QD/MAP-Elites) operator robustly raises strategy-mix entropy over truncation top-k (paired Delta entropy +0.27 to +1.12 bits; sign-test p<0.001; CIs exclude 0) and sustains more strategy cycling (strongest in crisis: Delta=+0.070, p=0.0004). (2) Selection does not improve realism: even a per-agent realism reward that provably steers selection does not raise 5-fact realism (Delta_5=-0.11,-0.08,+0.03; not significant). (3) Microstructure -> realism: enabling reflexive price feedback does raise realism (Delta_5=+0.13,+0.20,+0.20; crisis/bull p<0.05, all CIs positive). (4) Behavior -> fragility: amplifying behavioral bias raises a genomic fragility proxy (Delta=+10.5,+11.1,+14.4; bull p<0.001, all CIs positive) while leaving realism flat. The remaining mechanism -- consensus network topology -- shows no robust effect (honest null). The contribution is a decomposition: in these single-mechanism sweeps the mechanisms behave as approximately distinct control knobs over diversity, realism, and fragility.

AI.tldr: ABM market mechanisms are largely separable: selection controls diversity, microstructure affects realism, behavior drives fragility, while consensus topology has minimal impact.
AI.motivation: Evolutionary agent-based markets combine multiple mechanisms (selection, price formation, behavioral bias, consensus propagation), but it's unclear which mechanism controls which emergent property. The paper aims to disentangle these mechanisms to understand their individual contributions to market dynamics.
AI.method: Used a coevolving, endogenous-price simulator with 120 heterogeneous behavioral agents. Made four mechanisms pluggable and ran matched 3x20-seed interventions to test each mechanism's effect on emergent properties.
AI.result: Found separable effects: (1) Selection -> diversity (QD operator raises strategy-mix entropy and sustains strategy cycling); (2) Selection does not improve realism; (3) Microstructure -> realism (price feedback increases realism); (4) Behavior -> fragility (amplifying bias raises fragility proxy); (5) Consensus topology shows no robust effect.
AI.conclusion: The mechanisms in evolutionary agent-based markets behave as approximately distinct control knobs over diversity, realism, and fragility, providing a decomposition framework for understanding ABM market dynamics.

title: An LLM-Explainable DRL Framework for Passenger-Directed Autonomous Driving
summary: Autonomous vehicles offer the potential for safer and more efficient mobility, yet public trust remains limited due to the lack of transparency in their decision-making. This work addresses this issue by combining deep reinforcement learning (DRL) for adaptive driving control with large language model (LLM)-based explainability modules designed to communicate agent behavior to passengers. DRL agents were trained in simulation using a Dueling Double Deep Q-Network to follow distinct driving requests: \textit{fast}, \textit{comfort}, and \textit{stop}. They demonstrated stable learning, safe compliance with traffic rules, and reliable switching between modes within a single trip. In parallel, LLM modules were introduced to interpret passenger requests, determine when explanations were needed, and generate concise, safety-oriented justifications. Results show that this framework, serving as a proof of concept for integrating RL decision-making and LLMs, balances safety, adaptability, and explainability, and is most effective when requests are delayed or overridden due to safety constraints.

AI.tldr: DRL-based autonomous driving with LLM explainability modules improves transparency and public trust by providing safety-oriented justifications for driving decisions.
AI.motivation: Public trust in autonomous vehicles is limited due to lack of transparency in decision-making processes.
AI.method: Combined deep reinforcement learning (DRL) using Dueling Double Deep Q-Network for adaptive driving control with LLM-based modules for explanation generation.
AI.result: DRL agents demonstrated stable learning, safe compliance with traffic rules, and reliable switching between driving modes; LLM modules effectively interpreted requests and generated safety-oriented justifications.
AI.conclusion: The integrated RL+LLM framework successfully balances safety, adaptability, and explainability, particularly effective when safety constraints require overriding passenger requests.

title: Hierarchical Pooling for Sheaf Neural Networks
summary: Sheaf Neural Networks (SNNs) generalize Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) by replacing scalar node signals with stalk-valued signals and by using restriction maps to measure compatibility across edges. Unlike standard graph diffusion, which encourages neighboring node features to become similar, sheaf diffusion promotes consistency through the restriction maps and can therefore model more general relationships between neighboring nodes. However, existing sheaf neural architectures mainly operate at a fixed graph resolution and do not provide a principled pooling mechanism for building hierarchical representations. In this paper, we introduce Hierarchical Sheaf Pool (HiSP), a sheaf-aware pooling framework based on local spectral coarsening. Given a partition of the graph, HiSP constructs each coarse stalk by projecting fine stalk-valued features onto the low-frequency eigenmodes of the cluster-internal sheaf Laplacian. These local modes define a cochain-level prolongation map, which allows the fine sheaf energy to be represented on the coarse space through a Galerkin operator. We further analyze the approximation induced by coarsening by separating truncation loss, due to discarded local modes, from realization loss, due to representing the projected operator as a coarse sheaf. Finally, we implement HiSP as a GNN pooling layer compatible with SNNs and provide a PyG implementation supporting batching, lifted sheaf Laplacians, and hierarchical architectures.

AI.tldr: HiSP introduces a sheaf-aware pooling framework for Sheaf Neural Networks using local spectral coarsening to build hierarchical representations while preserving sheaf structure.
AI.motivation: Current Sheaf Neural Networks operate at fixed graph resolution without principled pooling mechanisms for hierarchical representation learning, limiting their ability to build multi-scale representations.
AI.method: HiSP constructs coarse stalks by projecting fine stalk-valued features onto low-frequency eigenmodes of cluster-internal sheaf Laplacian, creating cochain-level prolongation maps and Galerkin operators for coarse sheaf representation.
AI.result: The framework provides a sheaf-aware pooling layer compatible with SNNs, implemented in PyG with support for batching, lifted sheaf Laplacians, and hierarchical architectures, while analyzing approximation errors through truncation and realization losses.
AI.conclusion: HiSP addresses the hierarchical representation gap in Sheaf Neural Networks by introducing a principled spectral coarsening method that preserves sheaf structure, enabling multi-scale learning while maintaining theoretical understanding of approximation errors.

title: MAS-PromptBench: When Does Prompt Optimization Improve Multi-Agent LLM Systems?
summary: Multi-agent systems (MAS) offer a scalable path forward for agentic AI, comprising multiple LLM-based agents, each assigned a system prompt and a position within a workflow that governs inter-agent coordination and output aggregation. System prompts thus form a critical and accessible optimization surface: they specify agents' roles and behaviors, enabling system-level improvements without model finetuning. Although prompt optimization has shown substantial potential for single LLMs, extending it to MAS poses distinct challenges, notably an exponentially growing search space. It remains unclear whether, when, and by how much prompt optimization improves MAS performance, and how sensitive such gains are to system configuration. In this work, we systematically study system-prompt optimization across a broad range of MAS setups varying in task, workflow, communication protocol, and team size, benchmarking two prompt optimizers that naturally extend state-of-the-art single-agent methods. The results reveal its potential to unlock significant gains while exposing open challenges, characterizing when and how much prompt optimization helps across diverse MAS settings.

AI.tldr: System prompt optimization for multi-agent systems (MAS) can significantly improve performance, but faces exponential search space challenges compared to single-agent optimization.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent systems (MAS) with LLM-based agents offer scalable AI solutions, but their performance depends heavily on system prompts that define agent roles and behaviors. While prompt optimization shows promise for single LLMs, extending it to MAS presents unique challenges like exponentially growing search spaces, and there's limited understanding of when and how much optimization helps in MAS settings.
AI.method: The authors systematically study system-prompt optimization across diverse MAS configurations, varying tasks, workflows, communication protocols, and team sizes. They benchmark two prompt optimizers that extend state-of-the-art single-agent methods to MAS environments.
AI.result: The research reveals that prompt optimization can unlock significant performance gains in MAS, but also exposes open challenges. The study characterizes the conditions under which prompt optimization helps and quantifies the degree of improvement across different MAS settings.
AI.conclusion: System-prompt optimization represents a critical and accessible optimization surface for multi-agent systems, enabling system-level improvements without model fine-tuning.
