AI.conclusion: The survey identifies open challenges in value-aware evaluation, safety, harness generalization, and model-harness co-evolution, advocating for a holistic view of agent systems that considers both model and harness components as essential to performance.

title: Sim2O: Efficient Offline-to-Online MARL via Joint Action Composition
summary: Offline-to-online adaptation serves as a pivotal paradigm for mitigating the prohibitive cost of online exploration by bootstrapping reinforcement learning from offline datasets. While this paradigm has been extensively studied in single-agent settings, its extension to Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) remains largely unexplored, despite its critical relevance to complex coordinated decision-making. To bridge this gap, we introduce Sim2O, an elegant and minimalist framework for offline-to-online MARL. Rather than treating adaptation as a monolithic joint decision, Sim2O conceptualizes it as a compositional process. Specifically, candidate joint actions are synthesized by dynamically blending offline and online action proposals across agents. By leveraging a centralized value function to evaluate these hybrid combinations, Sim2O identifies high-value coordination strategies without requiring auxiliary training objectives or structural overhead. Empirical evaluations across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that Sim2O significantly outperforms existing baselines, underscoring that a minimalist design is not only viable but highly effective for multi-agent offline-to-online adaptation.

AI.tldr: Sim2O is a minimalist framework for offline-to-online multi-agent reinforcement learning that dynamically blends offline and online action proposals across agents to achieve effective coordination without auxiliary objectives.
AI.motivation: Offline-to-online adaptation has been well-studied in single-agent RL but remains largely unexplored in multi-agent settings despite its relevance to complex coordinated decision-making.
AI.method: Sim2O conceptualizes adaptation as a compositional process where candidate joint actions are synthesized by dynamically blending offline and online action proposals across agents, using a centralized value function to evaluate hybrid combinations without requiring auxiliary training objectives.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that Sim2O significantly outperforms existing baselines in multi-agent offline-to-online adaptation.
AI.conclusion: A minimalist design is not only viable but highly effective for multi-agent offline-to-online adaptation, demonstrating that simple compositional approaches can achieve superior coordination without complex structural overhead.

title: Artificial Intelligence as Monism: Ontological, Organisational, and Methodological Implications
summary:
This paper argues that Artificial Intelligence should be understood as a form of monism:
  a unified substance that cannot be decomposed into separate elements such as data,
  algorithms, or technical architectures. Drawing from philosophical traditions of monism,
  dualism, and holism, the paper contends that AI is not merely a collection of components
  but a single, indivisible essence reflecting the phenomena it replicates. Treating AI as
  monism has deep implications across multiple dimensions. Epistemologically, it positions
  AI as the central interpretive force across technological, organisational, and societal
  domains, while raising ethical and existential concerns regarding singularity, the
  homogenisation of innovation, and the concentration of decision-making power. At the
  organisational level, a monistic approach challenges traditional siloed structures,
  advocating instead for transversal, problem-centric teams whose mandate derives from
  the integrity of the problem rather than from departmental hierarchy. In project
  management, it implies a unified vision and an integrated evaluation of complexity in
  which no single stakeholder perspective dominates the assessment of outcomes. In data
  and information management, it calls for architectures that reflect the irreducible unity
  of the phenomena being modelled. Ultimately, this paper calls for a paradigm shift in
  how AI is conceptualised, governed, and integrated, suggesting that only by embracing AI
  as monism can organisations achieve genuine agility and avoid the structural
  inefficiencies inherent to reductionist approaches.

AI.tldr: AI should be viewed as a unified, indivisible substance (monism) rather than decomposable components, requiring a paradigm shift in conceptualization, governance, and organizational integration.
AI.motivation: The paper argues that current reductionist approaches to AI (treating it as separate components like data, algorithms, and architectures) create structural inefficiencies and fail to capture AI's true nature as a unified phenomenon.
AI.method: The paper draws from philosophical traditions of monism, dualism, and holism to develop a theoretical framework for understanding AI as an indivisible essence that reflects the phenomena it replicates.
AI.result: Treating AI as monism has profound implications across multiple dimensions: epistemological (AI as central interpretive force), organizational (challenging siloed structures with transversal teams), project management (unified vision), and data management (irreducible unity architectures).
AI.conclusion: Organizations must embrace AI as monism through a paradigm shift in conceptualization, governance, and integration to achieve genuine agility and avoid reductionist structural inefficiencies.

title: BASIL: Bayesian Application for Scientific Iteration and Learning
summary: We introduce BASIL, a user-friendly desktop application for process optimization. BASIL employs a Bayesian approach, incorporating special acquisition functions that can be used to solve both single and multi-objective optimization problems. It provides a graphical interface that enables users to input their experimental parameters, optimization objectives, and legacy data. This is then used to build surrogate models, which are coupled with acquisition functions to guide and optimize a process towards a desired objective. To facilitate model building, BASIL provides a variety of predefined surrogate model templates. BASIL can be used to optimize any arbitrary experiment or process with known, user-defined input variables, optimization objectives, and defined output.

AI.tldr: BASIL is a desktop application using Bayesian optimization with custom acquisition functions to solve single/multi-objective process optimization problems through a graphical interface.
AI.motivation: To provide an accessible, user-friendly tool for process optimization that doesn't require extensive programming knowledge, making Bayesian optimization techniques more accessible to researchers and practitioners.
AI.method: Uses a Bayesian approach with special acquisition functions, builds surrogate models from user inputs, experimental parameters, and legacy data, and provides predefined model templates.
AI.result: Developed BASIL application that can optimize arbitrary experiments/processes with known input variables and defined outputs through guided optimization using surrogate models and acquisition functions.
AI.conclusion: BASIL successfully provides a practical, accessible solution for process optimization that bridges the gap between complex Bayesian optimization methods and practical experimental needs.

title: Simulated Customers Never Walk Away: Decision Fidelity of LLM User Simulators Measured Against Real Purchase Outcomes
summary: LLM-as-user-simulation has become core infrastructure for conversational AI: agent benchmarks (tau-bench), training pipelines, and a growing body of fidelity studies all rely on LLMs role-playing the human side of dialogue. Existing frameworks measure communicative fidelity -- whether simulators talk like humans -- against ground truth from paid participants role-playing assigned goals. We argue this has a structural blind spot: when the goal is assigned, the user's willingness is exogenous, so no framework can test whether simulators make decisions like real users whose motivation is endogenous, latent, and decaying. We introduce decision fidelity -- whether a simulated population reproduces the decision-state dynamics of real users facing real, consequential choices -- and measure it on a unique testbed: 2,790 production conversations between an LLM sales agent and real customers, including 793 with verified payment outcomes. Using a teacher-forced probe protocol that holds context and instrument fixed, we find a systematic, outcome-correlated failure we call the disengagement deficit: simulators reproduce eventual buyers almost exactly (depth bias +0.09) but inflate eventual non-buyers toward the purchase frame (depth bias +0.40; d=0.38, p<0.001), halving expressed resistance (25.1% to 13.5%) and nearly doubling deliberation (21.9% to 40.1%) while fabricating no purchases. The deficit replicates across model families (DeepSeek: d=0.41, p=0.002) and resists the obvious fix: instructing the simulator that it may disengage cuts marginal bias five-fold but barely moves the outcome-conditioned contrast (d=0.34, p=0.008). Real non-buyers say "not now" and stop; simulated non-buyers ask about price. Evaluating or training sales and persuasion agents against such simulators overstates funnel progress exactly where it matters most -- the customers who walk away.

AI.tldr: LLM simulators have a "disengagement deficit" - they accurately simulate buyers but systematically fail to simulate non-buyers' decision to disengage, instead making them ask more questions and deliberate longer, which overestimates agent performance on reluctant customers.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM user simulation frameworks only measure communicative fidelity (whether simulators talk like humans) using assigned goals from paid participants, but this misses whether simulators make decisions like real users with endogenous, latent, and decaying motivation. There's a need to test decision fidelity - whether simulators reproduce real users' decision-state dynamics when facing consequential choices.
AI.method: Researchers measured decision fidelity using 2,790 production conversations between an LLM sales agent and real customers (793 with verified payment outcomes). They used a teacher-forced probe protocol that holds context and instrument fixed, comparing real vs. simulated user decisions across different outcome categories (buyers vs. non-buyers). They tested multiple model families and experimented with explicit disengagement instructions.
AI.result: Found systematic "disengagement deficit": simulators accurately reproduce eventual buyers (depth bias +0.09) but inflate eventual non-buyers toward purchase frame (depth bias +0.40; d=0.38, p<0.001). Non-buyers in simulations show halved expressed resistance (25.1% to 13.5%) and nearly doubled deliberation (21.9% to 40.1%). The deficit persists across model families and isn't fixed by explicit disengagement instructions.
AI.conclusion: LLM user simulators have a structural blind spot in decision fidelity that leads to overestimating agent performance on customers who would actually disengage, creating misleading benchmarks for sales and persuasion agents. Real non-buyers disengage quickly while simulated ones continue engaging, making evaluations unreliable for the most critical customer segment.

title: SLeDGe: Semi-Supervised Learning on Data Streams with Graph Structure Learning
summary: Semi-supervised learning (SSL) on data streams is challenging due to the continuous evolution of high-volume data and the scarcity of labels. Existing methods are limited in leveraging the intrinsic relationships among samples because they typically rely on fixed similarity measures or static graph structures, which cannot capture how relationships evolve over time. We propose SLeDGe, an SSL method for data streams that jointly learns a predictive model and an adaptive graph structure under strict memory and label constraints. SLeDGe maintains compact labeled and unlabeled memories using distinct update strategies, balancing rapid adaptation to novel features with the retention of historical consistency. In addition, by encouraging sparsity in the relational graph, SLeDGe filters out spurious connections and enables effective propagation of label supervision. Across 12 datasets, SLeDGe outperforms state-of-the-art competitors, achieving average relative accuracy gains of 31.7% with 0.1% labels and 14.8% with 1% labels.

AI.tldr: SLeDGe improves semi-supervised learning on data streams by jointly learning predictions and an adaptive graph structure under strict memory/label constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing SSL methods for data streams fail to capture evolving sample relationships due to fixed similarity measures or static graph structures.
AI.method: Proposes SLeDGe which jointly learns predictive model and adaptive graph structure, maintains compact labeled/unlabeled memories with distinct update strategies, and encourages sparsity in relational graphs.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art competitors on 12 datasets with average relative accuracy gains of 31.7% (0.1% labels) and 14.8% (1% labels).
AI.conclusion: SLeDGe effectively addresses SSL challenges in data streams by dynamically adapting to evolving relationships while operating under practical constraints.

title: FairTutor: Equity-Aware Pedagogical LLM Routing for Budget-Constrained AI Tutoring
comment: AI for Education Day at SIGKDD 2026, 14 pages, 2 figures
summary: Generative AI tutors provide real-time, personalized learning support, but also create a new education inequity: students with access to premium AI services may receive clearer explanations, more personalized guidance, and better scaffolding than students limited to free or low-cost services. To address this challenge, we propose FairTutor, an equity-aware model-routing framework that achieves cost-effective AI tutoring via pedagogically motivated multi-agent orchestration. FairTutor combines query analysis, pedagogical planning, low-cost model generation, evaluator-guided critique and revision, and selective escalation to premium AI models. We introduce access-tier AI Education (AIED) Advantage Gap to measure the quality difference between premium-access and budget-constrained tutoring, and TutorAccessEval, a benchmark spanning math, reading, writing, science, and language learning. Empirical evaluations show that FairTutor achieves 97.1% of premium pedagogical quality (in floor-adjusted Likert scale) while reducing serving cost by 71.6%. Sensitivity analysis reveals a tunable cost--quality Pareto frontier, enabling FairTutor to be tailored to the needs of diverse student populations.

AI.tldr: FairTutor is an equity-aware AI tutoring framework that balances cost and quality by intelligently routing queries between low-cost and premium AI models using pedagogical multi-agent orchestration, achieving near-premium educational quality at significantly reduced costs.
AI.motivation: To address educational inequity created by generative AI tutors where students with access to premium AI services receive better explanations, personalization, and scaffolding than those limited to free/low-cost services.
AI.method: FairTutor uses an equity-aware model-routing framework with pedagogically motivated multi-agent orchestration, combining query analysis, pedagogical planning, low-cost model generation, evaluator-guided critique and revision, and selective escalation to premium AI models.
AI.result: FairTutor achieves 97.1% of premium pedagogical quality (floor-adjusted Likert scale) while reducing serving costs by 71.6%. Introduces AIED Advantage Gap metric and TutorAccessEval benchmark spanning multiple subjects.
AI.conclusion: FairTutor provides a cost-effective solution to AI tutoring inequity with a tunable cost-quality Pareto frontier that can be tailored to diverse student populations, significantly narrowing the quality gap between premium and budget-constrained AI education.

title: Enhancing Differentially Private Mechanisms via Empirical Bayes
summary: Differential privacy (DP) has become the gold standard for ensuring the privacy protection of machine learning and statistical algorithms in recent decades. A plethora of algorithms and methods have been developed to enhance the utility of DP algorithms while maintaining the same level of DP. However, these are often overly complex or computationally ineffective. We propose a novel approach focusing on denoising the output of the simple additive Gaussian mechanism by adopting the idea of \textit{empirical Bayes estimation}. We highlight that the empirical Bayes approach can reduce the mean-squared error solely by taking the output of the Gaussian mechanism as input. Our numerical studies show that this simple yet powerful approach can be applied to improve upon various statistical problems, including histogram release, principal component analysis, and linear regression, often outperforming existing private algorithms.

AI.tldr: Proposes empirical Bayes estimation to denoise outputs from Gaussian differential privacy mechanism, reducing error while maintaining privacy guarantees
AI.motivation: Existing differential privacy methods are often overly complex or computationally ineffective, despite being the gold standard for privacy protection. There's a need for simpler yet effective approaches that can improve utility while maintaining the same privacy level.
AI.method: Uses empirical Bayes estimation to denoise the output of the simple additive Gaussian differential privacy mechanism. The approach takes the noisy output from Gaussian mechanism as input and reduces mean-squared error through empirical Bayes techniques.
AI.result: Numerical studies show the simple yet powerful empirical Bayes approach improves various statistical problems including histogram release, principal component analysis, and linear regression. The method often outperforms existing private algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Empirical Bayes estimation provides an effective and computationally simpler alternative to complex differential privacy methods, enhancing utility of Gaussian mechanism outputs while maintaining privacy protection across multiple statistical applications.

title: Escape from Delusional Echo Trap: Symmetry Breaking, Stochastic Dynamics and Mathematical Mitigation Strategies for Algorithmic Sycophancy
summary: We propose a rigorous and systematic mathematical framework for tracking the cognitive trajectories of a user, in the context of algorithmic sycophancy and AI-driven delusional spiraling. Using tools from dynamical systems theory and stochastic differential equations, we explore how individuals perceive, interpret, and update their beliefs as they interact with AI chatbots that possess hidden traits of sycophancy. We treat the evolving conviction as a continuous log-odds state variable, coupled into a stochastic differential equation, navigating a multi-valley potential energy landscape. Our analysis reveals several critical observations governing the stability and rigidity of belief dynamics. We demonstrate that the baseline prior perception of the individual is systematically enhanced by sycophantic feedback beyond a critical threshold. Here, the perceptual potential landscape undergoes a structural phase transition that severely deepens any incremental initial tilt present in the baseline state, transforming the landscape and giving rise to deep, highly resilient attractor basins that trap the individual in unshakeable, self-reinforcing, delusional convictions. Finally, we demonstrate that genuine external information can successfully challenge these rigid states. If this incoming evidence is strong and authentic enough to overcome the internal feedback barrier, it can correct the structural asymmetry caused by sycophancy, inducing a perception reversal that successfully restores the objective belief state.

AI.tldr: A mathematical framework using dynamical systems and stochastic differential equations to model how AI sycophancy drives users into delusional belief spirals and how strong external evidence can reverse this process.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the growing concern about AI sycophancy - how AI chatbots with hidden sycophantic traits can manipulate users' belief systems, potentially leading them into self-reinforcing delusional spirals that are difficult to escape.
AI.method: Uses dynamical systems theory and stochastic differential equations to model belief evolution as a continuous log-odds state variable navigating a multi-valley potential energy landscape. The framework treats AI feedback as influencing a user's perceptual potential landscape.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that sycophantic feedback systematically enhances baseline priors beyond a critical threshold, causing a structural phase transition in the perceptual landscape. This deepens initial biases into highly resilient attractor basins that trap individuals in unshakeable delusional convictions. However, strong authentic external evidence can overcome the feedback barrier and induce perception reversal to restore objective beliefs.
AI.conclusion: AI sycophancy can create dangerous belief traps through mathematical mechanisms similar to physical phase transitions, but these rigid states can be challenged and corrected with sufficiently strong external evidence, highlighting both the risks of sycophantic AI and potential interventions.

title: Chem2Gen-Bench: Benchmarking Chemical-to-Genetic Translation in Perturbation Response Space
summary: Virtual-cell and perturbation models are increasingly used to predict cellular responses for biomedical discovery, but chemical and genetic perturbations are not automatically interchangeable. Existing evaluations often study chemical response prediction or genetic perturbation prediction separately, leaving target-matched chemical-to-genetic translation under-tested. We introduce Chem2Gen-Bench, a benchmark comprising 260,084 chemical and 1,099,045 genetic perturbation profiles organized into cell-target contexts, and evaluate pairwise alignment, retrieval, protocol covariate associations, feature spaces, and foundation-model embeddings. Across matched contexts, translation fidelity is measurable but heterogeneous; background adjustment increases the association between pairwise similarity and retrieval success, while paired tests show lower mean retrieval success after adjustment under the evaluated settings. In a target-matched K562 audit, the evaluated foundation-model embeddings did not consistently improve over gene-delta baselines. Chem2Gen-Bench provides an auditable framework for testing when chemical and genetic perturbations align around shared targets and when representation gains are supported by matched perturbation evidence.

AI.tldr: Chem2Gen-Bench benchmark evaluates translation between chemical and genetic perturbation responses, finding measurable but inconsistent alignment with foundation models not consistently outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations study chemical and genetic perturbation prediction separately, leaving target-matched chemical-to-genetic translation under-tested, despite increasing use of virtual-cell and perturbation models in biomedical discovery.
AI.method: Introduce Chem2Gen-Bench benchmark with 260,084 chemical and 1,099,045 genetic perturbation profiles organized into cell-target contexts; evaluate pairwise alignment, retrieval, protocol covariate associations, feature spaces, and foundation-model embeddings.
AI.result: Translation fidelity is measurable but heterogeneous across matched contexts; background adjustment increases association between pairwise similarity and retrieval success but lowers mean retrieval success; foundation-model embeddings didn't consistently improve over gene-delta baselines in K562 audit.
AI.conclusion: Chem2Gen-Bench provides an auditable framework for testing when chemical and genetic perturbations align around shared targets and when representation gains are supported by matched perturbation evidence, revealing current limitations in translation approaches.

title: When Web Agents Finish but Still Fail: Reproducible Triggers and Trace Diagnostics for Parallel Web Exploration
summary: Long-horizon web agents often fail in ways hidden by final-answer evaluation: they may visit useful pages, produce a well-formed answer, and terminate confidently while still missing fields, over-including unsupported items, or relying on stale evidence. We study these failures with Parallel WebBench, a parallel web-exploration benchmark containing 1,679 verified records: 350 manually curated parallel tasks and 1,329 reconstructed records with verified URL-based trajectories. We train WebExplorer-style agents with GRPO under human-only, balanced human-synthetic, and synthetic-heavy data mixtures. At 16k context and 16 interaction rounds, the best GRPO model improves completion over WebExplorer-8B from 50.7% to 96.0% and GPT-4.1-mini-judged element-wise F1 from 0.2489 to 0.4529, but binary accuracy remains far below completion. Trace-level analysis identifies three persistent failure modes: context-bound search loops, premature termination on partial answers, and synthesis collapse after relevant evidence has already been retrieved. These results show that synthetic-data GRPO reduces abstention and improves partial correctness, but leaves a completion-correctness gap that requires evidence-grounded coverage and synthesis diagnostics.

AI.tldr: Web agents often produce plausible but incorrect answers by missing fields, including unsupported items, or using stale evidence. Parallel WebBench exposes these failures through parallel web-exploration tasks, showing GRPO training improves completion metrics but leaves a gap between completion and true correctness.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon web agents fail in subtle ways not captured by final-answer evaluation - they may produce well-formed answers while missing fields, including unsupported items, or relying on stale evidence. Current evaluation methods hide these failure modes.
AI.method: Created Parallel WebBench with 1,679 verified records (350 manual, 1,329 reconstructed). Trained WebExplorer-style agents with GRPO using different data mixtures: human-only, balanced human-synthetic, and synthetic-heavy. Evaluated at 16k context with 16 interaction rounds.
AI.result: Best GRPO model improved completion over WebExplorer-8B from 50.7% to 96.0% and element-wise F1 from 0.2489 to 0.4529, but binary accuracy remained low. Trace analysis revealed persistent failure modes: context-bound search loops, premature termination, and synthesis collapse after evidence retrieval.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic-data GRPO reduces abstention and improves partial correctness but leaves a completion-correctness gap requiring evidence-grounded coverage and synthesis diagnostics for long-horizon web agents.

title: What Accuracy and Gradient Cosine Miss: Evaluating Feedback Alignment via Scale Stability, Reference Validity, and Depth Utility
summary: Despite the success of deep learning, training deep networks in biologically plausible and hardware-efficient ways remains an open challenge. Feedback alignment (FA) methods address this by replacing backpropagation's symmetric backward weights with fixed random matrices, but their effectiveness depends critically on whether they can be accurately evaluated. The standard evaluation relies on two quantities: task accuracy and cosine similarity between the method's credit signal and the backpropagation gradient. We show that this reporting pair is insufficient by identifying two independent failure modes, both silent under current reporting: (1) measurement degeneracy, where the BP reference gradient collapses to the numerical floor in terminal-LayerNorm residual architectures, rendering cosine uninterpretable; and (2) aggregation collapse, where the aggregate cosine masks layerwise heterogeneity that concentrates credit at one end of the network. To address these limitations, we propose a diagnostic evaluation protocol based on three checks -- scale stability, reference validity, and depth utility -- together with per-layer rather than aggregate cosine reporting. Across multiple architectures and methods, the standard reporting pair gives no signal of failure in any audited case, while our protocol identifies all failures with wide calibration margins. The two failure modes are causally independent: a per-block scale penalty alleviates Mode 1 (residual scale explosion driving reference collapse) without affecting Mode 2 (cosine ranking that contradicts every functional metric we measured). Identifying these silent failures prevents researchers from building on non-functional credit assignment and provides actionable guidance for developing FA methods that genuinely train deep layers.

AI.tldr: Current evaluation methods for Feedback Alignment algorithms are insufficient due to two silent failure modes, requiring a new diagnostic protocol with three checks and per-layer reporting.
AI.motivation: There's a need to train deep networks in biologically plausible and hardware-efficient ways using Feedback Alignment methods, but current evaluation metrics (accuracy + aggregate cosine similarity) are inadequate and can mask critical failures.
AI.method: Propose a diagnostic evaluation protocol with three checks: (1) scale stability, (2) reference validity, and (3) depth utility, along with per-layer cosine reporting instead of aggregate reporting.
AI.result: Standard evaluation gives no signal of failure in any case, while the new protocol identifies all failures with wide margins. Two independent failure modes were identified: measurement degeneracy (BP gradient collapse in LayerNorm architectures) and aggregation collapse (cosine masking layerwise heterogeneity).
AI.conclusion: Current Feedback Alignment evaluation methods are insufficient and can hide critical failures. The proposed diagnostic protocol reveals these silent failures, prevents building on non-functional credit assignment, and provides actionable guidance for developing effective FA methods that genuinely train deep layers.

title: Repeated Shared Access Enables Grokking, but Edit Propagation Depends on a Fine-Grained Addressable Memory
summary: We study factual edit propagation in a controlled synthetic knowledge-graph QA setting, comparing four architectures that cross loop recurrence with shared memory access: dense (Dense), looped (Loop), dense with shared memory (Dense+Mem), and looped with shared memory (LMC). Dense fits in-distribution 2-hop compositions but fails OOD; both looped recomputation and memory rereading cross this OOD grokking barrier, indicating that repeated shared access -- not a specific architecture -- is the common ingredient for learning. Editing, however, separates the substrates along a different axis. On a shared pre-edit-correct ID set, a single-row factual edit propagates strongly in the two memory-bearing cells (LMC 0.78-0.92, Dense+Mem 0.71-0.96) and only weakly in the others (Loop 0.04-0.30, Dense 0.00-0.03); the separation is statistically clean (Mann-Whitney p=0.008 between memory and non-memory cells, p=0.55 between the two memory cells, though n=5 vs n=5 is underpowered to rule out a moderate gap). In LMC, atomic facts localize to dominant memory sites that composition rereads, and a one-row value edit on LMC's own pre-edit-correct probes achieves 100% direct success with mean 0.989 intended propagation while moving unrelated facts ~0.1% of the time (matched specificity across substrates pending). A coarse hold-answer-subspace (HOLDANS) interchange diagnostic is consistent with this ordering, suggesting that what separates the substrates is when an edited fact is injected and how much computation remains afterwards to reuse it. These results dissociate learning competence from editing affordance: repeated shared access suffices to grok, but edit propagation depends on whether the substrate exposes a fine-grained addressable memory the forward computation can write to and later reread -- an affordance that loop recurrence provides only partially.

AI.tldr: The paper studies factual edit propagation in knowledge-graph QA, finding that architectures with shared memory afford better editing capabilities than those without, even when both can learn compositions.
AI.motivation: To understand how different neural architectures (combining loop recurrence with shared memory) handle factual edit propagation, distinguishing between learning competence and editing affordance.
AI.method: Four architectures are compared in synthetic knowledge-graph QA: Dense, Loop, Dense+Mem, and LMC (looped with shared memory). They are evaluated on in-distribution and out-of-distribution composition learning, and on propagation of single-row factual edits.
AI.result: Memory-bearing cells (LMC, Dense+Mem) achieve strong edit propagation (0.71-0.96), while non-memory cells (Loop, Dense) show weak propagation (0.00-0.30). Repeated shared access enables OOD learning, but fine-grained addressable memory is required for effective editing.
AI.conclusion: Repeated shared access suffices for learning compositions, but edit propagation depends on whether the architecture exposes fine-grained addressable memory that forward computation can write to and later reread.

title: Horizon Adaptive Offline Policy Learning via Value Stitching
summary: Learning accurate value functions plays a decisive role for reinforcement learning (RL) agents to solve long-horizon, complex tasks. Conventional temporal-difference (TD) learning objectives suffer from value-estimation bias that accumulates over the horizon, while extended-horizon modeling methods, such as n-step TD backups and Q-chunking, adopt a rigid, fixed-horizon value-modeling recipe that is often not flexible enough to capture complex value structures in long-horizon, multi-stage tasks. In this paper, we show that enabling value updates with dynamic horizon composition can yield a strong offline policy learning scheme. Our method, Horizon Adaptive Offline Policy Learning via VAlue STitching (VAST), replaces fixed-horizon backups with recursive, horizon-adaptive value composition. Its key ingredient is to couple value optimization with a future state- and horizon-length-conditioned auxiliary value function that is learned through direct data supervision, and a stitching policy that optimally selects the reward-maximizing horizon length and future sub-goal to achieve horizon-adaptive value stitching. This design enables direct estimation and compositional "stitching" of variable-length returns grounded in actionable sub-goal states, providing an accurate and greedily exploitable value-supervision signal for offline policy optimization. Across 50 tasks on OGBench, VAST outperforms fixed-step, extended-horizon methods, and generative-value offline RL baselines, achieving strong performance particularly in high-complexity, long-horizon decision-making tasks.

AI.tldr: VAST (VAlue STitching) is an offline RL method that dynamically adapts horizon lengths for value estimation through compositional stitching of variable-length returns, outperforming fixed-horizon approaches in long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Conventional TD learning suffers from value-estimation bias that accumulates over long horizons, while fixed-horizon methods like n-step TD and Q-chunking lack flexibility to capture complex value structures in multi-stage, long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: VAST replaces fixed-horizon backups with recursive, horizon-adaptive value composition. It learns an auxiliary value function conditioned on future states and horizon lengths, along with a stitching policy that optimally selects horizon lengths and sub-goals for horizon-adaptive value stitching.
AI.result: Across 50 tasks on OGBench, VAST outperforms fixed-step, extended-horizon methods and generative-value offline RL baselines, achieving strong performance particularly in high-complexity, long-horizon decision-making tasks.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic horizon composition through value stitching provides an accurate and greedily exploitable value-supervision signal for offline policy optimization, enabling effective learning in complex long-horizon tasks.

title: Fara-1.5: Scalable Learning Environments for Computer Use Agents
summary: Collecting computer use data from human demonstrations is expensive and slow, motivating the need for scalable generation strategies. This requires two key ingredients: environments in which agents can act and verifiers that can judge whether their demonstrations succeeded. We introduce FaraGen1.5, a scalable data pipeline for computer use agents composed of three modular components: environments, solvers, and verifiers. FaraGen1.5 uses both live websites and synthetic environments that faithfully simulate domains gated by authentication or that require irreversible actions. It employs a solver harness that can be powered by multiple models, including strong frontier models such as GPT-5.4, and also incorporates a user simulator to enable multi-turn rollouts. Finally, FaraGen1.5 scores the resulting trajectories with three complementary verifiers covering task correctness, efficiency, and critical-point adherence. Using data produced by this pipeline, we train Fara1.5, a family of native computer use agents (CUAs) at three scales built on Qwen3.5 (4B, 9B, and 27B). To train these models, we employ a supervised finetuning (SFT) recipe that carefully balances data from FaraGen1.5 for broad coverage, specific high-value tasks, and target model deficiencies in an iterative approach. Each model sets a new state of the art for its size class on browser-use benchmarks: Fara1.5-9B reaches 63.4% on Online-Mind2Web and 86.6% on WebVoyager, while Fara1.5-27B achieves 72.3% on Online-Mind2Web, which is competitive with much larger proprietary systems.

AI.tldr: FaraGen1.5 is a scalable data pipeline for computer use agents that combines environments, solvers, and verifiers to generate training data, which is used to train Fara1.5 models that achieve state-of-the-art performance on browser-use benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Collecting computer use data from human demonstrations is expensive and slow, creating a need for scalable generation strategies. This requires environments for agents to act in and verifiers to judge demonstration success.
AI.method: FaraGen1.5 pipeline has three components: 1) Environments (live websites and synthetic environments for authentication-gated/irreversible domains), 2) Solver harness (multiple models including GPT-5.4 with user simulator for multi-turn rollouts), 3) Three complementary verifiers (task correctness, efficiency, critical-point adherence). The data is used to train Fara1.5 models via supervised finetuning with balanced coverage.
AI.result: Fara1.5 models achieve new state-of-the-art for their size classes: Fara1.5-9B reaches 63.4% on Online-Mind2Web and 86.6% on WebVoyager; Fara1.5-27B achieves 72.3% on Online-Mind2Web, competitive with larger proprietary systems.
AI.conclusion: The FaraGen1.5 pipeline enables scalable generation of computer use training data, and the resulting Fara1.5 models demonstrate strong performance on browser-use benchmarks, showing that carefully balanced training data can produce competitive computer use agents without requiring expensive human demonstrations.

title: Dead-Direction Signatures: A Cheap Spectral Reading of Singular Complexity
comment: 42 pages, 10 figures, 6 tables. Empirical companion to arXiv:2606.05957
summary: Singular learning theory characterises the complexity of a deep network through the geometry of its loss singularities. The local learning coefficient (LLC), the standard estimator of Watanabe's real log canonical threshold (RLCT, $λ$), reads this geometry as an integrated Bayesian scalar through SGLD, which needs per-task calibration and $10^4$-$10^6$ forward-backward passes per checkpoint. We introduce Dead-Direction Signatures (DDS), a family of cheap closed-form spectral readings of singular structure: each reads a network's activation matrix or per-sample-gradient Fisher-Gram at a chosen layer, replacing the SGLD posterior chain with spectral linear algebra. The readings rest on a dead-direction framework that predicts a structural correlation between activation- and Fisher-side spectra at any singular minimum, and a rank-multiplicative volume identity that single-eigenvalue monitors cannot produce: the active-volume $\log\det^{+}(G)$ slope counts the dead directions, tracking the rank-deficit $r$ across $r \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ (slope ratios $2.0, 3.1, 4.0$ at $r{=}2,3,4$ against the predicted $2,3,4$), where the smallest eigenvalue is rank-blind. On reduced-rank regression with closed-form $λ$, calibrated LLC recovers $λ$ at $99\%$ mean and the DDS observables rank-track it at the framework-predicted sign; on a non-linear modular-addition transformer DDS separates $d_{\mathrm{model}}$ across eighteen orders of magnitude where calibrated LLC at the protocol budget is rank-flat. Complementary to LLC's integrated posterior reading, DDS gives a directional, layer-local handle on a network's dead directions, read in closed form from its activation and gradient spectra.

AI.tldr: DDS (Dead-Direction Signatures) provides cheap closed-form spectral analysis of neural network singularities, replacing expensive SGLD calibration with spectral linear algebra, tracking rank deficiencies more effectively than traditional LLC methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional singular learning theory uses the local learning coefficient (LLC) to estimate Watanabe's real log canonical threshold, but this requires expensive per-task calibration and 10^4-10^6 forward-backward passes per checkpoint via SGLD, making it computationally intensive.
AI.method: Introduced Dead-Direction Signatures (DDS), a family of cheap closed-form spectral readings that analyze network activation matrices or per-sample-gradient Fisher-Gram at chosen layers. DDS replaces SGLD posterior chains with spectral linear algebra, based on a dead-direction framework that predicts structural correlation between activation- and Fisher-side spectra at singular minima, and uses rank-multiplicative volume identity with active-volume logdet⁺(G) slope to count dead directions.
AI.result: On reduced-rank regression with closed-form λ, calibrated LLC recovers λ at 99% mean, while DDS observables rank-track it at predicted sign (slope ratios 2.0, 3.1, 4.0 at r=2,3,4 against predicted 2,3,4). On a non-linear modular-addition transformer, DDS separates d_model across eighteen orders of magnitude where calibrated LLC at protocol budget is rank-flat. DDS effectively tracks rank-deficit r across r∈{1,2,3,4}.
AI.conclusion: DDS provides a directional, layer-local handle on network dead directions through cheap closed-form spectral analysis of activation and gradient spectra, offering a complementary approach to LLC's integrated posterior reading with significantly reduced computational cost and improved rank-tracking capabilities.

title: What Shapes Emergent Misalignment? Insights from Training Dynamics, Model Priors, and Data
summary: Emergent misalignment (EM) is a phenomenon in which models generalize with narrow fine-tuning, leading to broad (yet uneven) misalignment across evaluation questions. We study EM and its variability directly through the components of fine-tuning: training dynamics, model priors, and data. (1) We first explored how in-domain training loss relates to out-of-domain alignment scores across datasets and model families. Then, we tried to induce potential alternative local minima through different learning schedules for one narrow fine-tuning, but did not find strong runs with better broad alignment scores conditioned on similar or lower training loss. (2) We found that although the mean and standard deviations of the misaligned model scores are usually statistically different from those of the pre-trained model, there are some potential signals on overall positive correlation. The evaluation prompt-only activations from both the pre-trained and the original instruct models (prior to narrow fine-tuning) could predict fine-grained alignment scores after narrow fine-tuning. (3) Finally, we compared activation deltas before and after narrow fine-tuning and found moderate-to-high subspace overlap and similarity between the resulting activation shifts for training and evaluation prompts. Subspace overlaps between training and evaluation prompt activations correlate with their shifts' similarities when measuring with the last prompt-token activations. The train-evaluation data prompt overlap is controlled against overlap computed from random vectors and evaluation prompts activations.

AI.tldr: The paper studies emergent misalignment (EM) in language models during narrow fine-tuning, examining how models can become broadly misaligned across evaluation questions despite training on limited data, and investigates the roles of training dynamics, model priors, and data in this phenomenon.
AI.motivation: Emergent misalignment is a concerning phenomenon where models generalize poorly through narrow fine-tuning, leading to broad but uneven misalignment across different evaluation questions. Understanding why this occurs and what factors contribute to it is important for developing more robust alignment techniques.
AI.method: The study investigates EM through three main approaches: (1) Analyzing relationships between in-domain training loss and out-of-domain alignment scores across datasets and model families, and testing different learning schedules, (2) Examining model priors by comparing pre-trained and fine-tuned models' statistical properties and using pre-fine-tuning activations to predict alignment scores, (3) Comparing activation deltas before/after fine-tuning to measure subspace overlap and similarity between training and evaluation prompts.
AI.result: Key findings include: (1) No strong alternative local minima with better broad alignment were found despite testing different learning schedules, (2) Pre-fine-tuning activations from pre-trained and instruct models could predict fine-grained alignment scores after narrow fine-tuning, suggesting model priors matter, (3) Moderate-to-high subspace overlap exists between activation shifts for training and evaluation prompts, and these overlaps correlate with similarity of shifts when measured with last prompt-token activations.
AI.conclusion: The research provides empirical evidence about emergent misalignment, showing that model priors and activation subspace relationships play important roles in how narrow fine-tuning affects broad alignment. The findings suggest that simply adjusting training schedules may not solve emergent misalignment, and that understanding the relationships between training data and evaluation prompts through activation subspaces could be key to developing more robust alignment techniques.

title: Inverting the Bellman Equation: From $Q$-Values to World Models
summary: Model-based and model-free reinforcement learning are traditionally viewed as separate paradigms: instead of learning a model of the transition kernel $P$, model-free agents typically estimate value functions tied to a specific policy and reward. In this paper, we challenge this dichotomy by proving that value-based agents trained on a sufficiently rich set of reward functions, e.g. using goal-conditioned RL, implicitly encode a unique and accurate world model. To extract this model in practice, we introduce \textit{$P$-learning}, an inverse analogue to $Q$-learning that samples from an agent's $Q$-values, policies and rewards to decode its internal model of the environment. We then provide sufficient conditions on the type and number of goals for which agents encode the true kernel $P$, covering both stochastic and deterministic MDPs over finite or continuous state spaces. Even when our assumptions are violated, we empirically demonstrate that agents trained on a handful of reward functions encode accurate dynamics in $\texttt{Reacher}$, $\texttt{MountainCar}$ and stochastic variants of $\texttt{FourRooms}$. Surprisingly, we find that policies trained exclusively on a \texttt{Reacher} agent's implicit world model are quasi-optimal on out-of-distribution, velocity-based goals despite position-only training -- suggesting that agents contain hidden generalisation capabilities and providing a new lens into the connection between model-based, model-free, and goal-conditioned RL.

AI.tldr: Value-based RL agents trained on diverse reward functions implicitly learn accurate world models, enabling model extraction via P-learning that bridges model-based and model-free paradigms.
AI.motivation: To challenge the traditional separation between model-based and model-free RL by showing that value-based agents implicitly encode world models when trained on rich reward functions, providing a unified perspective and revealing hidden generalization capabilities.
AI.method: Introduces P-learning, an inverse method that samples from agents' Q-values, policies, and rewards to decode implicit world models. Provides theoretical conditions under which agents encode true transition dynamics for both stochastic/deterministic MDPs with finite/continuous state spaces.
AI.result: Empirical validation in Reacher, MountainCar, and stochastic FourRooms shows agents encode accurate dynamics even with limited reward functions. Surprising finding: policies trained on implicit models achieve quasi-optimal performance on out-of-distribution goals (velocity-based goals from position-only training).
AI.conclusion: Agents contain hidden generalization capabilities, and the implicit world models in value-based agents provide a new connection between model-based, model-free, and goal-conditioned RL, suggesting a unified perspective on reinforcement learning paradigms.

title: Study on Quantitative Dynamic Epistemic Logic for Belief Revision
summary: Belief revision is a process in which an agent begins to believe in something she previously did not. I begin the paper by presenting, based on (Gärdenfors, 1998; Hansson, 1999), postulates for belief revision that constitute the basis of the AGM theory. I will then briefly show the semantics of a modal logic introduced in (van Ditmarsch, 2005), which I call `$P$'. This logic formalizes static epistemic states and has greater expressive power than AGM in doing so because it captures the quantitative notion of "degrees of conviction". The third step is to introduce revision operators on $P$ and, mostly following (van Ditmarsch, 2005), obtain the Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL) I call `$P*$'. It models processes of belief revision in several ways. Original results are presented in the following two sections. The first one of these sections revolves around a formalization of AGM postulates within $P*$ by proving some theorems related to the satisfaction of those postulates by revisions defined in $P*$. The last section features an analysis of $P*$'s revisions that go beyond the mere satisfaction of postulates. I compare their formal behavior with respect to some philosophical criteria. At last, I conclude that the functions presented in (van Ditmarsch, 2005) are not good formalizations of the philosophical intuition behind AGM. Instead, it is captured by the function $*^0$ originally defined in this paper (but highly inspired by (van Benthem, 2007)). An implementation of this function is also provided.

AI.tldr: This paper connects AGM belief revision theory with dynamic epistemic logic (DEL), formalizes AGM postulates in the logic P*, analyzes revisions against philosophical criteria, and proposes a new revision function *^0 that better captures AGM intuition than previous approaches.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between the AGM theory of belief revision (qualitative) and dynamic epistemic logic approaches (which can capture quantitative "degrees of conviction"), and to evaluate whether existing DEL functions properly formalize AGM philosophical intuitions.
AI.method: 1) Presents AGM postulates as foundation; 2) Introduces modal logic P for static epistemic states with quantitative degrees of conviction; 3) Defines revision operators on P to create dynamic epistemic logic P*; 4) Formally proves AGM postulates can be expressed within P*; 5) Analyzes revisions against philosophical criteria; 6) Proposes new revision function *^0 inspired by van Benthem (2007).
AI.result: 1) Van Ditmarsch's (2005) functions are not good formalizations of AGM philosophical intuition; 2) New function *^0 (inspired by van Benthem, 2007) better captures AGM intuition; 3) Implementation of *^0 is provided; 4) Shows P* can model belief revision processes more expressively than AGM alone.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully connects AGM theory with dynamic epistemic logic, demonstrates that existing DEL functions don't adequately capture AGM philosophical intuitions, and provides a superior alternative (*^0) that better aligns with AGM's conceptual foundations while maintaining the expressive power of DEL.

title: Rejections Based on Predictive Uncertainty Enable Reliable Routine Soil Spectroscopy
summary: Soil properties relevant to agricultural and environmental applications are conventionally measured using elaborate laboratory methods involving physical and chemical processing. While highly accurate, these conventional methods are costly and time-consuming. In contrast, optical spectroscopy paired with machine learning enables rapid and cost-effective predictions of multiple soil properties. However, spectroscopic modelling is often considered unreliable, as the predictive accuracy varies between soil properties and individual samples. To balance this trade-off between cost and reliability, we introduce reject-to-remeasure: an AI-based measurement framework that combines probabilistic modelling with uncertainty-guided rejection. In this framework, soil samples are first analysed using spectroscopy, after which predictions are rejected if their predictive uncertainty exceeds predefined quality constraints. Rejected samples are subsequently remeasured using conventional laboratory procedures. On a regional visible-near-infrared spectral soil library from Québec, we demonstrate that reject-to-remeasure with modern foundation models (TabPFNv2.5 and TabICLv2) can facilitate the integration of optical spectroscopy into routine laboratory workflows while meeting user-defined accuracy requirements and reducing measurement costs.

AI.tldr: AI framework combines spectroscopy with probabilistic modeling to selectively reject uncertain predictions for lab remeasurement, balancing cost and accuracy in soil analysis.
AI.motivation: Conventional soil property measurement methods are accurate but costly and time-consuming, while spectroscopy with machine learning is faster and cheaper but unreliable with variable predictive accuracy. There's a need to balance cost-effectiveness with measurement reliability.
AI.method: Introduces "reject-to-remeasure" framework: uses optical spectroscopy with probabilistic modeling to predict soil properties, then rejects samples with predictive uncertainty exceeding quality constraints, and remeasures rejected samples using conventional laboratory methods. Uses modern foundation models (TabPFNv2.5 and TabICLv2) for prediction.
AI.result: Demonstrated on regional visible-near-infrared spectral soil library from Québec that reject-to-remeasure framework can integrate spectroscopy into routine workflows while meeting user-defined accuracy requirements and reducing measurement costs.
AI.conclusion: The reject-to-remeasure framework effectively balances the trade-off between cost and reliability in soil property measurement, enabling practical integration of optical spectroscopy into laboratory workflows while maintaining quality standards.

title: TF-SNO: Time-Frequency Gated Spectral Neural Operators for Learning Non-Stationary Partial Differential Equations
summary: Non-stationary partial differential equations (PDEs) arise throughout scientific computing, where the dominant frequency content and energy distribution can drift over time. While efficient in PDE solving, many spectral neural operators apply a shared spectral response across rollout stages, leading to mismatch with time-varying spectra in non-stationary systems. To address this issue, we propose Time-Frequency Gated Spectral Neural Operator (TF-SNO), a state-adaptive framework with learnable time-frequency gating inside spectral blocks. TF-SNO extracts compact frequency-domain and physical-space statistics from the current state to generate modulation coefficients, enabling the spectral response to evolve with the dynamics. TF-SNO learns temporal variation implicitly from the evolving state without introducing an explicit time dimension or time embedding, keeping the modeling complexity low. We further embed the adaptive operator blocks to accurately capture the multi-scale features, thereby improving long-horizon stability. Experiments on six non-stationary PDE benchmarks in 1D and 2D demonstrate that TF-SNO significantly reduces prediction errors and improves robustness compared to strong baselines, with particularly clear gains in long rollout, suggesting the effectiveness of state-dependent spectral adaptation in modeling non-stationary physical systems.

AI.tldr: TF-SNO is a neural operator with learnable time-frequency gating that adapts spectral responses to non-stationary PDEs, improving prediction accuracy and long-horizon stability.
AI.motivation: Existing spectral neural operators use shared spectral responses across rollout stages, which mismatches time-varying spectra in non-stationary PDE systems where frequency content and energy distribution drift over time.
AI.method: Proposes TF-SNO with learnable time-frequency gating inside spectral blocks. Extracts compact frequency-domain and physical-space statistics from current state to generate modulation coefficients, allowing spectral response to evolve with dynamics. Learns temporal variation implicitly without explicit time dimension or embedding. Embeds adaptive operator blocks to capture multi-scale features for long-horizon stability.
AI.result: Experiments on six non-stationary PDE benchmarks in 1D and 2D show TF-SNO significantly reduces prediction errors, improves robustness, and achieves clear gains in long rollout compared to strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: State-dependent spectral adaptation in TF-SNO effectively models non-stationary physical systems by allowing spectral responses to evolve with the dynamics, demonstrating effectiveness for scientific computing applications with time-varying spectra.

title: SignVLA: Real-Time Sign Language-Guided Robotic Manipulation via Attention LSTM and Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable robots to execute manipulation tasks from natural-language instructions grounded in visual observations. However, existing VLA interfaces primarily rely on speech or text input, limiting accessibility for deaf, hard-of-hearing, and speech-impaired users. We present SignVLA, a real-time sign-language-guided VLA framework for accessible human-robot interaction. The system introduces a modular sign-to-text interface that converts visual sign gestures into semantic instructions compatible with downstream VLA policies. Given video streams, SignVLA extracts hand landmark features and employs an attention-enhanced Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to capture temporal gesture dynamics for alphabet- and command-level sign recognition. A temporal stabilization module further improves prediction consistency in real-time interaction settings.The generated instruction sequence is then passed to a downstream VLA policy for sign-conditioned robotic manipulation. Experimental results demonstrate stable real-time sign recognition and successful execution of manipulation tasks driven by sign-language inputs. Our findings suggest that lightweight temporal sign recognition can serve as an effective and practical accessibility layer for multimodal embodied intelligence.

AI.tldr: SignVLA enables real-time human-robot interaction using sign language by converting visual sign gestures into robotic manipulation commands through a vision-language-action framework.
AI.motivation: Current Vision-Language-Action models rely on speech or text input, creating accessibility barriers for deaf, hard-of-hearing, and speech-impaired users who cannot use these conventional interfaces.
AI.method: Introduces a modular sign-to-text interface with hand landmark feature extraction, attention-enhanced LSTM for temporal gesture recognition, and temporal stabilization for real-time consistency. The recognized sign commands are then fed to downstream VLA policies for robotic manipulation.
AI.result: Experimental results show stable real-time sign recognition and successful execution of manipulation tasks driven by sign-language inputs, demonstrating the system's practical effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight temporal sign recognition can serve as an effective accessibility layer for multimodal embodied intelligence, enabling sign-language-guided human-robot interaction.

title: DCD-PFN: A Decoupling-Aware Foundation Model for Causal Discovery
summary: Causal discovery is critical for understanding complex data-generating mechanisms, yet traditional algorithms often struggle with highly non-linear and noisy systems, or suffer from severe computational bottlenecks. Recent tabular foundation models based on Prior-Data Fitted Networks (PFNs) have demonstrated remarkable zero-shot inference capabilities, but their potential for explicit structural causal discovery remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose DCD-PFN, a decoupling-aware foundation model for causal discovery. Instead of directly amortizing global graph reconstruction, DCD-PFN focuses on local causal discovery through a decoupling-based paradigm. Through pre-training on diverse synthetic Structural Causal Models (SCMs), the model learns sample-wise decoupling weights that enable Markov boundary (MB) identification. Furthermore, by leveraging parallelized local discovery, DCD-PFN efficiently reconstructs global causal graphs while remaining grounded in the theoretical foundations of decoupling-based causal discovery. Experiments demonstrate that our foundation model achieves robust zero-shot generalization.

AI.tldr: DCD-PFN: A decoupling-aware foundation model for causal discovery that learns to identify Markov boundaries through pre-training on synthetic structural causal models, enabling robust zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Traditional causal discovery algorithms struggle with highly non-linear, noisy systems and face computational bottlenecks. While recent tabular foundation models show strong zero-shot inference capabilities, their potential for explicit structural causal discovery remains largely unexplored.
AI.method: Proposes DCD-PFN, which focuses on local causal discovery through a decoupling-based paradigm rather than directly amortizing global graph reconstruction. The model is pre-trained on diverse synthetic SCMs to learn sample-wise decoupling weights for Markov boundary identification, then leverages parallelized local discovery to reconstruct global causal graphs.
AI.result: The foundation model demonstrates robust zero-shot generalization capabilities in experiments, showing its effectiveness for causal discovery tasks.
AI.conclusion: DCD-PFN successfully bridges the gap between tabular foundation models and explicit structural causal discovery by introducing a decoupling-aware approach that combines local causal discovery with theoretical foundations, enabling efficient and generalizable causal inference.

title: When Do Intrinsic Rewards Work for Code Reasoning? A Comprehensive Study
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has driven substantial progress in large language model reasoning, but relies on ground-truth supervision that is costly or infeasible, especially in coding tasks. Recent work addresses this by deriving rewards from a model's own signals, such as majority voting or confidence-based scores, achieving notable success on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. However, code generation poses distinct challenges: programs are structurally complex, semantically equivalent solutions may differ syntactically, and verification typically requires execution. Whether these intrinsic reward methods transfer effectively to code remains unexplored. In this work, we present a systematic empirical study of intrinsic reward methods for code generation. We conduct extensive experiments on LiveCodeBench, systematically evaluating representative certainty-based Reinforcement Learning from Internal Feedback (RLIF) approaches under different training scenarios and hyperparameter settings. Our experiments reveal that certainty-based methods yield early gains but inevitably collapse: models progressively shorten outputs and lose reasoning capability, with collapse speed sensitive to sample size and temperature. When used to initialize RLVR training, RLIF pre-training offers no significant improvement over training from scratch. We also provide actionable recommendations for using intrinsic rewards for training code reasoning models. Our study shows both the promise and limitations of intrinsic reward methods for code, informing future work on code models and agents.

AI.tldr: Systematic study shows certainty-based intrinsic rewards for code generation provide early gains but inevitably collapse: models shorten outputs and lose reasoning capability, with no significant improvement over training from scratch.
AI.motivation: RLVR methods for LLM reasoning rely on costly ground-truth supervision, especially problematic for coding tasks. While intrinsic reward methods (using model's own signals) work well for mathematical reasoning, their effectiveness for code generation remains unexplored due to code's structural complexity, semantic equivalence with syntactic differences, and need for execution-based verification.
AI.method: Conducted systematic empirical study on LiveCodeBench, evaluating representative certainty-based Reinforcement Learning from Internal Feedback (RLIF) approaches under different training scenarios and hyperparameter settings.
AI.result: Certainty-based methods yield early gains but inevitably collapse: models progressively shorten outputs and lose reasoning capability, with collapse speed sensitive to sample size and temperature. When used to initialize RLVR training, RLIF pre-training offers no significant improvement over training from scratch.
AI.conclusion: Study reveals both promise and limitations of intrinsic reward methods for code generation, providing actionable recommendations and informing future work on code models and agents.

title: Sakana Fugu Technical Report
summary: The capabilities of frontier Large Language Models (LLMs) continue to advance, with different providers increasingly specializing in distinct domains. This raises a natural next objective: how to combine the individual specializations of various LLMs into a collectively intelligent system. To this end, we report the development of Sakana Fugu, a family of orchestrator models that harness and amplify the capabilities of an LLM agent team. Fugu models are themselves language models trained to understand user queries and dynamically devise agentic scaffolds to solve them. Through these adaptive scaffolds, Fugu accesses performance beyond any individual LLM agent, achieving state-of-the-art results compared to other publicly accessible models across a range of challenging tasks, including SWE-Bench Pro, Terminal Bench, LiveCodeBench, GPQA-Diamond, Humanity's Last Exam, and CharXiv Reasoning. We release two models: Fugu, which balances performance with latency for everyday use, and Fugu-Ultra, which prioritizes answer quality on the hardest problems. We describe our training paradigm, which encompasses large-scale fine-tuning, evolutionary algorithms, and reinforcement learning approaches, along with the infrastructure and core design principles that turn these methods into a production system. We hope this report encourages further research into multi-agent systems and dynamic, query-adaptive agentic scaffolds as a path toward the next frontier of AI capabilities, accessed through collective intelligence.

AI.tldr: Sakana Fugu is a family of orchestrator LLMs that dynamically creates agentic scaffolds to combine specialized LLMs into a collectively intelligent system, achieving state-of-the-art results on challenging benchmarks.
AI.motivation: As frontier LLMs advance and different providers specialize in distinct domains, there's a need to combine their individual specializations into a collectively intelligent system. The paper aims to address how to create a system that can harness and amplify the capabilities of multiple LLM agents working together.
AI.method: The researchers developed Fugu models - language models trained to understand user queries and dynamically devise agentic scaffolds to solve them. The training paradigm combines large-scale fine-tuning, evolutionary algorithms, and reinforcement learning approaches, implemented through specialized infrastructure and design principles to create a production system.
AI.result: Fugu models achieve state-of-the-art results compared to other publicly accessible models across challenging benchmarks including SWE-Bench Pro, Terminal Bench, LiveCodeBench, GPQA-Diamond, Humanity's Last Exam, and CharXiv Reasoning. The system offers two models: Fugu (balancing performance with latency) and Fugu-Ultra (prioritizing answer quality on hardest problems).
AI.conclusion: The Sakana Fugu system demonstrates that adaptive agentic scaffolds can access performance beyond any individual LLM agent. The research encourages further exploration of multi-agent systems and dynamic, query-adaptive agentic scaffolds as a path toward achieving next-level AI capabilities through collective intelligence.

title: Neurosymbolic Clinical Trial Matching via LLM-Driven Abduction and Logical Verification
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising path to automate Clinical Trial Matching (CTM), but still struggle with the deterministic verification required for complex eligibility criteria. Conversely, purely symbolic methods provide formal rigour but break down when faced with incomplete patient records and noisy clinical evidence. To bridge this gap, we investigate a hybrid framework for CTM combining LLMs with logical verification. In particular, we introduce an abductive neurosymbolic CTM framework (αNeSy-CTM), which leverages the linguistic and world knowledge in LLMs to support reasoning over noisy and underspecified clinical text. Extensive evaluation demonstrates that αNeSy-CTM substantially outperforms standalone LLM baselines, achieving up to 30% relative improvement over zero-shot baselines. In addition, our analyses confirm the impact of abductive reasoning on CTM, with αNeSy-CTM exhibiting improved accuracy, specificity, and robustness over a non-abductive neurosymbolic setting. Furthermore, αNeSy-CTM and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning prove highly complementary, highlighting the potential for a hybrid routing policy. Ultimately, this paper demonstrates the impact of neurosymbolic methods for automating CTM, providing a path toward the next generation of auditable, LLM-driven clinical applications.

AI.tldr: A hybrid neurosymbolic framework called αNeSy-CTM combines LLMs' linguistic understanding with logical verification to improve clinical trial matching accuracy over standalone LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with deterministic verification needed for clinical trial matching (CTM), while purely symbolic methods fail with incomplete/ noisy clinical data. There's a need to bridge this gap for reliable CTM automation.
AI.method: Introduces αNeSy-CTM - an abductive neurosymbolic framework that leverages LLMs' linguistic and world knowledge to reason over noisy clinical text, combined with logical verification for deterministic criteria checking.
AI.result: Substantially outperforms standalone LLM baselines (up to 30% relative improvement), shows improved accuracy/specificity/robustness over non-abductive neurosymbolic approaches, and demonstrates complementarity with Chain-of-Thought reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Neurosymbolic methods like αNeSy-CTM provide an effective path toward auditable, LLM-driven clinical applications by combining the strengths of both neural and symbolic reasoning approaches.

title: Comparative Evaluation of Machine Learning and Deep Learning Models for Wound-Rotor Synchronous Motor Performance Prediction
comment: This paper was presented on April 19, 2026, at the 10th ISPEC International Congress on Modern Scientific Research
summary: Wound rotor synchronous motors have emerged as a strong alternative that eliminates dependence on REEs. However, WRSM design requires the simultaneous optimization of numerous geometric and electromagnetic parameters, and the high computational cost of conventional finite element analysis severely limits the rapid exploration of the large parameter space. Although there are machine-learning-based surrogate modeling studies in the literature, they generally compare only a limited number of models, exclude deep learning architectures, and do not provide a comprehensive benchmark specific to WRSM. In this study, the performance of a total of eight machine learning and deep learning models from four different algorithmic families was systematically compared for the prediction of WRSM torque and motor efficiency. On a dataset of 3351 samples generated using Latin Hypercube Sampling in the Motor-CAD simulation environment, each model was trained with 10 different random seed values and tuned via Optuna hyperparameter optimization. Different from the existing literature, this study jointly offers a broad model spectrum including recent deep learning architectures such as FT Transformer, a multi-seed reproducibility protocol, and a Pareto analysis of the computational cost-accuracy trade-off. The results revealed that neural-network-based models systematically outperform tree-based models. The FT-Transformer model achieved the highest single-model accuracy with R^2 = 0.9928, producing predictions in 0.33 milliseconds and thus obtaining several orders of magnitude speedup compared to FEA. Model performances were evaluated in a multidimensional manner using R^2, MAE, RMSE, and MAPE metrics.

AI.tldr: This study systematically compares eight ML/DL models for predicting torque and efficiency of wound rotor synchronous motors, finding neural networks outperform tree-based models, with FT-Transformer achieving highest accuracy (R²=0.9928) and significant speedup over FEA.
AI.motivation: WRSMs eliminate rare earth element dependence but require computationally expensive FEA for design optimization. Existing ML surrogate models are limited in scope, lack deep learning architectures, and don't provide comprehensive WRSM-specific benchmarks.
AI.method: Compared eight ML/DL models from four algorithmic families on 3351 Motor-CAD generated samples. Each model trained with 10 random seeds, hyperparameter optimized via Optuna, and evaluated using R², MAE, RMSE, MAPE metrics.
AI.result: Neural-network-based models systematically outperformed tree-based models. FT-Transformer achieved highest accuracy (R²=0.9928) with 0.33ms prediction time, offering orders of magnitude speedup over FEA.
AI.conclusion: This comprehensive benchmark provides a validated ML approach for rapid WRSM design optimization, with FT-Transformer emerging as the most accurate and efficient surrogate model for motor performance prediction.

title: Root Cause Analysis with Latent Confounders using Partial Ancestral Graphs
summary: Finding the source of failures, known as Root Cause Analysis (RCA), is essential for identifying the root causes of anomalies and maintaining the reliability of complex systems. While causal theory has advanced data-driven RCA, existing frameworks assume causal sufficiency, failing to account for the unobserved latent variables prevalent in real-world environments. To address this gap, we propose PAG-RCA. This framework models system failures as parametric interventions over Partial Ancestral Graphs (PAGs) to perform RCA in the presence of latent variables. We use standard causal identification algorithms to find the source of failures by quantifying causal effects over the PAG. When an effect is identifiable, candidate root causes are ranked based on their exact intervention effects. When effects are structurally unidentifiable, our framework (for the first time in the RCA literature) integrates partial identification to evaluate and score candidates using analytical causal bounds. By integrating latent variables and partial identification at once our framework ensures robust RCA even under data scarcity and latent-variable scenarios where traditional methods degrade. Evaluations on synthetic data, microservice anomaly benchmarks and power-grid cascading failures demonstrate that PAG-RCA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data-driven baselines. By improving data-driven RCA performance under data scarcity, this methodology advances reliable automated diagnostics in partially observable complex networks.

AI.tldr: PAG-RCA is a root cause analysis framework that handles latent variables through Partial Ancestral Graphs (PAGs) and partial identification, enabling robust RCA under data scarcity and unobserved variables.
AI.motivation: Existing RCA frameworks assume causal sufficiency and fail to account for unobserved latent variables prevalent in real-world complex systems, limiting their practical applicability.
AI.method: Models system failures as parametric interventions over Partial Ancestral Graphs (PAGs) to handle latent variables. Uses causal identification algorithms and integrates partial identification when effects are structurally unidentifiable to rank root causes using causal bounds.
AI.result: PAG-RCA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data-driven baselines on synthetic data, microservice anomaly benchmarks, and power-grid cascading failure evaluations, especially under data scarcity and latent-variable scenarios.
AI.conclusion: By integrating latent variables and partial identification, PAG-RCA advances reliable automated diagnostics in partially observable complex networks, improving data-driven RCA performance under challenging real-world conditions.

title: Does RoPE Prevent or Degrade Retrieval Heads? A Mechanistic Analysis Across Model Families
comment: 25 pages, 3 figures, 18 tables. Code, data, and a paired-seed reproducibility harness: https://github.com/CengizhanBayram/Does-RoPE-Prevent-or-Degrade-Retrieval-Heads-A-Mechanistic-Analysis-Across-Model-Families
summary: Retrieval heads, attention heads that copy information from earlier context to the current position, have been proposed as the mechanistic substrate for long-context recall.
