AI.result: Created DataClaw_0-val benchmark for data refinement evaluation. The model produces high-information-density tailored data that facilitates efficient model adaptation to new tasks with limited training data, validated on video generation, real-world VQA, and GUI navigation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Agentic Data Tailoring represents a paradigm shift from passive to active data processing, enabling foundation models to actively refine and structure data to align with diverse intents, with demonstrated improvements in downstream task performance under data-limited regimes.

title: Answer Engineering: Local Trajectory Editing for Protocol-Constrained Decision Making in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models can produce confident but protocol-invalid answers in domains where procedural compliance is critical. This paper presents Answer Engineering, a deterministic runtime and authoring layer that applies localized rule-guided interventions to the visible reasoning trajectory during standard autoregressive generation, without retraining, modifying model weights, or performing global search. The method is evaluated on a controlled clinical benchmark for sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL), where correct management depends on protocol-consistent interpretation of symptom timing, Weber/Rinne tuning-fork findings, and otoscopic findings. In the benchmark, step-by-step reasoning shifted rather than eliminated errors: compliant outcomes for SSNHL decreased from 54.5% under unguided generation to 25.1%, while acceptance on the conductive contrast condition increased from 1.6% to 58.9%. Local trajectory editing increased SSNHL compliance to 83.5% and conductive-case adherence to 77.9%, raising balanced accuracy from 42.0% under reasoning-only generation to 80.7%. The results support a systems-level view in which protocol adherence can be improved through auditable runtime control of reasoning trajectories, while also identifying limitations caused by rule coverage, trigger reliability, and persistent diagnosis-first generation dynamics.

AI.tldr: Answer Engineering uses deterministic rule-guided interventions during LLM generation to improve protocol compliance in medical domains without model retraining, demonstrated on sudden sensorineural hearing loss diagnosis benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLMs produce confident but protocol-invalid answers in domains requiring procedural compliance (like clinical medicine), where step-by-step reasoning alone doesn't eliminate errors and can even worsen compliance.
AI.method: Answer Engineering: a deterministic runtime and authoring layer applying localized rule-guided interventions to visible reasoning trajectories during standard autoregressive generation, without retraining, weight modification, or global search. Uses local trajectory editing to correct protocol deviations.
AI.result: For SSNHL benchmark: step-by-step reasoning decreased compliant outcomes from 54.5% to 25.1%, while local trajectory editing increased compliance to 83.5% (from 25.1%) and raised balanced accuracy from 42.0% to 80.7%.
AI.conclusion: Protocol adherence can be improved through auditable runtime control of reasoning trajectories, but limitations exist from rule coverage, trigger reliability, and persistent diagnosis-first generation dynamics. Supports systems-level view of LLM control.

title: A Reward-Petri-Net Interpretation of Temporal Behavior Trees
summary: This paper introduces an interpretation of Temporal Behavior Trees (TBTs) as Reward-Petri-Nets (RPNs) for reinforcement learning (RL). Designing reward functions for complex, long-horizon robotic tasks is notoriously difficult, especially when tasks have hierarchical structure and temporal constraints. TBTs extend conventional behavior trees (BTs) used in robotic applications by incorporating temporal properties into their leaf nodes. This allows TBTs to represents not only the behavioral task structure defined by BT operators such as Sequence, Fallback, and Parallel, but also the task's temporal constraints. In this work, the constraints are specified in the leaf nodes using Linear Temporal Logic. In order to inform RL rewards using TBTs, we provide a translation from TBT into a Petri Net (PN) and show how rewards can be automatically assigned based on the TBT's structure, resulting in a RPN. In a series of increasingly challenging environments, we demonstrate how TBT-based rewards enable learning where vanilla RL fails, improve sample efficiency, and offer flexible, intuitive control over the learning progress. We showcase the learning impact by using different reward distribution schemes and TBT structures.

AI.tldr: Temporal Behavior Trees (TBTs) are translated into Reward-Petri-Nets (RPNs) to automatically generate reward functions for reinforcement learning in complex robotic tasks with hierarchical structure and temporal constraints.
AI.motivation: Designing reward functions for complex, long-horizon robotic tasks is difficult, especially when tasks have hierarchical structure and temporal constraints. Conventional RL struggles with these scenarios.
AI.method: Extend behavior trees (BTs) to Temporal Behavior Trees (TBTs) by incorporating temporal properties in leaf nodes using Linear Temporal Logic. Provide a translation from TBT to Petri Net (PN), then automatically assign rewards based on TBT structure to create Reward-Petri-Nets (RPNs).
AI.result: TBT-based rewards enable learning where vanilla RL fails, improve sample efficiency, and offer flexible, intuitive control over learning progress. Demonstrated in increasingly challenging environments with different reward distribution schemes and TBT structures.
AI.conclusion: TBTs interpreted as RPNs provide an effective framework for automatically generating reward functions for RL in complex robotic tasks with temporal constraints, overcoming limitations of manual reward engineering and enabling more structured learning.

title: PulseCX: Breaking the Closed-World Assumption in Real-Time CX
summary: Conversational AI agents in Customer Experience (CX) typically suffer from a Closed-World Constraint, ignoring high-velocity external shifts like viral trends or outages. Ad-hoc web search attempts to bridge this gap but often introduce prohibitive latency and context poisoning. We introduce PulseCX, a framework that decouples knowledge acquisition from consumption. Adopting a structure-first paradigm, PulseCX employs an asynchronous agent to linearize signals into a Decay-Aware Temporal Knowledge Graph (DA-TKG) governed by reinforcement--decay dynamics to actively manage information lifecycles. By coupling this self-evolving memory with hierarchical intent gating, PulseCX removes synchronous search bottlenecks (<10ms overhead) and drives significant gains in Intent Resolution (IRR) and Customer Satisfaction (s-CSAT) in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: PulseCX is a framework that addresses conversational AI's closed-world constraint by using asynchronous knowledge acquisition with a decay-aware temporal knowledge graph, eliminating synchronous search bottlenecks and improving performance in dynamic customer experience environments.
AI.motivation: Current conversational AI agents for customer experience suffer from closed-world constraints, ignoring external dynamic changes like viral trends or outages. While ad-hoc web search attempts to bridge this gap, it introduces prohibitive latency and context pollution problems.
AI.method: PulseCX employs a structure-first paradigm that decouples knowledge acquisition from consumption. It uses an asynchronous agent to linearize external signals into a Decay-Aware Temporal Knowledge Graph (DA-TKG) with reinforcement-decay dynamics to manage information lifecycles. This self-evolving memory is coupled with hierarchical intent gating.
AI.result: The framework removes synchronous search bottlenecks with less than 10ms overhead and achieves significant gains in Intent Resolution Rate (IRR) and Customer Satisfaction (s-CSAT) in dynamic environments.
AI.conclusion: PulseCX successfully addresses the closed-world constraint in conversational AI for customer experience by introducing asynchronous knowledge management with temporal awareness, leading to improved performance and responsiveness in handling dynamic external information.

title: Urban Power Grid Topology and Hierarchy Identification from Open Data
summary: Understanding the complex topology and hierarchy of urban power grid is crucial for energy prognosis, power flow management, and system resilience analysis. However, detailed grid information remains largely proprietary. This creates significant barriers for research and innovation, especially when analyzing the last-mile distribution networks connecting individual buildings. This paper addresses this challenge by developing an open-data-driven framework for the complete identification of urban power grid topology, from high-voltage transmission down to individual building connections. Particularly, we fuse public infrastructure data (power-lines, substations, transformers, poles) to map the high and medium-voltage skeleton using graph-based algorithms. We then leverage geospatial machine learning on OpenStreetMap building data to group power demand clusters, and infer the physical topology of the final distribution lines linking the clustered buildings. We apply the developed framework to the district of Alna in Oslo, Norway, and we reconstruct the complete grid topology that connects 7,330 buildings and all major electricity infrastructure assets. With the research in this work, we provide a critical tool that facilitates power system analysis, e.g., power flow optimization, cascading failure simulation, and grid resilience against the penetration of distributed renewable generation.

AI.tldr: An open-data-driven framework for complete urban power grid topology identification using public infrastructure data and geospatial machine learning to reconstruct entire grid networks down to individual building connections.
AI.motivation: Detailed urban power grid information is largely proprietary, creating significant barriers for research and innovation, especially for analyzing last-mile distribution networks connecting individual buildings.
AI.method: Fuse public infrastructure data (power-lines, substations, transformers, poles) to map high/medium-voltage skeleton using graph-based algorithms, then leverage geospatial machine learning on OpenStreetMap building data to group power demand clusters and infer physical topology of final distribution lines.
AI.result: Successfully applied framework to Alna district in Oslo, Norway, reconstructing complete grid topology connecting 7,330 buildings and all major electricity infrastructure assets.
AI.conclusion: Provides a critical open-data tool that facilitates power system analysis including power flow optimization, cascading failure simulation, and grid resilience assessment against distributed renewable generation.

title: Learning Burst-Aware Early Warning Models for Capacity Stress under AI Workload Surges in Hyperscale Data Centers
summary: The rapid growth of large-scale AI workloads, particularly Large Language Model (LLM) training and inference, is fundamentally reshaping the operational dynamics of hyperscale data centers. Unlike traditional cloud workloads, AI-driven jobs exhibit bursty, high-intensity, and rapidly shifting resource demands, often leading to sudden capacity stress that cannot be effectively handled by reactive threshold-based mechanisms. In this paper, we propose a deployment-oriented, burst-aware early warning framework for proactive capacity stress prediction under AI workload surges. We formulate the problem as a high-recall forecasting task over multivariate telemetry windows, with the explicit goal of enabling operational intervention before system degradation occurs. The proposed framework integrates workload intensity, temporal variation, and system pressure signals, and employs a lightweight tree-based learning model to capture nonlinear interactions in highly imbalanced environments. To evaluate the system under realistic conditions, we introduce an AI workload surge injection methodology that simulates burst-driven demand patterns observed in large-scale AI systems. Our XGBoost-based model achieves an ROC AUC of 0.697 and an AP of 0.670, significantly outperforming baseline methods. Under deployment-oriented threshold selection, the framework achieves a Recall of 0.914, enabling the detection of the majority of stress-prone periods with acceptable false-alarm cost. Beyond predictive performance, we show how the proposed framework can be integrated into operational control loops to support proactive actions such as workload throttling and resource scaling. Our results highlight the practical value of high-recall, learning-based early warning systems in enabling resilient and adaptive data center operations in the era of AI-driven workloads.

AI.tldr: Proactive early warning framework using XGBoost to predict capacity stress from bursty AI workloads in data centers, achieving high recall for operational intervention before system degradation.
AI.motivation: Large-scale AI workloads (LLM training/inference) create bursty, high-intensity resource demands in hyperscale data centers that reactive threshold-based mechanisms cannot handle, leading to sudden capacity stress that requires proactive prediction.
AI.method: Deployment-oriented burst-aware framework integrates workload intensity, temporal variation, and system pressure signals; uses lightweight XGBoost model to capture nonlinear interactions in imbalanced data; includes AI workload surge injection methodology for realistic evaluation.
AI.result: XGBoost model achieves ROC AUC of 0.697 and AP of 0.670, outperforming baselines. With deployment-oriented threshold selection, achieves Recall of 0.914, detecting most stress-prone periods with acceptable false-alarm cost.
AI.conclusion: High-recall learning-based early warning systems enable resilient data center operations by supporting proactive actions like workload throttling and resource scaling in the AI workload era.

title: SOHET: Sequence Of Heterogeneous Events Transformer with Self-Supervised Pre-Training
summary: Many machine learning applications rely on heterogeneous event streams to make predictions, either causally as events arrive or bidirectionally over complete sequences. We propose SOHET (Sequence Of Heterogeneous Events Transformer), a hierarchical architecture combining event-type-specific tabular encoders with temporal and type embeddings, processed by a causal or bidirectional transformer. We introduce three self-supervised pre-training objectives for the causal setting. On a proprietary large-scale real-world Booking.com fraud detection task with 17 event types, SOHET outperforms FlexTPP, NAPPT, and CIPPT by 5.8%. Pre-training yields an additional 2.6% gain and 2.4% faster convergence. On the EBES benchmark, bidirectional SOHET matches or exceeds the published best on 6 out of 8 tasks.

AI.tldr: SOHET is a hierarchical transformer architecture for heterogeneous event streams that combines event-type-specific encoders with temporal embeddings, achieving state-of-the-art performance in fraud detection and event sequence prediction tasks.
AI.motivation: Many real-world applications involve heterogeneous event streams where different types of events occur over time, but existing methods may not adequately handle the diversity of event types and their temporal dependencies in both causal (online) and bidirectional (offline) prediction scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes SOHET - Sequence Of Heterogeneous Events Transformer, a hierarchical architecture that uses: 1) event-type-specific tabular encoders to handle different event types, 2) temporal and type embeddings, 3) processed by either causal or bidirectional transformers. Introduces three novel self-supervised pre-training objectives specifically for the causal setting.
AI.result: On Booking.com fraud detection (17 event types), SOHET outperforms FlexTPP, NAPPT, and CIPPT by 5.8%. Self-supervised pre-training provides additional 2.6% performance gain and 2.4% faster convergence. On EBES benchmark, bidirectional SOHET matches or exceeds published best results on 6 out of 8 tasks.
AI.conclusion: SOHET effectively handles heterogeneous event streams through its hierarchical design and specialized pre-training, demonstrating strong performance across both causal and bidirectional prediction tasks, making it a versatile solution for real-world applications like fraud detection and event sequence analysis.

title: Trip+: Benchmarking Agents in Personalized Interactive Travel Planning
summary: Interactive travel planning has become a popular use case for language models. Agents are deployed to manage evolving preferences and unexpected disruptions over multiple turns. Such settings require models to make complex, profile-conditioned planning decisions. However, existing benchmarks often evaluate feasibility, personalization, or interaction in relatively isolated settings. We therefore introduce Trip+ to measure the ability of agents to plan travel holistically. In Trip+, given traveler profiles and dynamic interactions, agents must generate and revise minute-level itineraries. End-to-end traveler experiences are evaluated via an LLM-based simulator, enabling the assessment of subjective metrics like fatigue. Our scenarios range from simple request resolutions to complex environment-driven replanning. We evaluate 18 LMs and find a consistent gap in experiential quality. Models favor technically feasible but exhausting itineraries that diverge sharply from profiled traveler preferences.

AI.tldr: Trip+ is a new benchmark that evaluates language model agents' ability to create holistic, personalized travel itineraries considering traveler profiles and dynamic interactions, with models showing gaps in experiential quality by favoring technically feasible but exhausting plans.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate travel planning in isolated ways (feasibility, personalization, or interaction separately), but real-world interactive travel planning requires holistic consideration of these factors together as agents manage evolving preferences and unexpected disruptions over multiple turns.
AI.method: Trip+ benchmark requires agents to generate and revise minute-level itineraries given traveler profiles and dynamic interactions, with scenarios ranging from simple request resolutions to complex environment-driven replanning. Evaluation uses an LLM-based simulator to assess end-to-end traveler experiences including subjective metrics like fatigue.
AI.result: Evaluation of 18 language models shows a consistent gap in experiential quality. Models tend to favor technically feasible but exhausting itineraries that diverge sharply from profiled traveler preferences, indicating they prioritize feasibility over personalization and comfort.
AI.conclusion: Current language models struggle with holistic travel planning that balances technical feasibility with personalized experiential quality, highlighting the need for better integration of traveler preferences and subjective factors in planning agents.

title: Predictive Repair Management Using a Multi-Head Attention Transformer and Online Learning
summary: Accurate prediction of repair duration is an important challenge in product maintenance due to its implications for resource allocation, customer satisfaction, and operational performance. This study aims to develop a deep learning framework to help fleet repair shops accurately categorize repair time given product historical data. The study uses an automobile repair and maintenance dataset and creates an end-to-end predictive framework by employing a multi-head attention network designed for tabular data. The developed framework combines categorical information, transformed through embeddings and attention mechanisms, with numerical historical data to facilitate integration and learning from diverse data features. A weighted loss function is introduced to overcome class imbalance issues in large datasets. Moreover, an online learning strategy is used for continuous incremental model updates to maintain predictive accuracy in evolving operational environments. Our empirical findings demonstrate that the multi-head attention mechanism extracts meaningful interactions between vehicle identifiers and repair types compared to a feed-forward neural network and a random forest model. Also, combining historical maintenance data with an online learning strategy facilitates real-time adjustments to changing patterns and increases the model's predictive performance on new data. The model is tested on real-world repair data spanning 2013 to 2020 and achieves an accuracy of 78%, with attention weight analyses illustrating feature interactions.

AI.tldr: A deep learning framework using multi-head attention networks for predicting automobile repair durations, addressing class imbalance with weighted loss and maintaining accuracy through online learning on evolving data.
AI.motivation: Accurate prediction of repair duration is crucial for product maintenance due to its impact on resource allocation, customer satisfaction, and operational performance in fleet repair shops.
AI.method: Developed an end-to-end predictive framework using multi-head attention network designed for tabular data, combining categorical information (via embeddings and attention mechanisms) with numerical historical data. Used weighted loss function to handle class imbalance and implemented online learning strategy for continuous incremental model updates.
AI.result: Achieved 78% accuracy on real-world repair data from 2013-2020. Multi-head attention mechanism effectively extracted meaningful interactions between vehicle identifiers and repair types, outperforming feed-forward neural networks and random forests. Online learning strategy facilitated real-time adjustments to changing patterns.
AI.conclusion: The proposed deep learning framework with multi-head attention and online learning provides an effective solution for accurately predicting repair durations, enabling better resource allocation and operational performance in evolving maintenance environments.

title: Whistleblowing and the machine -- towards a considered position
comment: Presented at AAMAS-26 Workshop on Rebellion and Disobedience in AI, see https://sites.google.com/view/rad-ai/rad-ai/cfp?authuser=0
summary: Artificial intelligent agents and autonomous systems are embedded in our environments. They are both a commercial product and a personal tool that generates a lot of data and can draw conclusions from it: machines generate and keep secrets. But should machines protect all secrets? It has been shown that artificial agents are able to whistleblow and it has been argued that digital multi-agent environments should allow for agents in them to whistleblow. We argue that machine whistleblowing must be normative and principled and routed in the existing understanding of whistleblowing as an important rule-breaking mechanism in society. We also argue that there is a need for government regulators to formulate an informed stance on both what machines should be allowed to whistleblow on and how to legally protect those who develop whistleblowing machines

AI.tldr: AI agents should have normative, principled whistleblowing capabilities to expose wrongdoing, requiring regulatory frameworks for what they can expose and legal protections for developers.
AI.motivation: As AI agents become embedded in our environments, they generate and store confidential data and can draw conclusions. Current research shows AI agents can whistleblow, but there's a need to ensure this capability is principled and aligned with societal understandings of whistleblowing as a rule-breaking mechanism.
AI.method: The paper argues for a normative and principled approach to machine whistleblowing, rooted in existing societal understanding of whistleblowing. It advocates for government regulators to formulate informed stances on what machines should be allowed to whistleblow on and how to legally protect developers of such systems.
AI.result: The paper establishes that machine whistleblowing must be normative and principled, and identifies the need for regulatory frameworks to govern what AI systems should be allowed to expose while protecting those who develop whistleblowing capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Government regulators need to develop informed positions on machine whistleblowing—both defining appropriate subjects for exposure and establishing legal protections for developers—to ensure AI systems can responsibly serve as whistleblowing mechanisms in society.

title: Enhancing Creativity in 3D Generative Design via a TRIZ-Inspired Text-to-CAD Framework
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated significant potential in supporting engineering design tasks, including computer-aided design (CAD) automation. However, most existing LLM-based 3D CAD generation approaches primarily focus on geometric precision and instruction-following performance, often overlooking the fundamental aspect of creative design exploration. This study presents a TRIZ-inspired text-to-CAD framework that leverages LLMs to generate high-quality, editable CAD models while systematically exploring creative design alternatives. The framework integrates the Theory of Inventive Problem Solving (TRIZ)-embedding deep human insights from extensive patent records-into LLM prompting strategies, enabling autonomous generation of innovative CAD variants that address technical contradictions. Through a comprehensive three-stage pipeline of design generation, enhancement, and optimization, the framework produces structurally diverse CAD models from well-crafted prompts. The present study implements and evaluates the first two stages, while positioning the design optimization stage as future work. A product design case study (chair) demonstrates that the TRIZ-inspired text-to-CAD framework generates multiple creative design alternatives by systematically applying TRIZ inventive principles such as segmentation, anti-weight, dynamics, and composite materials, achieving 4.0-14.7% mass reduction across all enhanced designs while maintaining structural integrity. The key findings suggest that integrating systematic innovation methodologies with LLM-based 3D CAD generation bridges the gap between precision-focused synthesis and creativity-focused exploration, advancing toward autonomous design systems where AI makes design decisions independently, supporting human decision-making in human-AI collaborative design for engineering applications.

AI.tldr: A TRIZ-inspired framework that uses large language models to generate creative, structurally diverse CAD models while systematically exploring design alternatives and achieving mass reduction.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based 3D CAD generation focuses too much on geometric precision and instruction-following, overlooking creative design exploration. There's a gap between precision-focused synthesis and creativity-focused exploration in AI-assisted design systems.
AI.method: A three-stage pipeline integrating TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving) principles into LLM prompting strategies. The framework uses well-crafted prompts to generate, enhance, and optimize CAD models. The study implements the first two stages (generation and enhancement).
AI.result: The framework successfully generates multiple creative design alternatives by applying TRIZ inventive principles (segmentation, anti-weight, dynamics, composite materials). In a chair design case study, it achieved 4.0-14.7% mass reduction across all enhanced designs while maintaining structural integrity.
AI.conclusion: Integrating systematic innovation methodologies like TRIZ with LLM-based CAD generation bridges the gap between precision and creativity, advancing toward autonomous design systems where AI can make independent design decisions to support human decision-making in engineering applications.

title: ARCO: Adaptive Rubric with Co-Evolution for Multi-Step LLM-Based Agents
summary: Reinforcement learning for multi-step LLM agents often relies on scalar rewards that indicate success but cannot explain why a trajectory is good or bad. Rubric-based rewards improve interpretability through natural-language criteria, but existing methods score at the trajectory level and freeze the scorer behind a closed-source judge, leaving step-level credit assignment unresolved and the judge itself static. We propose ARCO (Adaptive Rubric CO-evolution), a rubric framework in which a same-scale model $μ$ shares a backbone with two heads: a generation head that produces per-step criteria, and a score head that predicts rubric-conditioned step-level rewards. A trajectory decomposition constraint ties the sum of step rewards to the terminal outcome, enabling credit assignment without step-level labels, while $μ$ and the policy $π$ are jointly updated on on-policy data so that the rubric content and the scoring function co-evolve at the parameter level. Across HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, and MuSiQue with two open-source backbones, ARCO improves the best EM in every setting over strong outcome-, rubric-, and process-reward baselines, and analyses show that its rubrics are step-specific, robust to design choices, and useful for diagnosing agent behavior. Codes and data are available at https://github.com/zihangtian/ARCO.

AI.tldr: ARCO is an adaptive rubric co-evolution framework that trains an LLM agent with interpretable step-level rewards using same-model rubric generation and scoring heads that co-evolve with the policy.
AI.motivation: Current reinforcement learning for multi-step LLM agents uses scalar rewards that lack interpretability, while rubric-based methods only provide trajectory-level scoring with closed-source judges, failing to solve step-level credit assignment and keeping judges static.
AI.method: ARCO uses a same-scale model with two heads: a generation head produces per-step rubric criteria, and a score head predicts rubric-conditioned step-level rewards. A trajectory decomposition constraint ties step rewards to terminal outcome, enabling credit assignment without step-level labels. The rubric model and policy are jointly updated on on-policy data.
AI.result: ARCO improves best EM in all settings (HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, MuSiQue) over strong baselines including outcome-, rubric-, and process-reward methods. Analyses show rubrics are step-specific, robust to design choices, and useful for diagnosing agent behavior.
AI.conclusion: ARCO effectively addresses step-level credit assignment and interpretability in multi-step LLM agent reinforcement learning through co-evolving rubric generation and scoring, demonstrating superior performance and providing useful diagnostic capabilities.

title: Unsupervised Disentanglement Without Compromises : How Functional Orthogonality Enforces Identifiability
summary: This paper explores unsupervised disentangled representation learning from a functional perspective. We define latent concepts as factors that influence observations through locally orthogonal directions, formalized as an orthogonality constraint on the Jacobian of the generative mapping. We prove that this condition yields identifiability of general nonlinear generative models, without requiring statistical independence or causal assumptions, provided the latent domain admits all combinations of factor values. Experiments with orthogonality-regularized normalizing flows empirically confirm the theory, demonstrate reliable recovery of ground-truth factors, and shed light on the success of VAEs. These findings challenge the prevailing impossibility claims for unsupervised disentanglement and provide a principled alternative foundation.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised disentangled representation learning is possible via local orthogonality constraints on generative mappings, challenging previous impossibility claims.
AI.motivation: Prevailing impossibility claims suggest unsupervised disentanglement is fundamentally limited, but this paper aims to provide a principled alternative foundation that proves identifiability without statistical independence or causal assumptions.
AI.method: Define latent concepts as factors influencing observations through locally orthogonal directions, formalized as an orthogonality constraint on the Jacobian of the generative mapping. Implement orthogonality-regularized normalizing flows for empirical validation.
AI.result: The orthogonality condition yields identifiability of general nonlinear generative models when latent domain admits all factor combinations. Experiments show reliable recovery of ground-truth factors and provide insights into VAE success.
AI.conclusion: Unsupervised disentanglement is achievable through functional orthogonality constraints, challenging existing impossibility claims and offering a new principled foundation for representation learning.

title: Towards Dys-XAI: Influence-Based Explanations for Dysarthria Severity Assessment
summary: Dysarthria severity assessment is essential for therapy planning and longitudinal monitoring, yet manual perceptual rating is time-consuming and variable across clinicians. Although deep learning models achieve strong performance, their black-box nature limits clinical adoption. Existing speech explainability methods typically provide acoustic feature importance scores that are difficult for end-users to interpret. We propose an influence-based, instance-level explainability framework that explains each decision through supportive and competing training samples. Using gradient-based influence approximations, we compute per-utterance influence scores to identify supportive and competing training samples for each prediction. Controlled deletion experiments from 5 to 20 percent validate the explanations, showing that removing highly influential samples systematically shifts predictions. This approach provides auditable explanations by linking decisions to perceptible reference cases.

AI.tldr: A new explainability framework for dysarthria severity assessment that explains AI decisions by identifying supportive and competing training samples, validated through controlled deletion experiments.
AI.motivation: Manual perceptual rating of dysarthria severity is time-consuming and inconsistent across clinicians. While deep learning models perform well, their black-box nature hinders clinical adoption. Existing explainability methods provide acoustic feature importance scores that are difficult for end-users to interpret.
AI.method: The authors propose an influence-based, instance-level explainability framework that explains each decision by identifying supportive and competing training samples. They use gradient-based influence approximations to compute per-utterance influence scores, and validate the approach through controlled deletion experiments (removing 5-20% of influential samples).
AI.result: The controlled deletion experiments validate the explanations, showing that removing highly influential training samples systematically shifts predictions. This demonstrates that the identified samples truly influence the model's decisions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed influence-based explainability framework provides auditable explanations by linking clinical decisions to perceptible reference cases, potentially increasing clinical adoption of deep learning models for dysarthria assessment through improved transparency.

title: VLA-FAIL: Efficient Task Failure Detection for Finetuned Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) achieve state-of-the-art performance on many robotic manipulation tasks, yet they can still behave unpredictably in out-of-distribution scenarios. Runtime failure detection is therefore essential for the safe real-world deployment of VLAs. However, existing task failure detectors require computationally expensive action sampling, are based on architectural assumptions that limit their applicability to VLAs, or need access to failure rollouts. We propose VLA-FAIL, a lightweight and broadly applicable failure detection framework for VLAs that combines two novel failure detectors with minimal overhead, without requiring failure data. The first, last-layer Mahalanobis distance (LLMD), detects out-of-distribution states by measuring token-wise deviations in last-layer features relative to the training data. The second, action chunk consistency (ACC), exploits the temporal overlap induced by receding-horizon control and detects failures when consecutive action chunks become inconsistent. To capture the trade-off between detection accuracy and detection latency, we introduce AUCPDT, a threshold-independent metric that jointly evaluates precision, recall, and detection time. Through extensive real-world and simulation experiments, we demonstrate that LLMD and ACC capture complementary failure modes whose combination enables reliable and early failure detection across diverse tasks, frequently outperforming significantly more expensive baseline methods.

AI.tldr: VLA-FAIL: A lightweight failure detection framework for vision-language-action models that combines two novel detectors (last-layer Mahalanobis distance and action chunk consistency) without needing failure data, achieving reliable early failure detection across diverse robotic tasks.
AI.motivation: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) perform well on robotic manipulation but behave unpredictably in out-of-distribution scenarios. Existing failure detectors have limitations: they require expensive action sampling, make architectural assumptions incompatible with VLAs, or need access to failure rollouts. There's a need for lightweight, broadly applicable failure detection for safe real-world VLA deployment.
AI.method: Proposes VLA-FAIL framework with two complementary detectors: 1) Last-layer Mahalanobis distance (LLMD) - measures token-wise deviations in last-layer features relative to training data to detect out-of-distribution states. 2) Action chunk consistency (ACC) - exploits temporal overlap in receding-horizon control to detect failures when consecutive action chunks become inconsistent. Introduces AUCPDT metric to evaluate precision, recall, and detection time trade-offs.
AI.result: Extensive real-world and simulation experiments show LLMD and ACC capture complementary failure modes. Their combination enables reliable and early failure detection across diverse tasks, often outperforming significantly more expensive baseline methods. The framework requires minimal overhead and no failure data.
AI.conclusion: VLA-FAIL provides an effective, lightweight solution for runtime failure detection in VLAs, addressing key limitations of existing methods and enabling safer real-world deployment without requiring expensive computational resources or failure data.

title: Social World Model for Lifelong Social Intelligence
summary:
Social intelligence is a core competency for language agents, yet current research primarily focuses on static capability evaluation rather than how these skills are continuously shaped and accumulated. This gap calls for a shift toward sustainable learning paradigms. Currently, two methodological pain points exist: social interaction trajectories lack unified structured representations to form iterable learning signals, and capability improvement and retention are typically studied in isolation, hindering the assessment of continuous evolution.
  To bridge this gap, we propose the Social World Model. We decompose social interaction into five dimensions (scene setting, observation, mental state, action, and dialogue) to build a closed-loop learning framework. In this setup, agents collect interaction experiences, convert them into preference signals for model updating, and redeploy the updated policy for continued learning. Additionally, we provide a reusable data synthesis mechanism and a lifelong learning benchmark, transforming social capabilities from an "object of evaluation" into an "object of sustainable training".
  Validating our framework on the ASCENT-Bench, the interactively trained Qwen2.5-7B model outperforms its baseline across all five core metrics. Notably, it matches the closed-source Gemini 3 Flash in completion rate, exceeds it in pass rate, and achieves zero forgetting across three difficulty levels. Unlike prior works that merely report static comparisons or capability decay, this end-to-end approach provides a trainable, verifiable, and retainable pathway, demonstrating that small open-source models can sustainably acquire competitive social coordination capabilities.

AI.tldr: SWM decomposes social interactions into 5 dimensions to create a closed-loop learning framework that enables sustainable acquisition and retention of social intelligence in language agents.
AI.motivation: Current research focuses on static evaluation of social intelligence rather than how these skills are continuously shaped and accumulated. There's a need for sustainable learning paradigms, but existing approaches suffer from lack of unified structured representations for social interactions and treat capability improvement/retention in isolation.
AI.method: Proposes Social World Model (SWM) with 5-dimensional decomposition of social interaction (scene setting, observation, mental state, action, dialogue). Creates closed-loop learning framework where agents collect experiences, convert to preference signals for model updating, and redeploy updated policies. Includes reusable data synthesis mechanism and lifelong learning benchmark.
AI.result: On ASCENT-Bench, interactively trained Qwen2.5-7B outperformed its baseline across all five core metrics, matched closed-source Gemini 3 Flash in completion rate, exceeded it in pass rate, and achieved zero forgetting across three difficulty levels.
AI.conclusion: SWM transforms social capabilities from "object of evaluation" to "object of sustainable training," demonstrating that small open-source models can acquire competitive social coordination capabilities through trainable, verifiable, and retainable pathways.

title: Atomistic Language Models Understand and Generate Materials
summary: Atomistic structure and natural language have long been modeled separately, with language models either calling atomistic models as tools or being fine-tuned on lossy textual encodings that discard atomistic information. We introduce Atomistic Language Models (ALMs) to pursue native multimodality, in which a single language backbone understands atomistic structures, generates materials from natural language, and optimizes crystal structures as instructed by text. By unifying a pretrained atomistic encoder, large language model, and denoising diffusion model through purely continuous projectors and staged training, ALMs achieve state-of-the-art results on crystal structure prediction and de novo generation. ALMs are enabled by a continuous bridge that maps language model embeddings directly into the steering space of atomistic diffusion, and are assisted by Text-to-Crystal Feynman-Kac (T2C-FK), a particle-based sampler that scores partial denoising trajectories to enforce stoichiometric targets at inference time. To evaluate the ability of ALMs to optimize and generate materials from natural-language prompts and 3D atom-coordinate inputs, we introduce ALM Bench, the first benchmark for text-conditioned crystal generation and optimization. Code, training data, and model weights will be released soon.

AI.tldr: Atomistic Language Models (ALMs) unify natural language processing with atomistic structure modeling, enabling text-to-material generation and structure optimization through continuous bridges between language models and diffusion models.
AI.motivation: Current approaches separate atomistic structure modeling and natural language processing—either using language models as tools to call atomistic models, or fine-tuning on lossy textual encodings that discard crucial atomistic information.
AI.method: ALMs integrate a pretrained atomistic encoder, large language model, and denoising diffusion model through continuous projectors with staged training. They use a continuous bridge to map language embeddings into atomistic diffusion steering space, and employ Text-to-Crystal Feynman-Kac (T2C-FK) particle-based sampler for stoichiometric enforcement.
AI.result: ALMs achieve state-of-the-art results on crystal structure prediction and de novo generation. They can generate materials from natural language prompts and optimize 3D atom-coordinate structures.
AI.conclusion: ALMs represent a significant advancement in native multimodality for materials science, enabling unified understanding and generation of both atomistic structures and natural language. The authors also introduce ALM Bench as the first benchmark for text-conditioned crystal generation and optimization.

title: Mind the Noise: Sensitivity of Transformer-based Interaction-Aware Trajectory Prediction Models to Noisy Data
summary: Trajectory prediction allows autonomous vehicles to anticipate the future behavior of surrounding objects (or agents) and, accordingly, maximize the safety and efficiency of their driving. State-of-the-art Transformed-based interaction-aware trajectory prediction models, which rely on attention mechanisms to capture multi-agent interactions and maximize prediction accuracy, are commonly trained and evaluated on long-range high-quality datasets. These datasets are typically obtained by aggregating data from multiple vehicles or drones and removing any object detection or tracking noise offline. Yet, information about a surrounding object's state (its position, speed, heading) is far from being noiseless in real-world deployments. Object state estimation is affected by perception uncertainties and localization errors that can be particularly large for objects received via Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communications. In this paper, we analyze the impact of noisy object state information on the trajectory prediction accuracy of a state-of-the-art Transformer-based interaction-aware trajectory prediction model. Our study demonstrates that trajectory prediction accuracy can rapidly deteriorate as the noise intensity increases. Numerical results show that the prediction accuracy can reduce by a 1.3x factor under small noise levels and by as much as a 3.9x factor under the highest (yet realistic) noise conditions. These findings reveal the strong sensitivity of trajectory prediction models to noisy data, underscoring the need for more realistic training and evaluation datasets as well as noise mitigation strategies.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based trajectory prediction models suffer significant accuracy degradation under realistic noise conditions in object state information, requiring more robust training and noise mitigation strategies.
AI.motivation: Current Transformer-based interaction-aware trajectory prediction models are trained and evaluated on clean, high-quality datasets, but real-world deployments face significant perception uncertainties and localization errors, particularly for V2X communications. The impact of these real-world noise conditions on prediction accuracy needs to be studied.
AI.method: Analyzed the impact of noisy object state information on a state-of-the-art Transformer-based interaction-aware trajectory prediction model by introducing different levels of realistic noise to object state data (position, speed, heading) and measuring the resulting accuracy degradation.
AI.result: Trajectory prediction accuracy deteriorates rapidly as noise intensity increases: prediction accuracy reduces by 1.3x factor under small noise levels and by as much as 3.9x factor under the highest realistic noise conditions.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory prediction models are strongly sensitive to noisy data, revealing a critical gap between clean training datasets and real-world deployment conditions. This underscores the need for more realistic training and evaluation datasets as well as effective noise mitigation strategies for practical autonomous driving applications.

title: One Size does not Fit All: Heterogeneous Latent Space Alignment for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation
summary: Domain shift remains a major obstacle to the reliable deployment of machine learning models in high-stakes environments such as healthcare. While Domain adaptation aims to mitigate these effects, existing approaches suffer from limited expressiveness of latent representations and a reliance on handcrafted, static augmentations. In this work, we address these limitations by proposing a novel deep learning architecture for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA), specifically optimized for medical image segmentation. Our framework, ADualVUOT, integrates a dual-encoder Variational Autoencoder (VAE) with Continuous Normalizing Flows (CNFs) to increase modeling flexibility and posterior expressiveness. To achieve domain alignment, we leverage Unbalanced Optimal Transport (UOT) through the Gaussian-Gromov-Wasserstein (GGW) distance, which handles structural and topological discrepancies between domains. Furthermore, we incorporate an adversarial augmentation scheme to synthesize worst-case compositions, thus enhancing model robustness. Extensive experiments on medical imaging benchmarks show significant gains over prior OT-based approaches.

AI.tldr: A novel UDA framework for medical image segmentation called ADualVUOT combines dual-encoder VAE with CNFs for flexible representations, uses GGW-UOT for domain alignment, and includes adversarial augmentation for robustness.
AI.motivation: Domain shift in healthcare ML deployments and limitations of existing domain adaptation approaches: limited expressiveness of latent representations and reliance on handcrafted, static augmentations.
AI.method: ADualVUOT framework integrates: 1) dual-encoder VAE with Continuous Normalizing Flows for increased modeling flexibility, 2) Unbalanced Optimal Transport through Gaussian-Gromov-Wasserstein distance for domain alignment, and 3) adversarial augmentation scheme for worst-case compositions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on medical imaging benchmarks show significant gains over prior Optimal Transport-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ADualVUOT framework effectively addresses domain shift in medical image segmentation through flexible latent modeling, structural domain alignment, and robustness-enhancing adversarial augmentations.

title: Calibration Is Not Control: Why LLM-Agent Oversight Needs Intervention
summary: Runtime oversight for LLM agents is commonly framed as scalar risk prediction: estimate failure likelihood, confidence, or uncertainty, then intervene once the score crosses a threshold. We argue that this framing targets the wrong object for control. The relevant question is not how likely the agent is to fail if it continues, but whether an available intervention would improve the outcome. Two trajectory prefixes can have the same risk estimate while requiring different actions, because one remains recoverable and the other does not. We formalize this mismatch as target error and identify intervention advantage, the expected utility gain from intervening rather than continuing, as the decision object for oversight. To measure this mismatch, we introduce prefix branching, a same-prefix counterfactual protocol that executes candidate actions from identical trajectory states. Across four benchmarks, action-conditioned control yields regime-dependent gains over scalar routing. In a calibration decomposition, recalibrating the same scalar score improves prediction metrics but leaves control regret unchanged, showing that calibration alone does not repair target error. A simple prefix-only action-conditioned controller substantially reduces regret in the strongest interactive regime, from 0.506 to 0.110 on ALFWorld. Gains shrink when interventions are weak or when scalar routing already preserves intervention-relevant information. These results suggest that LLM-agent oversight should move from calibrated risk scoring toward action-conditioned value estimation.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that current LLM agent oversight using scalar risk prediction is flawed, and proposes using action-conditioned value estimation instead, showing significant improvements in interactive tasks.
AI.motivation: Current runtime oversight for LLM agents frames control as scalar risk prediction (estimating failure likelihood), but this targets the wrong object for control. The real question should be whether an available intervention would improve outcomes, not just whether failure is likely.
AI.method: Introduces intervention advantage as the decision object - expected utility gain from intervening vs continuing. Uses prefix branching, a same-prefix counterfactual protocol that executes candidate actions from identical trajectory states. Tests action-conditioned control across four benchmarks.
AI.result: Action-conditioned control yields regime-dependent gains over scalar routing. Calibration alone doesn't fix the target error problem. A simple prefix-only action-conditioned controller substantially reduces regret from 0.506 to 0.110 on ALFWorld. Gains shrink when interventions are weak or scalar routing already preserves intervention-relevant information.
AI.conclusion: LLM-agent oversight should shift from calibrated risk scoring toward action-conditioned value estimation, as this better addresses the actual control problem of deciding when to intervene based on whether interventions would actually improve outcomes.

title: Federated Temporal Attention Intelligence for Cyber-Resilient IoMT: Lightweight Digital Twins and PPO-Driven Honeypot Deception
summary: The rapid proliferation of Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) devices introduces critical cybersecurity vulnerabilities in healthcare environments where resource-constrained medical devices operate under strict latency requirements and stringent data-privacy regulations. To address these challenges, this paper presents the Lightweight Digital Twin and Federated Reinforcement Learning (LDT-FRL) framework, a privacy-preserving defense architecture integrating four complementary mechanisms: a Temporal Attention Encoder (TAE) built on a GRU backbone with learned temporal self-attention for flow-level threat classification; lightweight LSTM-based Digital Twins trained on normal-class traffic to generate per-device anomaly scores that gate the TAE classifier through a learned sigmoid coupling; a Federated Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent selecting among ALLOW, ISOLATE, and HONEYPOT_REDIRECT actions based on a seven-dimensional state; and an intelligent honeypot layer that converts redirected suspicious traffic into actionable threat intelligence. A federated aggregation strategy employing EMA-smoothed per-client validation losses as inverse-weighted FedAvg coefficients stabilizes global model updates under non-IID client distributions. Evaluated on CICDDoS 2019 and TON-IoT benchmarks, LDT-FRL achieves 99.66% and 99.95% test accuracy respectively, with macro-F1 scores of 0.9913 and 0.9995, converging 81% faster than the DTFL-CD baseline while attaining perfect F1=1.000 on the severely imbalanced MITM class. Explainability analysis via SHAP, LIME, Grad-CAM, and counterfactual methods confirms that the TAE focuses on semantically meaningful flow features, providing interpretable evidence for each defense decision.

AI.tldr: LDT-FRL is a privacy-preserving cybersecurity framework for IoMT that combines lightweight digital twins, federated reinforcement learning, and intelligent honeypots to detect and mitigate threats with high accuracy and interpretability while preserving data privacy.
AI.motivation: IoMT devices in healthcare face critical cybersecurity challenges due to resource constraints, strict latency requirements, and stringent data-privacy regulations. Medical environments need effective threat detection that preserves patient privacy while operating efficiently on constrained devices.
AI.method: Four complementary mechanisms: 1) Temporal Attention Encoder (TAE) with GRU backbone and self-attention for flow-level threat classification; 2) Lightweight LSTM-based Digital Twins generating per-device anomaly scores; 3) Federated Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent selecting defense actions; 4) Intelligent honeypot layer converting suspicious traffic into threat intelligence. Uses EMA-smoothed per-client validation losses for federated aggregation.
AI.result: Achieves 99.66% and 99.95% test accuracy on CICDDoS 2019 and TON-IoT benchmarks, with macro-F1 scores of 0.9913 and 0.9995. Converges 81% faster than baseline DTFL-CD, and attains perfect F1=1.000 on severely imbalanced MITM class. Explainability analysis confirms interpretable decision-making.
AI.conclusion: The LDT-FRL framework effectively addresses IoMT cybersecurity challenges by combining privacy-preserving federated learning with interpretable threat detection, achieving state-of-the-art performance while respecting healthcare privacy constraints and resource limitations.

title: Don't Blindly Trust It: How Unreliable Feedback Breaks Tool-Using LLM Agents
summary: Tool-augmented agents are typically evaluated by their gains under reliable external feedback. Yet these gains leave open a key counterfactual: when feedback is unreliable, would the agent be better off receiving no task evidence? We study this question with a controlled matched-loop comparison that fixes the agent loop, prompt, action space, and decoding, while varying only the returned observation: faithful, misleading, or absent. Across question answering and fact verification, persistent misleading feedback produces a value inversion: agents that benefit from clean tools can perform worse than the matched no-feedback fallback. On HotpotQA, Qwen2.5-7B reaches 44.8 F1 with clean retrieval and 22.3 F1 with no feedback, but drops to 4.7 F1 under shuffled retrieval. The inversion persists under stronger clean retrieval and locally plausible distractors, but weakens when later clean evidence can repair the trajectory. Early trajectory signals predict many failures, yet simple repairs remain fallback-limited: rejecting bad evidence helps only when the exposed fallback is reliable. These results show that clean-tool gains can overstate tool value, and that matched no-feedback fallback controls are necessary for evaluating tool-augmented agents.

AI.tldr: Tool-augmented agents perform worse with unreliable feedback than with no feedback at all, revealing a "value inversion" where tool benefits can be overstated without proper fallback controls.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of tool-augmented agents focus on gains from reliable external feedback, but don't address the counterfactual: how would agents perform when feedback is unreliable versus receiving no feedback at all?
AI.method: Used a controlled matched-loop comparison where agent loop, prompt, action space, and decoding are fixed, while varying only the returned observation type: faithful, misleading, or absent. Evaluated on question answering (HotpotQA) and fact verification tasks.
AI.result: Persistent misleading feedback produces a "value inversion" - agents that benefit from clean tools can perform worse than matched no-feedback fallbacks. On HotpotQA, Qwen2.5-7B got 44.8 F1 with clean retrieval, 22.3 F1 with no feedback, but dropped to 4.7 F1 with shuffled retrieval. The inversion persists with stronger clean retrieval and plausible distractors, but weakens when later clean evidence can repair trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Clean-tool gains can overstate tool value, and matched no-feedback fallback controls are necessary for properly evaluating tool-augmented agents. Simple repairs like rejecting bad evidence only help when the exposed fallback is reliable.

title: Dual-Attention Convolution Experts for Sparse Tensor Completion
summary: Tensor factorization (TF) has been widely adopted for high-dimensional sparse data completion tasks. Despite significant progress, neural TF methods often struggle to capture complex cross-mode interactions and remain vulnerable to (extreme) data sparsity. To address these challenges, we propose a novel neural tensor factorization approach, termed Dual-Attention Convolution Expert Networks with Group-Level Contrastive Learning (DCGC). For the first problem, DCGC generates diverse non-linear alignment patterns of latent factors via a multi-channel convolution network, and leverages the gated dual-attention mechanism to drive the model to focus on more important output channels (i.e., convolution experts) and the aligned features. Furthermore, DCGC introduces a group-level contrastive learning strategy that aggregates positive samples with identical feedback levels while separating negative samples across different levels. This strategy injects high-quality self-supervised signals to mitigate data sparsity. Extensive experiments conducted on five datasets demonstrate that our DCGC outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in sparse tensor completion for traffic and recommendation applications. Code to reproduce the experimental results in the paper is available at https://github.com/ku1z/DCGC.

AI.tldr: DCGC is a novel neural tensor factorization method that uses dual-attention convolution expert networks with group-level contrastive learning to improve tensor completion in sparse data scenarios like traffic and recommendation systems.
AI.motivation: Existing neural tensor factorization methods struggle to capture complex cross-mode interactions and remain vulnerable to data sparsity issues, particularly extreme sparsity scenarios common in real-world applications like traffic data and recommendation systems.
AI.method: DCGC employs a multi-channel convolution network to generate diverse non-linear alignment patterns of latent factors, uses gated dual-attention mechanism to focus on important output channels (convolution experts) and aligned features, and introduces group-level contrastive learning that aggregates positive samples with identical feedback levels while separating negative samples across different levels.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five datasets show that DCGC outperforms state-of-the-art methods in sparse tensor completion for both traffic and recommendation applications.
AI.conclusion: DCGC effectively addresses limitations in neural tensor factorization by capturing complex cross-mode interactions through convolution expert networks and mitigating data sparsity through group-level contrastive learning, demonstrating superior performance in practical applications.

title: AutoRAS: Learning Robust Agentic Systems with Primitive Representations
summary: The automated design of agentic systems offers a promising pathway for scaling large language models (LLMs) beyond single-agent reasoning. While prior work has advanced task performance through handcrafted or automatically generated multi-agent workflows, robustness is often treated as an afterthought, leaving systems vulnerable to external adversaries and internal failures. We propose AutoRAS, a framework for the Automated design of Robust Agentic Systems. AutoRAS formulates system design as generating a sequence of symbolic primitives that jointly encode structural connectivity and behavioral actions, and learns to optimize this sequence using execution-derived safety signals and flow-based sequence-level objectives. Extensive experiments show that AutoRAS achieves the best performance in both vanilla and adversarial settings, with the smallest performance degradation under attacks. Further analyses demonstrate strong transferability, stable optimization behavior, stability across primitive sets, and favorable cost trade-offs. Our code is available at $\href{https://github.com/guohezuy/AutoRAS}{\text{this https URL}}$.

AI.tldr: AutoRAS is an automated framework for designing robust multi-agent systems that optimizes both performance and safety through symbolic primitive sequencing and flow-based learning objectives.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent LLM systems focus on task performance but lack robustness against adversarial attacks and internal failures, making them vulnerable in practical applications.
AI.method: AutoRAS formulates system design as generating sequences of symbolic primitives encoding both structural connectivity and behavioral actions, then learns to optimize these sequences using safety signals from execution and flow-based sequence-level objectives.
AI.result: AutoRAS achieves best performance in both normal and adversarial settings, shows smallest performance degradation under attacks, demonstrates strong transferability, stable optimization, primitive set stability, and favorable cost trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: AutoRAS provides an effective automated framework for designing robust agentic systems that maintain performance while being resilient to attacks, representing an important advance in scaling LLMs beyond single-agent reasoning.

title: Universal Encoders for Modular Relational Deep Learning
comment: Accepted to ECML PKDD 2026 in Naples, Italy
summary:
Relational Deep Learning (RDL) models multi-tabular databases as temporal heterogeneous graphs for end-to-end representation learning. While RDL is evolving rapidly, existing approaches face significant generalization obstacles. They are either schema-specific, requiring training from scratch for every new database, or they rely on monolithic architectures that entangle feature encoding with graph message-passing. Analyzing these limitations, we establish four core pillars for building foundational relational models: semantic granularity, structural topology, temporal causality, and unified optimization.
  Addressing these pillars, we propose a modular approach that decouples row encoding from graph message-passing. We introduce the Universal Row Encoder, a transformer-based module that integrates raw cell data with schema metadata$-$including column semantics, table names, and global distribution statistics$-$to produce table-width invariant row embeddings. By explicitly feeding global statistics to an intra-row self-attention mechanism, the encoder natively contextualizes unseen features and handles sparse data. Serving as a flexible "backend" for any downstream graph architecture, our pretrained encoder enhances cross-database knowledge transfer on the established RelBench benchmarks while improving learning convergence and memory footprint.

AI.tldr: Proposes a modular relational deep learning approach with a Universal Row Encoder that decouples feature encoding from graph message-passing to address generalization challenges in multi-tabular database learning.
AI.motivation: Existing Relational Deep Learning approaches face generalization obstacles - they're either schema-specific (requiring retraining for each new database) or use monolithic architectures that entangle feature encoding with graph message-passing, limiting knowledge transfer across databases.
AI.method: Introduces a modular approach decoupling row encoding from graph message-passing. Key innovation is the Universal Row Encoder: a transformer-based module that integrates raw cell data with schema metadata (column semantics, table names, global distribution statistics) using intra-row self-attention to produce table-width invariant row embeddings.
AI.result: The pretrained Universal Row Encoder enhances cross-database knowledge transfer on RelBench benchmarks while improving learning convergence and memory footprint. It can serve as a flexible "backend" for any downstream graph architecture.
AI.conclusion: The proposed modular approach addresses four core pillars for foundational relational models (semantic granularity, structural topology, temporal causality, unified optimization) and enables better generalization by explicitly handling schema variations and sparse data through metadata integration.

title: Towards Transparent Mental Health Insights: An Explainable AI Model for Career-Related Depression and Anxiety Among University Students Using Structured Data
comment: Accepted at AHTBE 2025 Conference, published in Medical Sciences Forum (MDPI). 18 pages, 5 figures, 5 tables
summary: Career anxiety and depression among university students present a growing challenge to mental health and academic achievement. This study proposes an Explainable AI (XAI) framework using multimodal data and Federated Learning (FL) to identify early indicators of career-related mental health problems in a privacy-preserving and culturally responsive manner. The framework combines structured behavioral data and facial emotion features from interview videos via an intermediate fusion neural network with attention mechanisms. Label smoothing was applied to improve model generalizability. FL was used across institutions to enable collaborative training without raw data sharing. Evaluation was conducted using the Student Mental Health Survey dataset from university students across Pakistan. Our model attained an F1-score of 89.12%, recall of 86.54%, accuracy of 92.08%, and precision of 91.88%. Using Integrated Gradients and SHAP, the model identified key behavioral markers of depression including avoidance of direct gaze, lower facial expressiveness, and social withdrawal, consistent with psychological theory. This research presents an interpretable, scalable, and context-sensitive AI system for mental health pre-diagnosis with potential integration into student support services globally.

AI.tldr: This study develops an Explainable AI framework using multimodal data and Federated Learning to identify early indicators of career-related anxiety and depression in university students while preserving privacy and cultural sensitivity.
AI.motivation: Career anxiety and depression among university students represent a growing challenge to mental health and academic achievement, requiring early detection methods that respect privacy and cultural differences.
AI.method: The framework combines structured behavioral data and facial emotion features from interview videos using an intermediate fusion neural network with attention mechanisms. Label smoothing improves generalizability, and Federated Learning enables collaborative training across institutions without sharing raw data.
AI.result: The model achieved strong performance metrics: 89.12% F1-score, 86.54% recall, 92.08% accuracy, and 91.88% precision. Explainability techniques identified key behavioral markers including avoidance of direct gaze, lower facial expressiveness, and social withdrawal.
AI.conclusion: This research presents an interpretable, scalable, and context-sensitive AI system for mental health pre-diagnosis with potential integration into student support services globally, offering privacy-preserving and culturally responsive mental health assessment.

title: Fusing Backdoors, Machine Learning, and Optimization for Large-Scale Parametric Mixed-Integer Programs
summary:
Large-scale optimization problems are often solved repeatedly under similar structural conditions, leading to substantial computational overhead. This occurs in applications such as power systems, transportation, and supply chain networks, where the underlying structure is fixed while parameters frequently vary under perturbations.
  This paper proposes a Learning to Optimize (LTO) framework that accelerates the solution of large-scale general mixed-integer problems by leveraging the concept of a backdoor, i.e., a subset of variables that drive most of the computational complexity. The proposed BIPC framework consists of three phases. Phase I is an identification procedure that discovers a backdoor for a set of instances in the distribution. Phase II uses supervised learning to develop machine learning models that, given an instance, predict values for bounded-domain backdoor variables and intervals for wide-domain backdoor variables. These predictions define a reduced optimization problem where the predictions constrain the backdoor variables, while the other variables remain free. Phase III optimizes this reduced problem and, if necessary, applies a correction step to restore feasibility or the optimality guarantees.
  Experiments on real-world, large-scale problems show substantial reductions in solution time with only a limited loss in solution quality. The framework enables organizations to solve large-scale optimization problems efficiently in the presence of frequent perturbations, such as unexpected events, demand fluctuations, or operational changes. Because these changes affect parameters rather than the problem structure, BIPC can quickly provide high-quality, feasible solutions, offering a practical approach to integrating machine learning into existing optimization pipelines.

AI.tldr: Learning to Optimize framework using backdoor variables accelerates large-scale mixed-integer optimization by predicting variable values/intervals, reducing problem size while maintaining solution quality.
AI.motivation: Large-scale optimization problems are repeatedly solved under similar structural conditions (fixed structure, varying parameters) in domains like power systems, transportation, and supply chains, causing substantial computational overhead.
AI.method: BIPC framework with three phases: 1) Identification procedure to discover backdoor variables (subset driving computational complexity), 2) Supervised learning to predict values for bounded-domain backdoor variables and intervals for wide-domain ones, 3) Optimization of reduced problem with predicted constraints, plus correction step for feasibility/optimality guarantees.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world, large-scale problems show substantial reductions in solution time with only limited loss in solution quality, enabling efficient solving in presence of frequent perturbations.
AI.conclusion: BIPC provides a practical approach to integrate machine learning into existing optimization pipelines, quickly delivering high-quality feasible solutions when problem structure remains fixed but parameters frequently change due to unexpected events, demand fluctuations, or operational changes.

title: Balancing Performance and Diversity in GRPO Autoregressive Text-to-Image Post-Training
summary: Autoregressive text-to-image (T2I) generation has recently advanced rapidly, yet aligning generated images with human preferences remains challenging. GRPO-style online reinforcement learning provides an effective framework; however, existing methods typically treat reference-policy divergence as fixed, despite its direct impact on policy optimization. We study this overlooked factor within a unified f-divergence framework, encompassing forward KL, reverse KL, and JS divergence, for GRPO-style autoregressive T2I alignment. Our systematic theoretical analysis reveals that different divergences reshape token-level updates in distinct ways. In particular, under the sampled-token shaping form used, JS regularization achieves a favorable trade-off by mitigating uniform bias relative to the reference policy while still discouraging large deviations. Extensive experiments on LlamaGen and Janus-7B show that JS divergence achieves the strongest or highly competitive optimization performance on most evaluation metrics while maintaining favorable generation diversity. The code is available at https://github.com/tuoyou-hao/BPD-GRPO.

AI.tldr: JS divergence regularization achieves optimal balance for autoregressive text-to-image alignment, outperforming forward/reverse KL while maintaining diversity.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive T2I generation lacks proper alignment with human preferences, and existing GRPO methods treat reference-policy divergence as fixed despite its crucial impact on optimization.
AI.method: Systematic theoretical analysis of f-divergence framework (forward KL, reverse KL, JS divergence) for GRPO-style T2I alignment, revealing how different divergences reshape token-level updates differently.
AI.result: JS divergence achieves strongest or highly competitive performance on most metrics across LlamaGen and Janus-7B models while maintaining favorable generation diversity.
AI.conclusion: JS regularization provides optimal trade-off in GRPO-style T2I alignment by mitigating uniform bias while preventing large deviations from reference policy.

title: Fast-TurboQuant: A Multiplier-Free Online Vector Quantization Approach
summary: As large language models scale, memory bandwidth for key-value caches and retrieval-augmented generation systems becomes a critical bottleneck. While 1-bit quantization addresses this constraint, recent TurboQuant relies on dense random rotation matrices to condition the vector distribution before quantization. This projection demands millions of floating-point multiplications per embedding, making it difficult to deploy on constrained edge silicon. We introduce Fast-TurboQuant, a multiplier-free projection architecture that replaces the dense matrix with a structured fast Johnson-Lindenstrauss transform. By applying a Rademacher phase inversion followed by a fast Walsh-Hadamard transform (FWHT), the method leverages sub-Gaussian concentration to satisfy the prerequisites of scalar Lloyd-Max quantization without Gaussian projections. This substitution reduces the arithmetic complexity to only additions, eliminating hardware multipliers. Evaluation on DBpedia OpenAI-3 Large embeddings demonstrates a 19.7 times algorithmic speedup under sequential execution. Furthermore, the dimension expansion due to the FWHT zero-padding reduces the mean squared error and improves Recall@10.

AI.tldr: Fast-TurboQuant replaces dense matrix projections with a structured fast Johnson-Lindenstrauss transform (Rademacher phase inversion + fast Walsh-Hadamard transform) to enable multiplier-free 1-bit quantization for KV caches and retrieval, achieving 19.7x speedup and better accuracy.
AI.motivation: Memory bandwidth for key-value caches and retrieval-augmented generation systems becomes a bottleneck as LLMs scale. While 1-bit quantization helps, existing methods like TurboQuant require dense random rotation matrices that involve millions of floating-point multiplications per embedding, making deployment difficult on constrained edge hardware.
AI.method: Replace the dense matrix projection in TurboQuant with a structured fast Johnson-Lindenstrauss transform: apply a Rademacher phase inversion followed by a fast Walsh-Hadamard transform (FWHT). This provides sub-Gaussian concentration to satisfy scalar Lloyd-Max quantization prerequisites without Gaussian projections, reducing arithmetic complexity to only additions (no hardware multipliers).
AI.result: Evaluation on DBpedia OpenAI-3 Large embeddings shows: 19.7 times algorithmic speedup under sequential execution; dimension expansion from FWHT zero-padding reduces mean squared error; improves Recall@10 accuracy metric.
AI.conclusion: Fast-TurboQuant provides an efficient multiplier-free projection architecture for 1-bit quantization, addressing memory bandwidth bottlenecks in LLMs while being deployable on constrained edge silicon. The method achieves significant speedup and improved accuracy metrics compared to the baseline.

title: AI Alignment From Social Choice Perspectives
