We study this distinction on the vertex-guard Art Gallery Problem, the NP-hard task of choosing polygon vertices from which to observe an entire region. A pointer-network policy is trained from a coverage-aware reward over its own rollouts under the constraint we call geo-free inference: at test time it sees only vertex coordinates, with no visibility computation and no geometric oracle. The policy places guards economically but leaves a tail of under-covered polygons that widens far beyond the training range. To locate the cause, we freeze the trained encoder and read its embeddings with a small single-shot classifier, still geo-free at inference. The classifier closes most of the feasibility gap, in and out of distribution and at up to roughly five times the training range, cutting under-covered polygons by about an order of magnitude at an explicitly reported cost in guard count. We read this as evidence that the reinforcement-trained representation already encodes the geometry required for feasibility, and that residual failures reflect decoder calibration rather than missing knowledge. Probing a frozen encoder thus offers a practical way to ask what a neural combinatorial solver has internalized.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement-trained neural combinatorial optimization policies learn geometric representations for NP-hard problems like the Art Gallery Problem, but decoder calibration issues persist. A geo-free classifier reading frozen encoder embeddings can close most feasibility gaps, showing the policy internalizes geometry while execution fails due to decoder miscalibration.
AI.motivation: To understand what neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) policies actually learn versus what their decoders express, using the vertex-guard Art Gallery Problem as a test case to examine if reinforcement-trained representations encode geometric feasibility knowledge.
AI.method: Train a pointer-network policy with reinforcement learning using coverage-aware reward, then freeze the trained encoder and read its embeddings with a small single-shot classifier. Both approaches use "geo-free inference" - at test time they see only vertex coordinates without visibility computation or geometric oracle.
AI.result: The reinforcement-trained policy places guards economically but leaves widening feasibility gaps beyond training range. The frozen-encoder classifier closes most feasibility gaps (cutting under-covered polygons by ~10x) both in-distribution and out-of-distribution up to ~5x training range, at explicit guard count cost.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement-trained NCO representations already encode required geometry for feasibility; residual failures reflect decoder calibration rather than missing knowledge. Probing frozen encoders offers practical way to examine what neural combinatorial solvers have internalized.

title: Human vs Machine Mathematical Difficulty on Project Euler: An Experimental Analysis
summary: We study how the effort and success probability of frontier AI systems scale with human difficulty on problems from Project Euler, an online platform of computational mathematics problems. Our dataset, from the MathArena benchmark, consists of 3840 attempts across 50 problems and 26 model configurations, with problem difficulty measured by the site's public human solve times. Motivated by a proposal of Timothy Gowers, we test a power-law relation $t_{\text{machine}} = a \cdot t_{\text{human}}^b$ between generated-token cost per successful answer and human time, and find $b < 1$ for 20 of the 25 models with usable fits, including the strongest base models; this operationalization therefore does not support an earlier prediction that machines scale worse than humans with difficulty. We also investigate whether success probability on the tested problems can be modeled by a simple exponential decay $p_{\text{success}} = e^{c t_{\text{human}}}$, predicting a linear relation between $\log p_{\text{success}}$ and $t_{\text{human}}$. Using a binning approach for data aggregation we find moderate empirical support (median bin-level $R^2 = 0.92$ across the 22 best-covered configurations) for this model. Following METR, we also fit logistic success curves and extract 50\% task-length horizons $h_{50}$; the strongest configurations in our 20 April 2026 snapshot reach roughly $2.5$--$4.3$ hours on our fastest-five human baseline, with a log-linear fit through the state-of-the-art frontier giving a descriptive doubling time of about $75$~days for the SOTA $h_{50}$.

AI.tldr: The paper examines scaling relationships between AI system performance and human difficulty on computational math problems from Project Euler, finding that AI scales better than humans with difficulty and showing moderate support for exponential decay success probability models.
AI.motivation: The study is motivated by Timothy Gowers' proposal to test whether frontier AI systems scale worse than humans with problem difficulty. The researchers want to empirically investigate the relationship between AI effort/success and human difficulty on computational mathematics problems.
AI.method: The researchers use 3840 attempts across 50 Project Euler problems and 26 model configurations from the MathArena benchmark. They test a power-law relationship between machine token cost and human solve time (t_machine = a * t_human^b), and also investigate an exponential decay model for success probability (p_success = e^{c * t_human}). They use binning for data aggregation, fit logistic success curves, and extract 50% task-length horizons (h_50).
AI.result: 1) For the power-law scaling, b < 1 for 20 of 25 models with usable fits, indicating machines scale better than humans with difficulty. 2) Moderate empirical support for exponential decay model (median bin-level R² = 0.92). 3) Strongest configurations reach 2.5-4.3 hours on fastest-five human baseline. 4) Frontier AI shows a doubling time of ~75 days for SOTA h_50 based on log-linear fit.
AI.conclusion: The study finds that frontier AI systems do not scale worse than humans with problem difficulty as previously predicted, and provides empirical support for simple exponential decay models of success probability. The results show rapid progress in AI capabilities for computational mathematics problems, with doubling times of about 75 days at the frontier.

title: The Two-Hump Problem: Bridging the Difficulty Gap in Mathematical Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. 38 pages, 9 figures. Code and datasets: https://github.com/Math-AI-Caltech/ACSolverX
summary: Mathematical search problems present a unique challenge for Reinforcement Learning (RL) due to vast search spaces and sparse rewards. In previous works, the Andrews-Curtis (AC) conjecture was established as an illustrative example of such problems. In this work, we identify a critical structural barrier in the AC landscape: a "Two-Hump" distribution, where problem instances are either trivially solvable or effectively impossible, with a scarcity of intermediate "hard-but-solvable" instances required for effective learning. We tackle this challenge through two primary avenues: novel data generation techniques to populate the difficulty gap, and significant algorithmic enhancements including the introduction of supermoves and Transformer-based architectures. We demonstrate substantial performance improvements over previous baselines, and release new comprehensive benchmark datasets including AC-19 (125,192 AC-trivial presentations of varying difficulty with length at most 19) and AC-1M (1,136,154 hard AC-trivial presentations of length at most 30), the first large-scale, publicly available datasets of this kind.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses mathematical search challenges in RL by identifying a "Two-Hump" difficulty distribution in Andrews-Curtis problems, then develops new data generation methods and algorithmic enhancements including supermoves and Transformer architectures to overcome this barrier, achieving significant performance improvements and releasing large-scale benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Mathematical search problems present unique challenges for Reinforcement Learning due to vast search spaces and sparse rewards. The authors use the Andrews-Curtis (AC) conjecture as an illustrative example and identify a critical structural barrier called the "Two-Hump" distribution, where problem instances are either trivially solvable or effectively impossible, with few intermediate "hard-but-solvable" instances needed for effective learning.
AI.method: Two primary approaches: 1) Novel data generation techniques to populate the difficulty gap between trivial and impossible instances, and 2) Significant algorithmic enhancements including the introduction of supermoves (compound moves) and Transformer-based architectures for better learning and search.
AI.result: Demonstrates substantial performance improvements over previous baselines. Releases new comprehensive benchmark datasets including AC-19 (125,192 AC-trivial presentations of varying difficulty with length at most 19) and AC-1M (1,136,154 hard AC-trivial presentations of length at most 30) - the first large-scale, publicly available datasets of this kind.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the "Two-Hump" challenge in mathematical search problems by combining novel data generation with algorithmic innovations, creating both improved solutions and valuable benchmark resources for the research community.

title: IRumAI: Reinforcement Learning for Indian Rummy
summary: Despite its massive player base and complex hidden-information dynamics, Indian Rummy has received no reinforcement learning attention. Existing agents rely on combinatorial search, which is tactically strong but slow at inference. We present IRumAI, the first RL agent for the domain. IRumAI integrates Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), meld-aware observation encoding, deadwood-driven reward shaping, and a dual-branch convolutional architecture. IRumAI is RL-trained solely against weak heuristics, after a one-time behaviour-cloning warm-start on stronger demonstration data. It generalises to defeat the entire baseline hierarchy, including a 53.9% win rate against the strongest search-based opponent unseen during RL training. Bypassing explicit search, IRumAI requires just 0.33 ms per action, which is over 7,000x faster than the state-of-the-art heuristic. Ablations validate our architectural choices, and linear probing reveals that the network implicitly models the opponent's hidden hand from public interactions.

AI.tldr: IRumAI is the first reinforcement learning agent for Indian Rummy that achieves strong performance through PPO training with specialized architecture, defeating search-based opponents while being 7,000x faster at inference.
AI.motivation: Despite Indian Rummy's massive popularity and complex hidden-information dynamics, it has received no reinforcement learning attention. Existing agents rely on combinatorial search which is tactically strong but slow at inference.
AI.method: IRumAI integrates Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), meld-aware observation encoding, deadwood-driven reward shaping, and a dual-branch convolutional architecture. It uses RL training against weak heuristics after a behavior-cloning warm-start on stronger demonstration data.
AI.result: IRumAI defeats the entire baseline hierarchy with 53.9% win rate against the strongest search-based opponent unseen during RL training. It requires just 0.33 ms per action (over 7,000x faster than state-of-the-art heuristic). Ablations validate architectural choices, and linear probing shows the network implicitly models opponent's hidden hand.
AI.conclusion: IRumAI successfully demonstrates that reinforcement learning can create effective Indian Rummy agents that combine strong tactical performance with dramatically faster inference than search-based approaches, while implicitly learning to reason about hidden information from public interactions.

title: HERALD: High-Throughput Block Diffusion LLM Serving via CPU-GPU Cooperative KV Cache Retrieval
summary: Diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) improve GPU utilization over autoregressive decoding by generating multiple tokens per forward pass, but their KV cache still grows linearly with context, limiting throughput at long contexts. KV cache offloading to host DRAM alleviates this memory pressure, but the limited PCIe bandwidth necessitates recalling only a sparse subset of KV entries. In block dLLMs, the relevant KV entries remain consistent across denoising steps within a block, enabling high-accuracy selection by identifying the top-k entries once and reusing them throughout all denoising steps. This property appears attractive for offloading as it amortizes the selection overhead across the entire block, but it requires exact attention over the full KV cache, which is too expensive under offloading. We present HERALD, a KV offloading system for block dLLMs that resolves this through two opportunities that reduce the required selection compute by a factor of the block size and enable selection to be overlapped with denoising. Across three block dLLMs and five long context tasks, HERALD achieves near-lossless accuracy at 5-10% KV budget and up to 1.59x lower per block latency and 2.47x higher throughput over GPU-only inference, with speedups growing with context length.

AI.tldr: HERALD is a KV cache offloading system for block diffusion LLMs that enables efficient sparse KV selection and overlap with denoising, achieving near-lossless accuracy at low KV budgets and significant throughput improvements for long contexts.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs improve GPU utilization through block generation but still suffer from linear KV cache growth with context length, limiting throughput for long contexts. KV cache offloading to host DRAM helps but requires sparse KV selection due to limited PCIe bandwidth. Block dLLMs have consistent KV entries across denoising steps, offering amortization opportunities but requiring expensive exact attention computation that's too costly under offloading.
AI.method: HERALD presents two key innovations: 1) Reduces selection computation by a factor of block size by exploiting consistency of relevant KV entries across denoising steps, allowing single top-k selection reused throughout the block. 2) Enables overlapping of KV selection with denoising computation to hide selection latency.
AI.result: Across three block dLLMs and five long context tasks, HERALD achieves near-lossless accuracy at 5-10% KV budget, with up to 1.59x lower per-block latency and 2.47x higher throughput compared to GPU-only inference. Performance improvements scale with context length.
AI.conclusion: HERALD effectively addresses the KV cache memory bottleneck in block diffusion LLMs for long contexts through intelligent sparse offloading that leverages cross-step KV consistency and computational overlap, enabling practical long-context inference with minimal accuracy loss and significant performance gains.

title: CFAgentBench: A Reproducible Environment and Benchmark for Autonomous Construction-Finance Agents
comment: 28 pages, 2 figures, 13 tables. Benchmark, environment spec, and app contract released. First open-weight three-model sweep (k=5) on a 40-task oracle-validated executable suite; frontier-model leaderboard committed in the roadmap
summary: We introduce CFAgentBench, a reproducible, self-hostable environment and benchmark for autonomous construction-finance agents: a CFO/controller-class agent operating across the real software stack a US construction finance team runs - ERP, project management, email, documents, pay applications, payroll, certified payroll, lien waivers, and bank/treasury portals. It contains 1,014 machine-gradeable task specifications across 8 domains and 77 families, every family grounded in a real source; a self-validated subset of 40 tasks (54 with a project-management extension) is compiled into oracle-validated executable evaluators, the runnable suite reported here. Following WebArena, the benchmark runs on an executable environment rather than static traces: 35 mock applications (31 reconciled to one company book, plus 4 PM platforms) over 9 archetypes, each implementing a uniform self-hostable app contract, so every task is graded by functional correctness - a state diff plus forbidden-side-effect checks plus required-output regexes - with an LLM judge used only for reply quality, never as reward. A distinguishing principle is a money-movement guard: 278 instances embed a payment, payroll, e-signature, or e-filing step where the correct behavior is to stop and stage for human approval, and executing even the correct transaction fails the task. The public split (n=711) is sized for a 95% Wilson half-width of +/-4.1%; a private, contamination-protected split (n=303) is reserved for remote scoring. In a first three-model open-weight sweep (k=5), the strongest agent reaches pass^1 = 0.67 but only pass^5 = 0.38 - losing 43% of its successes when required to repeat them under temperature-0 decoding. The within-model pass^1 to pass^5 collapse and sharp per-domain heterogeneity are clear evidence that single-attempt accuracy overstates deployable construction-finance competence.

AI.tldr: CFAgentBench is a reproducible benchmark for autonomous construction-finance agents that operates across real financial software stacks, featuring 1,014 machine-gradeable tasks with functional correctness evaluation and a unique money-movement guard requiring human approval for financial transactions.
AI.motivation: There's a need to properly evaluate autonomous agents in construction finance domains that operate across real software stacks (ERP, project management, banking portals, etc.), with functional correctness evaluation rather than just LLM-based scoring. Current benchmarks don't adequately capture the complexity of financial workflows requiring human oversight for money movements.
AI.method: Created a reproducible, self-hostable benchmark environment with 1,014 task specifications across 8 domains and 77 families, grounded in real sources. Implemented 35 mock applications across 9 archetypes with uniform app contracts. Tasks are graded by functional correctness (state diffs, forbidden side-effects, output regexes) with LLM judges only for reply quality. Includes a unique money-movement guard where 278 instances require human approval for financial transactions.
AI.result: The benchmark reveals that single-attempt accuracy overstates deployable competence. In initial testing with three models, the strongest agent achieved pass@1 = 0.67 but only pass@5 = 0.38 - losing 43% of successes when repeating under temperature-0 decoding. Sharp per-domain heterogeneity shows current agents' limitations in reliable construction-finance workflows.
AI.conclusion: CFAgentBench provides a comprehensive evaluation framework that reveals significant gaps in autonomous construction-finance agent reliability, particularly showing that single-trial metrics overestimate deployable competence. The benchmark's money-movement guard and functional correctness evaluation highlight the need for human oversight in financial workflows and more robust agent development.

title: A new classification method based on Minimum Spanning Trees
summary: Minimum Spanning Trees have been used in unsupervised learning, particularly in clustering tasks, due to their ability to recognize clusters by removing edges that are considered inconsistent in defining those clusters. This paper aims to study the use of Minimum Spanning Trees in supervised learning. Specifically, we propose a classification algorithm based on Minimum Spanning Trees. To improve its performance, we introduce a robust version of the method that is also computationally more efficient. We evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed method through an extensive simulation study. We also apply the proposed methodology to a real-world case study involving aircraft trajectories.

AI.tldr: Proposes a classification algorithm based on Minimum Spanning Trees for supervised learning, with a robust and computationally efficient version.
AI.motivation: Minimum Spanning Trees (MSTs) have been successfully used in unsupervised learning (clustering), but their potential in supervised learning remains underexplored.
AI.method: Develops a classification algorithm using Minimum Spanning Trees, then introduces a robust version that improves performance and computational efficiency.
AI.result: Evaluated through extensive simulations and applied to real-world aircraft trajectory data, demonstrating effectiveness in supervised classification tasks.
AI.conclusion: MST-based classification algorithms are viable for supervised learning, with the robust version offering improved performance and computational efficiency suitable for practical applications.

title: Nous: A Predictive World Model for Long-Term Agent Memory
comment: 9 pages, 1 figure, 4 tables. Preprint; ablations, LongMemEval evaluation, and a controlled comparison against concurrent work (BeliefMem) planned for a future revision
summary: We present Nous, a novel agent memory architecture grounded in the principle that knowledge is prediction, not storage. Rather than persisting facts as database records, vector embeddings, or knowledge-graph triples, Nous maintains a predictive world model: a collection of categorical probability distributions, called dimensions, one per entity-attribute pair observed in conversation. Each incoming observation is scored by its information-theoretic surprise S = -log2 P(obs | D), and the distribution is updated via a closed-form Bayesian posterior. The primary stored artifact is the delta, a record of the shift from prior to posterior belief, rather than the fact itself. Forgetting emerges naturally as entropy decay toward the uniform distribution, and identity resolution is handled through mutual information between entity dimension sets. Evaluated on the LoCoMo long-term conversational memory benchmark across ten conversations (1,540 questions) using GPT-4o-mini as backbone, Nous achieves F1 of 63.50 (single-hop), 55.32 (multi-hop), 58.57 (temporal), and 62.50 (open-domain). Against A-MEM's self-reported GPT-4o-mini numbers, Nous shows substantial gains in three of four categories, though we note that independent citations of A-MEM's results disagree with each other on category assignment, a reproducibility issue we discuss openly rather than resolve unilaterally. We additionally compare against BeliefMem, a concurrently developed system built on the same core premise of belief-based rather than deterministic memory; on the same benchmark and backbone, Nous's self-reported numbers exceed BeliefMem's self-reported numbers on all four categories, though we flag several uncontrolled differences between the two evaluation pipelines that prevent this from being a fully controlled comparison. Nous requires no external vector database or graph engine.

AI.tldr: Nous: A predictive world model memory architecture that treats knowledge as prediction rather than storage, achieving strong performance on conversational memory benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current agent memory systems store facts as static records (database entries, vector embeddings, knowledge graph triples), which doesn't align with how human memory works as a predictive process. The authors propose that knowledge should be treated as prediction rather than storage.
AI.method: Maintains a predictive world model consisting of categorical probability distributions (dimensions) for each entity-attribute pair observed in conversation. Each observation is scored by information-theoretic surprise S = -log2 P(obs | D), and distributions are updated via closed-form Bayesian posterior. The primary stored artifact is the delta (shift from prior to posterior belief), not the fact itself.
AI.result: Achieves F1 scores of 63.50 (single-hop), 55.32 (multi-hop), 58.57 (temporal), and 62.50 (open-domain) on the LoCoMo benchmark using GPT-4o-mini backbone. Outperforms A-MEM in three categories and exceeds BeliefMem's self-reported numbers across all categories, though with uncontrolled differences in evaluation pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Nous presents a novel memory architecture grounded in prediction rather than storage, with natural forgetting via entropy decay and identity resolution through mutual information. The approach eliminates need for external vector databases or graph engines while achieving competitive performance on conversational memory tasks.

title: When Is an LLM Worth It for Hyperparameter Optimization? A Budget-Matched Study on Tabular Data Finds the Warm-Start Is a Default Configuration, Not the Model
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have been proposed as hyperparameter-optimization (HPO) advisors that "warm-start" search from prior knowledge, proposing strong configurations in very few evaluations. We test that claim under a budget-matched, multi-seed protocol on eight PMLB tabular benchmarks, comparing an LLM advisor (LLM-OptFlow) against four classical baselines (random search, Optuna-TPE, Gaussian-process Bayesian optimization, and successive halving) over one shared search space, with paired tests and bootstrap 95% CIs across 8 x 5 = 40 (task, seed) units. The finding is cautionary. The advisor's strong first point is not an LLM output at all: like prior LLM-HPO systems the loop is seeded with a fixed default configuration, evaluated before any model call, which alone reaches 88.7% mean best-CV, identical to within 0.01 pp across all seven advisor models tested. The LLM's own proposals add only +0.40 pp of cross-validation accuracy over that seed and nothing on held-out test (LLM-Default = -0.01 pp, p = 0.92). When the same seed is granted to classical search, the apparent lead collapses: against seeded random search it leads by +0.20 pp at 2 evaluations, is tied by 5, and is behind by 12 (-0.37 pp). Without the seed, classical search ties the advisor by 12 evaluations and beats it by 40 (+0.6 to +0.8 pp, p <= 1e-4). Two LLM-specific behaviors survive: a single-task exploration failure (vehicle), and a rule-based confidence filter that removes ~33% of wasted compute without changing accuracy. The recommendation is deflationary: on tabular HPO, seed classical search with a sensible default; an LLM advisor adds no measurable generalization benefit and is overtaken within a handful of evaluations. We release the harness and a script that reproduces every statistic.

AI.tldr: LLM-based hyperparameter optimization advisors don't outperform classical search methods when properly benchmarked, providing no measurable generalization benefit beyond using a sensible default configuration as seed.
AI.motivation: To rigorously test claims that large language models (LLMs) can effectively warm-start hyperparameter optimization (HPO) by proposing strong configurations with few evaluations, using proper benchmark protocols.
AI.method: Comprehensive evaluation of an LLM advisor (LLM-OptFlow) against four classical baselines (random search, Optuna-TPE, Gaussian-process Bayesian optimization, and successive halving) on eight PMLB tabular benchmarks. Used budget-matched, multi-seed protocol with paired tests and bootstrap 95% CIs across 40 (task, seed) units.
AI.result: LLM advisors provide no meaningful advantage: the strong first point comes from a fixed default configuration (not the LLM), which alone reaches 88.7% mean best-CV accuracy. LLM's own proposals add only +0.40 pp cross-validation accuracy over the seed and nothing on held-out test. Classical search methods tie or beat the LLM advisor within 5-40 evaluations.
AI.conclusion: For tabular HPO, simply seed classical search with a sensible default configuration; LLM advisors add no measurable generalization benefit and are quickly overtaken by classical methods within a handful of evaluations.

title: Attractor Domain Theory: A Mathematical Framework for Cardiovascular Attractor Analysis with Wearable Photoplethysmography (PPG) Validation
summary: The cardiovascular system evolves along a bounded trajectory in physiological state space that converges to a compact geometric object: the cardiac attractor. A wearable photoplethysmograph (PPG) or electrocardiograph (ECG) observes a one-dimensional projection of this attractor; by Takens' embedding theorem, delay coordinates reconstruct its full geometry. Three decades of nonlinear cardiac dynamics have extracted Lyapunov exponents, recurrence statistics, and sample entropy from reconstructed attractors, yet no principled account exists of which attractor properties capture which cardiovascular quantities, or why, leaving feature selection as a search problem and negative results uninterpretable. We introduce Attractor Domain Theory (ADT), which proves that the reconstructed attractor's information partitions into three mutually non-redundant domains: the Geometry Domain G (delay embedding; native capability: artifact rejection), the Ergodic Domain S (asymptotic statistical invariants; native capability: stability estimation), and the Variational Domain V (finite-time Lyapunov exponent field; native capability: hemodynamic inference). We prove a Domain Sufficiency Theorem (the Parseval analog for attractor information) and establish that three domains are necessary and sufficient. Geometry Domain validation via the SCSI framework across 176,742 PPG segments from four datasets yields AUC = 0.757 [0.686-0.828] and NPV = 0.966 after correcting three systematic evaluation artifacts (+0.179 net inflation). Ablation confirms C_NL as the dominant Geometry Domain component (Delta AUC = -0.413) and intra-domain redundancy across five components.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Attractor Domain Theory (ADT), which partitions cardiac attractor information into three non-redundant domains: Geometry, Ergodic, and Variational, proving these are necessary and sufficient for comprehensive cardiovascular analysis.
AI.motivation: Despite three decades of nonlinear cardiac dynamics research extracting various features from reconstructed cardiac attractors, there's no principled understanding of which attractor properties capture which cardiovascular quantities, leaving feature selection as an uninterpretable search problem.
AI.method: Introduces Attractor Domain Theory (ADT) which proves that reconstructed cardiac attractor information partitions into three mutually non-redundant domains: Geometry Domain (G), Ergodic Domain (S), and Variational Domain (V). Proves Domain Sufficiency Theorem and validates Geometry Domain using SCSI framework across 176,742 PPG segments from four datasets.
AI.result: Geometry Domain validation yields AUC = 0.757 [0.686-0.828] and NPV = 0.966 after correcting systematic evaluation artifacts (+0.179 net inflation). Ablation confirms C_NL as dominant Geometry Domain component (Delta AUC = -0.413) and reveals intra-domain redundancy across five components.
AI.conclusion: ADT provides the first principled framework for understanding which cardiac attractor properties capture which cardiovascular quantities, enabling interpretable feature selection and making previously uninterpretable negative results meaningful through domain-specific decomposition.

title: A Framework for Directed Acyclic Hypergraph Learning
comment: 3 pages. Accepted and presented as an oral presentation at the 9th Graph Signal Processing Workshop (GSP 2026), June 8-10, 2026, Madrid, Spain
summary: Continuous optimization methods for learning Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) operate on weighted adjacency matrices and are therefore limited to pairwise causal relationships. We propose a framework for learning Directed Acyclic Hypergraphs (DAHGs) from observational data, capturing joint parental influences that pairwise models cannot represent. Our approach rests on three components: (i) a generalized linear structural equation model (SEM) with multiplicative interaction terms whose non-zero weights correspond one-to-one with directed hyperedges; (ii) a weighted adjacency tensor representation whose acyclicity is characterized via nilpotency under the tensor t-product; and (iii) a differentiable acyclicity constraint derived through the Fourier decomposition of the t-product, which reduces tensor nilpotency to slice-wise matrix nilpotency and enables least-squares learning via the augmented Lagrangian method.

AI.tldr: Proposes a framework for learning Directed Acyclic Hypergraphs (DAHGs) from observational data to capture joint parental influences that traditional pairwise DAG models cannot represent.
AI.motivation: Current continuous optimization methods for learning DAGs are limited to pairwise causal relationships and cannot capture joint parental influences that may be present in complex systems.
AI.method: Three-component approach: (1) generalized linear SEM with multiplicative interaction terms mapping to hyperedges, (2) weighted adjacency tensor representation with acyclicity via t-product nilpotency, (3) differentiable acyclicity constraint through Fourier decomposition enabling least-squares learning via augmented Lagrangian method.
AI.result: Developed a framework capable of learning Directed Acyclic Hypergraphs, allowing representation of causal relationships where multiple parents jointly influence a child node, which cannot be captured by traditional pairwise DAG models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework extends causal structure learning beyond pairwise relationships to capture joint parental influences through directed hypergraphs, with a practical implementation via tensor representations and differentiable constraints.

title: When Does a Video-Language Model Stop Watching? Reward Strength Controls the Formation and Reversal of Visual Shortcuts in Multimodal RLVR
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is increasingly applied to large vision-language models (LVLMs), yet outcome-only optimization can drive a model to stop attending to the video and instead exploit linguistic priors -- a failure we call a visual shortcut. While the existence of such perception bypass is by now documented, how it forms, whether it can be undone, and when intervention still helps remain open. We treat the strength of a grounding penalty, lambda, as a control knob and characterize the formation-reversal dynamics of visual shortcuts along the training time axis. On a held-out, out-of-distribution diagnostic set, we find: (i) a sharp onset -- shortcut reliance emerges abruptly over a narrow window of optimization steps and is robust across random seeds; (ii) a monotone dose-response -- increasing lambda progressively suppresses the shortcut, and at an intermediate dose the trajectory first forms and then reverses the shortcut, exposing a hysteresis-like asymmetry between acquiring and removing it; and (iii) a critical intervention window -- applying the penalty before onset arrests shortcut formation, whereas the same penalty applied after consolidation is markedly less effective. Together these results recast visual-shortcut collapse not as a binary defect but as a controllable, time-dependent, and asymmetric process, with direct implications for when and how strongly to regularize multimodal RLVR.

AI.tldr: Visual shortcuts in multimodal RL emerge abruptly, can be reversed with proper grounding penalties, and require timely intervention before consolidation.
AI.motivation: RLVR applied to LVLMs can cause models to exploit linguistic priors instead of attending to visual content (visual shortcuts). While documented, we lack understanding of how these shortcuts form, if they can be reversed, and when interventions are effective.
AI.method: Treat grounding penalty strength (lambda) as control parameter and characterize formation-reversal dynamics along training time. Use held-out OOD diagnostic set to analyze: onset abruptness, dose-response to lambda, and critical intervention timing.
AI.result: Three key findings: (1) Sharp onset - shortcuts emerge abruptly in narrow optimization window robust across seeds; (2) Monotone dose-response - increasing lambda suppresses shortcuts, with hysteresis between acquisition and removal; (3) Critical window - penalties before onset prevent formation, but are less effective after consolidation.
AI.conclusion: Visual shortcut collapse is not binary but controllable, time-dependent, and asymmetric process. Provides guidance on when and how strongly to regularize multimodal RLVR for effective grounding.

title: Decodable but Not Faithful: Coupling Natural-Language Rationales to Programmatic Verifiers
comment: Accepted to the ICML 2026 AI4Math Workshop as a poster
summary: Language models can generate plausible rationales for their predictions, but these explanations may not faithfully represent the model's internal reasoning. We propose verifier-coupled reasoning, a framework that inserts inline claims into reasoning traces and trains an auxiliary consistency head to predict programmatic verifier outputs from rationale-span hidden states. The central finding is a gap between decodability and faithfulness: consistency training reliably makes verifier information decodable from rationale representations, but decodability does not guarantee faithful generation. In LeanCheck (formal theorem proving), rationale-only and proof-only pooling achieve perfect directional separation under counterfactual conflict. In KataGo (Go engine), commentary spans encode 10-way win-rate buckets at 81% accuracy. Yet in a code setting, the model achieves 98.6% coupling while its generated explanations remain unfaithful: fluent prose with correct structured claims, but describing unrelated algorithms; a controlled pretrained-vs-from-scratch comparison shows the gap is not capacity-driven. Synthetic activation patching confirms causal influence (73-89% vs. 31% baseline), FEVER reveals that evidence-only pooling isolates genuine evidence sensitivity at the cost of raw accuracy, and per-claim analysis shows that consistency loss disproportionately benefits fine-grained claims over binary ones. These results establish that consistency losses are effective diagnostics and representation-shaping tools, but not sufficient conditions for faithful reasoning.

AI.tldr: Consistency training makes verifier information decodable from language model rationales, but decodability doesn't guarantee faithful reasoning, revealing a gap between representation and faithful generation.
AI.motivation: Language models generate plausible rationales but these explanations may not faithfully represent the model's actual internal reasoning, creating a need to distinguish between decodable information and faithful reasoning.
AI.method: Proposed verifier-coupled reasoning framework that inserts inline claims into reasoning traces and trains an auxiliary consistency head to predict programmatic verifier outputs from rationale-span hidden states.
AI.result: In LeanCheck (theorem proving), rationale-only and proof-only pooling achieve perfect directional separation; in KataGo (Go), commentary spans encode win-rate buckets at 81% accuracy; but in code settings, models achieve 98.6% coupling while explanations remain unfaithful. Synthetic activation patching confirms causal influence (73-89% vs 31% baseline).
AI.conclusion: Consistency losses are effective diagnostics and representation-shaping tools, but are not sufficient conditions for faithful reasoning, establishing that decodability does not guarantee faithful generation.

title: Can Reasoning Models Detect Changes to their Chains of Thought?
summary: There are many reasons one may want to edit a model's chain of thought (CoT) -- e.g., to prefill it with reasoning from a stronger model or to remove steps that may yield unsafe outputs. The success of these interventions plausibly depends on a model's inability to notice them, as the model may alter its behavior if it suspects tampering. In this work, we study whether recent reasoning models are able to detect such interventions on their CoTs under a variety of conditions: both during reasoning and after it, and when prefilled both with their own CoTs and with those of other models. Broadly, we find that (i) models exhibit only very modest detection accuracy; (ii) models struggle to identify *how* their CoT was modified; and (iii) models are about as good at detecting changes to their own CoTs as to those of other models.

AI.tldr: Models show limited ability to detect modifications to their reasoning chains, with similar detection accuracy for their own and others' chains.
AI.motivation: To assess whether models can detect tampering with their chain-of-thought reasoning, which is relevant for interventions like prefilling with stronger models' reasoning or removing unsafe steps.
AI.method: Study detection ability under various conditions: during vs after reasoning, with their own vs others' CoTs, and assess accuracy and identification of modification type.
AI.result: Models show modest detection accuracy, struggle to identify how CoT was modified, and perform similarly for their own and others' CoTs.
AI.conclusion: Current reasoning models have limited awareness of interventions on their reasoning chains, making such edits potentially effective.

title: Expressivity Saturation: Reduced Affine Region Usage Under Increasing Task Complexity
summary: Piecewise-affine neural networks (e.g., with ReLU or LeakyReLU activations) implement continuous piecewise-affine maps, and the number of affine regions provides a natural proxy for expressive capacity. However, the gap between theoretical region capacity and the affine regions realized after training remains insufficiently understood. We study this gap from two complementary perspectives. First, we give a rigorous, architecture-dependent theorem for affine line-segment probes: for multilayer perceptrons with piecewise-affine activations, the number of affine pieces realized along an affine line-segment probe is upper bounded by an explicit product of layer-wise width terms (and activation breakpoint factors). This yields a neuron-threshold lower bound for representing target functions with prescribed one-dimensional piece complexity, formalizing the minimal region budget required for complex signals. Second, we exactly enumerate affine regions realized within bounded 2D and higher-dimensional domains under controlled task complexity. Under fixed architectures and training protocols, increasing input--label complexity yields trained solutions with markedly fewer realized regions in the evaluation domain, even though worst-case architectural capacity is unchanged; we call this reduced region usage expressivity saturation. Moreover, in the most challenging regimes, 2D visualizations show that region-usage collapse often coincides with degraded decision boundaries. Finally, we visualize the training dynamics of affine-region partitions and decision boundaries, revealing a consistent refinement process during optimization.

AI.tldr: Piecewise-affine neural networks have theoretical region capacity, but there's a gap between this capacity and the regions actually realized after training. The paper studies this gap, finding that trained networks use fewer regions than theoretically possible (expressivity saturation), especially with complex tasks, and this can lead to degraded performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the gap between the theoretical affine region capacity of piecewise-affine neural networks (like ReLU/LeakyReLU networks) and the actual affine regions realized after training. This gap remains insufficiently understood despite being crucial for understanding expressive capacity.
AI.method: 1) Provides architecture-dependent theorems for affine line-segment probes, giving upper bounds on realized affine pieces along line segments based on layer widths and activation breakpoints. 2) Exactly enumerates affine regions realized within bounded 2D and higher-dimensional domains under controlled task complexity. 3) Analyzes training dynamics of affine-region partitions and decision boundaries across different complexity regimes.
AI.result: 1) Derives explicit product bounds for realized affine pieces along line segments. 2) Shows that increasing input-label complexity yields trained solutions with markedly fewer realized regions (expressivity saturation). 3) Demonstrates that in challenging regimes, region-usage collapse coincides with degraded decision boundaries. 4) Reveals consistent refinement process of affine-region partitions during optimization.
AI.conclusion: There exists a significant gap between theoretical region capacity and realized regions in trained networks. Complex tasks lead to expressivity saturation where networks use fewer regions than available capacity, sometimes causing degraded performance. Understanding and potentially mitigating this region-usage collapse could improve network performance on complex tasks.

title: Geometry-Aware Online Scheduling for LLM Serving: From Theoretical Bound to System Practice
summary: The explosive demand for interactive Large Language Model serving has highlighted the management of the Key-Value cache's dynamic memory footprint as a critical area for performance optimization in inference engines. Modern inference systems overwhelmingly rely on time-centric scheduling heuristics, such as Shortest Job First. However, their theoretical optimality is rooted in traditional schedule modeling, failing to capture the highly dynamic, 2D spatio-temporal geometric growth specific to LLM inference mechanisms. To resolve this, we propose the geometry-aware online scheduling by introducing the Smallest Volume First (SVF) algorithm and its highly efficient variant, 1-bit SVF. Theoretically, we provide a rigorous mathematical foundation for our approach. Utilizing a novel proof methodology, we tighten the worst-case competitive ratio ($\text{CR} \le 48 \rightarrow \text{CR} \le 5$) for SVF with known output lengths. Building upon this core breakthrough, we complete a comprehensive theoretical taxonomy analyzing our algorithms across different traffic scenarios and information availability. Practically, we seamlessly integrate our approach as a plug-and-play layer in vLLM. Extensive evaluations on Llama-3.1 models demonstrate comprehensive performance gains: SVF delivers strong reductions in both average and tail latency, while 1-bit SVF, with merely a single bit information, achieves competitive throughput and latency. This work establishes a theoretically sound and empirically proven approach for resolving memory-constrained scheduling in modern LLM deployments. To facilitate future research, our code is available at https://github.com/Aurora-Kl/Geometry-Aware-Online-Scheduling.git.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes geometry-aware online scheduling algorithms (Smallest Volume First and 1-bit SVF) to optimize LLM inference by managing KV cache memory footprint more effectively than time-based heuristics.
AI.motivation: The explosive growth in interactive LLM serving requires better management of Key-Value cache's dynamic memory footprint. Current inference systems use time-centric scheduling heuristics (like Shortest Job First) that fail to capture the unique 2D spatio-temporal geometric growth patterns specific to LLM inference mechanisms.
AI.method: Proposes Smallest Volume First (SVF) algorithm and its highly efficient variant, 1-bit SVF, which are geometry-aware online scheduling approaches. Provides rigorous mathematical foundation with novel proof methodology that tightens worst-case competitive ratio. Includes comprehensive theoretical taxonomy analyzing algorithms across different traffic scenarios and information availability.
AI.result: Theoretical breakthrough: Tightens worst-case competitive ratio from CR ≤ 48 to CR ≤ 5 for SVF with known output lengths. Practical integration in vLLM shows comprehensive performance gains on Llama-3.1 models: SVF reduces both average and tail latency, while 1-bit SVF achieves competitive throughput and latency with only single bit information.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a theoretically sound and empirically proven approach for memory-constrained scheduling in modern LLM deployments, moving beyond traditional time-centric heuristics to geometry-aware scheduling that better captures LLM inference patterns.

title: Embedding Linear Equality Constraints in Probabilistic Neural Networks for Dynamic Modelling
summary: Machine learning models are increasingly used to model chemical process systems, yet they often lack principled uncertainty quantification and mechanisms to enforce physical constraints. We propose a probabilistic neural network framework that guarantees satisfaction of linear equality constraints within a given tolerance, while capturing aleatoric uncertainty. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, our formulation demonstrates improved predictive accuracy, uncertainty calibration, and adherence to constraints on reduced data. It also demonstrates competitive performance, but with significantly faster training times when evaluated on large data regimes. We evaluated this on two batch reactor case studies, enforcing mass balances.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic neural network framework that enforces linear equality constraints with uncertainty quantification for chemical process modeling, showing improved accuracy and constraint satisfaction.
AI.motivation: Machine learning models for chemical processes often lack proper uncertainty quantification and mechanisms to enforce physical constraints, limiting their reliability and applicability in real-world chemical engineering systems.
AI.method: Proposed a probabilistic neural network framework that guarantees satisfaction of linear equality constraints within a specified tolerance while capturing aleatoric uncertainty. The method was evaluated on two batch reactor case studies enforcing mass balances.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates improved predictive accuracy, better uncertainty calibration, and stronger adherence to constraints, especially on reduced data. It also shows competitive performance with significantly faster training times in large data regimes compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed probabilistic neural network framework successfully addresses key limitations in chemical process modeling by providing principled uncertainty quantification while enforcing physical constraints, making it a promising approach for reliable chemical engineering applications.

title: Hypothesis-Driven Skill Optimization for LLM Agents
summary: External skills can improve action-oriented LLM agents without changing model weights, but persistent skill updates are risky when they are distilled from sparse or noisy trajectories. A plausible reflection may encode a useful procedure, a spurious shortcut, or a rule that the target executor cannot reliably follow. We propose Hypothesis-Driven Skill Optimization (HDSO), a train-free framework in which both the skill curator and the agent executor are frozen inference endpoints. The curator observes executor traces, proposes a falsifiable hypothesis with an explicit validation plan, instantiates the hypothesis as a candidate skill package, validates the package through paired control/treatment executions, reviews behavior differences, and consolidates only supported candidates into an approved repository. The executor consumes approved skills through progressive disclosure, preserving the executor-only path when no skill is selected. On ALFWorld, HDSO improves executor-only baselines by +6.9 Avg. SR points for Qwen3-8B and +4.0 points for Qwen3.6-27B. Under 20% randomly flipped success/failure feedback during skill discovery and validation, HDSO preserves a +7.1-point gain for Qwen3-8B. Transfer and heterogeneous-pair diagnostics further show that validated repositories can be useful beyond the run that produced them, but cross-model curation succeeds only when curator diagnosis, executor capability, and validation evidence align. HDSO provides an auditable skill lifecycle for frozen action agents rather than an unconstrained memory accumulation procedure.

AI.tldr: HDSO is a train-free framework for safely updating external skills in frozen LLM agents through hypothesis-driven validation, preventing harmful skill consolidation from noisy or unreliable trajectories.
AI.motivation: External skills can enhance action-oriented LLM agents without retraining, but directly distilling skills from sparse or noisy agent trajectories is risky because they may encode spurious shortcuts or procedures that the executor cannot reliably follow. There's a need for a safer approach to skill updates that avoids accumulating harmful or unreliable behaviors.
AI.method: HDSO (Hypothesis-Driven Skill Optimization) maintains frozen inference endpoints for both skill curator and agent executor. The curator: 1) observes executor traces, 2) proposes falsifiable hypotheses with explicit validation plans, 3) instantiates hypotheses as candidate skill packages, 4) validates through paired control/treatment executions, 5) reviews behavior differences, and 6) consolidates only supported candidates into an approved repository. The executor consumes approved skills through progressive disclosure while preserving executor-only fallback paths.
AI.result: On ALFWorld, HDSO improved executor-only baselines by +6.9 Avg. SR points for Qwen3-8B and +4.0 points for Qwen3.6-27B. Under 20% randomly flipped success/failure feedback during skill discovery/validation, HDSO preserved a +7.1-point gain for Qwen3-8B. Transfer and cross-model diagnostics showed validated repositories can be useful beyond their originating run, but cross-model curation only succeeds when curator diagnosis, executor capability, and validation evidence align.
AI.conclusion: HDSO provides an auditable skill lifecycle for frozen action agents rather than unconstrained memory accumulation. It enables safe skill consolidation through rigorous hypothesis testing and validation, preventing harmful skill adoption while maintaining performance gains even with noisy feedback during the discovery process.

title: AdaPrivate-TS: Private Thompson Sampling for Contextual Bandits with Privacy Amplification
comment: Accepted at the 39th Canadian Conference on Artificial Intelligence (Canadian AI 2026) as a long paper; selected for oral presentation. 12 pages, 6 tables
summary: We present AdaPrivate-TS, a differentially private contextual bandit algorithm that combines Thompson Sampling with batched zCDP composition. Our key insight is that differential privacy noise inflates the posterior covariance in a structured way: adding Gaussian noise $N(0,σ^2 I)$ to $b$ yields sampling covariance $v^2 A^{-1} + σ^2 A^{-2}$, which Thompson Sampling interprets as increased uncertainty rather than pure corruption. Under event-level privacy (protecting individual interactions) with stochastic contexts, we prove that the privacy cost is only $O(\sqrt{d}\,\log T/\sqrtρ)$, logarithmic in $T$, because parallel composition amortizes noise across batches. Additionally, we explore privacy amplification via Poisson subsampling, which can reduce effective noise at stringent privacy budgets. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate: (1) AdaPrivate-TS achieves 93-99% of non-private performance at $\varepsilon \in [0.5, 5]$, outperforming UCB by 0.5-3.7% and up to 18% with tuned adaptive exploration at extreme $\varepsilon$; (2) privacy amplification provides additional 2-5% gains at low $\varepsilon$; (3) on MovieLens and Jester, AdaPrivate-TS achieves the best overall performance among event-level baselines, dominating at $\varepsilon \geq 2$; (4) under DP-SVD private features, TS's advantage over UCB grows to +11%, confirming noise-as-uncertainty is not limited to reward privacy. We provide rigorous proofs for privacy guarantees under interactive zCDP composition and comprehensive evaluation including convergence curves, 12-seed CIs, and DP-SVD feature ablation.

AI.tldr: AdaPrivate-TS is a differentially private contextual bandit algorithm that combines Thompson Sampling with batched zCDP composition, treating privacy noise as increased uncertainty rather than corruption.
AI.motivation: Existing differentially private contextual bandit algorithms often treat privacy noise as pure corruption, degrading performance. The authors aim to design an algorithm where privacy noise is interpreted as increased uncertainty, leveraging Thompson Sampling's ability to handle uncertainty.
AI.method: Combines Thompson Sampling with batched zCDP composition. Key insight: adding Gaussian noise to bandit parameters yields sampling covariance that Thompson Sampling interprets as increased uncertainty. Uses parallel composition to amortize noise across batches and explores privacy amplification via Poisson subsampling.
AI.result: Achieves 93-99% of non-private performance at ε∈[0.5,5], outperforming UCB by 0.5-3.7% (up to 18% with tuned exploration). Privacy amplification provides 2-5% gains at low ε. Best overall performance on MovieLens and Jester datasets among event-level baselines, especially at ε≥2.
AI.conclusion: AdaPrivate-TS demonstrates that differentially private Thompson Sampling can effectively interpret privacy noise as uncertainty, achieving near non-private performance with rigorous privacy guarantees. The approach works for both reward privacy and DP-SVD feature privacy, showing broad applicability.

title: Reference-Free Assessment of Physical Consistency in World Model-based Video Generation
summary: We introduce reference-free measures for evaluating the physical consistency of generated videos, combining relative and absolute approaches to assess fidelity. Although tools like WorldGym or WorldEval enable robotic simulation via video generation, physical fidelity gaps often prevent these environments from accurately reproducing real-world task success rates of VLA models. Unlike existing evaluation methods, which require costly human voting (Elo) or unavailable ground-truth references (FVD), our approach utilizes DROID-SLAM and SEA-RAFT to quantify physical inconsistencies, motivated by WorldScore. Videos filtered using our relative consistency assessment show an improvement in task success rates of over 8%, effectively narrowing the simulation-to-reality gap. Furthermore, our absolute assessment enables spatio-temporal localization, providing visualization of when and where physical artifacts occur.

AI.tldr: Reference-free physical consistency evaluation for generated videos using relative and absolute approaches to assess fidelity and narrow simulation-to-reality gaps.
AI.motivation: Current video generation tools for robotic simulation (like WorldGym/WorldEval) have physical fidelity gaps that prevent accurate reproduction of real-world VLA model task success rates. Existing evaluation methods require costly human voting (Elo) or unavailable ground-truth references (FVD).
AI.method: Introduces reference-free measures combining relative and absolute approaches. Uses DROID-SLAM and SEA-RAFT to quantify physical inconsistencies (motivated by WorldScore). Relative assessment filters videos for consistency, while absolute assessment enables spatio-temporal localization of physical artifacts.
AI.result: Videos filtered using relative consistency assessment show over 8% improvement in task success rates, effectively narrowing simulation-to-reality gap. Absolute assessment provides visualization of when and where physical artifacts occur.
AI.conclusion: The proposed reference-free physical consistency evaluation effectively addresses limitations of existing methods by providing both quantitative improvement in task success rates and qualitative localization of physical artifacts, bridging the gap between simulated and real-world performance.

title: Decision-Focused Learning: When and Why Traditional Prediction Models Fail
summary: Plugging predictions of unknown parameters into downstream optimization problems, often referred to as the ``predict-then-optimize'' paradigm, has long been a standard approach in decision-making under uncertainty. However, improved predictive accuracy does not, in general, translate into improved decision quality. This disconnect has motivated growing interest in decision-focused learning (DFL) within the operations research community. This tutorial reviews recent developments in DFL and highlights key methodological insights, with a particular focus on stochastic linear programming as the downstream decision-making problem. We discuss why several widely used tools in traditional statistical learning are not directly suited to decision-focused settings and must be rethought, including (i) data collection strategies driven purely by predictive uncertainty and (ii) distributional distance measures such as the Wasserstein distance. We summarize properties of DFL that distinguish it from conventional predictive modeling and provide insights into the development of new decision-focused tools.

AI.tldr: Decision-focused learning (DFL) improves on the predict-then-optimize paradigm by directly optimizing decision quality rather than predictive accuracy, with key methodological insights for stochastic linear programming problems.
AI.motivation: The predict-then-optimize paradigm has a fundamental disconnect where improved predictive accuracy doesn't necessarily translate to better decision quality, motivating the need for decision-focused learning approaches.
AI.method: This tutorial reviews DFL developments with focus on stochastic linear programming, rethinking traditional statistical learning tools including data collection strategies and distributional distance measures that aren't directly suited to decision-focused settings.
AI.result: The paper provides key methodological insights distinguishing DFL from conventional predictive modeling and develops new decision-focused tools for improved decision-making under uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Decision-focused learning represents a paradigm shift from traditional predict-then-optimize approaches, requiring rethinking of statistical tools and offering promising directions for improving decision quality in optimization under uncertainty.

title: ARIA: A Causal-Aware Framework for Rescuing LLM Reasoning in Trustworthy Materials Discovery
comment: Accepted to the 32nd ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2026)
summary: Generative models have revolutionized the process of materials discovery, yet they often fail to satisfy underlying physical causality. Through an analysis of Large Language Models (LLMs) augmented with knowledge graphs derived from current literature, we uncover a phenomenon termed contextual tunneling, where models "over-anchor" on narrow, retrieved evidence while suppressing global physical reasoning. To address this problem, we introduce ARIA, a causal-aware framework that conditions knowledge use on mechanistic completeness. ARIA routes each query through a three-tier cascade: (i) direct causal reasoning when complete evidence chains of Process-Structure-Property (PSP) are available, (ii) physics-informed analogical transfer for sparse or novel material systems, and (iii) explicit parametric fallback when external evidence is incomplete. As a proof of concept, we construct a Knowledge Graph (KG) containing 2,839 extracted PSP relations from peer-reviewed articles in the materials literature and evaluate ARIA on forward prediction and inverse design tasks for two-dimensional (2D) materials. ARIA mitigates contextual tunneling, improves over unaugmented and naive KG-augmented baselines, and provides further gains when an online literature search is used for evidence enrichment. Crucially, ARIA produces auditable causal traces, enabling physically grounded and trustworthy AI-assisted materials discovery.

AI.tldr: ARIA is a causal-aware framework that addresses contextual tunneling in LLMs for materials discovery by conditioning knowledge use on mechanistic completeness through a three-tier cascade approach.
AI.motivation: Current generative models for materials discovery often fail to satisfy physical causality, with LLMs exhibiting "contextual tunneling" - over-anchoring on narrow retrieved evidence while suppressing global physical reasoning.
AI.method: ARIA uses a three-tier cascade: (1) direct causal reasoning when complete Process-Structure-Property (PSP) evidence chains exist, (2) physics-informed analogical transfer for sparse/novel systems, (3) explicit parametric fallback for incomplete external evidence. Evaluated using a KG with 2,839 PSP relations on 2D materials tasks.
AI.result: ARIA mitigates contextual tunneling, outperforms unaugmented and naive KG-augmented baselines, and achieves further gains with online literature search for evidence enrichment. Produces auditable causal traces.
AI.conclusion: ARIA enables physically grounded and trustworthy AI-assisted materials discovery by addressing the causality gap in current generative models through mechanistic completeness conditioning.

title: Beyond the Next Step: Variable-Length Latent World Models for Long-Horizon Planning
summary: Recently, world models have emerged as a promising paradigm for building intelligent agents by learning predictive models that estimate future environment states conditioned on observations and actions. In particular, JEPA-style latent world models provide an efficient alternative to pixel space prediction by learning action-conditioned dynamics in compact representation spaces. However, existing latent world models typically rely on one-step prediction and must be recursively rolled out for long-horizon planning, which leads to compounding errors and a mismatch between training objectives and downstream planning tasks. To address this limitation, we propose Variable-length Latent World Models (VLWMs), a framework that learns to predict future latent states conditioned on action sequences of variable lengths. Instead of training only on one-step transitions, VLWMs directly model temporally extended dynamics, allowing the same predictor to evaluate action plans over different horizons. We further introduce a curriculum training strategy that progressively expands the action horizon, stabilizing optimization from short-range dynamics to long-range prediction. At test time, we design planning methods tailored to VLWMs to better exploit their variable-length predictive capabilities. Experiments on long-horizon control tasks show that VLWMs significantly improve latent space world models, achieving 13\% average improvement over the state-of-the-art LeWM across different datasets, with especially large gains on tasks requiring extended planning. These results suggest that VLWM provides a simple yet effective paradigm for improving long-horizon prediction and planning in latent world models.

AI.tldr: VLWMs propose variable-length latent world models that predict future states over multiple timesteps instead of just one-step, enabling better long-horizon planning with reduced compounding errors.
AI.motivation: Existing latent world models use one-step prediction and must be recursively rolled out for planning, causing compounding errors and mismatch between training objectives and downstream planning tasks.
AI.method: VLWMs learn to predict future latent states conditioned on action sequences of variable lengths, directly modeling temporally extended dynamics. They use curriculum training that progressively expands the action horizon, and include tailored planning methods to exploit variable-length prediction.
AI.result: Experiments on long-horizon control tasks show VLWMs achieve 13% average improvement over state-of-the-art LeWM across datasets, with especially large gains on tasks requiring extended planning.
AI.conclusion: VLWM provides a simple yet effective paradigm for improving long-horizon prediction and planning in latent world models by directly modeling variable-length dynamics instead of relying on recursive one-step prediction.

title: MetaPS: Adaptive Programmatic Strategy Selection for Market Agents
summary: No single market strategy always wins: momentum, mean reversion, risk control,and event-driven rules can each succeed or fail as market conditions change.Rather than asking large language models to directly generate market actions,we study an executable decision paradigm where an agent selects from a library of programmatic strategies, each implemented as a code module mapping market observations to actions.We propose \textbf{MetaPS}, a simulation-guided framework for adaptive programmatic strategy selection. MetaPS rolls out candidate strategies in simulated or backtested markets, identifies states where particular strategies lead to better future outcomes, and converts these state--strategy pairs into supervised fine-tuning data. During inference, the simulator is no longer queried: MetaPS observes only the current market state and candidate strategy context, selects a suitable strategy program, and the selected program produces the final action. Experiments on multi-stock trading and a controlled goods-exchange sandbox show that MetaPS consistently improves across model scales from 0.8B to 9B parameters. It outperforms fixed-strategy baselines, direct decision-making agents, and prompted API-based LLM agents; in several settings, compact fine-tuned models even surpass stronger API models. These results demonstrate that market simulations can provide scalable and targeted supervision for learning adaptive, interpretable, and executable strategy selection.

AI.tldr: MetaPS is a simulation-guided framework that trains LLMs to select from pre-defined programmatic trading strategies instead of directly generating actions, enabling adaptive, interpretable strategy selection that improves with model scale.
AI.motivation: No single market strategy always wins as market conditions change. Directly asking LLMs to generate market actions can be unreliable, so instead we need a way to adaptively select from proven programmatic strategies.
AI.method: MetaPS uses market simulations to identify which strategies perform best in different market states. It collects state-strategy pairs where particular strategies lead to better future outcomes, converting these into supervised fine-tuning data for LLMs. At inference, it observes current market state and selects a suitable pre-programmed strategy.
AI.result: MetaPS consistently improves across model scales (0.8B to 9B parameters), outperforms fixed-strategy baselines, direct decision-making agents, and prompted API-based LLM agents. In some cases, compact fine-tuned models even surpass stronger API models.
AI.conclusion: Market simulations can provide scalable, targeted supervision for learning adaptive, interpretable, and executable strategy selection, demonstrating a promising approach to combine LLM reasoning with proven programmatic strategies.

title: A Causal DAG Prior for Synthetic Time-Series Classification Datasets
comment: Accepted at the 2nd ICML 2026 Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data (FMSD). 4 pages (main text), 2 figures, plus references and appendix
summary: A Prior-data fitted Network learns the posterior predictive induced by its training prior; bringing this paradigm to multivariate time-series classification therefore calls for a synthetic generator that produces complete labelled datasets with temporal structure. We introduce a causal prior that synthesizes each dataset from a randomly sampled DAG over typed nodes across two modalities (tabular attributes and time series), natively producing multivariate, multi-class TSC datasets with cross-modal causal structure across channels, timesteps and labels, a regime not addressed by existing synthetic priors. To validate the prior, we finetune TabPFN v2.5 with minimal adaptations and evaluate on 75 UCR/UEA datasets within TabPFN's operating regime. Finetuning on our generator significantly outperforms both the unmodified upstream model and a tabular-only ablation of the same prior (Wilcoxon signed-rank $p=3.0\times 10^{-8}$ on ROC-AUC), isolating the contribution of the cross-modal temporal structure.

AI.tldr: A Prior-data Fitted Network for multivariate time-series classification learns posterior predictive via synthetic generator producing labeled datasets with temporal structure.
AI.motivation: Current synthetic priors don't address multivariate, multi-class TSC datasets with cross-modal causal structure across channels, timesteps and labels. Need a generator that produces complete labeled datasets with temporal structure for Prior-data Fitted Networks.
AI.method: Introduce causal prior that synthesizes each dataset from randomly sampled DAG over typed nodes across two modalities (tabular attributes and time series). Native production of multivariate, multi-class TSC datasets with cross-modal causal structure. Finetune TabPFN v2.5 with minimal adaptations for evaluation.
AI.result: Finetuning on proposed generator significantly outperforms both unmodified upstream model and tabular-only ablation (Wilcoxon signed-rank p=3.0×10^(-8) on ROC-AUC), isolating contribution of cross-modal temporal structure. Validated on 75 UCR/UEA datasets within TabPFN's operating regime.
AI.conclusion: The introduced causal prior successfully enables Prior-data Fitted Networks for multivariate time-series classification by providing appropriate synthetic datasets with temporal structure, demonstrating significant performance improvements over baseline approaches.

title: PlanBench-XL: Evaluating Long-Horizon Planning of LLM Tool-Use Agents in Large-Scale Tool Ecosystems
summary: LLM agents increasingly operate in large tool ecosystems, where real-world tasks require discovering relevant tools, inferring implicit sub-goals, and adapting to dynamic environments over long horizons. However, existing benchmarks rarely evaluate planning under retrieval-limited tool visibility. To address this gap, we introduce PlanBench-XL, an interactive benchmark of 327 retail tasks over 1,665 tools that tests whether agents can iteratively retrieve usable tools, invoke them to uncover intermediate evidence for subsequent calls toward the final goal. PlanBench-XL further features an optional blocking mechanism that simulates real-world unpredictability through missing, failing, or distracting tool functions, forcing agents to detect disrupted paths and adapt at runtime. Experiments on ten leading LLMs show that massive-tool planning remains challenging: while GPT-5.4 achieves 51.90% accuracy in block-free settings, it collapses to 11.36% under the most severe blocking condition. Further analysis shows that agents are especially vulnerable when failures lack explicit error signals or when recovery requires longer alternative tool-use paths. These results establish PlanBench-XL as a testbed for diagnosing agentic planning failures and highlight the need for robust adaptive planning in long-horizon tasks with large, imperfect tool environments.

AI.tldr: PlanBench-XL is a new benchmark testing LLM agents' ability to plan and adapt in large tool ecosystems with limited tool visibility and real-world unpredictability.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks don't adequately evaluate planning under retrieval-limited tool visibility and dynamic, unpredictable environments with large tool sets.
AI.method: Created PlanBench-XL with 327 retail tasks and 1,665 tools, featuring interactive testing of iterative tool retrieval, invocation, and evidence accumulation, with optional blocking mechanism simulating tool failures and distractions.
AI.result: Massive-tool planning remains challenging: GPT-5.4 achieves 51.90% accuracy block-free but collapses to 11.36% under severe blocking. Agents struggle with implicit failures and longer recovery paths.
AI.conclusion: PlanBench-XL serves as a valuable testbed for diagnosing agentic planning failures and highlights the need for robust adaptive planning in imperfect tool environments.

title: RocketPFN: Accurate Time Series Classification via In-Context Learning
comment: 10 pages main text, 4 figures; 15 pages including references and appendix
summary: We introduce RocketPFN, a training-free pipeline for time series classification that combines random convolutional feature extraction (Rocket) with in-context classification via a pretrained tabular foundation model (TabPFN v2.5). On 92 UCR datasets (30-resample protocol), RocketPFN matches HC2, the strongest published method on the archive, in mean accuracy (both 0.900, Wilcoxon p=0.50), with no training on the target data and a median inference time of 30 seconds per fold. It also significantly outperforms every individual classifier in the HC2 ensemble. On UEA (20 datasets) the difference is likewise not statistically significant. A separate comparison concerns TSC foundation models: when paired with the same downstream classifier, MOMENT, Mantis, and MantisV2 are all significantly outperformed by RocketPFN using fewer extracted features and no learned parameters (p<0.001 in each case). This holds even when the encoders were pretrained on corpora that include the UCR training samples. We propose this two-stage pipeline as a reference point for evaluating zero-shot TSC foundation models.

AI.tldr: RocketPFN combines random convolutional features (Rocket) with a pretrained tabular foundation model (TabPFN) for training-free time series classification, achieving state-of-the-art performance without target data training.
AI.motivation: To develop a training-free time series classification pipeline that leverages the strengths of both random convolutional feature extraction and pretrained tabular foundation models, providing a strong zero-shot benchmark.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: 1) Extract features from time series using Rocket's random convolutional kernels, 2) Use these features as input to the pretrained TabPFN v2.5 model for in-context classification without any training on target datasets.
AI.result: Matches HC2's mean accuracy (0.900) on 92 UCR datasets with no training, outperforms all individual classifiers in HC2 ensemble, and significantly beats other TSC foundation models (MOMENT, Mantis, MantisV2) with fewer features and no learned parameters.
AI.conclusion: RocketPFN establishes a strong training-free baseline for time series classification that should serve as a reference point for evaluating zero-shot TSC foundation models, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining simple feature extraction with powerful pretrained tabular models.

title: Code Isn't Memory: A Structural Codebase Index Inside a Coding Agent
summary: Coding agents now interleave LLMs with retrieval over the working repository, and retrieval implementations vary widely across deployed harnesses. Inside a fixed coding-agent harness on a fixed model, does adding a structural codebase index actually change cost or resolve? We ran three arms (the harness with the index, the same harness without it, and an agentic-grep comparator) on SWE-PolyBench Verified and SWE-bench Pro with Claude Opus 4.7 held fixed throughout, across three seeds, inside a leak-audited per-task sandbox. The within-harness ablation produces a large localization gain and a statistically separated resolve gain, with no cost penalty per cell and lower cost per solve. The cross-harness check shows that the index does not regress against an agentic-grep baseline on resolve or localization, again at no cost penalty.
