We release the per-cell exclusion ledger, the leak-audit script, the localization extractor, and the results database. The deployment question for a structural codebase index is thus not whether it is too expensive to run (across seeds, the index lands at a lower $/solved than agentic grep) but whether the workload includes multi-file changes where structural ranking pays off.

AI.tldr: The paper examines whether adding a structural codebase index to coding agents improves performance, showing it provides localization and resolution gains without cost penalties compared to agentic grep baselines.
AI.motivation: The motivation is that while coding agents increasingly use LLMs with repository retrieval, implementations vary widely. The authors want to determine if adding a structural codebase index actually improves performance (resolution) and reduces costs in practical deployments, addressing whether such indices are worth the implementation effort.
AI.method: The method involves three experimental arms: (1) coding harness with structural codebase index, (2) same harness without index, and (3) agentic-grep comparator. They tested on SWE-PolyBench Verified and SWE-bench Pro datasets using Claude Opus 4.7 model across three seeds in leak-audited per-task sandboxes. They conducted within-harness ablation studies and cross-harness comparisons.
AI.result: The structural codebase index produces large localization gains and statistically significant resolution improvements with no cost penalty per cell and lower cost per solve. It doesn't regress against agentic-grep baselines on resolution or localization, maintaining no cost penalty. The index achieves lower $/solved than agentic grep across seeds.
AI.conclusion: The key conclusion is that structural codebase indices are not too expensive to deploy (they actually reduce $/solved) and provide performance benefits. The deployment decision depends on whether the workload involves multi-file changes where structural ranking provides payoff, not on cost concerns.

title: What Do Lorentz-Equivariant Jet Taggers Learn?
comment: Accepted at the AI4Physics Workshop, ICML 2026. 21 pages, 15 figures
summary: We study what Lorentz-equivariant jet taggers learn internally, using equivariance tests, linear probes and grade ablations across five models including L-GATr, L-GATr-slim and LLoCa-T. Linear probes show that equivariant models suppress frame-dependent pseudorapidity to zero while encoding jet mass and N-subjettiness strongly. Grade ablations on L-GATr reveal that bivector channels are negligible for top-quark tagging while vector-like channels are dominant but seed variable, consistent with the network exploiting multiple representational pathways. These results characterize which physical features and algebraic grade structures carry discriminative information in equivariant taggers and may inform future development of such models.

AI.tldr: Lorentz-equivariant jet taggers encode jet mass and N-subjettiness strongly while suppressing frame-dependent pseudorapidity, with vector channels being most important for discriminative power.
AI.motivation: To understand what Lorentz-equivariant neural networks learn internally for jet tagging tasks, specifically examining which physical features and algebraic structures carry discriminative information.
AI.method: Used equivariance tests, linear probes, and grade ablations across five models (L-GATr, L-GATr-slim, LLoCa-T) to analyze internal representations and importance of different algebraic grades.
AI.result: Equivariant models suppress frame-dependent pseudorapidity to zero while strongly encoding jet mass and N-subjettiness. Bivector channels are negligible for top-quark tagging, while vector-like channels are dominant but seed variable, showing networks exploit multiple representational pathways.
AI.conclusion: The study characterizes which physical features and algebraic grade structures carry discriminative information in equivariant taggers, providing insights that may inform future development of such models for particle physics applications.

title: SVGym (SciVerseGym): An Environment for Reinforcement Learning and Bayesian Optimization in Crystal Discovery
summary: Machine-learned interatomic potentials now enable efficient atomistic evaluation for interactive materials discovery, yet closed-loop crystal search methods remain fragmented across bespoke pipelines for editing, relaxation, scoring, constraints, and bookkeeping. We introduce SciVerseGym, a Gymnasium-compatible environment for sequential crystal discovery that frames crystal design as a Markov decision process. Agents observe an atomistic structure, apply chemically meaningful edits, and receive feedback from a configurable evaluator. SciVerseGym supports local and global actions, including elemental substitution, lattice perturbation, atomic displacement, vacancy creation, and atom insertion, along with configurable chemical spaces, structure pools, atomistic and graph-based observations, custom rewards, optional relaxation, and stability or phonon-related diagnostics. Each step applies an edit, evaluates the candidate using a machine-learned interatomic potential or any ASE-compatible calculator, and returns the standard (obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info) tuple. By decoupling agent logic from materials infrastructure, SciVerseGym provides an open, reproducible, and extensible testbed for reinforcement learning, Bayesian optimization, evolutionary search, and language-agent workflows in closed-loop crystal discovery. Code is available at: https://github.com/Bin-Cao/SciVerseGym.

AI.tldr: SciVerseGym is a Gymnasium-compatible environment that frames crystal discovery as a Markov decision process, providing a unified testbed for reinforcement learning and other search methods in materials science.
AI.motivation: Current closed-loop crystal search methods are fragmented across bespoke pipelines for editing, relaxation, scoring, constraints, and bookkeeping, lacking a standardized framework for efficient materials discovery.
AI.method: Developed SciVerseGym as a Gymnasium-compatible environment where agents observe atomistic structures, apply chemically meaningful edits (elemental substitution, lattice perturbation, atomic displacement, vacancy creation, atom insertion), and receive feedback from configurable evaluators using machine-learned interatomic potentials.
AI.result: Created an open, reproducible, and extensible testbed that supports local/global actions, configurable chemical spaces, structure pools, atomistic/graph-based observations, custom rewards, optional relaxation, and stability/phonon diagnostics, returning standard (obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info) tuples.
AI.conclusion: By decoupling agent logic from materials infrastructure, SciVerseGym enables reproducible and extensible workflows for reinforcement learning, Bayesian optimization, evolutionary search, and language-agent approaches in closed-loop crystal discovery.

title: Discretizing Reward Models
summary: Despite their widespread use, the role of reward models in shaping reinforcement learning is poorly understood. Reward models offer a tempting promise: they automatically estimate response quality in the absence of verifiers or human judges. Unlike "verifiable rewards" which typically produce binary scores, reward models typically produce continuous scores, allowing them to be sensitive to fine-grained differences in responses. However, we show this apparent strength is a serious weakness: many popular reward models are oversensitive, assigning different scores to equally good responses. Theoretically, we show that seemingly perfect reward models can be highly oversensitive; empirically, this oversensitivity can lead to bad policies. In place of existing notions of "reward model accuracy," we propose evaluating reward models using distinct measures of "discriminative ability" and "specificity" (the complement of oversensitivity). As a solution, we describe a training-free algorithm that uses Monte Carlo dropout on any neural reward model to produce discrete reward clusters. Theoretically, we prove there exist discretizations that reduce oversensitivity at minimal expense of discriminative ability; empirically we show, in both controlled and natural RL settings, that discretizing rewards leads to less reward hacking and better policies than training on the original rewards.

AI.tldr: Reward models in RL are often oversensitive, assigning different scores to equally good responses, which leads to bad policies. The paper proposes evaluating reward models using discriminative ability vs specificity, and introduces a training-free discretization method using Monte Carlo dropout that reduces oversensitivity and improves policies.
AI.motivation: Despite widespread use of reward models in reinforcement learning, their role is poorly understood. Reward models promise automatic quality estimation without human judges, but their apparent strength (continuous scoring sensitivity) is actually a weakness - many are oversensitive and assign different scores to equally good responses, leading to poor policies.
AI.method: 1) Theoretical analysis showing perfect reward models can still be oversensitive; 2) Proposes distinct evaluation metrics: "discriminative ability" and "specificity" (complement of oversensitivity); 3) Develops training-free algorithm using Monte Carlo dropout on neural reward models to produce discrete reward clusters.
AI.result: 1) Theoretically proves existence of discretizations that reduce oversensitivity with minimal loss of discriminative ability; 2) Empirically demonstrates in controlled and natural RL settings that discretizing rewards reduces reward hacking and leads to better policies than using original continuous rewards.
AI.conclusion: Oversensitivity in reward models is a fundamental problem that can't be solved by just improving accuracy. Discretization through Monte Carlo dropout offers a practical, training-free solution that addresses oversensitivity, reduces reward hacking, and improves policy quality in RL systems.

title: Efficient Multimodal Clinical Question Answering for Pulmonary Embolism Risk Assessment
summary: Pulmonary embolism (PE) is a high risk cardiopulmonary condition whose management requires both timely diagnosis and reliable assessment of future clinical risk. Because PE care routinely combines computed tomography pulmonary angiography (CTPA), radiology interpretation, and longitudinal electronic health record (EHR) evidence, it provides a clinically meaningful setting for evaluating compact multimodal language models. In this work, we build a benchmark using efficient multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on INSPECT, a multimodal PE dataset containing 23,248 CTPA studies from 19,402 patients. We formulate eight diagnostic and prognostic tasks as structured clinical question answering problems and evaluate on typical efficient MLLMs under CTPA-Only, EHR-Only, and CTPA+EHR settings with zero-shot and few-shot prompting. Results show that Gemma4 E4B and Gemma4 E2B perform more strongly when EHR evidence is available, especially under CTPA+EHR input. Task level analysis further shows that PE diagnosis achieves higher performance than prognostic tasks, particularly readmission prediction. These observations suggest that compact multimodal models have the great potential in early stage PE risk detection and explanation.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a benchmark for evaluating compact multimodal language models on pulmonary embolism (PE) diagnosis and prognosis using combined CT imaging and EHR data, showing these models have strong potential for early PE risk detection.
AI.motivation: PE management requires timely diagnosis and risk assessment. The combination of CT pulmonary angiography, radiology interpretation, and EHR data provides a meaningful clinical setting for evaluating multimodal language models, especially as compact models could enable practical clinical deployment.
AI.method: Built a benchmark using the INSPECT dataset containing 23,248 CTPA studies from 19,402 patients. Formulated 8 diagnostic and prognostic tasks as structured clinical QA problems. Evaluated efficient MLLMs (Gemma4 E4B and E2B) under three settings: CTPA-Only, EHR-Only, and CTPA+EHR, using zero-shot and few-shot prompting.
AI.result: Gemma4 models performed better with EHR evidence, especially in combined CTPA+EHR setting. PE diagnosis tasks achieved higher performance than prognostic tasks, with readmission prediction being particularly challenging. Models showed promising capability in early-stage PE risk detection.
AI.conclusion: Compact multimodal models have significant potential for early-stage pulmonary embolism risk detection and explanation, with combined imaging and EHR data yielding better performance than either modality alone.

title: Causal Variational Deep Embedding: A Family of Interventional Generators for Confounded Images
summary: Deep generative models reproduce the observational distribution of their training data, inheriting any spurious associations it contains. A common source is an unobserved confounder that shapes both an attribute the user wants to control at sampling time and an attribute expected to vary in response. Existing causal generative approaches resolve the resulting ambiguity by imposing structural assumptions strong enough to single out one interventional distribution; in image domains, such assumptions are rarely warranted, and the data is generally consistent with a set of distinct causal mechanisms -- a feasible region of interventional distributions. We propose CauVaDE (Causal Variational Deep Embedding), built on a canonical augmented SCM in which the unobserved confounder collapses, without loss of generality, into a discrete latent cluster of bounded support while continuous variation is absorbed into independent noises. We prove that this canonical class is dense, in both observational and interventional Wasserstein distance, in the class of augmented SCMs compatible with a given causal diagram, and instantiate it as a mixture variational autoencoder whose cluster variable plays the role of the canonical confounder. An entropy regularizer with weight $γ$ on the cluster posterior then traces a family of candidate causal effects that fit the observational data to comparable likelihood while spanning the feasible region. Experiments on image data benchmarks show that CauVaDE produces diverse interventional samples and improves FID against an unconfounded reference.

AI.tldr: CauVaDE: A causal generative model that captures feasible interventional distributions without requiring strong structural assumptions, using a discrete latent cluster to represent unobserved confounders in image data.
AI.motivation: Deep generative models inherit spurious correlations from training data due to unobserved confounders. Existing causal approaches require strong structural assumptions to identify unique interventional distributions, which are rarely warranted in image domains where multiple causal mechanisms are consistent with the data.
AI.method: Proposes CauVaDE (Causal Variational Deep Embedding) built on a canonical augmented SCM where unobserved confounders collapse into a discrete latent cluster with bounded support, while continuous variation goes into independent noises. This is instantiated as a mixture variational autoencoder where cluster variable represents canonical confounder. An entropy regularizer with weight γ on cluster posterior traces a family of candidate causal effects spanning the feasible region.
AI.result: The canonical class is proven dense in observational and interventional Wasserstein distance among augmented SCMs compatible with a given causal diagram. Experiments on image benchmarks show CauVaDE produces diverse interventional samples and improves FID against unconfounded references.
AI.conclusion: CauVaDE provides a principled approach to handle unobserved confounders in image domains without requiring strong identifiability assumptions, capturing the feasible region of interventional distributions consistent with observational data.

title: A Differentiable Atari VCS:A Complex, Fully Known Ground Truth for Explainable AI
summary: Explanation requires ground truth: to verify an account of a system we must know its inner functioning-just what is missing where explainable AI (XAI) is most needed. Systems we can study fall into two camps. Simple, procedural one-decision trees, rule lists, sparse linear models-have a known but trivial mechanism, so explaining them tests nothing; genuinely complex ones-deep networks, real-world tasks-need XAI but have no ground-truth inner functioning, so an explanation can be plausible, confident, and wrong with no way to tell. We remove this dichotomy with a study object both genuinely complex and fully specified-inspectable by construction-and, so gradient methods apply, fully differentiable. We reimplement the Atari 2600 Video Computer System (VCS)-a real computer architecture, and the cradle of deep reinforcement learning-as two independent end-to-end differentiable emulators in Julia (jutari) and JAX (jaxtari), each validated bit-for-bit against xitari. Both reproduce xitari on all 64 supported Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) games: 64/64 byte-identical RAM and 64/64 pixel-identical screens. Treating the cartridge ROM as a weight tensor, RAM as a soft tape, and control flow as gates, we prove the differentiable (soft) execution equals the original (hard) one bit-for-bit in the forward pass at any finite temperature, while exposing surrogate gradients where the bit logic has none. The JAX port also opens a GPU path: batched differentiable rollouts reach millions of environment-steps/s on one commodity GPU. The system was built in roughly 137 active hours over 29 calendar days, much of it written autonomously by coding agents. This paper builds and validates the foundation, showing-theoretically and in a qualitative gradient study-that gradient-based XAI on it is feasible. Both ports' full code is available under the MIT license at https://github.com/akmaier/UnderstandingVCS.

AI.tldr: Researchers created fully differentiable Atari 2600 emulators (jutari in Julia and jaxtari in JAX) that provide inspectable complex systems for XAI research, overcoming the ground-truth problem while enabling gradient-based analysis.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental problem in XAI: simple systems are trivial to explain, while complex systems lack ground-truth inner workings, making explanations unverifiable. Researchers need a study object that is both genuinely complex and fully specified/inspectable.
AI.method: Created two end-to-end differentiable emulators of the Atari 2600 VCS (jutari in Julia and jaxtari in JAX) that reproduce the original bit-for-bit. Treated ROM as weight tensor, RAM as soft tape, and implemented differentiable control flow. Used coding agents for rapid development and validated against xitari on all 64 ALE games.
AI.result: Successfully implemented differentiable emulators that exactly reproduce original Atari 2600 behavior bit-for-bit. Proved differentiable execution equals original hard execution at any finite temperature. Achieved millions of environment-steps/second on commodity GPU via batched differentiable rollouts. Built complete system in 137 active hours over 29 days.
AI.conclusion: These differentiable Atari 2600 emulators provide a foundation for gradient-based XAI research on genuinely complex but fully inspectable systems, enabling verifiable explanation methods that overcome the traditional ground-truth dichotomy.

title: Causal Gaussian Processes for Robust Treatment Effect Evaluation with Unobserved Confounding
summary: The presence of confounding bias poses a key challenge in policy evaluation, as the target causal effects of actions are not identifiable (i.e., underdetermined) from observational data. On the other hand, existing confounding-robust evaluation strategies require detailed prior knowledge about the environment or apply only to discrete treatments and outcomes. This paper investigates causal effect evaluation over the continuous domain from confounded observations, while requiring only basic temporal ordering between the treatment and the outcome. We introduce a universal discretization of the exogenous domains that approximates the observational and interventional distributions of any causal model with arbitrary accuracy using a finite number of latent states. Building on this newfound universal approximation property, we develop a novel family of Causal Gaussian process (CGP) models that effectively approximate the observational and interventional distributions of any causal model with confounded observations.

AI.tldr: A new causal Gaussian process framework for estimating continuous treatment effects from confounded observational data using universal discretization of exogenous domains.
AI.motivation: Confounding bias makes causal effects unidentifiable from observational data. Existing robust evaluation methods require extensive prior knowledge or only work for discrete treatments/outcomes, creating a gap for continuous-domain problems.
AI.method: Introduces universal discretization of exogenous domains that approximates any causal model's distributions with arbitrary accuracy using finite latent states. Builds on this to develop novel Causal Gaussian Process (CGP) models that approximate both observational and interventional distributions from confounded observations.
AI.result: Developed a family of Causal Gaussian Process models capable of approximating continuous-domain causal effects from confounded observations, requiring only basic temporal ordering between treatment and outcome.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CGP framework addresses the limitation in causal effect evaluation for continuous domains under confounding, providing a general approach that works with minimal assumptions (just temporal ordering) while handling arbitrary accuracy approximations.

title: Self-Evolving Cognitive Framework via Causal World Modeling for Embodied Scientific Intelligence
summary: Current embodied world models are primarily optimized for predictive objectives, limiting their ability to generalize under distribution shifts and reason systematically about unseen situations and hypothetical interventions. We argue that embodied intelligence should move beyond predictive world modeling toward self-evolving cognitive systems that continually construct and refine internal causal representations through interaction with the environment. To this end, we propose a self-evolving cognitive framework via causal world modeling for embodied scientific intelligence, which integrates three complementary components: causal world modeling, intervention-driven causal reasoning, and continual cognitive refinement. The proposed framework continuously revises and expands its internal causal world model through causal discovery, intervention-driven feedback, and counterfactual reasoning, supporting continual cognitive refinement and enabling cognition itself to evolve over time. Furthermore, we reinterpret embodied interaction not merely as a means of trajectory optimization, but as an epistemic process for causal hypothesis generation, intervention-driven experimentation, and continual knowledge acquisition. This work provides a conceptual and theoretical foundation for a transition from predictive intelligence toward epistemic intelligence, in which intelligence emerges through the continual construction, revision, and refinement of causal world models via interaction with the environment. Accordingly, an intervention-driven causal-epistemic benchmarking paradigm is suggested for evaluating self-evolving embodied scientific intelligence.

AI.tldr: A framework for self-evolving cognitive systems that use causal world modeling and intervention-driven reasoning to move beyond predictive intelligence toward epistemic intelligence, enabling embodied agents to continually construct and refine causal understanding through environmental interaction.
AI.motivation: Current embodied world models are limited by their focus on predictive objectives, which restricts their ability to generalize under distribution shifts and reason systematically about unseen situations and hypothetical interventions. There's a need to move beyond predictive intelligence toward systems that can continually construct and refine causal understanding.
AI.method: Proposes a self-evolving cognitive framework with three components: 1) causal world modeling, 2) intervention-driven causal reasoning, and 3) continual cognitive refinement. The framework continuously revises internal causal models through causal discovery, intervention-driven feedback, and counterfactual reasoning.
AI.result: The paper provides a conceptual and theoretical foundation for transitioning from predictive intelligence to epistemic intelligence, where intelligence emerges through continual construction, revision, and refinement of causal world models via environmental interaction. It also suggests an intervention-driven causal-epistemic benchmarking paradigm.
AI.conclusion: Embodied intelligence should evolve beyond predictive world modeling toward self-evolving cognitive systems that continually construct causal representations through interaction. This shift reinterprets interaction as an epistemic process for causal hypothesis generation and knowledge acquisition, moving toward true epistemic intelligence.

title: Local Causal Attribution of Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
comment: Camera-ready version for the Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop at ICML 2026. 37 pages, 18 figures
summary: Understanding the causal structure of a language model's thought process is a problem of significant importance for both transparency and safety. In this work, we take a local approach toward this goal by analyzing the causal relationships among individual components, termed units, of a given, specific chain-of-thought trace. We construct a structural causal model on these units and relate each unit to the log probability of generating (subsequent) output units. Our algorithm, termed AttriCoT, is a black-box method that performs attribution by estimating importance parameters in the structural causal model using $O(U)$ forward passes through the model, where $U$ is the number of units. Evaluation of perturbation curves across 5 datasets and 4 reasoning models shows that AttriCoT produces attributions that are more faithful to the model's behavior than alternative methods. The attribution results also reveal notable differences in thought structure between models and domains.

AI.tldr: AttriCoT is a black-box method that constructs a structural causal model to analyze causal relationships among individual components in language model chain-of-thought reasoning, requiring only O(U) forward passes for attribution.
AI.motivation: Understanding the causal structure of language models' thought processes is important for transparency and safety. The paper takes a local approach to analyze causal relationships among individual components in specific chain-of-thought traces.
AI.method: Constructs a structural causal model on reasoning units and relates each unit to log probability of generating subsequent output units. AttriCoT is a black-box attribution method that estimates importance parameters in the structural model using O(U) forward passes where U is the number of units.
AI.result: Evaluation across 5 datasets and 4 reasoning models shows AttriCoT produces more faithful attributions than alternative methods. Attribution results reveal notable differences in thought structure between models and domains.
AI.conclusion: AttriCoT provides an effective approach for understanding causal structure in language model reasoning, offering insights into how different models and domains structure their thought processes through chain-of-thought reasoning.

title: PRIME: Evaluating Prompt Resolution Under Incompatible Instructions in LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often encounter conflicting prompts, although current instruction following benchmarks assess those meta-instructions in isolation, limiting the insights about how models process conflicting instructions. We introduce a framework \textit{PRIME}(\textit{Prompt Resolution under Incompatible Meta-Instructions Evaluation}) to analyze behavior of LLMs when provided with conflicting instructions. \textit{PRIME} purposefully produces calibrated conflicts across response length, output format, and reasoning; classifying model responses with a deterministic behavioral taxonomy. We are evaluating five instruction tuned open weight LLMs in two distinct settings, balanced and naturally distributed. The conclusion we reach upon analysis is that conflict type is more significant in affecting behavior than model scale, and various failure modes across different categories of conflict. Our findings emphasize the value of developing conflict awareness and suggest ability of LLM to follow instructions cannot be assessed through isolated constraints alone.

AI.tldr: PRIME framework evaluates LLMs' ability to handle conflicting meta-instructions, finding conflict type matters more than model size for predicting failure modes.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks assess meta-instructions in isolation, limiting insights into how LLMs process conflicting instructions, which is important for real-world applications where models receive contradictory guidance.
AI.method: Introduces PRIME framework to deliberately create calibrated conflicts across response length, output format, and reasoning dimensions; uses deterministic behavioral taxonomy to classify model responses; evaluates five instruction-tuned open-weight LLMs in balanced and naturally distributed settings.
AI.result: Conflict type is more significant than model scale in affecting behavior; identifies various failure modes across different conflict categories; shows LLMs struggle with certain types of instruction conflicts.
AI.conclusion: Developing conflict awareness is valuable for LLMs, and instruction-following ability cannot be properly assessed through isolated constraints alone; comprehensive evaluation must include conflicting instruction scenarios.

title: Mat-Pref: Verifiable-Reward Training Improves Compositional Reasoning in Inorganic Materials
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, Accepted at ICML AI4Physics 2026 Workshop
summary: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has driven rapid progress in mathematical and code reasoning, but when extended to science, existing benchmarks do not decompose what generalizes: do gains reflect structural transfer, property transfer, or memorization? We introduce Mat-Pref, a benchmark of 10,837 ionic-substitution questions across 11 inorganic structure families, grounded in density functional theory calculations from the Materials Project, with three evaluation splits that isolate in-distribution performance, generalization to entirely held-out structure families, and cross-property transfer: applying band-gap reasoning to hosts seen during training only through formation-energy supervision. Four zero-shot frontier models (70-671B parameters) remain in the 33-54% range on every split, confirming that scale alone does not resolve the compositional chemical reasoning this task demands. A two-stage pipeline of supervised fine-tuning followed by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) lifts Qwen3-8B to 65.2% in-distribution and 71.6% on held-out families, exceeding zero-shot Qwen3-235B by over 20 percentage points on both structural-generalization splits. Self-consistency sampling shows that the SFT policy can already produce correct answers but cannot reliably surface them as the modal response; GRPO reshapes the distribution so that correct answers become modal rather than merely reachable, and this sharper commitment is visible mechanistically: logit lens analysis reveals a ${\sim}$20pp advantage in answer crystallization at the critical decision layer. We formalize this observation as a distractor-permutation consistency metric under which GRPO narrows the gap between lenient scoring (at least one permutation correct) and strict scoring (all permutations correct) from 24.0 to 14.3 percentage points.

AI.tldr: Researchers introduce Mat-Pref, a new benchmark for evaluating reinforcement learning in materials science that isolates different generalization capabilities, showing that current large models struggle with compositional chemical reasoning, but targeted training can significantly improve performance through better answer selection mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards in science don't decompose what generalizes - whether gains come from structural transfer, property transfer, or memorization. The authors want to understand how models perform on compositional chemical reasoning tasks that require true understanding rather than just memorization.
AI.method: Introduced Mat-Pref benchmark with 10,837 ionic-substitution questions across 11 inorganic structure families based on DFT calculations. Used three evaluation splits: in-distribution performance, generalization to held-out structure families, and cross-property transfer. Tested four zero-shot frontier models (70-671B parameters) and developed a two-stage pipeline of supervised fine-tuning followed by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) on Qwen3-8B.
AI.result: Large zero-shot models (33-54% across all splits) show scale alone doesn't resolve compositional chemical reasoning. GRPO-trained Qwen3-8B achieved 65.2% in-distribution and 71.6% on held-out families, outperforming zero-shot Qwen3-235B by 20+ percentage points. GRPO reshapes answer distributions to make correct answers modal rather than just reachable, improving answer crystallization at decision layers and reducing the gap between lenient and strict scoring.
AI.conclusion: The Mat-Pref benchmark successfully isolates different generalization capabilities in materials science RL. While large models still struggle with compositional reasoning, targeted training pipelines can dramatically improve performance by improving answer selection mechanisms rather than just answer reachability, with GRPO effectively reshaping probability distributions toward correct answers.

title: VADAOrchestra: Neurosymbolic Orchestration of Adaptive Reasoning Workflows
summary: Decision-making in real-world settings rarely follows a fixed script. Instead, it unfolds as a dynamic reasoning process in which the appropriate course of action evolves as new context and data become available. Traditional Business Process Management systems provide rigor, determinism, and auditability, yet they generally struggle to adapt their execution at runtime. Conversely, agentic systems based on Large Language Models (LLMs) bring flexibility to decision-making, but they are inherently opaque, often unreliable, and suffer from significant scalability constraints when operating over large datasets. To combine these complementary paradigms, we introduce VADAOrchestra, a neurosymbolic framework that models complex workflows as evolving reasoning processes. The framework adopts a hybrid approach: given a user query and a collection of data sources, an LLM-based orchestrator incrementally plans and adapts the workflow. This is encoded as a logic program in a fragment of Datalog+/- where predicates correspond to tool invocations and rules represent both predefined domain dependencies and logic constructs synthesized on demand to manipulate intermediate results. All logical inference tasks are then executed by a state-of-the-art Datalog+/- symbolic engine. This approach provides a verifiable reasoning trace, supporting the auditability and reproducibility of the entire process. Furthermore, by decoupling high-level orchestration from symbolic inference, it addresses scalability concerns, enabling complex reasoning over large datasets through targeted data querying. We evaluate VADAOrchestra on real-world financial use cases, demonstrating faithfulness, scalability, and explainability compared to standard agentic architectures.

AI.tldr: VADAOrchestra is a neurosymbolic framework that combines LLM flexibility with symbolic reasoning to create verifiable, scalable workflows that adapt dynamically to real-world data and context.
AI.motivation: Real-world decision-making is dynamic and context-dependent, but traditional Business Process Management systems are rigid while LLM-based agentic systems lack auditability, reliability, and scalability for large datasets. There's a need to combine the strengths of both paradigms.
AI.method: A hybrid neurosymbolic approach where an LLM-based orchestrator incrementally plans and adapts workflows, encoding them as logic programs in Datalog+/-. Predicates represent tool invocations, rules represent domain dependencies, and all logical inference is handled by a symbolic Datalog+/- engine.
AI.result: VADAOrchestra provides verifiable reasoning traces for auditability and reproducibility, addresses scalability concerns through decoupled architecture, and enables complex reasoning over large datasets. Evaluation on financial use cases shows improved faithfulness, scalability, and explainability over standard agentic architectures.
AI.conclusion: The VADAOrchestra framework successfully bridges the gap between rigid business process systems and flexible but opaque LLM agents, creating a neurosymbolic solution that offers both adaptability and verifiability for complex, data-intensive workflows.

title: UniRank: Unified Rank Allocation for Low-Rank LLM Compression
summary: Low-rank decomposition serves as a promising compression paradigm for large language models, however, rank allocation remains challenging: manual rules lack generalizability, and learning-based approaches incur heavy computational overhead. To address these issues, we formulate global low-rank allocation as a sorting-and-truncation pipeline, and score each singular component via dual criteria: \textbf{Local} singular energy ratio that quantifies the intrinsic importance within the decomposed parameter matrix and \textbf{Global} functional importance (measured by input-output cosine similarity) that evaluates the functional significance of decomposed modules. We verify the strong correlation between high input-output cosine similarity and low effective rank through geometric interpretation and experimental validation. Furthermore, we propose rank-preserving fine-tuning, which performs direct LoRA tuning on decomposed weights and avoids extra information loss caused by re-truncation in conventional merging pipelines. Empirical results confirm that our method delivers sustained performance enhancements when combined with models featuring distinct decomposition schemes, model sizes and architectural designs, e.g. in one-shot compression without further fine-tuning, our method reduces perplexity by up to 50\% compared with uniform and heuristic allocation baselines. Code will be available at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/LLM-Pruning.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a method for global low-rank allocation in LLM compression using dual criteria (local singular energy + global functional importance) and rank-preserving fine-tuning to avoid information loss.
AI.motivation: Current rank allocation methods for low-rank decomposition of large language models face challenges: manual rules lack generalizability, while learning-based approaches incur heavy computational overhead. There's a need for efficient and effective rank allocation strategies.
AI.method: Formulates global low-rank allocation as a sorting-and-truncation pipeline, scoring each singular component using dual criteria: (1) local singular energy ratio (quantifies intrinsic importance within decomposed parameter matrix), and (2) global functional importance measured by input-output cosine similarity. Also proposes rank-preserving fine-tuning that performs direct LoRA tuning on decomposed weights to avoid information loss from re-truncation.
AI.result: The method verifies strong correlation between high input-output cosine similarity and low effective rank. Empirical results show sustained performance enhancements across models with different decomposition schemes, model sizes and architectures. In one-shot compression without fine-tuning, reduces perplexity by up to 50% compared to uniform and heuristic allocation baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides an effective solution to rank allocation challenges in low-rank decomposition for LLM compression, combining computational efficiency with strong performance improvements across diverse model configurations.

title: SCOPE: Evolving Symbolic World for Planning in Open-Ended Environments
summary: Recent works have explored integrating Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with classical planners that rely on symbolic representations of planning problems to generate long-horizon plans for complex embodied tasks. However, in open-ended environments, these symbolic representations obtained from perception are often incomplete, leading to suboptimal performance. To address this, we introduce SCOPE, a self-adaptive symbolic planning framework that supports refining action plans and evolving the symbolic world, i.e., the symbolic representations of open-ended environments. SCOPE comprises two synergistic modules: a Symbolic Execution Simulator (SESim) that conducts symbolic validation and real execution of action plans, leveraging the feedback to refine the plans and evolve the symbolic world; and a Self-Adaptive Symbolic Memory (SASMem) that further distills feedback into evolving symbolic knowledge to enhance long-horizon planning and modeling of the symbolic world. Experiments in open-ended environments show that SCOPE significantly improves the completeness of the symbolic world, the success rate of plans under environment perturbations, and cross-task grounding and adaptability across diverse embodied scenarios.

AI.tldr: SCOPE is a self-adaptive symbolic planning framework that integrates Vision-Language Models with classical planners to handle incomplete symbolic representations in open-ended environments through symbolic world evolution and plan refinement.
AI.motivation: Traditional symbolic planning approaches suffer from suboptimal performance in open-ended environments because symbolic representations obtained from perception are often incomplete. This incompleteness leads to poor planning outcomes when combined with classical planners that rely on these representations for long-horizon embodied tasks.
AI.method: SCOPE introduces a two-module framework: 1) Symbolic Execution Simulator (SESim) that validates and executes action plans while using feedback to refine plans and evolve the symbolic world representation, and 2) Self-Adaptive Symbolic Memory (SASMem) that distills feedback into evolving symbolic knowledge to enhance long-horizon planning and world modeling.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that SCOPE significantly improves symbolic world completeness, success rates of plans under environmental perturbations, and cross-task grounding and adaptability across diverse embodied scenarios in open-ended environments.
AI.conclusion: SCOPE effectively addresses the challenge of incomplete symbolic representations in open-ended environments by providing a self-adaptive framework that evolves both planning strategies and world models, leading to more robust and adaptable embodied task planning systems.

title: WiSP: A Working-Set View of Mixture-of-Experts Serving on Extremely Low-Resource Hardware
comment: 18 pages, 6 figures, 9 tables. Preprint, work in progress
summary: Modern Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models place most of their parameters in expert layers, yet only a small fraction of those experts are used for any token. The unused weights must still be stored where the GPU can reach them. On commodity GPUs the common fix is layer-level CPU offloading, which keeps memory low but streams all of a layer's experts across PCIe on every forward pass, losing much of MoE's sparsity benefit. We cast low-resource MoE serving as a working-set management problem on the GPU: routed expert weights and the key-value (KV) cache are two streams of memory demand competing for limited VRAM. We realize this in WiSP (Working-Set Paging), a routing-aware expert pager that plugs into an unmodified serving engine with byte-identical outputs. Keeping resident only the experts a workload reuses, WiSP reaches up to 1.95x the decode throughput of static offload at the same memory budget when the model does not fit. We also find that prefetching experts from predicted routing helps little in single-stream decode: the bottleneck is PCIe bandwidth, not prediction accuracy. This shifts the question from prefetching to allocation: how should VRAM be split between experts and the KV cache? We answer with MV-WSA (Marginal-Value Working-Set Allocation), which equalizes marginal latency benefit per byte subject to a KV admission floor. MV-WSA runs either as an offline configurator or as an online controller that resizes both pools while serving. In real serving the offline configurator is the only policy we test that does well on both prefill and decode; in trace-driven simulation it stays within a few percent of a per-workflow oracle while fixed splits are about 20% worse. The online controller adds a further 1.20x without changing model outputs.

AI.tldr: WiSP introduces routing-aware expert paging and MV-WSA working-set allocation to optimize Mixture-of-Experts serving on limited GPU memory, achieving up to 1.95x throughput over static offloading.
AI.motivation: Modern MoE models have most parameters in expert layers, but only a small fraction of experts are used per token. Current approaches like layer-level CPU offloading stream all experts across PCIe on every forward pass, losing MoE's sparsity benefits and creating inefficient memory usage.
AI.method: Two main components: 1) WiSP (Working-Set Paging) - a routing-aware expert pager that keeps only frequently used experts resident in GPU memory, 2) MV-WSA (Marginal-Value Working-Set Allocation) - an allocation strategy that optimally splits VRAM between experts and KV cache by equalizing marginal latency benefit per byte subject to admission constraints.
AI.result: WiSP achieves up to 1.95x decode throughput compared to static offloading at the same memory budget. MV-WSA offline configurator performs well on both prefill and decode phases, staying within a few percent of oracle performance while fixed splits are ~20% worse. Online controller adds further 1.20x improvement.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents an effective solution for low-resource MoE serving by treating it as a working-set management problem. The key insight is to optimize VRAM allocation between expert weights and KV cache through intelligent paging and marginal-value-based allocation, significantly improving throughput without changing model outputs.

title: Deep Learning-Based Sign Language Recognition from Videos and Cross-Lingual Translation to Indian Vernaculars
summary: Sign language is a primary mode of communication for the global deaf and hard-of-hearing community, yet automated tools that recognize sign gestures from video and translate them into natural language text remain limited, particularly for low-resource Indian languages. We present a two-stage deep learning pipeline that (i) classifies short sign language video clips into English word labels using a fine-tuned VideoMAE video transformer, and (ii) translates the predicted English label into Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali using Meta AI's No Language Left Behind (NLLB-200) multilingual translation model. The classification model is fine-tuned on a 13-class subset of the AI4Bharat Indian Sign Language video corpus from IIT Madras, processing 16-frame clips sampled uniformly from each video at 224 x 224 resolution. Under a small-scale academic setting (13 classes, 197 clips, 80-20 split), the fine-tuned model reaches 99% training accuracy and 78% validation accuracy after 15 epochs. We provide a per-class breakdown via a confusion matrix and classification report, identify the dominant failure modes (confusable adjective pairs such as ugly, deaf, blind, hat, and dress), and describe a Streamlit-based inference demo that takes a user-uploaded video and returns the predicted English label alongside its Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali translations. We discuss the scope, limitations (small label set, isolated-word rather than continuous signing, single-signer style sensitivity, ambiguity of single-word machine translation), and directions for future work, including expanding to sentence-level generation and a larger vocabulary. Code is released to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: A two-stage pipeline for Indian sign language recognition and translation: VideoMAE transformer classifies signs into English words, then NLLB-200 translates them into Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali, achieving 78% validation accuracy on a 13-class dataset.
AI.motivation: Sign language automated recognition tools are limited, especially for low-resource Indian languages. There's a need for systems that can translate sign language video into multiple Indian languages to better serve the deaf and hard-of-hearing community.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: 1) VideoMAE transformer fine-tuned on 13-class subset of AI4Bharat Indian Sign Language corpus (16-frame clips at 224x224 resolution), 2) NLLB-200 multilingual translation model translates English predictions into Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali.
AI.result: 99% training accuracy and 78% validation accuracy after 15 epochs on small dataset (197 clips, 80-20 split). Created confusion matrix showing confusable adjective pairs as dominant failure modes. Built Streamlit demo for real-time inference with multi-language output.
AI.conclusion: Proof-of-concept system demonstrates feasibility of automated sign language recognition and translation for Indian languages, but limitations include small vocabulary, isolated word recognition (not continuous signing), single-signer sensitivity, and machine translation ambiguity. Future work should expand to sentence-level and larger vocabulary.

title: Protein contacts are already in the attention: a single-forward-pass alternative to the Categorical Jacobian
summary: The Categorical Jacobian (CJ) of Zhang et al. (2024) reads protein contacts from a language model by perturbing every residue with every alternative amino acid, about 19L forward passes. We show the signal it reconstructs is already concentrated in a small subset of attention heads: averaging the top-K contact-relevant heads, selected on as few as 10 labeled proteins, recovers contacts in one forward pass and beats CJ on leakage-clean data for every bidirectional model where CJ is defined, and matches or beats it in-distribution (the exceptions being the smallest 8M model and a statistical tie on ESM Cambrian). Ablations localize the gain to labeled head selection, not averaging: at a matched label budget the unweighted mean ties a supervised L1 logistic regression on the same heads, so the parameter-free mean is selection's minimal form, not the source of the advantage. Our primary test is leakage-clean: on a CAMEO split where neither selection nor evaluation touches data the models have plausibly memorized, the head readout beats CJ on ESM-2-650M by +9 pp (N=29, p<0.001), with the within-model margin reproducing across architectures on a wider pretraining-aware set. Both methods fall 30-36 percentage points from their in-distribution Zhang numbers to the leakage-clean numbers, consistent with substantial pretraining overlap inflating prior numbers (a CAMEO-vs-Zhang difficulty shift contributes too, so we read it as an upper bound on the leakage component). We additionally introduce representation-CJ, a hidden-state generalization of the Jacobian for architectures without a masked-LM head; show that the optimal K tracks how diffusely a model spreads its contact heads; and find that both methods lose the contact signal on both causal LMs we test (ProGen2), suggesting attention-encoded pair structure may depend on bidirectional pretraining.

AI.tldr: A method that identifies top contact-relevant attention heads from minimal labeled data (as few as 10 proteins) matches or beats the computationally expensive Categorical Jacobian method for protein contact prediction, especially on leakage-clean data where pretraining overlap is minimized.
AI.motivation: The Categorical Jacobian (CJ) method for reading protein contacts from language models requires about 19L forward passes (perturbing every residue with every alternative amino acid), which is computationally expensive. The authors aim to show that the contact signal is already concentrated in a small subset of attention heads, allowing for more efficient contact prediction.
AI.method: Selects top-K contact-relevant attention heads based on as few as 10 labeled proteins, then averages these selected heads in a single forward pass. Introduces representation-CJ for architectures without masked-LM heads. Ablations compare to supervised L1 logistic regression to isolate the contribution of head selection vs. averaging.
AI.result: The head readout method beats CJ on leakage-clean data (+9 pp on ESM-2-650M, p<0.001) and matches or beats it in-distribution for all bidirectional models where CJ is defined. Both methods suffer significant performance drops (30-36 pp) when moving from in-distribution to leakage-clean data, suggesting pretraining overlap inflated prior benchmarks. The method fails on causal language models (ProGen2), suggesting bidirectional pretraining is needed for attention-encoded pair structure.
AI.conclusion: Protein contact information in language models is concentrated in a small, identifiable subset of attention heads. Simple averaging of these heads, selected with minimal labeled data, provides efficient and often superior contact prediction compared to expensive perturbation methods like CJ, especially when properly controlling for data leakage from pretraining overlap.

title: Grounded Scaling: Why Agentic AI Needs Deterministic Environments
summary: Long-chain agent execution fails exponentially in environments designed for human tolerance: with per-step determinism $δ< 1$, $k$-step chain success degrades as $δ^k$. The AGI-to-ASI scaling debate (Genewein et al., 2026) has so far framed progress as a race between compute growth and a list of frictions (data wall, abstraction barrier, embodied bottleneck, multi-agent trust); we argue that environment determinism is a complementary binding axis cutting across all four, for the broad class of agentic AI tasks whose outcomes are verifiable economically, physically, or through multi-party settlement. Three formal results pin down the regime: a Determinism-Efficiency Bound on chain-task success, a Verifier-Goodharting Floor on flywheel ceilings under imperfect rewards, and a convergence condition for environment-side skill evolution. We operationalise the framework as a Supply Certainty Index (SCI) over five measurable properties, a five-level Determinism Maturity Model (DMM) as adoption ladder, and a falsifiable open-question programme (OQ1-OQ5) with explicit null results that would force retraction. The position is platform-agnostic. We engage three competing positions: sim-to-real sufficiency, alignment sufficiency, and AI-as-normal-technology.

AI.tldr: A framework analyzing how environment determinism limits long-chain agent execution, with formal bounds on chain-task success and reward system reliability, plus measurement tools for assessing determinism maturity.
AI.motivation: Long-chain agent execution degrades exponentially in human-tolerated environments due to imperfect determinism, representing a critical constraint beyond traditional scaling debates about compute growth, data, abstraction, embodiment, and multi-agent trust.
AI.method: Develops theoretical bounds on chain-task success under imperfect determinism, analyzes reward system reliability with verifier Goodharting, and operationalizes the framework through a Supply Certainty Index, Determinism Maturity Model, and falsifiable research questions.
AI.result: Three formal results: Determinism-Efficiency Bound on chain-task success, Verifier-Goodharting Floor on reward reliability, and convergence conditions for environment-side skill evolution, plus practical measurement tools and engagement with competing positions.
AI.conclusion: Environment determinism is a fundamental binding constraint across all agentic AI scaling dimensions, requiring systematic assessment through proposed measurement frameworks rather than relying on sim-to-real, alignment, or technology-as-usual approaches.

title: A Verifiable Search Is Not a Learnable Chain-of-Thought
comment: 31 pages, 6 figures, 16 tables; Interactive walkthrough: https://nemotron.harshpatel.live ; Code, solvers, and per-row eval data: https://github.com/harshpatel1692/search-not-learnable
summary: It is tempting to assume any task solvable by a short program can be taught to a model as its chain-of-thought: write the steps out, fine-tune, and the model follows. This paper shows the assumption fails for an identifiable class of procedures. The testbed is nine reasoning tasks, each from a deterministic generator; public and hidden splits share generators, so held-out data proxies test accuracy. I reverse-engineer the generators into Python solvers, render them as chain-of-thought, and distill into a rank-<= 32 LoRA over a 30B (3.5B-active) Nemotron model. Forward-computable tasks install readily: lookup/arithmetic and an 8-bit boolean task transfer (>= 0.99 and 0.68). Cryptarithm does not: distilling its backtracking search holds at 0.01-0.07 across eleven chain-of-thought designs, RL from verifiable rewards, and self-training, even though a search solver answers 71% of instances. This is not a capability gap. The model does the arithmetic on 97-100% of lines and ranks the correct cipher in its top eight on 71%; it cannot carry the search forward as a left-to-right derivation. Fine-tuning learns the shape of a verifiable elimination step while its verdicts become unconditional templates, correct only 16-57% of the time ("verdict-as-token"). The ceiling holds across backbones from 3B to 671B and across fine-tuning and prompting; a controlled intervention isolates the cause: revealing the cipher key, which turns the derivation forward, lifts the same instances from 0.03 to 0.57. When a procedure's only solution is search over information-free structure, no faithful forward chain-of-thought exists to imitate. The task becomes learnable only by removing the search, precomputing its combinatorial core into a catalog and reducing the trace to recall plus verification; the 1st-place solution reaches Private LB 0.92 this way. What distills is memorization and verification, not search.

AI.tldr: Not all tasks solvable by short programs can be effectively taught to models through chain-of-thought fine-tuning, especially those requiring search over information-free structure.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the assumption that any task solvable by a short program can be effectively taught to models via chain-of-thought fine-tuning by identifying a specific class of procedures where this approach fails.
AI.method: The authors use nine reasoning tasks from deterministic generators, reverse-engineer them into Python solvers, render them as chain-of-thought, and distill them into rank-<=32 LoRA over a 30B Nemotron model. They test forward-computable tasks and search-based cryptarithm, comparing various training methods including fine-tuning, RL with verifiable rewards, and self-training.
AI.result: Forward-computable tasks (lookup/arithmetic, 8-bit boolean) transfer well (>=0.99 and 0.68), but cryptarithm with backtracking search fails (0.01-0.07 accuracy). Models can do arithmetic operations (97-100%) and rank correct cipher in top eight (71%), but cannot execute search as left-to-right derivation. The limitation persists across backbones (3B to 671B) and training methods, only resolving when cipher key is revealed (accuracy rises from 0.03 to 0.57).
AI.conclusion: When a procedure's only solution involves search over information-free structure, no faithful forward chain-of-thought exists for imitation. Such tasks become learnable only by removing the search, precomputing combinatorial core into a catalog, and reducing the trace to recall plus verification (achieving 0.92 accuracy). What distills is memorization and verification, not search capability.

title: Imagine to Ensure Safety in Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning
summary: This work investigates the safe exploration problem in reinforcement learning, where an agent must maximize cumulative performance while simultaneously satisfying safety constraints. This challenge becomes even more pronounced in long-horizon tasks, where existing safe methods face fundamental limitations due to compounding estimation errors and restricted exploration capabilities. To address this problem, we propose a method that combines a learnable world model with two complementary policies a high-level policy and a low-level policy to promote safety at both hierarchical levels. The high-level policy generates intermediate subgoals that bias exploration toward safe regions, while the low-level policy uses imagined rollouts in the learned world model to reduce unsafe behaviors when reaching these subgoals. The proposed method was evaluated on challenging long-horizon navigation and manipulation tasks with high-dimensional action spaces, where it significantly outperforms existing Safe RL baselines in both success rate and strong empirical constraint satisfaction, consistently meeting the prescribed safety budget across seeds, while prior approaches fail to effectively solve these complex long-horizon scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proposes a hierarchical safe RL method using a learnable world model with high-level and low-level policies to address long-horizon tasks with safety constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing safe RL methods struggle with long-horizon tasks due to compounding estimation errors and limited exploration capabilities, making safe exploration particularly challenging in complex scenarios.
AI.method: Combines a learnable world model with hierarchical policies: high-level policy generates safe subgoals to guide exploration, while low-level policy uses imagined rollouts in the world model to minimize unsafe behaviors when reaching subgoals.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms existing Safe RL baselines on challenging long-horizon navigation and manipulation tasks in terms of success rate and constraint satisfaction, consistently meeting safety budgets across seeds.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical approach with world model imagination effectively addresses safe exploration in long-horizon tasks, solving complex scenarios that previous methods fail to handle.

title: Cohort-Anchored Foundation Models for Electronic Health Records: From Risk Scores to Auditable Peer Cohorts
summary: Foundation models have achieved remarkable performance across medical question answering, imaging, and electronic health record (EHR) tasks, yet reliable clinical deployment remains challenging due to limited interpretability, vulnerability to distribution shift, and weak alignment with clinician reasoning. We argue that these limitations arise because existing approaches prioritize representation learning while treating patient comparison as an emergent property rather than a primary source of clinical evidence. To address this gap, we propose CAFM, a Cohort-Anchored Foundation Model framework that elevates patient cohorts to a first-class object throughout the learning pipeline. The framework consists of four stages: deviation-aware data curation, cohort-conditioned pretraining, multimodal cohort alignment, and clinician-in-the-loop refinement. Together, these stages improve data quality, organize representations around clinically meaningful cohort structure, preserve modality-specific relationships, and support auditable clinical decision-making. The framework is compositional and can augment existing EHR foundation models without modifying their underlying encoders. We illustrate CAFM through four clinical case studies spanning acute kidney injury prediction, cardiovascular risk stratification from electrocardiograms, optic neuropathy triage from orbital imaging, and electroretinogram-grounded report generation. We further present five empirically testable hypotheses and identify open challenges in data quality, irregular temporality, multimodal learning, distribution shift, and evaluation beyond predictive accuracy. We argue that explicitly anchoring foundation models to patient cohorts provides a principled path toward trustworthy clinical AI.

AI.tldr: CAFM is a Cohort-Anchored Foundation Model framework that elevates patient cohorts to a first-class object in clinical AI to address limitations of current foundation models like poor interpretability and misalignment with clinician reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current medical foundation models have limited clinical deployment due to poor interpretability, vulnerability to distribution shift, and misalignment with clinician reasoning. The authors argue this stems from prioritizing representation learning while treating patient comparison as emergent rather than a primary evidence source.
AI.method: Four-stage framework: 1) Deviation-aware data curation, 2) Cohort-conditioned pretraining, 3) Multimodal cohort alignment, 4) Clinician-in-the-loop refinement. The framework is compositional and can augment existing EHR foundation models without modifying their encoders.
AI.result: Demonstrated through four clinical case studies: acute kidney injury prediction, cardiovascular risk stratification from ECGs, optic neuropathy triage from orbital imaging, and electroretinogram-grounded report generation. Presented five empirically testable hypotheses and identified key challenges.
AI.conclusion: Anchoring foundation models to patient cohorts provides a principled path toward trustworthy clinical AI by improving data quality, organizing representations around clinically meaningful cohort structure, and supporting auditable clinical decision-making.

title: Governance Decay: How Context Compaction Silently Erases Safety Constraints in Long-Horizon LLM Agents
summary: Modern LLM agents increasingly rely on context compaction, summarization, or eviction to keep long-running sessions within a token budget. We show that this context-management layer is a safety-critical failure surface: in-context governance constraints that agents reliably obey while visible can be silently removed by compaction, causing the same agent to perform prohibited tool actions later in the session. We call this failure mode Governance Decay. We introduce ConstraintRot, a benchmark of long-horizon agent scenarios with deterministic tool-call grading, and measure compaction-induced violations across seven model families. Across 1,323 episodes, violation rises from 0% with the policy in full context to 30% after compaction, reaching 59% for some models; when the constraint survives the summary, violation remains 0%, but when it is dropped, violation reaches 38%. We further study a Compaction-Eviction Attack, in which adversarial in-context content biases the summarizer to omit a legitimate policy, and show that optimized injections defeat every evaluated model. Finally, we propose Constraint Pinning, a simple training-free mitigation that quarantines governance constraints from lossy compaction and restores violation to 0% in our benchmark. These results identify context management as a first-class governance surface for deployed LLM agents.

AI.tldr: Context compaction in LLM agents can silently remove governance constraints, causing agents to perform prohibited actions - a failure mode called Governance Decay. The paper introduces ConstraintRot benchmark, measures violations, demonstrates attacks, and proposes Constraint Pinning mitigation.
AI.motivation: LLM agents use context management (compaction, summarization, eviction) to stay within token budgets, but this creates a safety-critical failure surface where governance constraints that agents obey when visible can be silently removed, allowing prohibited actions.
AI.method: Introduced ConstraintRot benchmark for long-horizon agent scenarios with deterministic tool-call grading. Measured compaction-induced violations across seven model families (1,323 episodes). Studied Compaction-Eviction Attack where adversarial content biases summarizers to omit legitimate policies. Proposed Constraint Pinning as a training-free mitigation.
AI.result: Violation rose from 0% with policy in full context to 30% after compaction (reaching 59% for some models). When constraint survives summary: 0% violation; when dropped: 38% violation. Optimized adversarial injections defeated every evaluated model. Constraint Pinning restored violation to 0% in benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Context management represents a first-class governance surface for deployed LLM agents. Governance Decay is a serious safety failure mode that requires attention, and Constraint Pinning offers an effective training-free mitigation to protect governance constraints from lossy compaction.

title: Ranking-and-Selection with Multiple Correct Answers and Non-Answerable Estimates
comment: Accepted for oral presentation at the 2026 Winter Simulation Conference
summary: We study fixed-precision ranking-and-selection in structured settings where the answer may be non-unique and where noisy estimates may temporarily admit no valid answer at all. This phenomenon arises naturally in problems such as multi-fidelity ranking-and-selection and identifying a Condorcet winner from pairwise comparisons. To address this, we propose a unified framework based on answer-wise acceptance sets, restricted generalized likelihood ratio stopping, and an answer-pitfall decomposition that yields a max-max-min characteristic value and a common sampling principle. We introduce ENDS, a general procedure that combines estimation, nomination, pitfall detection, and cost-aware information-directed selection. We instantiate ENDS for various problems by deriving explicit formulas. Extensive numerical experiments show that this unified recipe performs well across a broad range of pure-exploration problems and offers a practical framework and proof-of-concept algorithmic recipe.

AI.tldr: ENDS: A unified framework for fixed-precision ranking-and-selection in structured settings with non-unique answers and noisy estimates, combining estimation, nomination, pitfall detection, and cost-aware information-directed selection.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in structured ranking-and-selection problems where answers may be non-unique and noisy estimates may temporarily admit no valid answer, which arises in problems like multi-fidelity ranking-and-selection and identifying Condorcet winners from pairwise comparisons.
AI.method: Propose unified framework based on answer-wise acceptance sets, restricted generalized likelihood ratio stopping, and answer-pitfall decomposition yielding max-max-min characteristic value. Introduce ENDS procedure combining estimation, nomination, pitfall detection, and cost-aware information-directed selection.
AI.result: Extensive numerical experiments show ENDS performs well across broad range of pure-exploration problems. Derive explicit formulas for instantiating ENDS in various problems.
AI.conclusion: The ENDS framework offers a practical solution and proof-of-concept algorithmic recipe for structured ranking-and-selection problems with non-unique answers and noisy estimates, providing effective performance across diverse pure-exploration problems.

title: MacAgentBench: Benchmarking AI Agents on Real-World macOS Desktop
summary: Computer use agents (CUAs) have advanced rapidly in desktop automation, and a growing number of users deploy CUAs such as OpenClaw on Mac Mini for always-on automation. However, existing benchmarks, including those for macOS, evaluate agents without framework augmentation and rely on binary evaluation. As a result, they fail to capture both the framework capabilities leveraged by modern CUAs and the partial progress on long-horizon, multi-application tasks. We present MacAgentBench, a comprehensive macOS agent benchmark comprising 676 tasks across 25 applications, with nearly 60% involving both GUI and CLI interaction. The benchmark adopts deterministic rule-based evaluation and introduces fine-grained multi-checkpoint scoring with capability annotations for multi-application tasks. Experiments across three frameworks and 16 models show that the best configuration, Claude Opus 4.6 on OpenClaw, attains 73.7% Pass@1, while this advantage is primarily driven by the skill library rather than by framework design. Fine-grained metrics further reveal that models with similar Pass@1 can differ substantially in sub-goal completion. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/JetAstra/MacAgentBench.

AI.tldr: MacAgentBench is a comprehensive macOS agent benchmark with 676 tasks across 25 applications, featuring deterministic rule-based evaluation and fine-grained scoring to better assess computer use agents' performance on long-horizon, multi-application tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for computer use agents (CUAs) fail to capture framework capabilities used by modern CUAs and don't properly evaluate partial progress on complex, multi-application tasks. They rely on binary evaluation without framework augmentation.
AI.method: Created MacAgentBench with 676 tasks across 25 applications (60% involving both GUI and CLI interaction). Used deterministic rule-based evaluation with fine-grained multi-checkpoint scoring and capability annotations for multi-application tasks.
AI.result: Experiments across 3 frameworks and 16 models show Claude Opus 4.6 on OpenClaw achieves 73.7% Pass@1. Fine-grained metrics reveal models with similar Pass@1 can differ substantially in sub-goal completion. Skill library was more important than framework design.
AI.conclusion: MacAgentBench provides a more comprehensive evaluation of CUAs by capturing framework capabilities and partial progress on complex tasks. The benchmark reveals that skill libraries are more critical than framework design for achieving high performance on macOS automation tasks.

title: AgroSense 2.0: Cross-Modal Transformer Fusion with Geospatial Raster Integration and Interpretable Multi-Task Learning for Precision Crop Recommendation
summary: Crop recommendation systems in precision agriculture have long suffered from a fundamental modality gap: visual soil characterization and chemical nutrient profiling are typically treated as independent inference problems, with fusion often reduced to late-stage feature concatenation. AgroSense~2.0 addresses this limitation through three architectural advances. First, we introduce continental-scale geospatial integration via a seven-band soil raster (\texttt{india\_soil\_7bands.tif}) spanning India, encoding Nitrogen, pH, SOC, Clay, Sand, Silt, and Bulk Density as $32\times32$ spatial patches, a modality entirely absent from prior work. Second, we replace naive feature concatenation with a cross-modal Transformer fusion module, where tabular nutrient features attend over image representations via multi-head attention, enabling richer inter-modal dependency modeling than shallow fusion. Third, we adopt a multi-task objective jointly optimizing soil classification and crop recommendation through a shared backbone, improving generalization via complementary cross-task signal. To enhance interpretability, we apply TreeSHAP to the tabular branch, revealing crop-conditioned nutrient sensitivity: humidity and rainfall emerge as the most influential features globally, while crop-specific profiles diverge meaningfully rainfall dominates rice, nitrogen and potassium dominate maize, and humidity and nitrogen dominate coffee. These explanations provide transparency into model decisions and surface both agronomically consistent patterns and dataset-specific divergences worth further study. Together, these contributions establish AgroSense~2.0 as a more principled, interpretable, and geospatially grounded framework for precision agriculture.

AI.tldr: AgroSense 2.0 introduces geospatial soil raster integration, cross-modal Transformer fusion, and multi-task learning to bridge the modality gap between visual soil characterization and chemical nutrient profiling for crop recommendation systems.
AI.motivation: Current crop recommendation systems suffer from a fundamental modality gap where visual soil characterization and chemical nutrient profiling are treated as independent inference problems, with fusion often reduced to simple late-stage feature concatenation. This limits the ability to model richer inter-modal dependencies needed for effective precision agriculture.
AI.method: 1) Continental-scale geospatial integration via a seven-band soil raster covering India, encoding Nitrogen, pH, SOC, Clay, Sand, Silt, and Bulk Density as 32×32 spatial patches. 2) Cross-modal Transformer fusion module replacing naive concatenation, where tabular nutrient features attend over image representations via multi-head attention. 3) Multi-task objective jointly optimizing soil classification and crop recommendation through a shared backbone. 4) TreeSHAP for interpretability of tabular branch decisions.
AI.result: The system reveals crop-conditioned nutrient sensitivity patterns through interpretability analysis: humidity and rainfall emerge as most influential globally, with crop-specific divergences (rainfall dominates for rice, nitrogen and potassium for maize, humidity and nitrogen for coffee). The approach establishes more principled, interpretable, and geospatially grounded framework for precision agriculture.
AI.conclusion: AgroSense 2.0 successfully addresses the modality gap in crop recommendation systems through geospatial integration, cross-modal Transformer fusion, and multi-task learning, creating a more effective, interpretable, and geographically grounded framework for precision agriculture that provides transparent model decisions and surfaces agriculturally meaningful patterns.

title: Text2DSL: LLM-Based Code Generation for Domain-Specific Languages
comment: 14 pages, 4 figures, 5 tables. Accepted at KES 2026 (Knowledge-Based Intelligent Information and Engineering Systems), Procedia Computer Science, Elsevier
summary: Domain-specific languages (DSLs) are widely used for managing operating system security policies, yet manually authoring rules in such languages demands high expertise and is error-prone. This paper formalises the task of automatic DSL code generation from natural language descriptions - Text2DSL - as a distinct problem class, separate from Text-to-SQL and general-purpose code generation. We introduce the PolkitBench dataset comprising 4,204 verified natural-language-to-Polkit-rule pairs, each validated through a three-level AST-based pipeline.
