Controlled prompt experiments on two MoE models of different scale and provenance - GigaChat-10B-A1.8B (1.8B active parameters) and Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B (3B active) - demonstrate the critical role of structured context (BNF grammar, API specification, permitted identifier vocabulary) for LLM-based DSL code generation. Across both models, supplying context raises syntactic validity to 98.6-99.4%, structural validity by +9.7 to +35.5 pp, and the CodeBLEU score by +60% to +95%. The consistency of the effect across models of different scale and provenance indicates that, for the Text2DSL class of problems, injecting a formal target-language specification into the prompt context is a robust enabling factor for high-quality generation without model fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Text2DSL: Automatic generation of domain-specific language (DSL) code from natural language using LLMs with structured context (grammar, API specs, vocabulary) improves generation quality without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Manually authoring security policy rules in domain-specific languages (DSLs) like Polkit requires high expertise and is error-prone. Automatic generation from natural language could democratize security policy management.
AI.method: 1) Formalizes Text2DSL as distinct problem class; 2) Creates PolkitBench dataset with 4,204 verified NL-to-Polkit pairs; 3) Conducts controlled prompt experiments with two MoE models (GigaChat-10B-A1.8B and Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B) using structured context (BNF grammar, API specification, permitted vocabulary).
AI.result: Structured context dramatically improves generation quality: syntactic validity up to 99.4%, structural validity improved by 9.7-35.5 percentage points, CodeBLEU score increased by 60-95%. Effects consistent across different model scales and provenances.
AI.conclusion: For Text2DSL problems, injecting formal target-language specifications into prompt context is a robust enabling factor for high-quality DSL code generation without model fine-tuning, making it accessible to non-experts.

title: Data Pruning: Redundant, Problematic, and Interdependent Samples
comment: This work is a preprint of a published paper by the same name
summary: The performance of deep learning models is affected by not only data quantity but also data quality. Data pruning is a process by which practitioners can reduce the size of a dataset by only keeping the most important training data points, thereby achieving similar test set performance. We empirically investigate two popular data pruning methods under noisy and noiseless conditions and show that these methods fail in the presence of significant label noise. We highlight that the success of data pruning is distinctly affected by three factors: redundancy in the dataset, the presence of problematic samples, and interdependence between samples. We perform a detailed investigation on commonly used benchmark classification datasets and neural network architectures. We find that our observations are consistent across data distributions and training protocols.

AI.tldr: Data pruning methods that select important training samples fail when significant label noise is present, despite working well in noiseless conditions.
AI.motivation: Deep learning performance depends on both data quantity and quality. Data pruning aims to reduce dataset size while maintaining performance, but its effectiveness under noisy conditions hasn't been thoroughly studied.
AI.method: Empirical investigation of two popular data pruning methods under both noisy and noiseless conditions using benchmark classification datasets and neural network architectures. The study examines three key factors: dataset redundancy, problematic samples, and sample interdependence.
AI.result: Data pruning methods fail in the presence of significant label noise. Their success depends on three factors: redundancy, problematic samples, and sample interdependence. These findings are consistent across different data distributions and training protocols.
AI.conclusion: Data pruning is not robust to label noise, which limits its practical applicability. Understanding the three identified factors (redundancy, problematic samples, sample interdependence) is crucial for developing more reliable data pruning methods.

title: PaperClaw: Harnessing Agents for Autonomous Research and Human-in-the-Loop Refinement
summary: Large language models have become capable reasoners and tool users that write and run code and search the literature, which makes automating the research process itself a realistic goal. We present PAPERCLAW, a harnessed multi-agent system that carries a project autonomously, from a field of study to a finished paper. PAPERCLAW curates a domain from a field's live literature, datasets, and code; brainstorms it into an idea with a pre-registered main-result contract; and drives a stoppable hypothesis map through an iterative propose, test, reflect loop that grows only from measured verdicts and halts once the evidence supports the idea, at which point it writes a venue-compliant paper. A full-lifecycle memory keeps each stage in a single living record, so a long run can be paused, inspected, and resumed without losing context. At the centre is an in-cycle research assistant with research tools and skills: it can drive the whole pipeline on its own, while the same interface lets a person step in at any stage, turning a first autonomous draft into a stronger paper through human-in-the-loop refinement. Throughout, PAPERCLAW keeps its output grounded and checkable, citing only references validated against open scholarly indexes and reporting results that genuinely ran. An evaluation with an LLM judge finds that PAPERCLAW produces strong papers both fully autonomously and with human-in-the-loop refinement.

AI.tldr: PAPERCLAW is an autonomous multi-agent system that can conduct complete research projects from idea to finished paper, with optional human-in-the-loop refinement.
AI.motivation: Large language models have advanced to become capable reasoners and tool users that can write/run code and search literature, making automation of the entire research process a realistic and valuable goal.
AI.method: A harnessed multi-agent system with: 1) Domain curation from live literature, datasets, and code; 2) Brainstorming into ideas with pre-registered main-result contracts; 3) Stoppable hypothesis mapping through iterative propose-test-reflect loops; 4) Full-lifecycle memory for pausing/resuming; 5) In-cycle research assistant with tools and skills; 6) Grounded output with validated citations and actually-run results.
AI.result: Evaluation with an LLM judge finds that PAPERCLAW produces strong papers both fully autonomously and with human-in-the-loop refinement.
AI.conclusion: PAPERCLAW demonstrates that autonomous research systems are feasible and can produce quality academic papers, with the added benefit of human-in-the-loop refinement capability for stronger outputs.

title: Selective Ensemble Based on Preference-Directed Multi-Objective Bandits
summary: Selective ensemble for modern machine learning systems requires choosing promising model candidates under limited evaluation budgets, while downstream tasks often specify only partial preferences over capabilities such as accuracy, robustness, and reasoning. This setting naturally gives rise to a sequential decision problem under partially specified linear preferences. We formalize it as preference-directed multi-objective bandits (PDMOB), where admissible trade-offs are represented by a polyhedral preference cone. Based on this formulation, we introduce Pareto $C$-optimality, which recovers standard Pareto optimality and single-weight scalarization as special cases. We then propose the preference-directed upper confidence bound (PrefUCB) algorithm, which maintains directional confidence intervals to guide exploration. We analyze both indicator-based and gap-weighted regret, and establish instance-dependent logarithmic bounds for both criteria, recovering the optimal logarithmic dependence on the horizon $T$ in classical special cases. Experiments on large pre-trained model selective ensemble tasks and online asset allocation under institutional mandates validate the efficacy of our method.

AI.tldr: Selective ensemble model selection under partial preferences is formulated as preference-directed multi-objective bandits (PDMOB), solved with PrefUCB algorithm achieving logarithmic regret.
AI.motivation: Modern ML systems need to choose promising model candidates under limited evaluation budgets, with downstream tasks often specifying only partial preferences over capabilities like accuracy, robustness, and reasoning.
AI.method: Formulate as preference-directed multi-objective bandits (PDMOB) with polyhedral preference cones, propose Pareto C-optimality, and develop preference-directed upper confidence bound (PrefUCB) algorithm with directional confidence intervals.
AI.result: Established instance-dependent logarithmic bounds for both indicator-based and gap-weighted regret, recovering optimal logarithmic dependence on horizon T in classical cases. Validated on pre-trained model selective ensemble and online asset allocation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The PDMOB framework and PrefUCB algorithm provide effective solutions for selective ensemble problems under partial preferences with theoretical guarantees and practical validation.

title: SkillAudit: From Fixed-Suite Benchmarking to Skill-Centered Assessment
comment: Preprint. Project page: https://skillaudit.github.io/. Code and evaluation artifacts: https://github.com/SkillAudit/skillaudit
summary: Agent skills have become a practical way to extend large language model agents, but the growing skill ecosystem still lacks a reliable way to judge whether a skill is worth deploying. Existing evaluation methods remain largely anchored to fixed task suites, assessing skills through performance on predefined tasks and environments. As skill marketplaces expand, this paradigm becomes inadequate: fixed suites can conflate a skill's marginal contribution with backbone strength and miss its value when tasks fall outside the skill's intended scope. We introduce SkillAudit, an end-to-end framework for skill-centered assessment that takes an arbitrary agent skill as input and automatically generates a comprehensive, multi-dimensional evaluation report spanning utility, efficiency/cost, and safety. SkillAudit focuses on the skill artifact itself and constructs capability-aligned evaluation tasks directly from the skill package. The generated tasks are conducted in isolated sandbox environments to collect execution evidence, followed by automated checks with LLM-based judging to produce auditable results. To dissect the agent skills, we propose the baseline comparison principle to measure utility and efficiency/cost, and introduce a two-stage detection paradigm combining static semantic analysis with dynamic runtime verification to assess safety risks. After scanning top-ranked real-world skill packages spanning 23 occupational categories, we found that over 7% of skills are at risky status.

AI.tldr: SkillAudit is a framework that automatically evaluates LLM agent skills through multi-dimensional assessment (utility, efficiency/cost, safety) by generating capability-aligned tasks in sandbox environments, uncovering that over 7% of real-world skills are risky.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation methods for agent skills rely on fixed task suites, which are inadequate as skill marketplaces expand. These methods conflate skill contributions with backbone model strength and miss skill value when tasks fall outside predefined scopes. There's a need for reliable assessment of whether skills are worth deploying.
AI.method: SkillAudit takes any agent skill as input and automatically: 1) Generates comprehensive multi-dimensional evaluation (utility, efficiency/cost, safety), 2) Constructs capability-aligned evaluation tasks directly from skill packages, 3) Conducts tasks in isolated sandbox environments to collect execution evidence, 4) Uses automated LLM-based judging with baseline comparison for utility/efficiency and two-stage detection (static semantic analysis + dynamic runtime verification) for safety assessment.
AI.result: After scanning top-ranked real-world skill packages spanning 23 occupational categories, the framework found that over 7% of skills are at risky status, demonstrating the practical need for such comprehensive skill assessment.
AI.conclusion: SkillAudit provides an end-to-end, skill-centered assessment framework that addresses limitations of fixed task suites, offering a more reliable way to judge agent skill value and deployment worthiness through automated, multi-dimensional evaluation with safety verification.

title: DevoTG: Temporal Graph Neural Networks for Modeling C. elegans Developmental Connectomics
summary: Understanding how a nervous system wires itself from birth to adulthood is a fundamental challenge in developmental neuroscience. We present DevoTG, a temporal graph framework that applies Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNs) to two complementary representations of C. elegans neural development: a Continuous-Time Dynamic Graph (CTDG) of cell division events derived from cell lineage data, and a Discrete-Time Dynamic Graph (DTDG) of the developing synaptic connectome spanning eight reconstructed electron-microscopy datasets. On the lineage prediction task, our TGN achieves a mean test AUC of 0.839 +/- 0.007 (5 seeds; validation AUC 0.937 +/- 0.001), outperforming a static GNN with the identical architecture by 26 AUC points (0.577 +/- 0.080), demonstrating that temporal memory is the decisive factor. Applied to the connectome DTDG, DevoTG identifies three connection stability classes (stable, developmental, and variable) across 225 neurons and 858 to 2,496 connections over development (L1 birth to adult), providing a temporal-graph-theoretic complement to the individual-variability classification of Witvliet et al. Analysis of hub command interneurons AVA, AVB, and AVE reveals their persistent centrality and how their integration roles are progressively reinforced across larval stages. Accompanying interactive visualizations (3D animated networks, centrality heatmaps, and a spatiotemporal lineage graph) make developmental dynamics accessible for biological hypothesis generation. DevoTG is open-source and designed for extension to other developing nervous systems. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/DevoLearn/DevoGraph/tree/main/DevoTG.

AI.tldr: DevoTG applies Temporal Graph Neural Networks to analyze C. elegans neural development through lineage predictions and connectome dynamics, outperforming static models and revealing temporal patterns in neural wiring.
AI.motivation: Understanding how nervous systems wire themselves from birth to adulthood is a fundamental challenge in developmental neuroscience, requiring tools to analyze temporal patterns in neural development.
AI.method: Uses Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNs) on two representations: Continuous-Time Dynamic Graph of cell division events from lineage data, and Discrete-Time Dynamic Graph of synaptic connectome across eight electron-microscopy datasets.
AI.result: TGN achieves mean test AUC of 0.839 on lineage prediction (outperforming static GNN by 26 AUC points), identifies three connection stability classes across 225 neurons and 858-2,496 connections, reveals persistent centrality of hub command interneurons (AVA, AVB, AVE), and provides interactive visualizations.
AI.conclusion: DevoTG demonstrates temporal memory is crucial for understanding neural development, provides novel temporal-graph-theoretic classification of connections, reveals progressive reinforcement of hub interneuron roles, and offers an open-source framework extensible to other developing nervous systems.

title: Confident but Conflicted: Internal Uncertainty and Cognitive Dissonance Resolution in LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) frequently encounter inputs that disagree with their prior outputs, through user pushback, retrieved documents, or web search results. While the way they resolve such conflicts -- a process we frame as cognitive dissonance resolution -- has been characterized behaviorally, its connection to internal model uncertainty is not well understood. To study this systematically, we vary persuasion attempts along two dimensions, source authority and evidence quality, across 12 health-science claims of stratified epistemic status. Dissonance can be resolved through persuasion, backfire, or immunity. We introduce Trust Elasticity (TE), an econometrics-inspired measure of how readily a model is persuaded toward conflicting evidence. Across four LLMs, TE varies substantially, while clearly false claims elicit near-zero TE across all models. On two open-weight models, we further find that this variation is associated with two complementary internal uncertainty indicators, Confidence Miscalibration in Qwen and Internal Uncertainty Change in Llama. These results link cross-model behavioral variation to a measurable internal property and point to interventions targeting internal uncertainty as future work.

AI.tldr: LLMs' responses to conflicting evidence show varying "Trust Elasticity" - how readily they change beliefs when confronted with contradictory information, which correlates with internal uncertainty measures.
AI.motivation: Large language models frequently encounter inputs that disagree with their prior outputs through user pushback, retrieved documents, or web search results. The process of how they resolve such conflicts (cognitive dissonance resolution) has been characterized behaviorally, but its connection to internal model uncertainty is not well understood.
AI.method: Researchers varied persuasion attempts along two dimensions (source authority and evidence quality) across 12 health-science claims of stratified epistemic status. They introduced "Trust Elasticity" (TE), an econometrics-inspired measure of how readily a model is persuaded toward conflicting evidence. They studied four LLMs and examined internal uncertainty indicators on two open-weight models: Confidence Miscalibration in Qwen and Internal Uncertainty Change in Llama.
AI.result: Trust Elasticity varies substantially across four LLMs, while clearly false claims elicit near-zero TE across all models. On two open-weight models, this variation is associated with two complementary internal uncertainty indicators: Confidence Miscalibration in Qwen and Internal Uncertainty Change in Llama. The results link cross-model behavioral variation to measurable internal properties.
AI.conclusion: The study connects behavioral variation in how LLMs handle conflicting evidence (cognitive dissonance resolution) to measurable internal uncertainty properties. This points to potential interventions targeting internal uncertainty as future work, providing a framework for understanding and potentially improving how models handle contradictory information.

title: Modularized Reinforcement Learning on LLMs: From MDP Creation to Exploration and Learning
summary:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has become central to LLM post-training, yet the methods that dominate current pipelines, PPO and GRPO, represent only a narrow slice of what RL offers. Understanding why these methods prevail, and what alternatives exist, requires a principled examination of the design decisions that underlie any RL algorithm.
  This survey organizes that examination around three stages of algorithm construction. We begin with MDP creation: how the reward function, state space, action space, termination condition, and discount factor are, or could be, defined for LLM training. We then turn to exploration, covering temperature sampling, entropy regularization, intrinsic motivation, tree search, and curriculum learning. Finally, we address learning along four classical RL dimensions: model-free versus model-based, value-based versus policy-based versus actor-critic, on-policy versus off-policy, and credit assignment, including both Monte Carlo methods, which rely on full return estimates, and bootstrapping methods, which update estimates using other learned predictions.
  Mapping the LLM literature onto this taxonomy reveals a strikingly non-uniform distribution of research effort. Critic-free policy gradients and Monte Carlo credit assignment are densely populated, while value-based methods, off-policy actor-critic training, and bootstrapping-based credit assignment remain largely unexplored despite well-established counterparts in classical RL. These gaps represent concrete opportunities for transferring proven RL techniques to LLM training.
  By making these gaps explicit alongside the methods that have proven effective, this survey offers researchers in both RL and LLMs a shared framework for understanding current practice and identifying promising directions for future work.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey examining RL methods in LLM post-training, revealing current methods (PPO, GRPO) represent only a narrow slice of available RL techniques and identifying unexplored opportunities.
AI.motivation: Current LLM post-training pipelines are dominated by a narrow set of RL methods (PPO and GRPO), representing only a small fraction of what RL has to offer. There's a need to understand why these methods prevail and what alternative RL techniques could be applied to LLM training.
AI.method: The survey organizes RL algorithm construction into three stages: 1) MDP creation (reward function, state space, action space, termination, discount factor), 2) Exploration (temperature sampling, entropy regularization, intrinsic motivation, tree search, curriculum learning), and 3) Learning dimensions (model-free vs model-based, value-based vs policy-based vs actor-critic, on-policy vs off-policy, and credit assignment methods). The LLM literature is mapped onto this taxonomy to identify research gaps.
AI.result: The analysis reveals a strikingly non-uniform distribution of research effort in LLM RL training. Critic-free policy gradients and Monte Carlo credit assignment are densely populated, while value-based methods, off-policy actor-critic training, and bootstrapping-based credit assignment remain largely unexplored despite having well-established counterparts in classical RL.
AI.conclusion: These identified gaps represent concrete opportunities for transferring proven RL techniques to LLM training. The survey provides researchers in both RL and LLMs with a shared framework for understanding current practices and identifying promising future research directions to broaden the application of RL in LLM post-training.

title: AgentLens: Interpretable Safety Steering via Mechanistic Subspaces for Multi-Turn Coding Agent
summary: Coding agents based on large language models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable autonomous capabilities, but they also introduce significant safety and misuse risks during multi-turn interactions with external environments. Existing safety mechanisms mainly rely on external guardrails, which have a limited ability to perform fine-grained behavioral control during execution. Meanwhile, recent mechanistic interpretability methods for LLM safety are mostly confined to single-turn or jailbreak-style QA settings, limiting their ability to capture the evolving risk dynamics of multi-turn agent execution. In this paper, we investigate the safety of multi-turn coding agents from an internal perspective. We propose AgentLens (Mechanistic Subspace Intervention and Steering), a white-box defense framework that performs runtime safety detection and representation-level mitigation for coding agents. Unlike conventional agent guardrails, AgentLens detect harmful execution states from step-level hidden representations and mitigate unsafe behavior by intervening in a 10-dimensional subspace within a single layer. To support this research, we introduce the Mechanistic Agent Safety (MAS) benchmark, comprising comprehensively annotated multi-turn execution trajectories across 194 tasks using LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Gemma-2-9B. Extensive experiments show that AgentLens achieves strong safety detection performance, provides preliminary evidence for lookahead risk anticipation, and substantially reduces harmful actions of the coding agent, establishing a foundation for applying mechanistic interpretability to dynamic LLM agent safety. The code is available at: https://github.com/EddyLuo1232/AgentLens

AI.tldr: AgentLens is a white-box defense framework that uses mechanistic interpretability to detect and mitigate safety risks in multi-turn coding agents through runtime monitoring and subspace interventions in LLM hidden representations.
AI.motivation: Existing safety mechanisms for LLM-based coding agents rely on external guardrails with limited fine-grained behavioral control, and current mechanistic interpretability methods focus on single-turn/jailbreak settings, missing the evolving risk dynamics of multi-turn agent execution.
AI.method: Propose AgentLens (Mechanistic Subspace Intervention and Steering) - a white-box framework that performs runtime safety detection from step-level hidden representations and mitigates unsafe behavior by intervening in a low-dimensional subspace (10-dimensional) within a single layer.
AI.result: AgentLens achieves strong safety detection performance, shows preliminary evidence for lookahead risk anticipation, and substantially reduces harmful actions of coding agents. Introduces the Mechanistic Agent Safety (MAS) benchmark with 194 annotated multi-turn tasks across three models (LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, Gemma-2-9B).
AI.conclusion: AgentLens establishes a foundation for applying mechanistic interpretability to dynamic LLM agent safety, addressing the limitations of existing external guardrails and single-turn safety methods by providing fine-grained internal control of multi-turn agent execution.

title: On the Curse of Dimensionality in Private Sparse Covariance Estimation and PCA
summary:
We study high-dimensional differentially private (DP) covariance estimation in the operator norm, and principal component analysis (PCA), under $k$-row-column sparsity ($k$-RCS) of the covariance matrix. In the non-private setting, it is known that $\mathsf{poly}(k, \log d)$ samples suffice to solve both of these problems. However, the only comparable result known under DP (Wang et al. 2021) requires $Ω(d)$ samples under standard parameterizations of the problem. We investigate when this curse of dimensionality is inherent for sparse covariance estimation tasks under DP.
  On the upper bound front, we show that a $\mathsf{poly}(k, \log d)$ sample complexity for PCA is possible under DP, if we also posit sparsity of the leading eigenvector. We complement this result with $\mathsf{poly}(d)$ lower bounds under DP for both sparse covariance estimation and PCA, establishing an exponential gap between the private and non-private variants of these problems when $k = \mathsf{polylog}(d)$. To our knowledge, no such separation has previously been demonstrated for any sparse estimation problems in private high-dimensional statistics. Our techniques are flexible enough that they imply stronger lower bounds even for the well-studied problem of standard DP PCA, without sparsity assumptions.

AI.tldr: Differentially private sparse covariance estimation and PCA require exponentially more samples than non-private versions when the sparsity parameter is polylog(d), revealing a fundamental privacy-efficiency tradeoff.
AI.motivation: Existing DP results for sparse covariance estimation require Ω(d) samples while non-private versions need only poly(k, log d) samples. The paper investigates whether this exponential gap is inherent to DP versions of these problems.
AI.method: The paper provides both upper and lower bounds: (1) Upper bound showing poly(k, log d) samples suffice for DP PCA when the leading eigenvector is also sparse; (2) Lower bounds showing poly(d) samples are necessary for both sparse covariance estimation and PCA under DP, establishing exponential separation.
AI.result: The results demonstrate an exponential separation between private and non-private sparse estimation when k = polylog(d). Upper bounds show poly(k, log d) samples are possible under additional sparsity assumptions, while lower bounds show poly(d) samples are necessary in general under DP.
AI.conclusion: There is a fundamental curse of dimensionality inherent to differentially private sparse covariance estimation and PCA, with an exponential sample complexity gap compared to non-private settings, especially when sparsity is polylogarithmic in dimension.

title: Skin-Deep: A Geometric Diagnostic for Alignment Fragility in Large Language Model Representations
comment: 16 pages, 3 figures, 12 tables. The first two authors contributed equally. Code (pre-attack GFS diagnostic): https://github.com/js-lee-AI/skin-deep
summary: Alignment tuning is meant to make harmful-request refusal robust, yet this safety behavior can be erased by a small set of benign fine-tuning examples. This is a deployment risk for open-weight models because a checkpoint can pass refusal tests at release time and later lose refusal under low-cost downstream fine-tuning. Prior work has established these refusal failures, but existing studies do not show how to detect this fragility in the aligned model itself before an attack or fine-tuning intervention is run. We introduce Skin-Deep, a geometric diagnostic that detects alignment fragility directly from the aligned model's hidden-state activations before such an intervention is run and compresses the layer-wise safety geometry into a single scalar, the Geometric Fragility Score (GFS). Applied to twenty-one instruction-tuned models spanning six alignment recipes and 3B--32B parameters, Skin-Deep reveals a recurring low-rank safety subspace across model families. Direction ablations show that removing directions in this subspace weakens harmful-request refusal, providing causal evidence that the recovered geometry underlies refusal behavior. Crucially, GFS identifies, before any fine-tuning, the initially safe model that retains the most refusal after small-scale LoRA fine-tuning. These results establish GFS as a practical pre-deployment diagnostic for flagging fragile refusal behavior without running an attack.

AI.tldr: Skin-Deep is a geometric diagnostic tool that detects alignment fragility in AI models before deployment by analyzing hidden-state activations and computing a Geometric Fragility Score to flag models vulnerable to safety erosion from downstream fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Alignment tuning aims to make models robust against harmful requests, but this safety behavior can be erased by small sets of benign fine-tuning examples. Current methods cannot detect this fragility before an attack occurs, creating deployment risks for open-weight models that might pass safety tests initially but lose refusal capabilities after downstream fine-tuning.
AI.method: The researchers introduce Skin-Deep, a geometric diagnostic that analyzes aligned models' hidden-state activations without running interventions. It compresses layer-wise safety geometry into a single scalar called the Geometric Fragility Score (GFS). They applied this to 21 instruction-tuned models across 6 alignment recipes and 3B-32B parameters.
AI.result: Skin-Deep reveals a recurring low-rank safety subspace across different model families. Direction ablations demonstrate that removing directions in this subspace weakens harmful-request refusal, providing causal evidence that this geometry underlies refusal behavior. Crucially, GFS successfully identifies which initially safe model retains the most refusal after small-scale LoRA fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The Geometric Fragility Score serves as a practical pre-deployment diagnostic tool that can flag fragile refusal behavior without needing to run attacks, addressing a critical safety gap in model deployment by identifying vulnerabilities before they can be exploited through fine-tuning.

title: Learning by Shifting: Temporal View Construction for Time Series Contrastive Learning
comment: Published in the European Conference on Machine Learning and Principles and Practice of Knowledge Discovery in Databases (ECMLPKDD)
summary: Supervised learning demands large quantities of labeled data, a bottleneck that is expensive and reliant on domain-specific expertise. Self-supervised learning, particularly contrastive learning, has emerged as a compelling alternative, enabling rich representation learning directly from unlabeled data. Yet its success hinges critically on the design of positive and negative sample pairs. Existing approaches for time series rely on hand-crafted augmentations and masking heuristics that embed strong domain assumptions, often limiting generalization across diverse temporal patterns and potentially introducing spurious correlations. In this work, we challenge this paradigm by demonstrating that explicitly encoding temporal shift invariance through a simple, deterministic view construction is sufficient to learn strong representations for time series classification. By exploiting temporal structure, our method, Shift Invariant Feature Training (ShiFT), achieves state-of-the-art performance on six diverse real-world time series benchmark datasets, as well as the UCR and UEA archives, while reducing training time. Beyond empirical performance, we present a systematic analysis of contrastive learning dynamics in time series settings, examining the effects of batch size and the number of negatives on downstream performance. Our findings provide practical insights for designing efficient contrastive learning frameworks for time series representation learning. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/sfi-norwai/ShiFT.

AI.tldr: Shift Invariant Feature Training (ShiFT) is a self-supervised contrastive learning method for time series that uses simple deterministic temporal shifts instead of complex hand-crafted augmentations, achieving state-of-the-art performance with reduced training time.
AI.motivation: Supervised learning requires expensive labeled data, while existing self-supervised approaches for time series rely on hand-crafted augmentations and masking heuristics that embed strong domain assumptions, limiting generalization across diverse temporal patterns and potentially introducing spurious correlations.
AI.method: ShiFT explicitly encodes temporal shift invariance through simple, deterministic view construction. Instead of complex augmentations, it exploits temporal structure by creating positive pairs through temporal shifts, avoiding the need for hand-crafted transformations that may embed domain biases.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on six diverse real-world time series benchmark datasets and the UCR and UEA archives while reducing training time. The method also provides systematic analysis of contrastive learning dynamics, examining effects of batch size and number of negatives on downstream performance.
AI.conclusion: Explicit temporal shift invariance through simple deterministic view construction is sufficient for strong time series representations, challenging the need for complex hand-crafted augmentations. The approach provides practical insights for designing efficient contrastive learning frameworks for time series representation learning.

title: VISTA Architect: A graph database-oriented health AI system demonstrated in multidisciplinary tumor boards
comment: 22 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables; includes Supplementary Information. Code: https://github.com/VISTA-Stanford/vista-architect (tag v0.1.0-preprint, commit 8837d44)
summary:
We introduce VISTA Architect, a database-oriented AI architecture for integrating large language models (LLMs) with longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs). At ingestion, it transforms complex clinical documentation into a persistent, provenance-linked knowledge graph, eliminating repeated reprocessing of raw records at query time. The architecture has two layers: a source-faithful MEDS Graph preserving granular EHR structure with full provenance, and a clinically abstracted Timeline Object Architecture (TOA) that uses graph-guided LLM extraction to synthesize a concise timeline of deduplicated, temporally coherent clinical events. This addresses key limitations of direct long-context prompting and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), which often miss temporal relationships and incur high cost and latency from repeated raw-text processing. By precomputing clinical synthesis once, downstream queries access an organized patient state and traverse to source documentation only when detailed verification is needed.
  We demonstrate the system in multidisciplinary thoracic oncology tumor boards at Stanford Medicine, where precise reconstruction of patient histories is critical. Across 1,180 patients, VISTA Architect achieved 96.4% accuracy (mean 9.75/10) on 15 tumor board-salient variables (17,700 evaluations; 95% CI 96.1-96.7%), surpassing a matched BM25 RAG baseline and recent benchmarks for LLM-based clinical extraction. An agentic interface reduced preparation for a 30-patient held-out cohort to about 2.2 minutes without sacrificing accuracy. While configured here for thoracic oncology, the modular design adapts to other specialties through customizable event definitions, episode structures, and agentic tools; validation beyond thoracic oncology remains future work.

AI.tldr: VISTA Architect is a database-oriented AI architecture that transforms complex EHR documentation into a dual-layer knowledge graph for efficient clinical querying, eliminating repeated raw text processing and enabling accurate patient timeline reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Direct long-context prompting and RAG approaches for EHR analysis often miss temporal relationships and incur high computational costs from repeated raw-text processing, making them inefficient for clinical applications requiring precise patient history reconstruction.
AI.method: Two-layer architecture: 1) source-faithful MEDS Graph preserving granular EHR structure with full provenance, and 2) clinically abstracted Timeline Object Architecture (TOA) using graph-guided LLM extraction to create deduplicated, temporally coherent event timelines.
AI.result: Achieved 96.4% accuracy on 15 tumor board-salient variables across 1,180 patients, surpassing BM25 RAG baselines. Reduced preparation time for 30-patient cohort to ~2.2 minutes using agentic interface while maintaining accuracy.
AI.conclusion: VISTA Architect provides an efficient, accurate solution for EHR integration with LLMs by precomputing clinical synthesis, enabling downstream queries to access organized patient states and only traverse source documentation when needed for verification, with potential for broader clinical applications.

title: A Standard Processing Pipeline for High-accuracy Measurement of Few-shot Regression on Laser Induced Breakdown Spectroscopy
summary: Laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS) faces challenges in high-accuracy quantitative measurement under few-shot scenarios due to spectral noise and data scarcity. Traditional preprocessing methods often fail to preserve subtle spectral features or capture nonlinear correlations. This work proposes a standardized processing pipeline integrating diffusion-based denoising, attention-based autoencoder for dimensionality reduction, group shuffling data augmentation, and ordinary least squares regression. The diffusion module employs a 3D UNet architecture to remove spectral noise while preserving essential emission features. The attention-autoencoder captures nonlinear spectral correlations, effectively reducing high-dimensional spectral data to compact latent representations. Group shuffling data augmentation enhances model robustness by creating synthetic samples through feature group permutation. Experimental results on multiple elemental concentrations demonstrate that our Diffusion-DA-AE pipeline achieves superior performance with a mean RMAE of 0.2847, representing 37.7\% and 37.6\% improvements over baseline autoencoder and traditional PCA-PLS regression, respectively. The framework's effectiveness validates its generalizability and establishes a new benchmark for few-shot LIBS regression.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Diffusion-DA-AE, a novel standardized processing pipeline for few-shot LIBS regression that integrates diffusion-based denoising, attention-based autoencoder dimensionality reduction, group shuffling data augmentation, and ordinary least squares regression to achieve state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS) struggles with high-accuracy quantitative measurement in few-shot scenarios due to spectral noise and data scarcity. Traditional preprocessing methods fail to preserve subtle spectral features or capture nonlinear correlations between spectral data and elemental concentrations.
AI.method: The proposed Diffusion-DA-AE pipeline consists of four components: (1) diffusion-based denoising using a 3D UNet architecture to remove noise while preserving essential emission features, (2) attention-based autoencoder for capturing nonlinear spectral correlations and reducing high-dimensional data to compact latent representations, (3) group shuffling data augmentation that creates synthetic samples through feature group permutation to enhance robustness, and (4) ordinary least squares regression for final concentration prediction.
AI.result: Experimental results on multiple elemental concentrations show the Diffusion-DA-AE pipeline achieves superior performance with a mean RMAE (relative mean absolute error) of 0.2847, representing 37.7% and 37.6% improvements over baseline autoencoder and traditional PCA-PLS regression methods, respectively.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively addresses few-shot LIBS regression challenges, demonstrates strong generalizability, and establishes a new benchmark for quantitative measurement in data-scarce scenarios. The integration of advanced deep learning techniques with traditional regression provides a robust solution for spectral analysis.

title: Safety-Aware Evaluation of LLM-Generated Driver Intervention Messages through Multi-Task Risk Fusion
summary: Existing driver intervention systems rely on auditory alerts and fixed templates, failing to leverage multi-task recognition outputs. General-purpose metrics such as BLEU and BERTScore cannot capture intervention-specific quality dimensions including risk-urgency alignment, cognitive load, and driver acceptability. In this paper, we propose the Driver Safety-Aware Intervention Score (DSAIS), a domain-specific metric evaluating five dimensions through a hybrid architecture combining lightweight rule-based computation with LLM Judge evaluation, together with an end-to-end framework integrating four-task recognition outputs into an LLM through risk fusion, state history management, and dynamic prompt construction. Experiments on the AIDE dataset with five models and seven conditions demonstrate that DSAIS achieves ICC 0.798-0.840 across three architecturally distinct judges and Cohen's d > 1.5 across all control conditions. Multi-dimensional sub-score analysis quantifies the contextual adaptability gap between rule-based and LLM-based systems, revealing that multi-task integration improves contextual relevance by 9.1% over rule-based baselines. Ablation experiments demonstrate that each framework component contributes to contextual relevance, with sub-score decomposition revealing gains that aggregate scoring masks. Driver emotion recognition is identified as the most critical upstream factor, and compact local LLMs (7B--9B parameters) achieve quality superior to API-based models, providing practical design guidelines for in-vehicle deployment.

AI.tldr: DSAIS is a new metric for evaluating driver intervention systems that combines rule-based computation with LLM evaluation across five safety dimensions, showing significant improvements over general metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing driver intervention systems use auditory alerts and fixed templates without leveraging multi-task recognition outputs, and general-purpose metrics like BLEU and BERTScore fail to capture intervention-specific quality dimensions like risk-urgency alignment, cognitive load, and driver acceptability.
AI.method: Proposes Driver Safety-Aware Intervention Score (DSAIS), a hybrid architecture combining lightweight rule-based computation with LLM Judge evaluation. Also presents an end-to-end framework integrating four-task recognition outputs into an LLM through risk fusion, state history management, and dynamic prompt construction.
AI.result: On the AIDE dataset with five models and seven conditions, DSAIS achieves ICC 0.798-0.840 across three architecturally distinct judges and Cohen's d > 1.5 across all control conditions. Multi-task integration improves contextual relevance by 9.1% over rule-based baselines. Driver emotion recognition is the most critical upstream factor, and compact local LLMs (7B-9B parameters) achieve superior quality to API-based models.
AI.conclusion: DSAIS provides an effective domain-specific metric for evaluating driver intervention systems, with practical design guidelines showing that compact local LLMs outperform API-based models for in-vehicle deployment, and multi-task integration significantly improves contextual relevance.

title: VegSim: A Geospatial World Model for Scenario-Conditioned Vegetation Simulation
summary: Vegetation monitoring under climate stress requires answering not only how it will evolve given the expected weather, but how it would respond to alternative meteorological conditions. Forecasting models return the expected vegetation state for the observed weather and cannot answer these scenario-conditioned questions, because future weather is fixed to the recorded trajectory. We present VegSim, a geospatial world model for scenario-conditioned vegetation simulation. VegSim infers a latent vegetation state from sparse satellite-derived NDVI histories, past meteorological covariates, and static spatial context, propagates it forward under future weather forcing through recurrent latent dynamics, and decodes predictive NDVI quantiles at each lead time. Because future forcing enters as a controllable input, the same trained model supports probabilistic forecasting under observed weather and conditional simulation under user-defined meteorological forcing, without supervision on scenario responses. We evaluate VegSim on GreenEarthNet across in-distribution data and spatial, temporal, and joint spatial-temporal shift, where it achieves strong point and probabilistic accuracy against time series and Earth observation forecasting baselines while using a compact architecture. We then simulate vegetation responses across Europe under four meteorological scenarios, and in a France summer 2022 case study, obtaining spatially coherent patterns consistent with known sensitivity to temperature and precipitation. The code is available at https://github.com/arco-group/vegsim.

AI.tldr: VegSim is a geospatial world model that can simulate vegetation responses under different meteorological scenarios, not just forecast under observed weather, enabling scenario-conditioned vegetation analysis.
AI.motivation: Current vegetation forecasting models can only predict expected vegetation states under observed weather trajectories, but cannot answer scenario-conditioned questions about how vegetation would respond to alternative meteorological conditions, which is crucial for monitoring vegetation under climate stress.
AI.method: VegSim is a geospatial world model that: 1) infers latent vegetation state from sparse satellite-derived NDVI histories, past meteorological covariates, and static spatial context, 2) propagates it forward under future weather forcing through recurrent latent dynamics, and 3) decodes predictive NDVI quantiles at each lead time. Future weather forcing enters as a controllable input, enabling conditional simulation without supervision on scenario responses.
AI.result: VegSim achieves strong point and probabilistic accuracy against time series and Earth observation forecasting baselines on GreenEarthNet across in-distribution data and spatial, temporal, and joint spatial-temporal shift scenarios. It successfully simulates vegetation responses across Europe under four meteorological scenarios, producing spatially coherent patterns consistent with known sensitivity to temperature and precipitation, particularly demonstrated in a France summer 2022 case study.
AI.conclusion: VegSim provides a novel approach to scenario-conditioned vegetation simulation that goes beyond traditional forecasting by allowing users to explore vegetation responses under user-defined meteorological scenarios, offering a valuable tool for understanding vegetation dynamics under climate stress with potential applications in climate adaptation planning.

title: Beyond Penalizing Mistakes: Stabilizing Efficiency Training in Large Reasoning Models via Adaptive Correct-Only Rewards
summary: Training large language models to reason efficiently is a critical challenge. While integrating length-penalizing rewards into Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) aims to reduce verbosity, it frequently triggers reward collapse, severely degrading reasoning capabilities. Through a systematic evaluation of various reward configurations, we identify the root mechanism: GRPO's group normalization creates divergent advantages when incorrect answers receive continuous length penalties. Consequently, methods penalizing the length of incorrect answers are structurally prone to collapse under sustained optimization. Furthermore, restricting penalties exclusively to correct answers avoids this primary failure, but leaves the model susceptible to a stochastic collapse driven by response over-compression. To robustly prevent both failure modes, we propose ACOER (Adaptive Correct-Only Efficiency Reward). ACOER eliminates the structural penalty loop by isolating brevity bonuses to correct completions and prevents stochastic compression via dynamic budget normalization and control-loop penalty adjustments. Evaluated across diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks, ACOER improves overall accuracy compared to the base model while reducing token generation by over 60%, establishing a fundamentally stable approach for efficiency-aware optimization.

AI.tldr: ACOER (Adaptive Correct-Only Efficiency Reward) prevents reward collapse in LLM efficiency optimization by isolating brevity bonuses to correct answers only and adding protection against over-compression.
AI.motivation: Current methods for making LLMs more efficient (using length-penalizing rewards in GRPO) often cause "reward collapse" that degrades reasoning capabilities, so there's a need for a more stable approach.
AI.method: The paper proposes ACOER which isolates brevity bonuses exclusively to correct completions to avoid structural penalty loops, and adds dynamic budget normalization with control-loop penalty adjustments to prevent stochastic compression.
AI.result: ACOER improves overall accuracy compared to the base model while reducing token generation by over 60% across diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ACOER establishes a fundamentally stable approach for efficiency-aware optimization in LLMs by robustly preventing both structural reward collapse and stochastic compression failure modes.

title: SPOTR: Spatio-temporal Pooling One-Token Reconstruction for Universal Physiological Signal Self-supervised Learning
comment: The paper has been accepted by IJCAI-ECAI 2026
summary: Physiological signals such as EEG, ECG, and PPG are widely used in clinical monitoring. Recent self-supervised learning (SSL) methods offer an attractive way to leverage unlabeled recordings, yet they still fall short in practice. In particular, current SSL methods struggle across heterogeneous datasets, often distorting clinically meaningful structures or learning shortcuts from temporal and cross-channel redundancy. Consequently, existing SSL methods often deliver limited performance under linear probing, a lightweight adaptation setting that better matches real-world medical scenarios. Moreover, most Transformer-based SSL models encode a flattened spatiotemporal token sequence, incurring high computation and memory cost, and are typically developed within a single modality. To address these limitations, we present SPOTR (Spatio-temporal Pooling One-Token Reconstruction), a compress-reconstruct pretraining framework that introduces a single-token global bottleneck for physiological signals. SPOTR compresses each waveform into a single-token representation and reconstructs the signal conditioned only on this representation. Meanwhile, SPOTR introduces an efficient spatio-temporal compaction module to reduce computation and memory cost. Pretrained on 20 datasets spanning EEG, iEEG, ECG, and PPG, SPOTR consistently outperforms the strongest baseline under linear probing, improving average AUC by 18.49%, 21.71%, 17.86%, and 4.64%, respectively. Compared with a representative general-purpose time-series foundation model, SPOTR achieves around 78% lower latency and 52% lower peak GPU memory on average. The code can be found at https://github.com/5GYYYYY/SPOTR.

AI.tldr: SPOTR is a self-supervised learning framework that introduces single-token global bottlenecks for physiological signals, achieving significant performance improvements and computational efficiency across multiple medical signal modalities.
AI.motivation: Current self-supervised learning methods for physiological signals (EEG, ECG, PPG) struggle with heterogeneous datasets, often distorting clinically meaningful structures or learning shortcuts from temporal/cross-channel redundancy. Existing SSL methods deliver limited performance in linear probing settings that better match real-world medical scenarios, and most Transformer-based SSL models have high computational costs.
AI.method: SPOTR uses a compress-reconstruct pretraining framework with a single-token global bottleneck for physiological signals. It compresses each waveform into a single-token representation and reconstructs the signal conditioned only on this representation. The framework includes an efficient spatio-temporal compaction module to reduce computation and memory costs.
AI.result: Pretrained on 20 datasets spanning EEG, iEEG, ECG, and PPG, SPOTR consistently outperforms baselines under linear probing, improving average AUC by 18.49%, 21.71%, 17.86%, and 4.64% respectively. Compared with a representative general-purpose time-series foundation model, SPOTR achieves around 78% lower latency and 52% lower peak GPU memory on average.
AI.conclusion: SPOTR effectively addresses limitations of current SSL methods for physiological signals by introducing a single-token global bottleneck approach, achieving both superior performance in linear probing settings and significant computational efficiency improvements, making it more practical for real-world medical applications.

title: Text Dictates, Music Decorates: Energy-based Attention for Editable Dance Motion Generation
summary: Choreographic motion generation poses unique challenges for AI, demanding precise semantic control over complex, temporally structured, and expressive full-body dynamics. While existing models can synthesize motion from music, they remain largely black boxes. Conversely, attempting to condition generation on both text and music frequently leads to modality collapse, where dense acoustic rhythms overwhelm sparse semantic text prompts, destroying user controllability. To resolve this spatial-temporal conflict, we propose STREAM (Structural-Temporal Rhythmic Energy-based Attention for Motion), a modality-decoupled diffusion transformer. STREAM strictly separates conditioning pathways: global text semantics dictate the kinematic structure via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN), while a novel Bimodal Energy-Based Attention Module (BEAM) routes these features to the musical beat without overwriting the semantics. We further introduce Motorica++, a newly curated dataset enriched with domain-specific dance vocabulary and frame-level semantic annotations from existing Motorica dataset. Additionally, to rigorously quantify zero-shot editability, we propose the Exchange Evaluation Protocol and Editable Dance Score (EDS). Through extensive experiments, STREAM achieves state-of-the-art alignment between motion and music while perfectly preserving choreographic semantics, positioning AI not merely as a reactive synthesizer, but as a controllable, collaborative partner for artistic direction. The source code and datasets are available at https://github.com/SeongJong-Yoo/STREAM.

AI.tldr: STREAM is a modality-decoupled diffusion transformer that separates text and music conditioning pathways to prevent modality collapse, enabling precise semantic control over choreographic motion generation while maintaining strong music alignment.
AI.motivation: Existing motion generation models are black boxes with poor controllability, and attempts to condition on both text and music lead to modality collapse where dense acoustic rhythms overwhelm sparse semantic text prompts, destroying user controllability.
AI.method: Proposes STREAM with strictly separated conditioning pathways: global text semantics dictate kinematic structure via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN), while a novel Bimodal Energy-Based Attention Module (BEAM) routes these features to musical beats without overwriting semantics. Also introduces Motorica++ dataset and Exchange Evaluation Protocol with Editable Dance Score (EDS) for evaluation.
AI.result: STREAM achieves state-of-the-art alignment between motion and music while perfectly preserving choreographic semantics, positioning AI as a controllable collaborative partner rather than just a reactive synthesizer.
AI.conclusion: The proposed modality-decoupled approach successfully resolves the spatial-temporal conflict in choreographic motion generation, enabling precise semantic control and strong music-motion alignment simultaneously, advancing AI toward being a collaborative artistic tool.

title: Prefix-Guided On-Policy Distillation: Mining Golden Trajectories from Rollouts
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) improves reasoning models by applying dense teacher supervision on student-sampled trajectories. However, scaling OPD to long-horizon mathematical reasoning exposes a reliability and efficiency problem: standard OPD assigns every sampled candidate the same long rollout budget, even though some trajectories may quickly become weakly aligned with the teacher and provide less useful supervision. Prior analyses suggest that successful OPD depends on local teacher-student compatibility, which can be measured by top-k overlap on student-visited prefixes. When this overlap is low, continuing to generate or train on long suffixes may waste computation and introduce noisy learning signal. To address this, we introduce Prefix-Guided On-Policy Distillation (PG-OPD), a simple rollout-allocation framework that uses fixed-length prefixes to estimate trajectory value before expensive long-horizon generation. PG-OPD first decodes every sampled candidate to the same prefix length, computes teacher-student top-k overlap within an early probe window of that prefix, and selectively continues high-overlap candidates to a fixed long length. Low-overlap candidates stop at the fixed prefix, avoiding unnecessary suffix generation. Across diverse teacher-student combinations on AMC, AIME, and HMMT benchmarks, PG-OPD improves average accuracy by up to 4.80 points while reducing training time by up to 2.46x. These results suggest that prefix-level compatibility provides a practical signal for directing OPD computation toward trajectories that remain learnable from the teacher.

AI.tldr: PG-OPD is a method that improves on-policy distillation efficiency by using early prefix analysis to selectively allocate rollout budgets only to promising student trajectories, reducing computation while improving performance.
AI.motivation: Standard on-policy distillation wastes computational resources by giving all sampled trajectories the same long rollout budget, even when some trajectories quickly become weakly aligned with the teacher, providing less useful supervision and introducing noisy learning signals.
AI.method: PG-OPD decodes all candidates to a fixed prefix length, computes teacher-student top-k overlap within an early probe window of that prefix, then selectively continues only high-overlap candidates to full length while stopping low-overlap candidates at the prefix, avoiding unnecessary suffix generation.
AI.result: Across diverse teacher-student combinations on AMC, AIME, and HMMT benchmarks, PG-OPD improves average accuracy by up to 4.80 points while reducing training time by up to 2.46x.
AI.conclusion: Prefix-level compatibility provides a practical signal for directing OPD computation toward trajectories that remain learnable from the teacher, addressing the reliability and efficiency problems in scaling OPD to long-horizon mathematical reasoning.

title: Load Testing for Machine Learning Model Serving Systems at Scale
comment: The paper is accepted at ECSA 2026
summary: Machine learning (ML) model serving has become a dominant consumer of GPU infrastructure, yet capacity planning in these systems remains largely ad hoc. Under-provisioning leads to service-level objective (SLO) violations and production incidents, while over-provisioning results in substantial resource waste. This paper presents \sys, an industrial load testing framework for ML serving systems that systematically estimates serving capacity through an adaptive, feedback-driven search strategy. The approach leverages real-time performance signals, incorporating dampening, spike tolerance, and convergence detection to efficiently identify maximum sustainable throughput under SLO constraints. We evaluate \sys through a longitudinal analysis of 14 industrial case studies spanning four ML architecture classes: recommendation, ranking, vision, and NLP. This study demonstrates that systematic load testing leads to substantial improvements in GPU resource efficiency and operational reliability. Prior to adopting \sys, a significant fraction of model launches were under-provisioned, resulting in recurring incidents; these issues were substantially reduced after deployment. Our results show that ML-specific design decisions are critical to accurate capacity estimation: workload calibration using recorded traffic reduces estimation error from approximately 30\% to 2--6\%, while proper warmup handling yields a 22.2\% improvement in accuracy. Further analysis reveals key factors influencing prediction error, including model size and co-location effects. This paper distills six lessons and derive architectural guidelines for ML load testing, offering actionable insights for building reliable and efficient ML serving systems.

AI.tldr: An industrial load testing framework called \sys that systematically estimates ML serving capacity through adaptive feedback-driven search, reducing estimation errors and improving GPU resource efficiency in production ML systems.
AI.motivation: Capacity planning for ML model serving on GPU infrastructure is currently ad hoc, leading to either under-provisioning (causing SLO violations and incidents) or over-provisioning (wasting substantial resources).
AI.method: \sys uses an adaptive, feedback-driven search strategy with real-time performance signals, incorporating dampening, spike tolerance, and convergence detection to identify maximum sustainable throughput under SLO constraints.
AI.result: Systematic load testing substantially improved GPU resource efficiency and operational reliability: ML-specific design reduced estimation error from ~30% to 2-6%, warmup handling improved accuracy by 22.2%, and previously under-provisioned model launches with recurring incidents were significantly reduced.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents six lessons and architectural guidelines for ML load testing, emphasizing that ML-specific design decisions (workload calibration, warmup handling) are critical for accurate capacity estimation and building reliable, efficient ML serving systems.

title: GroundEval: A Deterministic Replacement for LLM-as-Judge in Stateful Agent Evaluation
summary:
Before letting an agent operate over real context, can you prove it used the right evidence? GroundEval turns that question into a deterministic test of what the agent searched, fetched, cited, and was permitted to access. In one case study, two frontier LLM judges scored a plausible agent response above 0.85. But the trace told a different story: the agent had never retrieved the artifact its answer depended on, yielding a GroundEval score of 0.000.
  We introduce GroundEval, a judge-free framework for evaluating agents against grounded, time-bounded, and access-controlled evidence. GroundEval uses a domain configuration to generate questions, lets the agent choose how to answer, and then scores both the final answer and the recorded trajectory that produced it. The benchmark targets three failures that LLM-as-judge evaluation struggles to detect: whether an agent checked before claiming absence, reasoned only from evidence available to the actor at the relevant time, and used the correct causal mechanism rather than a plausible one. These correspond to three tracks: Silence, Perspective, and Counterfactual. GroundEval exposes when plausible answers rest on invalid evidence paths, and produces structured per-question diagnostics that pair tool activity with the agent's turn-level narration, making each score inspectable rather than merely reported. What our case studies turned up is that this gap isn't some rare corner case. It's exactly the blind spot that final-answer and judge-based scoring were never built to catch.

AI.tldr: GroundEval is a judge-free framework for evaluating AI agents that examines whether agents properly use evidence through their search, fetch, and citation behaviors, rather than just judging final answers.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-as-judge evaluations struggle to detect when plausible-sounding agent responses are based on invalid evidence paths. There's a need to evaluate whether agents actually retrieve and use the right evidence, check before claiming absence, reason from evidence available at relevant times, and use correct causal mechanisms rather than just plausible ones.
AI.method: GroundEval uses domain configurations to generate questions, lets agents choose how to answer, then scores both the final answer and the recorded trajectory that produced it. It employs three tracks: Silence (checking before claiming absence), Perspective (reasoning only from evidence available at relevant time), and Counterfactual (using correct causal mechanism vs plausible one).
AI.result: In case studies, GroundEval revealed significant gaps where frontier LLM judges scored plausible agent responses highly (above 0.85), but the actual agent trajectories showed they never retrieved the evidence their answers depended on, yielding GroundEval scores of 0.000.
AI.conclusion: The evidence gap detected by GroundEval isn't a rare corner case but a fundamental blind spot in traditional final-answer and judge-based scoring. GroundEval's structured diagnostics make scores inspectable by pairing tool activity with agent narration, providing more transparent and reliable agent evaluation.

title: Channel Location Constrains the Auditability of Subliminal Learning
summary: Subliminal learning lets a student inherit a teacher's hidden trait from distillation data that never names it. We ask when such transfer can be audited before training. The answer is not model identity or scale alone, but channel location: the carrier through which the trait reaches the student. We find three regimes. In a controlled initialization-dependent body channel, a pre-training screen works. Coverage, the cosine between the student's initial distillation update and the teacher's fine-tuning displacement, predicts held-out transfer (Spearman $ρ\approx 0.95$; AUROC 0.997). In pretrained language models, masked single-token traits instead ride convergent vocabulary geometry. This channel is initialization-independent, so initialization-alignment screens, including coverage, are not mechanistic; the useful handles are post-hoc detection and targeted mitigation. Even when a single-token named entity is removed from the loss, the student's held-out probability for that entity rises to 0.40 on average ($\sim 2500\times$), and a related semantic class transfers. In an untied-head model, orthogonalizing the trait's output row against entangled neighbours collapses leakage, while equal-size random-subspace edits do not. Thus removing a target string from distillation labels does not remove the corresponding preference: neighbouring tokens can carry it. Finally, conditional behaviours can route through the network body. For sycophancy, with agreement and correction markers masked from the loss, transfer reaches about 0.63 of the teacher's effect, localizes to body computation, and evades four audits across two model families. We scope this as masked transfer of a condition-present policy. Channel location is necessary for deciding which audits can be sound. It is not a deployment-ready screen: an audit used outside its carrier regime can give false assurance.

AI.tldr: Subliminal learning transfers hidden traits from teacher to student via distillation, with effectiveness depending on the channel location (body, vocabulary geometry, or network body) rather than just model identity or scale.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates when subliminal transfer of hidden traits from teacher to student models can be audited before training, focusing on understanding the mechanisms behind how these traits transfer during distillation processes.
AI.method: The research identifies three transfer regimes: 1) controlled initialization-dependent body channel (using pre-training screens and coverage metric), 2) masked single-token traits riding convergent vocabulary geometry (initialization-independent), and 3) conditional behaviors routing through network body. The study uses coverage prediction metrics, targeted mitigation techniques, and localization experiments across different model families.
AI.result: 1) In body channel: coverage predicts transfer with high accuracy (Spearman ρ≈0.95; AUROC 0.997). 2) In vocabulary channel: even when named entities are removed from loss, student probability rises to 0.40 (~2500× increase) with semantic class transfer; orthogonalizing output rows against neighbors collapses leakage. 3) For sycophancy behaviors: transfer reaches 63% of teacher's effect through body computation, evading four audits across two model families.
AI.conclusion: Channel location, not model identity or scale, determines when subliminal transfer can be audited. Audits must match the carrier regime to avoid false assurance, as traits can transfer through unexpected channels like vocabulary geometry or network body even when seemingly removed from training objectives.

title: Learning Filters with Certainty
summary: Hash-based data structures such as Bloom filters are widely used in network systems for tasks including caching, anomaly detection, and machine learning pipelines. They typically provide binary indications of whether an element belongs to a set of interest, e.g., the contents of a cache. When uncertainty arises due to hash collisions, a positive indication is returned to avoid false negatives. We argue that the certainty associated with such indications can itself be useful information. This work focuses on Counting Bloom Filters (CBFs), a Bloom-filter variant that maintains counters rather than bits. Besides supporting insertions and deletions, these counters provide additional information that can be used to estimate the certainty of positive membership indications. We show how this certainty signal can be exploited in architectures that combine Bloom Filters with machine learning (ML) models.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using certainty signals from Counting Bloom Filters to enhance machine learning models in network systems.
AI.motivation: Traditional hash-based data structures like Bloom filters only provide binary membership indications, but the certainty associated with these indications can be valuable information. Bloom filters typically return false positives when hash collisions occur, and this uncertainty information could be useful for improving system performance.
AI.method: The work focuses on Counting Bloom Filters (CBFs), which maintain counters rather than bits. These counters support insertions/deletions and provide additional information to estimate the certainty of positive membership indications. The paper explores how to exploit this certainty signal in architectures that combine Bloom Filters with machine learning models.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that CBF counters can provide certainty estimates for membership indications, which can be leveraged to improve ML-based network systems. This goes beyond traditional binary membership signals by incorporating confidence levels.
AI.conclusion: Certainty information from Counting Bloom Filters can be valuable for enhancing machine learning models in network applications, providing richer data beyond simple binary membership decisions.

title: Cluster-Specific Localized Drift Detection for Efficient Batch Model Adaptation under Controlled Distribution Shift
summary: Machine learning systems deployed in dynamic environments frequently operate under nonstationary data distributions, where controlled distribution shift can progressively degrade predictive performance. However, many widely used tabular benchmark datasets lack explicit temporal structure, limiting reproducible evaluation of drift adaptation methods. This work proposes a cluster-induced distribution shift simulation framework that transforms static tabular datasets into controlled evolving data streams through structured perturbations across featurespace partitions. Using this framework, six adaptation strategies are systematically evaluated: static learning, sliding-window retraining, global ADWIN retraining, cluster-local ADWIN retraining, random subspace drift detection, and feature-partitioned drift detection. Experiments are conducted on five benchmark datasets covering both classification and regression tasks using diverse predictive model families, including linear models, k-Nearest Neighbours, tree ensembles, boosting methods, and adaptive online learners.

AI.tldr: A framework for simulating distribution shift in tabular datasets enables systematic evaluation of six drift adaptation strategies across multiple benchmarks and model families.
AI.motivation: Existing tabular benchmark datasets lack temporal structure, making it difficult to reproducibly evaluate machine learning methods for handling nonstationary data distributions in dynamic environments where distribution shift degrades predictive performance.
AI.method: Proposes a cluster-induced distribution shift simulation framework that transforms static tabular datasets into controlled evolving data streams through structured perturbations across feature-space partitions. Systematically evaluates six adaptation strategies: static learning, sliding-window retraining, global ADWIN retraining, cluster-local ADWIN retraining, random subspace drift detection, and feature-partitioned drift detection.
AI.result: Experiments conducted on five benchmark datasets covering classification and regression tasks using diverse predictive model families including linear models, k-Nearest Neighbours, tree ensembles, boosting methods, and adaptive online learners.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables reproducible evaluation of drift adaptation methods on tabular data by simulating controlled distribution shift, facilitating systematic comparison of different adaptation strategies across various model types and tasks.

title: The Origins of Stochasticity: Comprehensive Investigations on Uncertainty Quantification for Large Language Models
summary: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled sophisticated reasoning and content generation, yet their inherent stochasticity poses significant challenges for ensuring predictive credibility. While traditional uncertainty taxonomy paradigms, such as the dichotomy of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties, provide conceptual foundations, they often fail to capture the multi-component and multi-stage nature of LLM generation and struggle to evaluate the effectiveness of various Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) methods. In this paper, we propose a granular uncertainty taxonomy that systematically attributes LLM uncertainty into input-level, parameter-level, token-level, and decoding-process sources. Correspondingly, we categorize existing UQ methods into Bayesian, ensemble, consensus-based, and single-pass approaches. Furthermore, we introduce a comprehensive evaluation framework covering diverse generation settings and metrics. We empirically evaluate 21 typical UQ methods across three prominent LLM families, including Qwen3, Llama 3.2, and DeepSeek-V3, on benchmarks such as TriviaQA, GSM8K, and HumanEval. Our experimental results demonstrate that (i) the effectiveness of UQ methods is sensitive to task types and generation settings; (ii) consensus-based methods, typed Deg and EigV, consistently outperform other UQ approaches; and (iii) larger model scales correlate with lower uncertainty estimates, suggesting an empirical scaling law for LLM uncertainty. This work bridges the gap between theoretical origins and practical deployment, providing a versatile diagnostic tool for systematically quantifying uncertainty in LLM applications.

AI.tldr: Proposes a granular uncertainty taxonomy for LLMs, categorizes UQ methods, evaluates 21 methods across 3 LLM families, finds consensus-based methods perform best, and discovers model scaling reduces uncertainty.
AI.motivation: LLMs have stochasticity that challenges predictive credibility, but traditional uncertainty taxonomies fail to capture the multi-component, multi-stage nature of LLM generation and struggle to evaluate UQ method effectiveness.
AI.method: Proposes granular uncertainty taxonomy (input-level, parameter-level, token-level, decoding-process sources), categorizes UQ methods into Bayesian, ensemble, consensus-based, single-pass approaches, and introduces comprehensive evaluation framework with diverse generation settings and metrics.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation of 21 UQ methods across Qwen3, Llama 3.2, and DeepSeek-V3 on TriviaQA, GSM8K, and HumanEval shows: (1) UQ effectiveness varies by task and generation settings; (2) consensus-based methods (Deg and EigV) consistently outperform others; (3) larger model scales correlate with lower uncertainty estimates.
