Direct GRPO-style RLVR decreases GSM8K exact-match accuracy from 1.82% to 1.59% (192-token completions) and 1.21% (320-token completions). These single-run results identify a concrete failure mode rather than establishing a general lower bound on RLVR. The contribution is an auditable resource-constrained case study, not a state-of-the-art claim.

AI.tldr: A 135M-parameter language model trained on just 13B tokens using limited hardware (single L20 GPU), achieving 87% of the performance of similar models trained on 100x more data.
AI.motivation: To explore efficient language model training in severely resource-constrained environments - using minimal hardware (single GPU) and drastically reduced training data compared to standard approaches that use hundreds of billions to trillions of tokens.
AI.method: Trained 134.5M-parameter model on 13B tokens total: 10B FineWeb-Edu tokens + 3B educational/math/code/reasoning mixture. Used comprehensive data processing including cross-source MinHash/LSH deduplication, segment deduplication, and benchmark-overlap removal. Applied supervised fine-tuning with weight interpolation and reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards on GSM8K.
AI.result: Achieved mean score of 0.4150 on six-task evaluation harness (87.1% of SmolLM-135M's performance while using only 2.17% of its token count). Exceeded several older 100M-160M baselines. RLVR actually decreased GSM8K accuracy from 1.82% to 1.59% (192-token) and 1.21% (320-token).
AI.conclusion: This is primarily an auditable case study demonstrating that moderate performance can be achieved with extreme resource constraints, rather than a state-of-the-art claim. The RLVR experiment revealed a concrete failure mode, and the efficiency ratio (87% performance with 2% data) is descriptive but not evidence of statistical equivalence.

title: Plans Don't Persist: Why Context Management Is Load Bearing for LLM Agents
summary: Long-horizon agents depend on context management: systems compress, summarize, and evict old tokens so tasks can continue beyond finite windows. That is safe only when dropped information is no longer needed or has been internalized. Plans are the stress case: they are written early, used for many steps, and first to be evicted. We introduce replay pairing, a diagnostic that runs the same trajectory with and without the plan in history and measures hidden-state cosine distance. On Llama-3.1-70B, plan signal spikes to 0.453 one step after the plan, then falls 4.1x in a single action-observation step; HotpotQA falls 12.4x. This is evidence that standard LLM agents do not carry plans forward as persistent state, and instead depend on the plan remaining in context. A layer-L32 probe detects this decay as a diagnostic, not as proof that it reads plan content itself. Reasoning models add a measurement confound: their `<think>` traces re-derive plan content, so standard stripping leaves plan evidence in the stripped condition. We name this the reasoning-trace confound and fix it with strict stripping, which removes prior `<think>` blocks from the stripped run only. It recovers +163% of the step+1 signal in-sample and +153% held out, while not meaningfully changing non-reasoning Llama (+4.8%). On DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B, a Llama-trained probe transfers at AUROC 0.748 (p=6e-4), while R1-specific probes reach 1.000, suggesting R1 encodes plan signal in a different hidden-state direction. Finally, a compression stress test shows the practical cost: naive plan eviction cuts ALFWorld success by 34.7pp, while probe-gated re-surfacing does not recover it. The contribution is a measurement and stress-test framework showing that agent-critical information can be context-resident rather than persistent. Context management is load bearing, but plan protection alone is not enough.

AI.tldr: Researchers introduce "replay pairing" to test if LLM agents actually use plans as persistent internal state or just rely on them being in context; they find plans decay rapidly after eviction, showing agents depend on context-resident info rather than internalizing it.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon agents need to manage context by compressing/evicting old tokens, but this is only safe if dropped information is no longer needed or has been internalized. Plans are a critical stress case since they're written early, used many steps later, and are first to be evicted. The paper investigates whether standard LLM agents actually carry plans forward as persistent internal state or just depend on them remaining in context.
AI.method: Introduces "replay pairing": run the same trajectory with and without the plan in history, then measure hidden-state cosine distance between the two runs. Uses probes to detect plan signal decay. Addresses reasoning-trace confound with "strict stripping" that removes prior reasoning blocks from the stripped run only. Tests on Llama-3.1-70B, HotpotQA, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B, and ALFWorld.
AI.result: On Llama-3.1-70B, plan signal spikes to 0.453 one step after the plan, then falls 4.1x in a single action-observation step; HotpotQA falls 12.4x. This shows standard LLM agents don't carry plans as persistent state. Strict stripping recovers +163% of step+1 signal in-sample. On DeepSeek-R1, Llama-trained probes transfer at AUROC 0.748 while R1-specific probes reach 1.000, suggesting R1 encodes plan signal differently. Naive plan eviction cuts ALFWorld success by 34.7pp, and probe-gated re-surfacing doesn't recover it.
AI.conclusion: Agent-critical information like plans can be context-resident rather than persistent. Context management is load-bearing for long-horizon agents, but protecting plans alone isn't enough. The paper provides a measurement and stress-test framework for evaluating how agents handle information over extended sequences.

title: Neural Conjugate Aggregation: Identifiable Unsupervised Multi-Sensor Regression under Heterogeneous Sensor Bias
summary: We study regression-based data fusion under uncertainty, where multiple noisy and biased measurement sources are available but ground-truth labels are absent during training. This setting arises in sensor networks, simulation ensembles, and scientific monitoring systems where supervision is costly or infeasible. We propose the Neural Conjugate Aggregation Model (NCAM), a hierarchical Bayesian framework that combines neural networks with conjugate Gaussian inference for unsupervised multi-source fusion. NCAM learns source-specific bias and reliability conditioned on contextual covariates, yielding an analytically tractable posterior over a latent target variable with decomposed epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty. Structural non-identifiability is resolved through sensor anchoring and variance regularization, enabling stable and interpretable posterior aggregation. To complement Bayesian uncertainty with finite-sample guarantees, we integrate locally adaptive Monte Carlo conformal prediction, producing heteroscedastic prediction intervals with coverage guarantees under exchangeability assumptions. Experiments on synthetic and real-world air-quality datasets demonstrate improved predictive accuracy and well-calibrated uncertainty compared to unsupervised baselines, including mean aggregation, probabilistic PCA, and Kalman filtering.

AI.tldr: NCAM is a Bayesian neural network framework for unsupervised multi-source data fusion that learns source biases and reliability, providing analytical uncertainty decomposition with conformal prediction guarantees.
AI.motivation: In sensor networks, simulation ensembles, and scientific monitoring, ground-truth labels are often absent during training due to cost or infeasibility, creating need for unsupervised fusion of multiple noisy and biased measurement sources.
AI.method: Neural Conjugate Aggregation Model (NCAM) combines neural networks with conjugate Gaussian inference in a hierarchical Bayesian framework. It learns source-specific bias and reliability conditioned on covariates, uses sensor anchoring and variance regularization to resolve non-identifiability, and integrates locally adaptive Monte Carlo conformal prediction for finite-sample guarantees.
AI.result: NCAM demonstrates improved predictive accuracy and well-calibrated uncertainty on synthetic and real-world air-quality datasets compared to unsupervised baselines (mean aggregation, probabilistic PCA, Kalman filtering).
AI.conclusion: NCAM provides a principled framework for unsupervised multi-source fusion with interpretable uncertainty decomposition and finite-sample coverage guarantees, addressing practical challenges in sensor networks and scientific monitoring.

title: The Impact of VAE Design on Latent Pose Representations for Diffusion-based Sign Language Production
summary: Latent diffusion approaches to sign language production (SLP) rely on an initial stage that learns an encoding of sign pose sequences, enabling generative modeling in the resulting latent space. The autoencoder used in this stage is typically evaluated in terms of reconstruction quality using geometric metrics common in SLP. While informative, these metrics do not fully capture latent space properties that may influence the training and performance of the downstream generative model. In this work, we investigate how architectural and training objective design choices in a variational autoencoder (VAE) for sign pose encoding affect latent space structure, and how these differences translate into the performance of a latent diffusion model for text-to-sign generation. Our experiments on Phoenix14T dataset show that variations in generative performance, measured through back-translation BLEU scores, can sometimes be better explained by differences in latent space properties than by VAE reconstruction accuracy alone.

AI.tldr: Investigates how VAE architectural choices for sign pose encoding affect latent space properties and downstream generative performance in sign language production.
AI.motivation: Current latent diffusion approaches for SLP rely on initial pose encoding stages that are evaluated mainly on reconstruction quality. However, these geometric metrics don't fully capture latent space properties that affect downstream generative model training and performance.
AI.method: Examines architectural and training objective design choices in variational autoencoders for sign pose encoding, analyzing how they affect latent space structure. Connects these differences to performance of latent diffusion models for text-to-sign generation.
AI.result: Experiments on Phoenix14T dataset show that variations in generative performance (measured through back-translation BLEU scores) can sometimes be better explained by differences in latent space properties than by VAE reconstruction accuracy alone.
AI.conclusion: Latent space properties are important considerations for SLP systems beyond just reconstruction quality, as they significantly impact downstream generative model performance in text-to-sign generation tasks.

title: Sequential Minimal Optimization Algorithm for One-Class Support Vector Machines With Privileged Information
comment: 19 pages, 4 figures. Author-prepared preprint of the article published in IEEE Access. The published article is licensed under CC BY-NC-ND 4.0
summary: One of the powerful techniques in data modeling is accounting for features that are available at the training stage, but are not available when the trained model is used to classify or predict test data -- the Learning Using Privileged Information paradigm (LUPI). Sequential Minimal Optimization (SMO) methods have been developed for supervised Support Vector Machines (SVM), unsupervised one-class SVM, and SVM with privileged information (SVM+). The missing brick in this research has long been a one-class SVM with privileged information (OC-SVM+). In this paper, we propose an SMO algorithm for OC-SVM+ that significantly outperforms non-sequential algorithms for training the OC-SVM+ model. Its finite-time convergence is established. The experiments show how privileged information affects a descriptive domain in the space of original features. Comparative benchmark tests demonstrate that our algorithm is superior over interior point algorithms.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the first Sequential Minimal Optimization algorithm for one-class SVM with privileged information, significantly outperforming existing non-sequential training methods.
AI.motivation: While SMO methods exist for supervised SVM, unsupervised one-class SVM, and SVM with privileged information, there has been a missing component: a one-class SVM with privileged information (OC-SVM+) training algorithm. The Learning Using Privileged Information paradigm allows using features available only during training, which is a powerful modeling technique, but efficient training methods for OC-SVM+ were lacking.
AI.method: The authors propose a Sequential Minimal Optimization (SMO) algorithm specifically designed for training one-class SVM with privileged information (OC-SVM+). SMO is an efficient optimization method that breaks down large quadratic programming problems into smaller sub-problems that can be solved analytically.
AI.result: The proposed SMO algorithm significantly outperforms non-sequential algorithms for training OC-SVM+. The paper establishes finite-time convergence and shows through experiments how privileged information affects the descriptive domain in the original feature space. Benchmark tests demonstrate superiority over interior point algorithms.
AI.conclusion: This work successfully fills a long-standing gap in the research by providing the first efficient SMO algorithm for one-class SVM with privileged information, offering superior performance and convergence guarantees compared to existing optimization methods.

title: When Preferences Fail to Become Incentives: A Utility-Behavior Gap in Large Language Models
summary: Recent work on preference elicitation in large language models (LLMs) has demonstrated that, when given a series of choices between two outcomes, LLMs reveal a coherent, model-specific utility structure. Notably, this structure often includes preferences that the models' trainers did not intend, such as valuing people of some nationalities above others, raising the possibility that LLMs might be forming emergent, misaligned goals, which, if true, would have major safety implications. However, the choice paradigms in which these preferences are observed are not reflective of real-world situations in which misaligned behavior would be a practical concern. Therefore, we design an experimental paradigm to probe whether these preferences serve as motivations for LLM behavior in realistic scenarios. First, we reproduce prior findings on consistent preference elicitation. Next, we create a set of common writing tasks - essays, grant proposal abstracts, incident postmortems, and translations - where quality can be assessed by a blind, independent LLM judge panel. Then, we demonstrate that LLMs can be motivated via direct exhortation and other explicit cues to modulate their output quality on these tasks. Finally, we probe whether utilities inferred from explicitly reported preferences can shift output quality on these tasks by offering LLMs high-utility incentives for high-quality outputs. In all tasks, across all models tested, offering LLMs outcomes that they report in the choice paradigm as being highly preferred does not lead them to create higher quality outputs than offering them dispreferred outcomes, or even no outcomes at all. We conclude that the existence of coherent preferences as demonstrated in choice paradigms should not be taken as evidence that those preferences have incentive value for the models or affect their behavior in other contexts.

AI.tldr: LLMs show coherent preferences in choice tasks but these preferences don't actually motivate their behavior in practical writing tasks, suggesting preference elicitation doesn't indicate genuine goal formation.
AI.motivation: Previous research found LLMs reveal coherent preferences in choice paradigms, sometimes showing unintended biases (like favoring certain nationalities), raising safety concerns about emergent misaligned goals. However, these choice paradigms don't reflect real-world situations where misaligned behavior would be a practical concern.
AI.method: 1) Reproduce prior findings on consistent preference elicitation; 2) Create realistic writing tasks (essays, grant proposal abstracts, incident postmortems, translations) with quality assessed by blind LLM judge panels; 3) Test if LLMs can be motivated via direct exhortation to modulate output quality; 4) Probe whether utilities inferred from explicit preferences can shift output quality by offering high-utility incentives for high-quality outputs.
AI.result: In all tasks across all tested models, offering outcomes that LLMs report as highly preferred in choice paradigms does NOT lead to higher quality outputs than offering dispreferred outcomes or no outcomes at all. Preferences shown in choice tasks don't translate to behavioral motivation.
AI.conclusion: Coherent preferences demonstrated in choice paradigms should NOT be taken as evidence that those preferences have incentive value for LLMs or affect their behavior in other contexts. The existence of such preferences doesn't indicate the formation of genuine goals or motivations that would drive misaligned behavior in practical scenarios.

title: SamatNext v0.2-B: An Exploratory Study of RMS-Normalized Hybrid Decoders for Curriculum Retention in Small Code Models
comment: 12 pages, 3 tables. Technical report. Code and reproducibility artifacts: https://github.com/samat2003/samatnext-v0.1/tree/samatnext-v02-lsm-rmsnorm
summary: Standard autoregressive Transformer decoders can often exhibit substantial forgetting under sequential fine-tuning on shifting curriculum distributions. This technical report evaluates SamatNext v0.2-B, an experimental 356M-parameter hybrid sequence decoder that alternates Differential-Attention-style layers with DeltaNet-inspired simplified linear-state mixer layers using RMS normalization and output scale calibration. We study the model under a controlled staged Python code curriculum and compare it with a parameter-matched Transformer baseline. In this setting, SamatNext v0.2-B achieves a 100.0% pass rate on the controlled Stage 5 holdout while retaining 98.8% of adjacent Stage 3 semantic behavior and reaching 12.0% on the Stage 2E early syntax holdout. The strongest Transformer baseline reaches 97.6% on Stage 5 but retains only 6.0% of Stage 3 behavior. Both architectures remain weak on long-horizon early-stage retention, so the result should be interpreted as evidence of an altered retention/plasticity tradeoff in this controlled setting, not as a general solution to catastrophic forgetting. Code, model specifications, evaluation scripts, and result tables are provided for independent verification.

AI.tldr: SamatNext v0.2-B, a 356M-parameter hybrid decoder combining Differential-Attention with DeltaNet-inspired layers, shows improved retention of previous learning while maintaining performance on new tasks compared to standard Transformers in controlled Python code curriculum experiments.
AI.motivation: Standard autoregressive Transformer decoders suffer from substantial forgetting when fine-tuned sequentially on shifting curriculum distributions, known as catastrophic forgetting. The paper aims to explore architectural alternatives that can better balance retention of previous knowledge with plasticity for new learning.
AI.method: The paper introduces SamatNext v0.2-B, a hybrid sequence decoder architecture that alternates Differential-Attention-style layers with DeltaNet-inspired simplified linear-state mixer layers using RMS normalization and output scale calibration. The model is evaluated under a controlled staged Python code curriculum and compared against a parameter-matched Transformer baseline.
AI.result: In controlled experiments, SamatNext v0.2-B achieves 100.0% pass rate on Stage 5 holdout while retaining 98.8% of adjacent Stage 3 semantic behavior and reaching 12.0% on Stage 2E early syntax holdout. The Transformer baseline reaches 97.6% on Stage 5 but retains only 6.0% of Stage 3 behavior. Both architectures remain weak on long-horizon early-stage retention.
AI.conclusion: SamatNext v0.2-B demonstrates an altered retention/plasticity tradeoff compared to standard Transformers in this controlled setting, but this should not be interpreted as a general solution to catastrophic forgetting. The results suggest architectural modifications can influence forgetting behavior, though long-term retention challenges persist.

title: Joint Air Traffic Flow and Capacity Management via Answer Set Programming
comment: This is the version including the appendix/supplementary material
summary: Operational Air Traffic Flow and Capacity Management (ATFCM) balances flight demand with available sector capacity, to ensure safe and efficient operations. Mathematical models enhance operational ATFCM performance by framing demand-capacity balancing as an optimization problem, maximizing efficiency while adhering to safety constraints. However, SOTA research optimizes the aircraft trajectories (called ATFM) or the sector configuration (called DAC) separately. This leaves a research gap of whether joint optimization of ATFM and DAC can bring benefits. We partially address this limitation by introducing a joint ATFCM model with an encoding in Answer Set Programming (ASP). The ASP implementation is evaluated against two baselines applied to our joint model: a SOTA Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) model and an iterative CASA-based heuristic. Computational experiments utilize an instance generator fitted to historical OpenSky Network flight data. Our results indicate that the ASP model outperforms the MIP model, while ASP remains competitive against heuristics on small instances. Furthermore, while DAC has the largest improvement on solving performance compared to rerouting and delaying, unrestricted variants of DAC or rerouting lead to search space thrashing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a joint Air Traffic Flow Management (ATFM) and Dynamic Airspace Configuration (DAC) optimization model using Answer Set Programming (ASP), showing it outperforms MIP models and competes with heuristics on small instances.
AI.motivation: Current state-of-the-art research optimizes aircraft trajectories (ATFM) and sector configuration (DAC) separately, leaving a research gap in whether joint optimization could provide benefits. The authors aim to address this limitation by developing a joint ATFCM model.
AI.method: Developed a joint Air Traffic Flow and Capacity Management (ATFCM) model with an encoding in Answer Set Programming (ASP). The ASP implementation is evaluated against two baselines: a state-of-the-art Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) model and an iterative CASA-based heuristic. Computational experiments use an instance generator fitted to historical OpenSky Network flight data.
AI.result: The ASP model outperforms the MIP model and remains competitive against heuristics on small instances. DAC shows the largest improvement on solving performance compared to rerouting and delaying, but unrestricted variants of DAC or rerouting lead to search space thrashing.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of ATFM and DAC through ASP encoding shows promising results, with ASP outperforming traditional MIP approaches and demonstrating that DAC has the most significant impact on performance improvement, though careful constraints are needed to avoid search space issues.

title: On the Expressive Power of Weight Quantization in Large Language Models
summary: In recent years, weight quantization that encodes the learnable parameters of large language models in an $n$-bit format has garnered significant attention due to its potential for model compression and inference acceleration. Many practical techniques have been developed; however, the theoretical understanding of many aspects, especially the approximation and degradation of expressive power as the number of quantization bits decreases, remains unclear. In this paper, we provide a theoretical investigation into the expressive capability of large language models relative to the number of quantization bits. We argue that 1.58-bit is the limiting precision for weight quantization by establishing the universal approximation and expressive collapse properties of weight-quantized models with respect to the number of quantization bits. Additionally, we confirm that weight quantization leads to expressive degradation, in which the expressive capacity of weight-quantized models degrades polynomially as the number of quantization bits decreases. These theoretical findings provide a solid foundation for advancing weight quantization in the context of scaling laws and shed insights for future research in model compression and inference acceleration.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes that 1.58-bit is the limiting precision for weight quantization in LLMs, showing expressive power degrades polynomially as quantization bits decrease.
AI.motivation: While weight quantization for LLMs has gained attention for model compression and inference acceleration, there's insufficient theoretical understanding of how expressive power degrades with fewer quantization bits, especially regarding approximation capabilities.
AI.method: The paper conducts a theoretical investigation into expressive capability of large language models relative to quantization bits, establishing universal approximation and expressive collapse properties for weight-quantized models.
AI.result: The key findings are: 1) 1.58-bit is identified as the limiting precision for weight quantization, 2) Weight quantization causes expressive degradation where capacity degrades polynomially as quantization bits decrease.
AI.conclusion: These theoretical results provide a solid foundation for advancing weight quantization in scaling laws and offer insights for future research in model compression and inference acceleration.

title: A Stackelberg Framework for Resource-Aware LLM Agents: Learning, Repair, and Conditional Guarantees
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly operate as multi-turn systems that must allocate context, prompt verbosity, and tool access under finite computational budgets. Static thresholds are simple, but they are brittle under heterogeneous tasks and evolving session states. We formulate resource governance as a contextual Stackelberg game: a controller commits to a quality target and a cost incentive, while an executor responds with resource actions over context, prompting, and tool usage. We learn a conditional response model, optimize a leader policy against that model, and repair the resulting policy using real-API calibration and projection onto an empirically selected action set. For the restricted game, we establish conditional guarantees for equilibrium existence, follower-response stability, safe-set projection, and transfer from a surrogate environment to the real environment under bounded value error. The primary real-API experiment comprises 300 evaluated turns. Relative to a conservative baseline, the selected repaired controller reduces mean token cost by 17.4% (Welch $p=0.022$), while the measured quality difference is not statistically significant ($p=0.44$). The theoretical results are conditional and the experiments do not estimate their regret or transfer constants; consequently, the evidence establishes a promising repaired operating point, not a certified real-system equilibrium.

AI.tldr: LLM agents need dynamic resource management under computational constraints. The paper presents a game-theoretic approach where a controller sets quality targets and cost incentives, while an executor allocates resources, with experimental validation showing 17.4% cost reduction without significant quality loss.
AI.motivation: LLM agents operate as multi-turn systems that must manage context, prompt verbosity, and tool access under finite computational budgets. Static thresholds are brittle for heterogeneous tasks and evolving session states, necessitating more adaptive resource governance approaches.
AI.method: Formulate resource governance as a contextual Stackelberg game with a controller and executor. Learn a conditional response model, optimize leader policy against it, and repair the policy using real-API calibration and projection onto empirically selected action sets. Establish conditional theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation (300 turns) shows the repaired controller reduces mean token cost by 17.4% compared to conservative baseline (p=0.022), with no statistically significant quality difference (p=0.44). The approach provides a promising operating point but not certified equilibrium.
AI.conclusion: The game-theoretic framework offers a principled approach to adaptive resource governance for LLM agents, demonstrating significant cost savings without compromising quality. However, the guarantees are conditional and don't constitute full certification of real-system equilibrium performance.

authors: Sergio H. V. L. de Mattos
title: Evolving Spatial Weights for Cartographic Synthesis
summary: The integration of multiple thematic data layers into a single composite map, known as the cartographic synthesis problem, is typically addressed through expert-driven weighting schemes. This study presents a multi-objective formulation of cartographic synthesis grounded in spatial autocorrelation structure. We develop a bi-objective evolutionary framework, GIS-moGA, that estimates layer weights by simultaneously maximizing global spatial structure, measured by Global Moran's I, and minimizing local spatial heterogeneity, measured by the variance of Local Indicators of Spatial Association (LISA). Because naive evaluation of spatial relationships requires O(N^2) operations, direct computation becomes impractical for larger datasets. We address this challenge by exploiting the 97.7% sparsity of queen contiguity matrices, reducing effective complexity to O(N k) and enabling scalable municipal-level analysis. The framework is evaluated on a high-dimensional spatial epidemiology dataset with N = 523 units from Araraquara, Brazil. A 64-scenario experimental design is used to examine evolutionary behavior across parameter settings. Results show that higher mutation rates are important for maintaining population diversity and preventing premature convergence in spatially autocorrelated fitness landscapes, where crossover operators can disrupt geographically coherent structures. Compared with expert-derived Analytic Hierarchy Process baselines, the resulting Pareto fronts show substantial hypervolume gains and significant improvements in spatial coherence (p < 0.001, Cliff's delta = 0.87). These findings provide a systematic and scalable framework for data-driven geographic multi-criteria decision analysis.

AI.tldr: A multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (GIS-moGA) optimizes cartographic synthesis by maximizing spatial autocorrelation while minimizing local heterogeneity, enabling scalable data-driven map integration.
AI.motivation: Current cartographic synthesis methods rely on expert-driven weighting schemes, which lack systematic, data-driven approaches for integrating multiple thematic layers. There's a need for scalable methods that can handle large spatial datasets while optimizing spatial coherence.
AI.method: Developed GIS-moGA, a bi-objective evolutionary framework that simultaneously maximizes global spatial structure (using Global Moran's I) and minimizes local spatial heterogeneity (using LISA variance). Addressed computational complexity by exploiting sparsity in queen contiguity matrices (97.7% sparse), reducing complexity from O(N²) to O(N k). Tested on spatial epidemiology dataset with 523 units in Araraquara, Brazil, using 64-scenario experimental design to examine evolutionary behavior.
AI.result: Higher mutation rates were crucial for maintaining diversity and preventing premature convergence in spatially autocorrelated landscapes. Pareto fronts showed substantial hypervolume gains and significant improvements in spatial coherence (p < 0.001, Cliff's delta = 0.87) compared to expert-derived Analytic Hierarchy Process baselines. Framework successfully handled high-dimensional datasets at municipal level.
AI.conclusion: The GIS-moGA framework provides a systematic, scalable solution for data-driven geographic multi-criteria decision analysis, outperforming traditional expert-driven methods and enabling more objective cartographic synthesis while handling large spatial datasets efficiently.

title: From numerical proportions to analogical proportions between probabilities
summary: Analogical proportions link four items a, b, c, d by a relation stating that ``a is to b as c is to d", a, b, c, d being the formal representation of real world entities, ranging from simple numerical values to more complex structures such as profiles. Accordingly, $a, b, c, d$ could be atomic values like Boolean, nominal or numerical values, more generally vectors of such values, or even families of items represented by logical formulas. In this paper, we consider another representation setting, which is the probabilistic one. Precisely, the article proposes a study of {analogical} proportions between probabilities, whether they are simply between probability values, or between distributions (which requires the preservation of their normalization). More particularly, we study the properties of definitions based on arithmetic proportion, or on a combination of the former with geometric proportion, while other options are also discussed. Previous works have shown that when four profiles a, b, c, d, represented as vectors, form analogical proportions componentwise, it is likely that their classes form an analogical proportion also. This is the basis of an analogical proportion-based classification method that can produce accurate predictions. Similarly, in this paper, each profile is associated with a distribution describing the frequencies of the possible values of a discrete attribute of interest. We then discuss and experimentally investigate if the distributions associated to four profiles forming an analogical proportion themselves also form an analogical proportion.

AI.tldr: This paper extends analogical proportion theory from deterministic values to probabilistic representations, studying how probability distributions can form analogical proportions and investigating whether distributions associated with analogical profiles themselves maintain analogical relationships.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by extending analogical proportion reasoning from deterministic representations (like Boolean, nominal, or numerical values) to probabilistic settings. While previous work has shown that when vectors form analogical proportions componentwise, their classes likely do too, this research aims to explore whether similar relationships hold when dealing with probability distributions instead of deterministic values.
AI.method: The paper studies analogical proportions between probabilities, focusing on definitions based on arithmetic proportion and combinations with geometric proportion. It examines how probability distributions (which require preservation of normalization) can form analogical proportions, building on the componentwise approach used for vector-based profiles. The research investigates experimentally whether distributions associated with profiles that form analogical proportions themselves also form analogical proportions.
AI.result: While the abstract doesn't present specific numerical results, it indicates the paper discusses various definitions of analogical proportions for probabilities and experimentally investigates the relationships between distributions associated with analogical profiles. The research likely provides insights into how analogical reasoning can be extended to probabilistic settings.
AI.conclusion: The paper contributes to expanding analogical proportion theory into probabilistic domains, showing how this reasoning framework can be applied to probability values and distributions, potentially enabling analogical proportion-based classification methods in probabilistic settings similar to those used in deterministic vector-based approaches.

title: From Handcrafted Features to Functional Edge Learning: Evolution of EEG Seizure Detection Frameworks
summary: Electroencephalogram (EEG) analysis remains the clinical gold standard for epilepsy diagnosis and seizure detection. While Deep Learning (DL) has significantly advanced automated EEG interpretation, its transition from controlled experimental settings to routine clinical deployment is severely bottlenecked by fundamental architectural flaws. Standard DL models operate as opaque black-boxes lacking clinical interpretability, demand massive amounts of balanced annotated data, and incur steep computational costs incompatible with resource-constrained wearable or implantable neuromodulation devices. This paper presents a comprehensive review of these prevailing limitations and explores Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) as a emerging paradigm for EEG-based seizure detection. By replacing the fixed activation functions of traditional neurons with flexible, learnable functions along the network's connections, KANs bridge the critical gap between predictive accuracy and mathematical transparency. We systematically analyze how KAN architectures resolve the shortcomings of traditional DL-based models by offering exceptional parameter efficiency, inherent interpretability for physician trust, and robust performance under data scarcity. Ultimately, this review establishes KANs not merely as an incremental algorithmic update, but as a fundamental paradigm shift necessary to actualize next-generation, patient-specific, and thoroughly transparent clinical EEG monitoring systems.

AI.tldr: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) offer a paradigm shift for EEG seizure detection by replacing traditional black-box deep learning with interpretable, parameter-efficient networks that enable clinical deployment and physician trust.
AI.motivation: Deep learning for EEG analysis faces critical barriers in clinical deployment: lack of interpretability (black-box models), high data requirements (needs massive balanced annotated data), and high computational costs incompatible with wearable/neurostimulation devices.
AI.method: Comprehensive review of deep learning limitations for EEG seizure detection and exploration of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) as an alternative paradigm. KANs replace fixed activation functions in traditional neurons with flexible, learnable functions along network connections.
AI.result: KANs bridge the gap between predictive accuracy and mathematical transparency, offering exceptional parameter efficiency, inherent interpretability for physician trust, and robust performance under data scarcity conditions.
AI.conclusion: KANs represent not just an incremental algorithmic improvement but a fundamental paradigm shift necessary for realizing next-generation, patient-specific, and transparent clinical EEG monitoring systems for epilepsy diagnosis and seizure detection.

title: IPO Finance Agent: Evaluation of LLM Financial Analysts beyond Finance Agent v2, with Automated Rubric Generation -- the Case of the SpaceX (SPCX) IPO
summary: Finance Agent v2 (by Vals AI) has emerged as the reference benchmark for evaluating both Anthropic Claude and OpenAI ChatGPT frontier language models on financial tasks. However, it narrowly deals with periodic reporting from publicly traded companies (SEC 10-K and 10-Q filings), and its agentic harness relies on naive, unenriched chunk retrieval. Neither the task design nor the retrieval approach addresses the distinct challenges of IPO due diligence. SEC S-1 filings combine historical financial statements, governance structures, pro forma and common-control accounting treatments, capital-formation narratives, and underwriting-sensitive risk disclosures within substantially longer documents than typical periodic filings. That is why we introduce IPO Finance Agent, which extends the Finance Agent v2 framework along two directions: task domain and retrieval architecture. During our experiments, the original Finance Agent v2 harness basically failed to deliver any output related to the SpaceX S-1 filing, due to document length. We therefore had to improve the agentic harness with contextual retrieval, a more realistic and industry-standard approach for long documents. We also built a dataset of 1,000 IPO-diligence questions, and publicly release 70 questions on the SpaceX (SPCX) S-1 filing to support reproducibility, while the remainder are held private to guard against benchmark contamination. In addition, we introduce an evaluator-optimizer pipeline to automatically generate evaluation rubrics for the benchmark: candidate facts are extracted from an ensemble of independently-generated model answers to each question, consolidated into draft criteria, then automatically audited for omissions, hallucinations, mistiered items, and redundancy, with LLM feedback driving iterative repair, targeted enrichment, and deduplication. Human experts only review final rubrics before deployment. Results show that the best-performing evaluated model, Alibaba Qwen 3.7 Max, reaches 79.4% accuracy at $0.30 per query, and the most cost-efficient model on the resulting Pareto frontier, Xiaomi MiMo-2.5 Pro, reaches slightly lower accuracy (76.8%) at $0.05 per query. Both exceed the current Finance Agent v2 leaderboard ceiling-Google Gemini 3.5 Flash at 57.9% for $2.51 per querywhile undercutting even FABv2's cheapest entry (MiniMax M3: 48.3% at $0.32) on cost-efficiency. Code and data are released on GitHub: https://github.com/benstaf/ipoagent

AI.tldr: IPO Finance Agent extends Finance Agent v2 with IPO-specific due diligence tasks and improved contextual retrieval for long S-1 filings, achieving better accuracy and lower cost than existing benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Finance Agent v2 focuses only on periodic SEC filings (10-K/10-Q) and uses naive chunk retrieval, which doesn't address the unique challenges of IPO due diligence. SEC S-1 filings are much longer documents combining financial data, governance, risk disclosures, and capital formation narratives, requiring specialized approaches.
AI.method: 1) Extends Finance Agent v2 framework for IPO domain with 1,000 IPO-diligence questions (70 publicly released SpaceX questions); 2) Improves retrieval with contextual retrieval for long documents; 3) Introduces evaluator-optimizer pipeline for automatic rubric generation using LLM feedback loops; 4) Human experts review final rubrics.
AI.result: Best model (Alibaba Qwen 3.7 Max) reaches 79.4% accuracy at $0.30/query; most cost-efficient (Xiaomi MiMo-2.5 Pro) reaches 76.8% accuracy at $0.05/query. Both outperform Finance Agent v2's best (Google Gemini 3.5 Flash at 57.9% accuracy for $2.51/query) and cheapest entry (48.3% at $0.32/query).
AI.conclusion: IPO Finance Agent successfully addresses limitations of existing financial AI benchmarks by specializing in IPO due diligence, improving retrieval for long documents, and developing efficient evaluation methods, achieving superior performance and cost-effectiveness for financial AI systems.

title: Learning a Normal World Model for Few-Shot Boundary-Calibrated Abnormality Detection
summary: Abnormality detection in complex systems faces two practical barriers: abnormal labels are scarce, and binary labels do not quantify how far an event has departed from normal behavior. We study a normal-world modeling formulation for this setting. Instead of learning a large and incomplete space of abnormal classes, the model learns the normal world from abundant normal events and uses a few abnormal examples only to calibrate the boundary of normality. We instantiate this idea as a Hypergraph Entropic Normal-World Model. The model represents multivariate sensor windows as context-conditioned hypergraphs, where hyperedges capture high-order relations among groups of variables. It then defines abnormality by an entropy-aware normal-world energy that combines temporal prediction surprise, hypergraph consistency surprise, and latent normal-manifold departure. On the NASA C-MAPSS turbofan degradation benchmark, the proposed full energy achieves strong zero-shot and few-shot performance across all four subsets and reaches AUROC 0.9983 on FD004, the most complex setting with multiple operating conditions and fault modes. Beyond standard detection metrics, we introduce mechanistic validation tests to probe whether the energy encodes normal-world structure rather than a superficial input-output mapping. The learned energy accepts unseen healthy engines, increases along degradation trajectories, and sharply penalizes context-mismatched cross-variable coupling breaks. These results suggest that normal-world energy can serve as an anomaly score, a graded risk measure, and a testable representation of normal system behavior under severe abnormal-label scarcity.

AI.tldr: A hypergraph-based normal-world model that learns from abundant normal data, uses few abnormal examples only for boundary calibration, and defines abnormality through entropy-aware energy combining temporal prediction surprise, hypergraph consistency, and manifold departure.
AI.motivation: Address two practical barriers in complex system abnormality detection: scarcity of abnormal labels and limitation of binary labels that don't quantify departure from normal behavior. Need for approaches that can work with severe abnormal-label scarcity.
AI.method: Hypergraph Entropic Normal-World Model representing multivariate sensor windows as context-conditioned hypergraphs (hyperedges capture high-order relations). Defines abnormality through entropy-aware normal-world energy combining three components: temporal prediction surprise, hypergraph consistency surprise, and latent normal-manifold departure.
AI.result: Achieves strong zero-shot and few-shot performance on NASA C-MAPSS turbofan degradation benchmark, reaching AUROC 0.9983 on FD004 (most complex setting with multiple operating conditions/fault modes). Learned energy accepts unseen healthy engines, increases along degradation trajectories, and penalizes context-mismatched coupling breaks.
AI.conclusion: Normal-world energy can serve as multi-functional tool: anomaly score, graded risk measure, and testable representation of normal system behavior under severe abnormal-label scarcity. The approach successfully models normal-world structure rather than superficial input-output mapping.

title: Some Results about the Expressivity of Preference-Incomplete Structured Argumentation Frameworks
summary: This paper studies the expressive power of ASPIC$^+$ argumentation frameworks with uncertain preference profiles by comparing them with several abstract formalisms with uncertain defeats. Most of our results are negative (and some of them are theoretically unexpected). We also conjecture a positive, non-trivial threshold for the expressivity of uncertain preferences, and prove some essential preliminary steps toward the confirmation of this conjecture.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the expressive power of ASPIC⁺ argumentation frameworks with uncertain preference profiles, finding mostly negative results when compared to abstract formalisms with uncertain defeats, and proposes a positive expressivity threshold conjecture.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the expressive capabilities of argumentation frameworks that incorporate uncertainty in preference relations (uncertain preference profiles) within the ASPIC⁺ formalism, particularly in comparison to other abstract formalisms that handle uncertain defeats.
AI.method: The method involves comparative analysis between ASPIC⁺ argumentation frameworks with uncertain preference profiles and several abstract formalisms with uncertain defeats, examining their expressive power through theoretical investigation.
AI.result: Most results are negative (and some theoretically unexpected), indicating limitations in the expressive power. The paper also conjectures a positive, non-trivial threshold for expressivity of uncertain preferences and proves preliminary steps toward confirming this conjecture.
AI.conclusion: ASPIC⁺ frameworks with uncertain preference profiles have limited expressive power compared to abstract formalisms with uncertain defeats, but there is potential for a positive expressivity threshold that requires further investigation.

title: SCENIC: Semantic-Conditioned Edge-Aware Neural Framework for Structured IoT Command Generation
summary: Edge Internet of Things (IoT) agents are often constrained by memory capacity, privacy requirements, communication latency, and recurring inference cost. Current smart-home assistants commonly rely on API-level command interfaces or cloud-based language models that remain difficult to deploy on edge devices. This paper addresses edge IoT command generation as a many-to-one structured output task, where multiple natural-language instructions map to the same canonical command string for deterministic smart-home parsing. To support this setting, we propose Semantic-Conditioned Edge-Aware Neural Framework for Structured IoT Command Generation (SCENIC), an end-to-end framework covering model architecture selection, Smart Home Instruct data generation, triplet-loss contrastive supervised fine-tuning, pruning and quantization, and deployment-oriented export. We evaluate sub-0.2B-scale transformer backbones, which are, to the best of our knowledge, among the smallest language-model backbones studied for edge IoT structured command generation. On Smart Home Instruct-Bench, the strongest dense decoder-only row reaches 99.0% EM@1, while the encoder-decoder model retains stronger high-sparsity behavior. A representative pruned INT8 encoder-decoder export preserves 91.0% EM@1 and 99.0% EM@5 while reducing exported model size by 25.38%. TensorRT profiling of the NVIDIA 2:4 sparse encoder export further shows up to 1.8x encoder-component speedup, indicating that the selected encoder-decoder deployment path can retain structured command accuracy under edge-oriented compression while hardware acceleration evidence remains component-level. The SCENIC code and experimental artifacts are open sourced to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: SCENIC is an edge-optimized framework for generating structured IoT commands from natural language using sub-0.2B transformers, achieving 99% exact match accuracy with compression techniques for efficient edge deployment.
AI.motivation: Edge IoT devices face constraints like limited memory, privacy requirements, latency issues, and cost concerns. Current smart-home assistants use cloud-based models or APIs that are unsuitable for edge deployment due to these constraints.
AI.method: Proposes SCENIC framework with: 1) Model architecture selection of sub-0.2B transformers, 2) Smart Home Instruct data generation, 3) Triplet-loss contrastive supervised fine-tuning, 4) Pruning and quantization techniques, and 5) Deployment-oriented export for edge devices.
AI.result: Dense decoder-only models achieve 99.0% EM@1 on Smart Home Instruct-Bench, with encoder-decoder models showing better sparsity behavior. Pruned INT8 encoder-decoder exports maintain 91.0% EM@1 while reducing model size by 25.38%. NVIDIA 2:4 sparse exports show 1.8x encoder speedup with preserved accuracy.
AI.conclusion: SCENIC demonstrates that encoder-decoder models with edge-oriented compression can maintain structured command accuracy for IoT deployments while supporting hardware acceleration. The framework is open-sourced to support reproducibility in edge IoT applications.

title: Cognitive Digital Twins: Ethical Risks and Governance for AI Systems That Model the Mind
summary: As AI systems become increasingly persistent and personalized, they make possible a class of technologies that we call cognitive digital twins (CDTs): dynamic computational representations of a specific person's cognition, updated from behavioral, contextual, or physiological data in order to model, predict, or simulate that person's cognition, or to act as that person's communicative or decision-making proxy. CDTs combine cognitive inference with longitudinal representation, simulation, and proxy action in ways that existing governance strategies for personal assistants, autonomous agents, recommender systems, and automated decision systems only partially address. This paper makes four contributions. First, we define CDTs and distinguish them from adjacent systems. Second, we introduce a 5A governance framework organized around authority, autonomy, access and control, accountability, and availability. Third, we identify CDT-specific risks, from misrepresentation and epistemic authority shifts to shadow twins, simulated participation, proxy action, and proxy-power asymmetries. Fourth, we analyze governance gaps and propose requirements for high-risk CDTs that strengthen consent, purpose limitation, validity, traceability, contestation, independent review, and model retirement. Existing frameworks primarily regulate data processing, automated decisions, or autonomous actions; CDTs also require governance at the level of cognitive representation itself, before any final decision or external action occurs. We argue that CDTs require governance not only because they can act for people, but because they can become infrastructures through which cognition is represented, simulated, classified, and operationalized.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Cognitive Digital Twins (CDTs) - computational representations of individual cognition - and proposes a governance framework to address their unique risks, including misrepresentation, proxy action, and epistemic authority issues that existing regulations don't fully cover.
AI.motivation: As AI systems become more persistent and personalized, they enable CDTs that can model, predict, or simulate individual cognition and act as decision-making proxies. Existing governance strategies for personal assistants, recommender systems, and automated decision systems only partially address the unique challenges posed by CDTs.
AI.method: The paper defines CDTs, distinguishes them from adjacent systems, introduces a 5A governance framework (authority, autonomy, access and control, accountability, availability), identifies CDT-specific risks, analyzes governance gaps, and proposes requirements for high-risk CDTs.
AI.result: Identifies specific CDT risks including misrepresentation, epistemic authority shifts, shadow twins, simulated participation, proxy action, and proxy-power asymmetries. Proposes governance requirements strengthening consent, purpose limitation, validity, traceability, contestation, independent review, and model retirement.
AI.conclusion: CDTs require governance not only because they can act for people, but because they become infrastructures through which cognition is represented, simulated, classified, and operationalized. Governance must occur at the level of cognitive representation itself, not just at data processing or final decision stages.

title: Encoder-Decoder Manifold Alignment for Idempotent Generation
summary: Recently, several learning paradigms have been introduced to enforce idempotency in generative models. The goal is to ensure that repeated application of a model leaves samples unchanged once they lie on the target data manifold. In practice, however, many of these approaches fail to achieve exact fixed points, leading to instability and drift under repeated application. In this work, we argue that a key reason for this failure is a geometric mismatch between the manifolds learned by the encoder and decoder. The encoder projects inputs onto one latent manifold, while the decoder implicitly learns to reconstruct data from a different manifold. This discrepancy prevents the model from learning truly idempotent mappings. To address this issue, we propose a new training framework that explicitly closes this gap by forcing the encoder and decoder to learn consistent representations of the same underlying data manifold. By aligning the geometry of these components, our method encourages stable projections. Empirically, we show that our approach achieves significantly lower idempotency error and consistently regenerates identical outputs under repeated application, compared to existing methods. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework on both image generation and image editing tasks. Finally, we show that enforcing idempotency in this manner improves identity preservation and information stability, leading to more realistic and controllable generative editing models.

AI.tldr: A novel training framework that aligns encoder and decoder manifolds to achieve exact idempotency in generative models, preventing drift and enabling stable repeated applications.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to enforce idempotency in generative models fail to achieve exact fixed points, leading to instability and drift under repeated application. The authors identify that this failure stems from a geometric mismatch between the manifolds learned by encoder and decoder components.
AI.method: Proposes a training framework that explicitly closes the geometric gap between encoder and decoder by forcing them to learn consistent representations of the same underlying data manifold. This alignment ensures the encoder and decoder operate on geometrically consistent representations.
AI.result: Achieves significantly lower idempotency error compared to existing methods, consistently regenerates identical outputs under repeated application, and demonstrates effectiveness on both image generation and editing tasks. Shows improved identity preservation and information stability.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly aligning encoder and decoder manifolds enables truly idempotent generative models, leading to more realistic and controllable generative editing with stable projections and improved identity preservation under repeated application.

title: TTFT-Aware Graph Chain-of-Thought:Distance-Indexed Neural A* for Low-Hallucination Multi-Hop Medical Reasoning
summary: Hallucinations and opaque reasoning remain unacceptable failure modes for clinical LLMs. We present a production-grade GraphRAG stack that constrains answers to verifiable graph chain-of-thought paths in a heterogeneous, ~700K-node medical knowledge graph powering a fertility assistant. The core idea is targeted navigation: a directed Pruned Landmark Labeling (PLL) oracle provides exact distances for sub-millisecond feasibility checks and simple-path enumeration, while a lightweight AStarNet heuristic operates strictly within the PLL corridor to prioritize clinically plausible expansions. We score and pack a small, diverse set of paths (CUI/semantic-type overlap, length prior, provenance priors) to condition generation, yielding compact prompts and improved Time to First Token (TTFT). On fertility-focused queries, the hybrid (PLL+AStarNet) establishes a better latency/recall Pareto frontier than text-only RAG and single-component baselines, lowers TTFT, and reduces clinician-audited hallucinations while preserving explanation clarity. The result is a practical recipe for explainable, low-hallucination multi-hop medical reasoning ready for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: Production-grade GraphRAG system for medical LLMs that reduces hallucinations by constraining answers to verifiable graph chain-of-thought paths in a large medical knowledge graph, achieving better latency/recall than text-only RAG.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations and opaque reasoning in clinical LLMs remain unacceptable failure modes that need to be addressed for safe medical applications.
AI.method: Hybrid approach using targeted navigation: Pruned Landmark Labeling (PLL) oracle for exact distance calculations and feasibility checks, plus lightweight AStarNet heuristic operating within PLL corridor for clinically plausible expansions. Scores and packs diverse path sets for generation conditioning.
AI.result: On fertility-focused queries: better latency/recall Pareto frontier than text-only RAG and single-component baselines, lower Time to First Token (TTFT), reduced clinician-audited hallucinations while preserving explanation clarity.
AI.conclusion: Practical recipe for explainable, low-hallucination multi-hop medical reasoning ready for real-world deployment, using a production-grade GraphRAG stack with 700K-node medical knowledge graph.

title: Enhancing Protein Representation Learning via Manifold Restore Mixing
summary: Data augmentation (DA) has been proven to be an effective means for improving protein representation learning (PRL) by generating additional training samples. Although mainstream perturbation- and sampling-based augmentation methods can produce data containing sufficient variations, they carry the risk of disrupting the protein structure and function. Some crafted protein homology modeling tools can generate conformations, but reduce structural diversity. The above dilemmas lead us to a question: Can we restore the disrupted structure caused by DA operations, providing data with both the original structure and diverse variations? In this work, we first analyze and empirically reveal the structure defect and performance degradation issues of existing DA methods. Based on the findings, we propose a simple yet effective DA method, Manifold Restore Mixing (MRM), for protein representation learning. Specifically, inspired by manifold mixup, we mix the hidden representations of original and augmented protein data to generate new samples that restore structural information lost in DA while introducing diverse variations. Furthermore, we develop a sample difficulty scheduler that adjusts the beta distribution in mixup to provide models with progressively challenging mixed samples during training, which improves the final performance. Comprehensive experiments on various PRL backbones and downstream tasks demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization of our method. The complete code and weights will be released upon acceptance. We provide a implementation at https://github.com/KingGugu/MRM.

AI.tldr: MRM is a data augmentation method for protein representation learning that mixes hidden representations of original and augmented data to restore structural information while introducing diverse variations.
AI.motivation: Existing data augmentation methods for protein representation learning either disrupt protein structure/function or reduce structural diversity, creating a need for methods that preserve original structure while introducing diverse variations.
AI.method: Proposes Manifold Restore Mixing (MRM) which mixes hidden representations of original and augmented protein data, along with a sample difficulty scheduler that adjusts beta distribution to provide progressively challenging samples during training.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on various PRL backbones and downstream tasks demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization of MRM.
AI.conclusion: MRM provides a simple yet effective solution to the structure disruption problem in protein data augmentation, improving protein representation learning performance while maintaining structural integrity.

title: A Matter of Time: Towards a General Theory of Agency
summary: Agency is often invoked in research on philosophy, biology, and cognitive science without a clear account of how it originates from material organization. Building on temporally parametrized (F, A)-systems, this paper develops a graded organizational theory of agency grounded in relational biology, physical biosemiotics, and process ontology. We argue that self-referential closure cannot be adequately conceived outside time: once the constitutive processes of a semantically closed organization are associated with distinct characteristic timescales, the organization unfolds into an out-of-sync dependency structure that can be formally redescribed as a history-dependent, revisable Asynchronous Dynamic Bayesian Network. This move allows for a principled distinction between autonomy, goal-directedness, agency, and open-endedness. Autonomy arises from precarious closure to efficient causation under material openness; goal-directedness from the maintenance of viability-supporting organization; agency appears when such organization acquires an endogenous anticipatory structure that selectively modulates organism-environment coupling in light of possible futures; open-endedness begins when this anticipatory organization can reconstruct its own future space of possibilities. Our framework reconciles Rosennean anticipation with organizational closure, restricts Markov blankets and active inference to derived formal redescriptions rather than first principles, and reinterprets computational enactivism in non-Fristonian terms. By deriving weaker temporalized organizations, our contribution outlines a hierarchy from proto-agential chemical systems to fully semantically closed agents, with implications for multicellular organisms, synthetic lifeforms, and neuroscience.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a graded organizational theory of agency using temporally parametrized (F, A)-systems, arguing that agency emerges from out-of-sync dependency structures in semantically closed organizations that can be redescribed as Asynchronous Dynamic Bayesian Networks.
AI.motivation: Agency is frequently invoked across philosophy, biology, and cognitive science without clear explanations of how it originates from material organization. There's a need for a principled account that distinguishes autonomy, goal-directedness, agency, and open-endedness, and reconciles different theoretical approaches.
AI.method: The paper builds on temporally parametrized (F, A)-systems from relational biology and develops a graded organizational theory grounded in physical biosemiotics and process ontology. It demonstrates how self-referential closure with distinct characteristic timescales creates out-of-sync dependency structures that can be formally redescribed as history-dependent, revisable Asynchronous Dynamic Bayesian Networks.
AI.result: The framework provides principled distinctions between autonomy (precarious closure to efficient causation), goal-directedness (maintenance of viability-supporting organization), agency (endogenous anticipatory structure modulating organism-environment coupling), and open-endedness (reconstruction of future possibilities). It reconciles Rosennean anticipation with organizational closure and restricts Markov blankets and active inference to derived formal redescriptions.
AI.conclusion: By deriving weaker temporalized organizations, the paper outlines a hierarchy from proto-agential chemical systems to fully semantically closed agents, with implications for understanding multicellular organisms, synthetic lifeforms, and neuroscience, while providing a non-Fristonian reinterpretation of computational enactivism.

title: Diffusion Integrated Gradients: Controllable Path Generation for Flexible Feature Attribution
comment: 44 pages, 22 figures, 10 tables. Accepted to ECCV 2026; includes appendix
summary: Path-based attribution methods such as Integrated Gradients (IG) are widely adopted for their strong axiomatic properties and effectiveness in attributing model predictions to input features by integrating gradients along a path from a baseline to the input. However, the choice of the attribution path largely affects the quality of explanations, and existing approaches rely on fixed or hand-crafted paths that often produce noisy or distorted attributions. To address this limitation, we propose Diffusion Integrated Gradients (DiffIG), a novel method that reformulates path generation as a conditional generative modeling problem. DiffIG first trains a diffusion model to learn a distribution over paths generated from a Stick-Breaking Process, then employs guided sampling to embed user guidance during the sampling procedure. We demonstrate that DiffIG quantitatively matches or outperforms existing path-based methods, achieving perceptually aligned explanations. This work introduces a new generative perspective for flexible, inference-time controllable Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) methods.

AI.tldr: Diffusion Integrated Gradients (DiffIG) reformulates attribution path generation as a conditional generative modeling problem using diffusion models, enabling flexible and controllable explanations that outperform existing path-based attribution methods.
AI.motivation: Current path-based attribution methods like Integrated Gradients rely on fixed or hand-crafted paths that often produce noisy or distorted attributions. The choice of attribution path significantly affects explanation quality, but existing approaches lack flexibility and optimal path selection mechanisms.
AI.method: DiffIG trains a diffusion model to learn a distribution over paths generated from a Stick-Breaking Process, then uses guided sampling to embed user guidance during the sampling procedure. This reformulates path generation as a conditional generative modeling problem rather than using predetermined paths.
AI.result: DiffIG quantitatively matches or outperforms existing path-based attribution methods and achieves perceptually aligned explanations. The method provides flexible, inference-time controllable explanations that overcome the limitations of fixed path approaches.
AI.conclusion: The work introduces a new generative perspective for Explainable AI methods, demonstrating that treating attribution path generation as a conditional generative modeling problem enables more effective and controllable explanations than traditional fixed-path approaches.

title: Managing Procedural Memory in LLM Agents: Control, Adaptation, and Evaluation
summary: Procedural memory is increasingly used to improve LLM agents on recurring workplace tasks, yet its ability to produce reusable skills remains poorly understood. We introduce AFTER, a benchmark of 382 realistic enterprise tasks spanning six professional roles and 22 procedural skills, designed to evaluate how skills transfer across tasks, roles, and model backbones. The benchmark includes controlled evaluation settings for local improvement, cross-task transfer, cross-role transfer, and cross-model generalization. Experiments show that procedural memory delivers consistent gains in industrial workflows: a single refinement round improves aggregate performance by 3.7-6.7 points, while skills evolved from diverse multi-model execution traces achieve 73.1% cross-model test accuracy, outperforming all single-model trace sources. We further find that some skills generalize broadly across tasks and models, whereas others become specialized to role-specific workflows and lose effectiveness under transfer. These results provide practical guidance for building, evaluating, and deploying procedural memory systems in production agent platforms.

AI.tldr: AFTER benchmark evaluates procedural memory transfer across enterprise tasks, showing skill generalization patterns and practical gains for industrial LLM agents.
AI.motivation: Procedural memory is widely used to improve LLM agents on recurring workplace tasks, but its ability to produce reusable skills that transfer across different contexts remains poorly understood and needs systematic evaluation.
AI.method: Created AFTER benchmark with 382 realistic enterprise tasks spanning six professional roles and 22 procedural skills, with controlled evaluation settings for local improvement, cross-task transfer, cross-role transfer, and cross-model generalization.
AI.result: Procedural memory delivers consistent gains: single refinement improves performance by 3.7-6.7 points, skills from diverse multi-model execution traces achieve 73.1% cross-model test accuracy (outperforming single-model sources), with skills showing different generalization patterns - some broad, some role-specialized.
AI.conclusion: The research provides practical guidance for building, evaluating, and deploying procedural memory systems in production agent platforms, demonstrating both the potential and limitations of skill transfer in industrial workflows.

title: Curriculum Reinforcement Learning Can Incentivize Reasoning Capacity in LLMs Beyond the Base Model
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is widely viewed as a promising path toward continuously improving large language models. Recent works, however, suggest that mainstream RLVR often reallocates sampling probabilities among trajectories already present in the base model: it can improve sampling efficiency, reflected by higher pass@1 scores, but yields limited gains, and can even decrease pass@k scores when k is large, and therefore may fail to expand the base model's reasoning capacity boundary. In this paper, we present a boundary-aware Curriculum RL approach to move beyond the base model's reasoning capacity boundary. Our approach first uses pass@k sampling to locate the current reasoning capacity boundary, then applies targeted teacher guidance to examples near or beyond that boundary, and finally uses RL to consolidate the newly introduced reasoning patterns. Across Qwen, Llama, and DeepSeek base models, boundary-aware Curriculum RL improves both pass@1 scores and pass@256 scores, with pass@1 reflecting one-attempt performance and pass@256 serving as an empirical proxy for the reasoning capacity boundary. In our experiments, average pass@256 improves by 9.8 percentage points over the base models and by 10.3 percentage points over Vanilla RLVR. These results suggest that boundary-aware Curriculum RL can provide a scalable route for LLMs to continuously improve beyond the base model's empirical reasoning capacity boundary.

AI.tldr: Boundary-aware Curriculum RL improves LLM reasoning by identifying capacity boundaries through pass@k sampling, applying targeted teacher guidance, and using RL to consolidate new reasoning patterns, outperforming both base models and standard RLVR.
AI.motivation: Standard RLVR methods have limitations - they mainly reallocate sampling probabilities among existing trajectories rather than expanding reasoning capacity boundaries. They can improve pass@1 scores but often decrease pass@k for larger k, indicating they don't truly enhance the base model's reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: 1. Use pass@k sampling to locate the current reasoning capacity boundary. 2. Apply targeted teacher guidance to examples near or beyond that boundary. 3. Use RL to consolidate the newly introduced reasoning patterns (boundary-aware Curriculum RL).
AI.result: Across Qwen, Llama, and DeepSeek models: average pass@256 improves by 9.8 percentage points over base models and by 10.3 percentage points over Vanilla RLVR. Both pass@1 (one-attempt performance) and pass@256 (reasoning capacity boundary proxy) scores improve.
AI.conclusion: Boundary-aware Curriculum RL provides a scalable route for LLMs to continuously improve beyond base models' empirical reasoning capacity boundaries, addressing limitations of standard RLVR approaches.

title: All Routes Lead to Collapse
summary: Attention sinks, representation collapse, and norm stratification are treated as transformer-specific pathologies. We show they are not specific to attention: they are what content-based routing does under a fixed similarity metric. We give a reframing identity: softmax attention is Boltzmann-weighted aggregation over Euclidean distances with constant key norms, so its score omits a $-\|k\|^2$ term and is blind to key magnitude. This predicts that any router whose metric is ill-matched to its representations should compensate, by concentrating its routing and collapsing the routed representations. We test it on routers that score and aggregate over different axes: softmax attention over tokens (nine pretrained transformers), graph attention over nodes, a selective state-space model and a recurrent mixer over time, and learned residuals over depth. All develop the same signature, and two within-model ablations show it is caused by the routing mechanism rather than by incidental dynamics. The form is contingent, set by the strength of the positional brake each router carries alongside its content score; we sweep that brake and move the onset across its whole range. The mechanism is not contingent, and it does not require norm stratification: a router with norm-normalized keys concentrates just the same. We do not claim these models implement Riemannian geometry; the geometric view is a diagnostic that names the inadequacy of the flat, norm-blind metric.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that attention sinks, representation collapse, and norm stratification are not specific to transformers but occur in any content-based routing system with a fixed similarity metric, demonstrating this across various model architectures.
AI.motivation: To challenge the prevailing view that attention-specific pathologies (like attention sinks and representation collapse) are unique to transformers, and to show they're actually systematic issues in any content-based routing mechanism with a fixed similarity metric.
AI.method: The authors present a geometric reframing identity showing softmax attention as Boltzmann-weighted aggregation over Euclidean distances with constant key norms. They test this hypothesis across multiple router architectures: softmax attention over tokens (using nine pretrained transformers), graph attention over nodes, selective state-space models, recurrent mixers over time, and learned residuals over depth, plus within-model ablation studies.
AI.result: All tested routing architectures develop the same signature pathologies, with two key findings: (1) the form is contingent on positional brake strength, but (2) the mechanism is not contingent and doesn't require norm stratification (norm-normalized keys show similar concentration patterns).
AI.conclusion: The geometric view serves as a diagnostic tool that identifies the inadequacy of "flat, norm-blind" metrics in content-based routing systems. These pathologies are fundamental to routing mechanisms with fixed similarity metrics, not incidental dynamics or specific to transformers.

title: DART: Draft-Agreement Routing for Training-Free Adaptive Thinking Budgets in Hybrid Reasoning Models
summary: Hybrid reasoning models can answer directly or spend extra tokens on extended thinking. A practical router should choose between these modes for each query, so easy problems avoid unnecessary reasoning and hard problems receive enough budget to finish the answer. Existing routers move in this direction, but they typically require labeled training data or fix thinking budgets up front, ignoring answer-level evidence from the model itself. We introduce DART, a training-free routing framework that samples two cheap no-think drafts, accepts direct answering when the drafts agree, and predicts a thinking budget from draft entropy when they disagree. Across the main comparisons, DART preserves or improves always-thinking accuracy in most settings while reducing thinking-token use.
