summary: Physical simulations for intricate geometries with path-dependent constitutive models face difficulties due to the enormous computational cost they require. Recently, the emergence of generative AI models, which succeed in image and video synthesis tasks, has provided a promise to further improve simulations. Although U-Net-based denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) have been adopted for elastic stress field generation, they typically require hundreds of sampling steps, and applications of generative models to path-dependent, e.g. plastic, stress fields remain very limited. In this work, we propose a novel flow matching (FM) model based on a transformer backbone for high-resolution path-dependent stress field generation with stochastic loading-unloading paths and geometry. The proposed model operates within the latent space of a variational autoencoder (VAE) and formulates the simulation of plastic fields as a video synthesis task, directly generating the stress fields across all time steps. Meanwhile, we design a non-Gaussian source distribution for flow matching, such that crossings among conditional transport paths are reduced during training. This enables our model to generate satisfactory samples in one step without relying on distillation. In addition, we introduce token-level loading embeddings and two auxiliary networks to further enhance the model performance in path-dependent simulation. The results demonstrate that, even with a limited training dataset, our model can accurately generate high-resolution path-dependent fields. It is much more computationally efficient than finite element analysis, providing a speedup of 6 to 7 times over FEM on CPUs and approximately two orders of magnitude speedup on consumer-grade GPUs.

AI.tldr: A transformer-based flow matching model for generating high-resolution path-dependent plastic stress fields with stochastic loading-unloading paths and geometries, achieving significant speedups over FEM.
AI.motivation: Physical simulations for complex geometries with path-dependent constitutive models (like plasticity) are computationally expensive. While generative AI has shown promise for elastic stress fields, existing methods require many sampling steps and struggle with path-dependent plasticity simulations.
AI.method: Proposes a flow matching model with transformer backbone operating in VAE latent space, treating plastic field simulation as video synthesis task. Uses non-Gaussian source distribution to reduce path crossings during training, token-level loading embeddings, and two auxiliary networks for enhanced performance.
AI.result: Model accurately generates high-resolution path-dependent stress fields even with limited training data. Achieves 6-7x speedup over FEM on CPUs and ~100x speedup on consumer-grade GPUs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed transformer-based flow matching model enables efficient, accurate generation of path-dependent plastic stress fields, offering substantial computational advantages over traditional FEM for complex geometries and stochastic loading conditions.

title: Factored Gossip DiLoCo: Reducing Blocking Communication in DiLoCo
summary: To make large-scale distributed training practical outside high-bandwidth datacenters, we must reduce blocking, high-volume synchronization. While DiLoCo communicates infrequently, its outer synchronization remains bandwidth-heavy and brittle to stragglers and transient failures. We relax exact synchronization to approximate synchronization via mixing/gossip, which degrades gracefully under delays and communication failures. This allows us to factorize DiLoCo synchronization into a non-blocking mixing step that overlaps computation with no staleness, and a blocking mixing step that tightens worker agreement, yielding a tunable trade-off between compute utilization and optimization stability. On up to billion-parameter language models in low-bandwidth settings, our framework substantially improves compute utilization compared to DiLoCo, with training progress ranging from comparable to closely matching it, and is more robust to failures.

AI.tldr: A gossip-based approximate synchronization method improves distributed training efficiency in low-bandwidth settings by reducing blocking communication and increasing robustness to failures.
AI.motivation: Large-scale distributed training outside high-bandwidth datacenters suffers from blocking, high-volume synchronization. Current methods like DiLoCo still have bandwidth-heavy and brittle outer synchronization that's vulnerable to stragglers and transient failures.
AI.method: Relax exact synchronization to approximate synchronization using mixing/gossip techniques. Factorize DiLoCo synchronization into: 1) non-blocking mixing that overlaps computation without staleness, and 2) blocking mixing that tightens worker agreement. This creates a tunable trade-off between compute utilization and optimization stability.
AI.result: On billion-parameter language models in low-bandwidth settings, the framework substantially improves compute utilization compared to DiLoCo, with training progress ranging from comparable to closely matching DiLoCo, and demonstrates better robustness to failures.
AI.conclusion: Approximate synchronization via gossip/mixing enables practical large-scale distributed training in low-bandwidth environments by gracefully degrading under delays and communication failures, offering a flexible balance between efficiency and stability.

title: Noise is Signal: Density-Based Outliers as Leading Indicators of Occupational Emergence in Labor Market Text
summary: Standard NLP pipelines for occupational clustering discard the 10-15% of job postings that density-based methods assign to noise. We argue this is an error: in rapidly evolving domains, low posting density signals novelty, not incoherence. We formalize this as the Emergence-Density Inversion (EDI) hypothesis and test it longitudinally on 84,988 job postings across eight quarters (Q4 2022-Q3 2024). EDI is partially confirmed: high-EOS outlier groups transition to stable clusters in 1.4 +/- 0.6 quarters vs. 4.1 +/- 1.2 for low-EOS groups (p < 0.001), though the signal fails in approximately 19% of cases, which we characterize as a failure analysis. We extend the Emerging Occupation Score (EOS) with Temporal Velocity and Cross-Platform Convergence, improving 2-quarter cluster-formation prediction from F1 = 0.61 to 0.74, outperforming Isolation Forest, LOF, GLOSH, and BERTrend baselines. A retrospective study on three now-established roles (MLOps Engineer, DevOps/SRE, Data Engineer) confirms EOS signalled 2-3 quarters before cluster formation, providing held-out validation. A held-out annotator panel (kappa = 0.74) rates EOS > 0.75 as coherent emerging occupations with 77% precision. Prompt Engineer, AI Safety Researcher, Foundation Model Engineer, and Agent Systems Engineer, all absent from O*NET, are top-4 in Q3 2024 and form stable clusters by Q1 2025.

AI.tldr: Density-based occupational clustering methods discard 10-15% of job postings as noise, but this research shows these low-density postings actually signal emerging occupations in rapidly evolving domains.
AI.motivation: Standard NLP pipelines for occupational clustering discard job postings classified as noise by density-based methods, but the authors argue this is an error because in rapidly evolving domains, low posting density signals novelty rather than incoherence.
AI.method: The paper introduces the Emergence-Density Inversion (EDI) hypothesis and tests it longitudinally on 84,988 job postings across eight quarters (Q4 2022-Q3 2024). They extend the Emerging Occupation Score (EOS) with Temporal Velocity and Cross-Platform Convergence metrics, and compare against Isolation Forest, LOF, GLOSH, and BERTrend baselines.
AI.result: High-EOS outlier groups transition to stable clusters in 1.4 ± 0.6 quarters vs. 4.1 ± 1.2 for low-EOS groups (p < 0.001). The improved EOS method boosts 2-quarter cluster-formation prediction from F1 = 0.61 to 0.74. EOS signals emerging occupations 2-3 quarters before cluster formation, and EOS > 0.75 achieves 77% precision for coherent emerging occupations. Four emerging roles (Prompt Engineer, AI Safety Researcher, etc.) absent from O*NET were top-4 in Q3 2024 and formed stable clusters by Q1 2025.
AI.conclusion: Low-density job postings should not be automatically discarded as noise in occupational clustering pipelines, as they often represent emerging occupations in rapidly evolving domains. The extended EOS method provides an effective tool for early detection of emerging job roles before they become established clusters.

title: Statistical Matching via Schrödinger Bridge beyond Conditional Independence
summary: Statistical matching combines partially overlapping datasets that share covariates $X$ but observe the target $Y$ and auxiliary variables $Z$ separately. Classical approaches typically invoke the conditional independence assumption (CIA), which makes the problem identifiable but fundamentally implies that the imported auxiliary variable provides no additional predictive power for $Y$ once $X$ is known. To capture this latent $Y$--$Z$ dependence, we propose a novel dependency-aware Schrödinger bridge for predictive statistical matching. Our approach couples the two separated databases by tilting the conservative CIA baseline with a transportation-based compatibility cost, recovering an informative joint distribution. The resulting statistical learning framework yields full probabilistic posterior rules for bidirectional imputation. Theoretically, we establish a sufficient condition under which the learned bridge strictly improves over the CIA baseline, alongside an exact joint recovery guarantee in the Gaussian setting under an appropriate cost. Across synthetic benchmarks and real-world datasets (CelebA and Adult), we demonstrate that our dependency-aware completion consistently improves downstream predictive utility, proving especially beneficial in settings like data recoding where the underlying population exhibits strong $Y$--$Z$ dependence.

AI.tldr: A novel dependency-aware Schrödinger bridge method for statistical matching that captures Y-Z dependence, improving predictive utility over classical conditional independence approaches.
AI.motivation: Classical statistical matching approaches rely on the conditional independence assumption (CIA), which assumes no Y-Z dependence given X, limiting predictive power when such dependencies exist in real-world data.
AI.method: Proposes a dependency-aware Schrödinger bridge that couples separated databases by tilting the CIA baseline with a transportation-based compatibility cost, recovering informative joint distributions and enabling bidirectional probabilistic imputation.
AI.result: The method consistently improves downstream predictive utility across synthetic benchmarks and real datasets (CelebA and Adult), especially beneficial in settings with strong Y-Z dependence like data recoding. Theoretical guarantees show strict improvement over CIA baseline under certain conditions.
AI.conclusion: The dependency-aware Schrödinger bridge framework effectively addresses limitations of classical statistical matching by capturing latent Y-Z dependence, providing practical improvements for real-world applications where auxiliary variables carry additional predictive information.

title: GeoRouteNet: Geometry-Enhanced Non-Autoregressive Neural Solver for the Traveling Salesman Problem
summary: The traveling salesman problem (TSP) is a canonical NP-hard combinatorial optimization benchmark that tests the representational capacity and generalization of neural solvers. While non-autoregressive (NAR) approaches offer parallel inference, they often lack sufficient geometric inductive bias and stable training signals, leading to degraded performance under cross-scale and cross-distribution shifts. We propose GeoRouteNet, a geometry-enhanced NAR neural solver for Euclidean TSP. On the model side, GeoRouteNet incorporates centered node features, learnable radial distance basis functions, distance-aware graph attention with explicit edge messaging, LayerNorm-SwiGLU feed-forward blocks, and cross-layer attentive residual mixing. On the training side, we design multi-candidate self-comparison reinforcement learning (MCS-RL), which samples multiple candidate tours per instance, constructs adaptive baselines from greedy and peer candidates, and adds winner-candidate guidance with annealed entropy regularization. On 10,000 random TSP50 instances, GeoRouteNet achieves a 0.32% optimality gap under Beam-1000 decoding. On TSP100, the gap is 1.26%. On 27 stratified TSPLIB EUC_2D instances, the overall gap drops from 17.12% (NAR4TSP reproduction) to 3.60%, while batch inference throughput substantially exceeds that of Concorde and LKH3. Ablation studies confirm that geometric structure enhancement and multi-candidate training are complementary: structure improvements dominate cross-distribution gains, while MCS-RL further stabilizes solution quality when paired with a strong geometric encoder.

AI.tldr: GeoRouteNet is a geometry-enhanced non-autoregressive neural solver for Euclidean TSP that achieves state-of-the-art performance through geometric inductive bias enhancements and multi-candidate self-comparison reinforcement learning training.
AI.motivation: Existing non-autoregressive TSP solvers lack sufficient geometric inductive bias and stable training signals, leading to poor performance under cross-scale and cross-distribution shifts. There's a need for improved neural solvers that can handle these challenges while maintaining parallel inference capabilities.
AI.method: GeoRouteNet incorporates geometric enhancements including centered node features, learnable radial distance basis functions, distance-aware graph attention with explicit edge messaging, LayerNorm-SwiGLU feed-forward blocks, and cross-layer attentive residual mixing. Training uses multi-candidate self-comparison RL which samples multiple tours per instance, constructs adaptive baselines from greedy and peer candidates, and adds winner-candidate guidance with annealed entropy regularization.
AI.result: On 10,000 random TSP50 instances, achieves 0.32% optimality gap under Beam-1000 decoding (1.26% on TSP100). On 27 TSPLIB EUC_2D instances, reduces gap from 17.12% to 3.60% while maintaining high batch inference throughput exceeding Concorde and LKH3. Ablation shows geometric enhancements dominate cross-distribution gains while MCS-RL stabilizes solution quality.
AI.conclusion: GeoRouteNet demonstrates that combining strong geometric inductive bias with multi-candidate RL training enables non-autoregressive TSP solvers to achieve near-optimal performance with excellent generalization across scales and distributions while maintaining efficient parallel inference.

title: Explainable AI for Mental Health Prediction in Drug-Affected Populations with Dragonfly Algorithm and GAN Oversampling
comment: Accepted for presenting at the 16th International Conference on Advanced Computer Information Technologies (ACIT 2026). To appear in IEEE conference proceedings
summary: Mental illnesses among drug users are an increasing international issue, particularly in regions where early detection cannot be easily undertaken. The current literature tends to ignore the use of AI-based mental health analysis in drug users, and low quality of the class imbalance treatment, low interpretability, and optimal hyperparameter optimization can lower predictive quality and clinical utility. This study present a detailed, explainable machine learning (ML) model of multiclass mental health prediction, using a multidimensional data set of drug-affected persons. We combine hybrid PCA-Information Gain (PCA-IG) feature selection, Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)-based oversampling, and Dragonfly Algorithm (DA)-optimized XGBoost to address some of the limitations of existing methods. The suggested framework is effective to work with high-dimensional categorical data, address the issue of class imbalance, and improve predictive performance due to intelligent hyperparameter tuning. The experimental findings show that the XGBoost model optimized using the DA, in combination with GAN-based oversampling, has an accuracy of 94.17% and a weighted F1-score of 93.80%, which is better than the traditional and baseline models. The behavioral, lifestyle, and health factors, particularly sleep quality, physical health, and emotional regulation, are strongly predictive of mental health, with demographic factors having little impact, as seen through feature analysis. SHAP-based explainable AI provides easy-to-understand, instance-level information, enhancing interpretability and trust in models to be used in clinical settings. The results indicate that this framework has the potential to generate valid mental health forecasting tools, which would facilitate early intervention and enhance the treatment of drug-influenced people.

AI.tldr: This study proposes an explainable ML framework for multiclass mental health prediction in drug users, combining PCA-IG feature selection, GAN-based oversampling, and Dragonfly Algorithm-optimized XGBoost to achieve 94.17% accuracy with enhanced interpretability.
AI.motivation: Mental illnesses among drug users are an increasing international problem, especially where early detection is difficult. Existing literature has gaps: ignoring AI-based mental health analysis for drug users, poor class imbalance treatment, low interpretability, and suboptimal hyperparameter tuning that reduces predictive quality and clinical utility.
AI.method: The study presents a detailed, explainable ML model using a multidimensional dataset of drug-affected persons. The framework combines: 1) Hybrid PCA-Information Gain feature selection for high-dimensional categorical data, 2) Generative Adversarial Network-based oversampling to address class imbalance, and 3) Dragonfly Algorithm-optimized XGBoost for intelligent hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: The DA-optimized XGBoost with GAN-based oversampling achieved 94.17% accuracy and 93.80% weighted F1-score, outperforming traditional baseline models. Feature analysis revealed that behavioral, lifestyle, and health factors (especially sleep quality, physical health, emotional regulation) strongly predict mental health, while demographic factors have little impact. SHAP-based explainable AI provided instance-level interpretability.
AI.conclusion: The framework shows potential for creating valid mental health forecasting tools that facilitate early intervention and improve treatment for drug-influenced individuals. The enhanced interpretability and trust from SHAP explanations make it suitable for clinical applications.

title: Towards Robust Personalized Federated Learning: Vulnerability Assessment and Defense Co-Design
summary: The proliferation of IoT devices has fueled distributed edge systems to collect vast amounts of sensitive data, creating fertile ground for on-device machine learning applications. While federated learning (FL) mitigates privacy concerns by exchanging model parameters instead of raw data, we identify a critical blind spot in current research. We examine the most commonly used personalized federated learning (PFL) methods, which allow clients to maintain private, personalized models to address data heterogeneity across clients. Through systematic analysis, we reveal that PFL methods exhibit heightened vulnerability to transfer-based adversarial attacks compared to centralized learning paradigms. Wherein, malicious clients can exploit local model knowledge to craft adversarial examples that can compromise peer clients' personalized models. We establish this vulnerability through both theoretical analysis and empirical evaluation across multiple benchmark datasets, demonstrating significant accuracy drops across various PFL methods. To address this challenge, we propose a defense framework combining stochastic input noise, input-scaled trace regularization, and parameter sensitivity maximization to improve FL's robustness. Our findings establish the first systematic study of adversarial threats in PFL systems, providing both diagnostic tools and practical countermeasures.

AI.tldr: Personalized Federated Learning (PFL) methods are vulnerable to transfer-based adversarial attacks where malicious clients can compromise peers' personalized models, requiring new defense mechanisms.
AI.motivation: While federated learning addresses privacy by exchanging model parameters instead of raw data, current research overlooks security vulnerabilities in personalized FL, especially how malicious clients can exploit local model knowledge to attack peer clients.
AI.method: Conducted systematic analysis of common PFL methods, theoretical analysis of vulnerability, empirical evaluation across multiple datasets, and proposed a defense framework combining stochastic input noise, input-scaled trace regularization, and parameter sensitivity maximization.
AI.result: PFL methods show heightened vulnerability to transfer-based adversarial attacks compared to centralized learning, with significant accuracy drops demonstrated across various PFL methods and datasets through both theoretical and empirical evidence.
AI.conclusion: This is the first systematic study of adversarial threats in PFL systems, establishing vulnerability while providing diagnostic tools and practical defense mechanisms to improve FL robustness against malicious clients.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Multimodal Learning for Enzyme-Substrate Interaction Prediction Under Low-Homology Shift
summary: Enzyme substrate interaction (ESI) prediction is a fundamental computational task for biocatalyst discovery and reaction screening in large biochemical spaces. In practical settings, ESI prediction is challenged by sparse positive supervision and low-homology distribution shift, where test enzymes share limited sequence identity with those observed during training. To address these challenges, we propose RAMMESI, a retrieval-augmented multimodal framework for robust ESI prediction. RAMMESI learns explicit pairwise enzyme-substrate representations through directional cross-modal interaction modeling and adaptive fusion. To enhance robustness, RAMMESI retrieves neighboring enzymes at inference time, recombines them with the query substrate, and aggregates the resulting pairwise predictions as contextual evidence. To improve learning under sparse positive supervision, we further adopt an imbalance-aware weighted-BCE objective. Experiments on two ESI benchmarks under sequence-identity-aware splits demonstrate that RAMMESI achieves consistently strong performance, with particular advantages in more challenging low-identity regimes. In addition, the retrieval module improves multiple ESI backbones in a plug-and-play manner, suggesting that retrieval provides a general mechanism for improving robustness under homology shift.

AI.tldr: RAMMESI is a retrieval-augmented multimodal framework for enzyme-substrate interaction prediction that addresses sparse supervision and homology distribution shifts through cross-modal modeling, adaptive fusion, and inference-time retrieval of neighboring enzymes.
AI.motivation: Enzyme substrate interaction prediction faces two main challenges: sparse positive supervision (limited labeled positive examples) and low-homology distribution shift where test enzymes share limited sequence identity with training enzymes, making generalization difficult.
AI.method: RAMMESI uses directional cross-modal interaction modeling and adaptive fusion to learn explicit pairwise enzyme-substrate representations. It incorporates a retrieval mechanism that fetches neighboring enzymes at inference time, recombines them with query substrates, and aggregates predictions as contextual evidence. Also uses an imbalance-aware weighted-BCE objective to handle sparse positives.
AI.result: Experiments on two ESI benchmarks under sequence-identity-aware splits show RAMMESI achieves consistently strong performance, particularly excelling in challenging low-identity regimes. The retrieval module improves multiple ESI backbones in plug-and-play fashion.
AI.conclusion: RAMMESI provides a robust solution for ESI prediction under homology shift, and retrieval serves as a general mechanism for improving robustness across different model architectures in biocatalyst discovery applications.

title: BranchShine: Compact Raw-Audio-to-IPA Transcription with a RoPE E-Branchformer Encoder
summary: Speech-to-IPA transcription is useful when the desired output is pronunciation rather than orthographic text, but competitive multilingual systems are often large and evaluation is sensitive to normalization choices. This paper presents BranchShine, a 33M-parameter raw-audio CTC recognizer with a lightweight convolutional front end and a 19-block RoPE E-Branchformer encoder. We find that BranchShine provides a compact and competitive operating point for IPA transcription under matched normalization and scoring. On a 16,660-utterance multilingual test set covering 41 language labels, BranchShine obtains 9.19% whitespace-insensitive IPA character error rate, compared with 9.78% for the 575.00M-parameter PhoneticXEUS baseline. A secondary child speech reading analysis shows a complementary operating profile: BranchShine is more conservative on incorrect readings, while Whisper-Medium is stronger on exact acceptance of correct readings. Overall, the results indicate that a compact raw-audio-to-IPA model can approach much larger baselines on character-level IPA transcription.

AI.tldr: BranchShine is a compact 33M-parameter raw-audio-to-IPA transcription model that achieves competitive performance (9.19% character error rate) against much larger baselines (575M parameters) on multilingual tasks.
AI.motivation: Current speech-to-IPA transcription systems are often large and computationally expensive, and their evaluation is sensitive to normalization choices. There's a need for more compact yet competitive models for IPA transcription that can handle multilingual input effectively.
AI.method: Developed BranchShine, a 33M-parameter raw-audio CTC recognizer featuring a lightweight convolutional front end and a 19-block RoPE E-Branchformer encoder. The model was evaluated on a 16,660-utterance multilingual test set covering 41 languages.
AI.result: BranchShine achieved 9.19% whitespace-insensitive IPA character error rate, outperforming the 575M-parameter PhoneticXEUS baseline (9.78%). In child speech reading analysis, BranchShine showed more conservative behavior on incorrect readings, while Whisper-Medium was better at accepting correct readings exactly.
AI.conclusion: Compact raw-audio-to-IPA models like BranchShine can approach the performance of much larger baselines on character-level IPA transcription, offering an efficient alternative for multilingual applications.

title: CLIP-guided Diffusion Model for Backdoor Generation in Sensor-based Human Activity Recognition
summary: Sensors are critical components of modern intelligent devices. The proliferation of the Internet of Things (IoT) and wearable mobile devices has enabled the integration of such sensors to monitor the environment and enable users to take predictive actions. Human activity recognition (HAR) is a popular application in which Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)-based sensors, such as accelerometers and gyroscopes, are used to provide insights into health, training, and medical diagnosis. However, the accuracy of such a model is hindered by the lack of data. The diffusion model-based technique has proven successful in generating synthetic data for training HAR models. In this paper, we propose a backdoor training technique, IMU-DM-CLIP, that leverages a diffusion model to enable trigger-based attacks on HAR models. Our empirical analysis shows that the attack is successful even with a very small backdoor injection rate of 10\% and 10\% of the data guided for the diffusion model.

AI.tldr: A backdoor attack method called IMU-DM-CLIP uses diffusion models to generate synthetic IMU sensor data with hidden triggers, enabling successful attacks on human activity recognition models with minimal data poisoning.
AI.motivation: Human activity recognition using IMU sensors lacks sufficient training data, and diffusion models have shown success in generating synthetic data. However, this presents a security vulnerability where synthetic data could be used for backdoor attacks on HAR models.
AI.method: Proposes IMU-DM-CLIP, a backdoor training technique that leverages diffusion models to generate synthetic IMU sensor data containing hidden triggers. The method requires only 10% backdoor injection rate and 10% guided diffusion data to implant malicious functionality.
AI.result: Empirical analysis shows successful backdoor attacks on HAR models with very small injection rates (10% backdoor injection and 10% guided diffusion data), demonstrating the effectiveness and stealthiness of the attack.
AI.conclusion: The research reveals a significant security vulnerability in HAR systems where diffusion models for data augmentation can be exploited for stealthy backdoor attacks, highlighting the need for robust security measures in sensor-based AI applications.

title: RLM-Cascade: Response-Level Speculative Decoding for Cost-Efficient LLM API Serving
summary: We present RLM-Cascade, a proxy-layer system that applies speculative decoding at the response level to reduce LLM API costs without requiring model architecture access or a shared vocabulary. A fast, inexpensive draft model generates a candidate response; a capable verify model accepts, enhances, or is bypassed entirely depending on a lightweight complexity router. On a real-world agentic coding workload (Claude Code), RLM-Cascade achieves a draft-use rate of 88.8% across 125 production requests, reducing API cost by 45.8% relative to a direct Opus baseline. Counter-intuitively, the proxy also reduces end-to-end latency: median response time is 2,026 ms versus 3,698 ms for Native Opus -- a 1.83X speedup at p50 -- because the SKIPPED path (DeepSeek only, no Opus call) dominates the workload distribution. Quality matches or exceeds the Opus baseline: 100% pass rate on a 20-task Code/Math/Instruct benchmark versus 95% for Native Opus. We further describe a rule-based complexity router that selects the SKIPPED path for simple agentic turns and a hybrid tool-call strategy that bypasses the speculative pipeline for schema-critical tool-selection turns. RLM-Cascade is deployed in production as an enterprise AI infrastructure component and published as open source with a live metrics dashboard and Prometheus endpoint.

AI.tldr: RLM-Cascade is a proxy-layer system using response-level speculative decoding to reduce LLM API costs by 45.8% while speeding up responses 1.83X, with quality equal to or better than baseline.
AI.motivation: To reduce LLM API costs without requiring access to model architecture or shared vocabulary, while also potentially improving latency and maintaining or enhancing quality.
AI.method: Uses a fast draft model to generate candidate responses, with a capable verify model that can accept, enhance, or bypass responses based on a lightweight complexity router. Includes a rule-based complexity router for simple tasks and hybrid tool-call strategy for schema-critical turns.
AI.result: Achieved 88.8% draft-use rate, 45.8% cost reduction, 1.83X speedup (median 2,026 ms vs 3,698 ms), and 100% pass rate on benchmarks vs 95% for baseline. Successfully deployed in production enterprise infrastructure.
AI.conclusion: RLM-Cascade effectively reduces LLM API costs and latency while maintaining or improving quality through response-level speculative decoding and intelligent routing, demonstrating production viability and open-source availability.

title: The Unseen Hand: Manipulating Model Fairness and SHAP with Targeted Identity Re-Association Attacks
summary: As machine learning models grow more influential and opaque, algorithmic fairness and explainability are critical for ensuring accountability. However, we demonstrate that these auditing mechanisms are themselves vulnerable to subtle manipulation, camouflaging the influence of protected features. While prior work on data-agnostic attacks has exposed this vulnerability, they leave behind detectable artifacts that compromise their stealth. We introduce Targeted Identity Re-Association (TIRA) attacks, a novel family of attacks that iteratively and probabilistically manipulate a model's outputs without requiring access to the model's internals or feature representations. We formalize two algorithms: Probabilistic Micro-Shuffling (PMiS), which applies localized adjacent swaps, and Probabilistic Rank-Shift Micro-Perturbation (PRSMP), which introduces small, randomized rank shifts. We empirically demonstrate that TIRA attacks are highly effective at pushing fairness metrics towards ideal values. Crucially, TIRA attacks successfully confound SHAP-based explanations, leaving effectively zero residual attribution for protected features, a major improvement over prior work.

AI.tldr: TIRA attacks manipulate ML fairness audits by subtly altering model outputs to hide protected feature influence without requiring internal model access, using probabilistic algorithms that evade detection better than previous methods.
AI.motivation: Machine learning fairness and explainability audits are vulnerable to manipulation that can camouflage the influence of protected features, but existing attacks leave detectable artifacts. The authors aim to develop more stealthy attacks that can successfully hide protected feature influence in fairness audits.
AI.method: Introduces Targeted Identity Re-Association (TIRA) attacks with two probabilistic algorithms: Probabilistic Micro-Shuffling (PMiS) applies localized adjacent swaps, and Probabilistic Rank-Shift Micro-Perturbation (PRSMP) introduces small randomized rank shifts. Both operate by iteratively manipulating model outputs without accessing model internals or feature representations.
AI.result: TIRA attacks are highly effective at pushing fairness metrics toward ideal values and successfully confound SHAP-based explanations, leaving effectively zero residual attribution for protected features - a significant improvement over prior work that left detectable artifacts.
AI.conclusion: Fairness and explainability auditing mechanisms are vulnerable to subtle TIRA attacks that can hide protected feature influence, raising serious concerns about the reliability of current auditing practices and highlighting the need for more robust audit defense mechanisms.

title: When AUC 0.998 Is Not Enough: A Candidate Evaluation Protocol for Hidden-State Probes of Indirect Prompt Injection in Multimodal Computer-Use Agents
comment: 17 pages, 3 figures. Camera-ready version for EvalMG '26, The 2nd Workshop on Evaluation for Multimodal Generation, co-located with SIGIR 2026
summary: Hidden-state probing -- a linear classifier on a frozen vision-language model's internal activations -- has emerged as an attractive evaluation tool for flagging indirect prompt injection (IPI) in multimodal computer-use agents before the agent emits a corrupted action. We argue, on a single-backbone cautionary case study (Qwen2.5-VL-7B on Mind2Web, teacher-forced replay), that a high probing AUC on a clean-vs-attack split is not, on its own, evidence of malicious-content detection. Two post-hoc diagnostics -- a paired-construction scalar baseline on text-side injections, and same-step nuisance-matched visual controls on the overlay surface -- do not license an unqualified malicious-content interpretation of the headline while leaving room for partly-semantic readings. We package the diagnostics as a candidate control set with reporting heuristics for what a high clean-vs-attack AUC does and does not license. Labels are injection-surface-present, not attack success; generalisation beyond this backbone and benchmark is a conjecture.

AI.tldr: Hidden-state probing for detecting indirect prompt injection in multimodal agents has limitations - high AUC alone doesn't prove malicious-content detection, requiring additional diagnostic controls.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether hidden-state probing (linear classifiers on frozen vision-language model activations) reliably detects indirect prompt injection in multimodal computer-use agents, given that high AUC scores on clean-vs-attack splits are currently being used as evidence of malicious-content detection.
AI.method: Case study using Qwen2.5-VL-7B on Mind2Web dataset with teacher-forced replay. Introduces two post-hoc diagnostics: (1) paired-construction scalar baseline for text-side injections, and (2) same-step nuisance-matched visual controls for overlay surfaces. Creates a candidate control set with reporting heuristics to interpret what high AUC actually indicates.
AI.result: High probing AUC on clean-vs-attack split alone does not constitute evidence of malicious-content detection. The diagnostics show that headline results don't support unqualified malicious-content interpretations but allow for partly-semantic readings. The labels indicate injection-surface presence rather than attack success.
AI.conclusion: Hidden-state probing evaluations for indirect prompt injection need careful interpretation and additional diagnostic controls. Researchers should use proposed heuristics to understand what high AUC scores actually measure, distinguishing between detection of injection surfaces versus actual malicious content. Generalization beyond this specific backbone and benchmark remains conjectural.

title: Discovering Crystal Structure Prediction Algorithms with an AI Co-Scientist
summary: We introduce Human-AI Co-discovery system (HACO) for scientific algorithm discovery through cross-domain search and sparse human steering. Starting from the goal of generating crystal structures from chemical compositions, HACO searched across generative modeling methodologies from multiple fields and identified MaskGIT, a masked generative model from vision, as a promising framework for crystal structure prediction (CSP). HACO instantiated this masked formulation as a discrete token model of crystal structure; guided by sparse high-level human objectives, it then added crystallographic symmetry tokens, space group stratified sampling for polymorph coverage, and sub-bin coordinate refinement, yielding the Masked Generative Crystal Transformer (MaskGXT). On the MP-20 polymorph split, MaskGXT reaches 79.06% match-everyone-to-reference (METRe) accuracy, compared with 70.87% for the strongest evaluated baseline. MaskGXT also attains the best match rate on standard MP-20 and MPTS-52 CSP benchmarks. These results provide evidence that, in domains offering cheap, fast, and well-aligned validation, transfer-guided interactive AI co-scientists can contribute to scientific algorithm discovery by identifying transferable modeling principles and combining them with targeted human domain guidance.

AI.tldr: HACO system discovers MaskGXT for crystal structure prediction by cross-domain search (identifying MaskGIT from vision) and human guidance, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on CSP benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To discover scientific algorithms for crystal structure prediction from chemical compositions through human-AI collaboration, rather than relying solely on domain-specific approaches.
AI.method: Human-AI Co-discovery (HACO) system searches across generative modeling methodologies from multiple fields, identifies MaskGIT from computer vision, adapts it with crystallographic symmetry tokens, space group stratified sampling, and sub-bin coordinate refinement to create MaskGXT.
AI.result: MaskGXT achieves 79.06% METRe accuracy on MP-20 polymorph split (vs 70.87% best baseline), and best match rates on standard MP-20 and MPTS-52 crystal structure prediction benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: In domains with cheap/fast validation, transfer-guided interactive AI co-scientists can contribute to scientific algorithm discovery by identifying transferable modeling principles and combining them with targeted human domain guidance.

title: SpotAttention: Plug-In Block-Sparse Routing for Pretrained Long-Context Transformers
summary: Long contexts have become standard in pretrained LLMs, yet they remain expensive to run: prefill compute grows quadratically with sequence length, and every decode step re-reads a key-value cache that grows linearly with it. Sparse attention cuts these costs by attending only to a relevant subset of past tokens, but selecting that subset is itself expensive. We present SpotAttention, a lightweight selector that attaches to a frozen pretrained transformer and learns by KL distillation to estimate its attention distribution. The selector picks the top-K keys each query attends to, and because its estimate is a calibrated distribution, a dual top-p rule reads the per-query, per-layer budget directly from it. Across Qwen3 (dense, 4B-32B) and Qwen3.5 (hybrid linear/full attention, 4B-9B), SpotAttention matches dense accuracy at contexts up to 128K tokens, eight times the training length. Decode at L=128K runs 3.9x faster than FlashAttention and 1.8x faster than Twilight, the strongest training-free baseline. Quantizing the selector's K-cache to INT4 or FP4 microscale shrinks it 3.5x at no accuracy cost.

AI.tldr: SpotAttention is a lightweight selector that attaches to frozen pretrained transformers to reduce long-context computational costs by learning to estimate attention distributions via KL distillation, enabling efficient sparse attention with top-K selection while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Long contexts in LLMs are computationally expensive: prefill grows quadratically with sequence length, and decode steps re-read linearly growing key-value caches. While sparse attention reduces costs, selecting relevant token subsets itself introduces significant overhead.
AI.method: SpotAttention attaches to frozen pretrained transformers and learns by KL distillation to estimate attention distributions. It picks top-K keys each query attends to, using a dual top-p rule that reads per-query, per-layer budgets directly from the calibrated distribution.
AI.result: Across Qwen3 (4B-32B) and Qwen3.5 (4B-9B), SpotAttention matches dense accuracy at contexts up to 128K tokens (8x training length). Decode at 128K runs 3.9x faster than FlashAttention and 1.8x faster than Twilight. Quantizing selector's K-cache to INT4/FP4 shrinks it 3.5x with no accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: SpotAttention provides an efficient solution for long-context inference by combining lightweight learned attention estimation with sparse selection, achieving substantial speedups while maintaining accuracy, with further benefits from quantization techniques.

title: Priority-Aware Learning-Unlearning Correction for Dynamic Decentralized LoRA Fine-Tuning
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed at the network edge to provide pervasive generative AI services, decentralized federated learning (DFL) provides a vital mechanism for privacy-preserving, domain-specific fine-tuning through peer-to-peer exchanges of parameter-efficient updates. However, the dynamic nature of practical decentralized edge networks, where devices may dynamically join or leave the collaborative training process, requires the system to continuously adapt to new data while selectively removing prior contributions. This correction process remains a significant bottleneck, as individual device updates become deeply entangled within the global fine-tuned parameters. To address this challenge, we propose a priority-aware learning-unlearning correction framework based on orthogonal LoRA that can enhance the knowledge evaluation through topology adjustment. Specifically, we first design an orthogonal LoRA mechanism that yields post-training contribution coordinates, enabling history-free projection addition and deletion in response to membership changes. We then analyze the correction bottleneck and develop a priority-aware policy that selects among topology refinement, local correction, proximal damping, and synchronization scheduling according to the dominant residual term. A resource allocation algorithm is further developed to allocate limited communication across layer groups, prioritizing the primary bottlenecks within per-round wireless constraints. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves robust post-event correction for both device join and leave events and validate that different residual regimes necessitate distinct correction actions.

AI.tldr: A priority-aware learning-unlearning framework using orthogonal LoRA for decentralized federated learning at network edges enables efficient correction when devices dynamically join/leave by managing knowledge contributions through topology adjustment and resource allocation.
AI.motivation: Large language models deployed at network edges need privacy-preserving fine-tuning via decentralized federated learning, but dynamic device membership (join/leave events) creates correction bottlenecks as individual updates become entangled in global parameters.
AI.method: 1) Orthogonal LoRA mechanism for post-training contribution coordinates enabling history-free projection addition/deletion; 2) Priority-aware policy selecting among topology refinement, local correction, proximal damping, and synchronization scheduling based on dominant residual terms; 3) Resource allocation algorithm for communication allocation across layer groups.
AI.result: The framework achieves robust post-event correction for both device join and leave events, validates that different residual regimes necessitate distinct correction actions, and works within per-round wireless constraints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed orthogonal LoRA-based correction framework effectively addresses dynamic membership challenges in decentralized federated learning at network edges by enabling efficient learning and unlearning through priority-aware policies and resource-aware implementation.

title: Physiology-Aware CNN and Zero-Shot Multimodal LLMs for ECG Image Classification: A Comparative Study
summary: Multimodal large language models (LLMs) are increasingly adopted to interpret 12-lead ECG images, though the interpretations often lack validation. However, ECG image understanding significantly differs from general images as it depends on precise waveform morphology, lead relationships and accurate interval measurements. This study investigated whether zero-shot multimodal LLMs can reliably distinguish normal and abnormal ECG images and, in parallel, evaluated CNN-based models for clinically grounded references. Standard 12-lead ECG recordings were rendered as single-page images for a binary normal-abnormal classification task. Three prominent LLMs (GPT-5.2, GPT-4.1, and Gemini-2.5 Pro) were tested using a fixed zero-shot prompt across multiple runs. In parallel, a physiology-aware CNN-based model was developed with the capability to aggregate features from the predefined anatomical lead groups. The model was compared with ResNet18, DenseNet121, VGG16 baselines, and all the models were evaluated on an internal test set and external PTB-XL dataset. Across seeds, CNN-based models demonstrated stable discrimination, with average internal ROC-AUC of 0.92-0.94, and external ROC-AUC of 0.85-0.86. The proposed LeadGroupECG model significantly improved over its backbone internally without compromising external generalization. It remained competitive with other baselines, while consistently highlighting anatomical lead-group contributions. In contrast, zero-shot LLM discrimination remained near-chance (ROC-AUC around 0.5). The PR-AUC improved slightly when ECGs used a grid-based calibration background compared with the grid-free ECGs. Although multimodal LLMs can generate reasonable ECG narratives, their zero-shot diagnostic discrimination remains limited. Therefore, clinically framed, domain-specific architectures remain essential for AI-based ECG interpretation.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot multimodal LLMs fail at ECG classification (ROC-AUC ~0.5), while specialized CNN models achieve strong performance (ROC-AUC 0.92-0.94), showing domain-specific architectures are essential for reliable ECG interpretation.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs are increasingly used for ECG image interpretation without validation, but ECG analysis requires precise waveform morphology, lead relationships, and interval measurements rather than general image understanding.
AI.method: Tested three LLMs (GPT-5.2, GPT-4.1, Gemini-2.5 Pro) with zero-shot prompts for binary normal-abnormal ECG classification, while developing a physiology-aware CNN (LeadGroupECG) that aggregates features from anatomical lead groups and comparing it with ResNet18, DenseNet121, VGG16 baselines on internal test set and external PTB-XL dataset.
AI.result: CNN models achieved stable discrimination (internal ROC-AUC 0.92-0.94, external 0.85-0.86), with LeadGroupECG improving over backbone while maintaining generalization. LLMs performed near chance (ROC-AUC ~0.5), with slight PR-AUC improvement only when ECGs had grid-based calibration backgrounds.
AI.conclusion: While multimodal LLMs can generate reasonable ECG narratives, their zero-shot diagnostic discrimination remains limited, so clinically framed, domain-specific architectures remain essential for reliable AI-based ECG interpretation.

title: Learning Graphs through Continuous Information Entropy Fields
summary: Graph theory is inherently descriptive, capturing what relationships exist but not why they arise, because it treats edges as primitive constructs. This paper proposes a new explanatory framework for graph learning, where relationships emerge from latent continuous information entropy fields, and a graph becomes a discrete instantiation of an underlying field. To formalize this field, we introduce the Field-informed Graph Network (FGN). It learns a scalar field from node features and leverages it to modulate message passing. The information-theoretic objective balances structural fidelity with field smoothness, forming a self-reinforcing loop. In this loop, the field modulates information diffusion through field-modulated weighting, and the updated node representations iteratively refine the field. As a result, FGN learns by simulating its own co-evolution. Extensive experiments on node classification and graph classification benchmarks demonstrate superior performance, robustness to perturbations, and structurally coherent field representations.

AI.tldr: FGN introduces a continuous information entropy field as explanatory framework for graphs, where edges emerge from underlying fields rather than being primitive constructs.
AI.motivation: Traditional graph theory treats edges as primitive constructs that only describe relationships without explaining why they arise. The paper aims to move beyond this descriptive limitation by providing an explanatory framework.
AI.method: Proposes Field-informed Graph Network (FGN) that learns a scalar field from node features and uses it to modulate message passing. Uses information-theoretic objective balancing structural fidelity with field smoothness, creating a self-reinforcing loop where field modulates information diffusion and updated node representations refine the field.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on node classification and graph classification benchmarks demonstrate superior performance, robustness to perturbations, and structurally coherent field representations.
AI.conclusion: FGN provides a novel explanatory framework where relationships emerge from latent continuous information entropy fields, representing a shift from descriptive to explanatory graph learning that simulates co-evolution of structure and representation.

title: PromptDyG: Test-Time Prompt Adaptation on Dynamic Graphs
summary: Activities in numerous evolving systems can be represented as dynamic graphs in snapshot form at different time intervals, i.e., discrete-time dynamic graphs (DTDGs). Existing methods show impressive advances in capturing historical temporal evolution patterns in DTDGs, but they focus on addressing an offline learning setting, where models are trained using historical snapshots once and then evaluated to all subsequent graph snapshots without further updating. This fails to capture 1) the nature of evolving complexities across graph snapshots and 2) the distribution shift in the testing graph snapshots. To address these problems, we propose PromptDyG, a novel framework that leverages unsupervised test-time Prompt adaptation for Dynamic Graph learning under a live-update online setting. The key insight is that an expressive dynamic graph prompt can be learned on a frozen backbone via minimization of feature-wise, label-free entropy to efficiently and continuously model the evolving patterns. We show theoretically that this unsupervised prompt adaptation can guarantee a larger similarity margin between positive and negative pairs, facilitating more accurate dynamic predictions. It is further confirmed by our extensive empirical results on six benchmark datasets that show consistent and significant improvements of PromptDyG over state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: PromptDyG is a novel framework that uses test-time prompt adaptation for dynamic graph learning to handle evolving complexities and distribution shifts in continuously updating graph systems.
AI.motivation: Existing dynamic graph learning methods use offline settings where models are trained once on historical data and then applied to all future snapshots without updates. This approach fails to capture: 1) evolving complexities across graph snapshots, and 2) distribution shifts in testing graph snapshots.
AI.method: The proposed PromptDyG framework leverages unsupervised test-time prompt adaptation for dynamic graph learning under a live-update online setting. It learns an expressive dynamic graph prompt on a frozen backbone by minimizing feature-wise, label-free entropy to efficiently model evolving patterns.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows unsupervised prompt adaptation guarantees a larger similarity margin between positive and negative pairs, facilitating more accurate dynamic predictions. Extensive empirical results on six benchmark datasets show consistent and significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: PromptDyG effectively addresses limitations of offline dynamic graph learning by enabling continuous adaptation to evolving graph patterns through test-time prompt optimization, providing superior performance for live-update scenarios.

title: GRAIN: Group Aggregation via Min-Norm Objective
summary: Learning instability is a long-standing problem across machine learning, but it is especially acute in the overparameterized regime that defines modern deep learning: large models fine-tuned or trained on limited data traverse flat loss landscapes with many nearly-equivalent minima, and stochastic factors (initialization, data order, dropout, hardware non-determinism) can route optimization to very different solutions. The rise of large pretrained models (LPMs) makes the problem more urgent: training cost is high, downstream data is often small, and repeated runs for variance reduction are prohibitive. We introduce \textbf{GRAIN} (\textbf{G}roup \textbf{A}ggregation via m\textbf{IN}-norm objective), a lightweight training algorithm that replaces the mean aggregation used in mini-batch optimization (both across mini-batches and within a mini-batch) with a min-norm convex combination of group-wise gradients. \mName guarantees a non-negative inner product between the aggregated update and every group gradient, resolving intra- and inner-batch gradient conflict, and retains an $\mathcal{O}(1/T)$ convergence rate comparable to SGD. Under mild smoothness and absolute-continuity assumptions, the min-norm solution differs almost surely from the arithmetic mean, which yields a uniform-stability bound for \mName strictly tighter than the standard bound for SGD. Empirically across generation, classification, and regression at LPM scale, \mName delivers consistent improvements in mean performance and reductions in run-to-run variance over a broad suite of tasks, with no extra training-time or storage cost beyond a single backward pass.

AI.tldr: GRAIN is a new training algorithm that replaces mean aggregation in mini-batch optimization with min-norm convex combination of group gradients, reducing instability and variance in large pretrained model fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Learning instability is a major problem in overparameterized deep learning, especially for large pretrained models (LPMs) fine-tuned on limited data. Stochastic factors cause optimization to find very different solutions, and repeated runs for variance reduction are prohibitive due to high training costs.
AI.method: GRAIN replaces mean aggregation (across mini-batches and within mini-batch) with a min-norm convex combination of group-wise gradients. This guarantees non-negative inner product between aggregated update and every group gradient, resolving gradient conflicts while maintaining O(1/T) convergence rate comparable to SGD.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows min-norm solution differs almost surely from arithmetic mean, yielding tighter uniform-stability bound than SGD. Empirical results across generation, classification, and regression tasks at LPM scale show consistent improvements in mean performance and reductions in run-to-run variance, with no extra training-time or storage costs.
AI.conclusion: GRAIN provides a lightweight, effective solution to learning instability in overparameterized regimes, particularly valuable for fine-tuning large pretrained models where variance reduction is crucial but repeated runs are impractical.

title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sparse-Reward Search in Commutative Algebra
comment: 21 pages, 15 figures, 3 tables. Accepted at ICML 2026
summary: Applying machine learning techniques to solving long-standing mathematical conjectures can be particularly challenging due to their extreme reward sparsity. As an illustrative example, we consider Kalai's algebraic Hirsch conjecture and recast the construction of its counterexamples as a sparse-reward reinforcement learning problem on graphs. We propose a constrained options-based HRL framework with an equivariant graph neural network policy, which allows us to learn useful temporal abstractions for this task. We evaluate our approach over a wide range of degrees and demonstrate that it consistently outperforms classical RL algorithms as well as greedy search. By exploiting the hierarchical structure of the problem, we effectively provide a first-of-its-kind application of HRL to a problem in commutative algebra.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework with graph neural networks to construct counterexamples for Kalai's algebraic Hirsch conjecture, outperforming classical RL and greedy search.
AI.motivation: Machine learning techniques struggle with long-standing mathematical conjectures due to extreme reward sparsity. The paper uses Kalai's algebraic Hirsch conjecture as an illustrative case where constructing counterexamples presents a sparse-reward RL challenge on graphs.
AI.method: A constrained options-based hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) framework with an equivariant graph neural network policy is proposed. This learns useful temporal abstractions for constructing graph-based counterexamples.
AI.result: The approach is evaluated over a wide range of degrees and consistently outperforms classical RL algorithms as well as greedy search methods in constructing counterexamples.
AI.conclusion: By exploiting the hierarchical structure of the problem, the paper provides the first application of HRL to a problem in commutative algebra, demonstrating its effectiveness for sparse-reward mathematical discovery tasks.

title: EEG Benchmarking Needs a Task Specification Layer: NeuroDoc for Rulebook-Guided, Executable Benchmark Construction
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) foundation models increasingly rely on multi-dataset training and evaluation, yet public EEG datasets still lack a shared task specification layer that can turn heterogeneous recordings into reusable benchmark units. Existing standards organize files, metadata, and provenance, but they do not specify EEG tasks under a common language and rulebook, leaving critical task semantics scattered across papers, code, and manual interpretation. We investigate whether heterogeneous public EEG datasets can be standardized through a structured task specification language paired with a shared rulebook. Our methodology represents each benchmark entry as a task document synchronized with an executable task kernel, with the rulebook defining task fields, evidence requirements, document-kernel alignment, review states, and machine-checkable constraints. Using this methodology, we release a community-reviewed EEG benchmark corpus centered on 53 completed and reviewed entries with 245 task definitions spanning diverse paradigms, and we introduce NeuroDoc and NeuroAudit as the operational support layer for rulebook-guided drafting, upgrading, review, amendment, and release management. We further examine whether the resulting benchmark units can be instantiated in a shared downstream setting across four EEG foundation model backbones, providing execution-based evidence for reusable, auditable, and executable EEG benchmarking infrastructure.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a standardized task specification language and rulebook for EEG foundation models, creating reusable benchmark units from heterogeneous EEG datasets, with implementation demonstrated across four model backbones.
AI.motivation: EEG foundation models need multi-dataset training/evaluation, but public EEG datasets lack a shared task specification layer that can convert heterogeneous recordings into reusable benchmark units. Existing standards handle files and metadata but not task semantics under a common language.
AI.method: Developed a structured task specification language paired with a shared rulebook. Each benchmark entry is represented as a task document synchronized with an executable task kernel. The rulebook defines task fields, evidence requirements, document-kernel alignment, review states, and machine-checkable constraints.
AI.result: Released a community-reviewed EEG benchmark corpus with 53 completed/reviewed entries containing 245 task definitions spanning diverse paradigms. Created NeuroDoc and NeuroAudit as operational support layers for rulebook-guided drafting, upgrading, review, amendment, and release management.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables heterogeneous EEG datasets to be standardized into reusable benchmark units. The methodology provides execution-based evidence for creating reusable, auditable, and executable EEG benchmarking infrastructure that can be instantiated across multiple EEG foundation model backbones.

title: FORGE: Fused On-Register Gradient Elimination for Memory-Efficient LLM Training
summary: Reverse-mode differentiation computes every weight gradient, writes it to memory, and only then lets the optimizer read it back. This two-phase schedule sets the memory ceiling of modern training: at the seam between the phases, every layer's gradient is live at once. We argue that this materialized gradient is an artifact of how differentiation is staged, not a quantity that learning requires -- and we eliminate it. FORGE folds the optimizer step into the backward pass and applies it one tile at a time, entirely in registers, so each gradient tile is consumed the instant it is produced and never becomes a tensor. The fusion changes only when the update happens, not what it computes: in full precision the fused step is provably exact -- the identical optimizer update, for every element-wise rule -- and that exactness survives tensor- and sequence-parallel sharding; in the bf16 and 8-bit regimes used in practice it is faithful rather than bit-identical, its deviation bounded and, for the weight store, rendered unbiased by stochastic rounding. Because each gradient tile is born and consumed in the same registers, it is never converted down to bf16 to be stored and read back; FORGE thus preserves the full-precision fidelity that both bf16 and 8-bit optimizers lose to that conversion. Nor is the method tied to one architecture or one optimizer: linear layers are ubiquitous, and FORGE reclaims the gradient memory of any of them under any element-wise rule. Empirically FORGE more than halves the memory of an optimizer step and, at the small batch sizes typical of fine-tuning and continued pretraining, runs about 1.5x faster; integrated into tensor-parallel Megatron-LM it fits 8B training at four times the micro-batch a standard optimizer allows on the same GPUs.

AI.tldr: FORGE eliminates gradient memory by fusing optimizer updates directly into backward pass, processing gradients tile-by-tile in registers instead of materializing all gradients at once.
AI.motivation: Current training memory is bottlenecked by needing to store all gradients between backward pass and optimizer step. This gradient materialization is an artifact of differentiation staging, not a requirement for learning itself.
AI.method: FORGE folds optimizer step into backward pass and applies updates one tile at a time entirely in registers. Each gradient tile is consumed instantly after production, never becoming a stored tensor. The method maintains exactness in full precision, works with tensor/sequence parallelism, and handles bf16/8-bit regimes with bounded deviation.
AI.result: FORGE more than halves optimizer step memory and runs ~1.5x faster at typical fine-tuning batch sizes. Integrated into Megatron-LM, it enables 8B training with 4x larger micro-batches on same GPUs. Preserves full-precision fidelity lost by bf16/8-bit optimizers.
AI.conclusion: FORGE eliminates the gradient memory bottleneck by fusing optimizer updates into backward pass, enabling larger models/batches without sacrificing training fidelity, working with various architectures and element-wise optimizer rules.

title: Provable Benefits of RLVR over SFT for Reasoning Models: Learning to Backtrack Efficiently
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated that reinforcement fine-tuning of pretrained base models can lead to significant gains in reasoning performance at inference time. In this work, we theoretically analyze why reinforcement fine-tuning induces better reasoning ability than purely supervised fine-tuning (SFT) methods. We model chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning as a pathfinding problem on graphs and compare the popular method of reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) against traditional SFT. We prove that SFT, when trained on golden shortest paths without negative examples, fails to learn how to efficiently backtrack. In contrast, an RLVR-trained model can learn how to efficiently backtrack from dead ends using only outcome reward. This leads to an exponential separation in inference-time compute between the two methods, and demonstrates that RLVR leads the model to learn the location of difficult decisions in a reasoning chain, ultimately allowing for better allocation of inference-time compute. Finally, we show that the reasoning traces of an RLVR model can be distilled to train a base model to backtrack efficiently as well.

AI.tldr: RLVR fine-tuning enables LLMs to learn efficient backtracking for chain-of-thought reasoning, providing exponential inference-time compute advantages over supervised fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Recent advances show reinforcement fine-tuning improves LLM reasoning performance, but there's a lack of theoretical understanding of why reinforcement methods outperform supervised fine-tuning for chain-of-thought reasoning.
AI.method: The authors model chain-of-thought reasoning as a pathfinding problem on graphs and theoretically compare reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) against supervised fine-tuning. They prove SFT fails to learn efficient backtracking when trained only on golden shortest paths, while RLVR learns backtracking using outcome rewards.
AI.result: RLVR-trained models learn to backtrack efficiently from dead ends, leading to exponential separation in inference-time compute compared to SFT. RLVR helps models learn the location of difficult decisions in reasoning chains for better compute allocation. The reasoning traces from RLVR models can be distilled to train base models for efficient backtracking.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement fine-tuning with verifiable rewards provides fundamental advantages over supervised fine-tuning for chain-of-thought reasoning by enabling efficient backtracking learning, leading to significant inference-time compute improvements and better allocation of reasoning resources.

title: Neural Operator Processes for Probabilistic Operator Learning under Partial Observations
summary: Neural operators learn mappings between function spaces, but are typically developed with dense input-output training fields and fully observed inputs at inference. Many scientific problems require instead predicting solution fields from sparse, irregular, or partial observations under uncertainty. We introduce Neural Operator Processes (NOPs), a framework that unifies neural-process conditioning with neural-operator decoding to predict full output fields from limited context. NOPs condition on sparse joint input-output observations and support deterministic and probabilistic prediction within a shared encoder-decoder architecture. We study two conditioning strategies, convolutional pooled summaries and query-aligned attention, and analyze how their interaction with latent stochastic variables depends on PDE geometry. Across function regression and three PDE benchmarks, we find that sparse conditional operator learning is viable and can match dense-grid behavior in several regimes, that preserving local context-query geometry is essential in non-periodic settings but less so in spectrally smooth periodic regimes, and that uncertainty-aware operator learning succeeds when latent conditioning complements rather than overwrites the local geometric pathway. These results provide a basis for probabilistic operator learning under partial observations and help bridge operator learning and probabilistic meta-learning in function space.

AI.tldr: Neural Operator Processes (NOPs) combine neural processes with neural operators to predict full solution fields from sparse, irregular observations, supporting both deterministic and probabilistic predictions under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Traditional neural operators require dense input-output training fields and fully observed inputs at inference, but many scientific problems involve predicting solution fields from sparse, irregular, or partial observations under uncertainty, requiring a new approach.
AI.method: NOPs unify neural-process conditioning with neural-operator decoding using a shared encoder-decoder architecture. They propose two conditioning strategies: convolutional pooled summaries and query-aligned attention, and analyze their interaction with latent stochastic variables depending on PDE geometry.
AI.result: Sparse conditional operator learning is viable and can match dense-grid behavior in several regimes. Preserving local context-query geometry is essential in non-periodic settings but less so in spectrally smooth periodic regimes. Uncertainty-aware operator learning succeeds when latent conditioning complements rather than overwrites the local geometric pathway.
AI.conclusion: NOPs provide a foundation for probabilistic operator learning under partial observations, bridging operator learning and probabilistic meta-learning in function space, with insights into how conditioning strategies should adapt to problem geometry.

title: DT-GOL: Dual-Track Geometric Online Learning in Nonstationary Environment with Label Delay
summary:
Online learning is crucial for handling complex data streams in big data applications. Recent research has begun to focus on dynamic scenarios, i.e., non-stationary environments. However, a crucial yet often overlooked aspect is label latency, where new data may not receive labels in time due to the slow and expensive labeling process, thus hindering rapid adaptation to dynamic environments.
  To resolve this impasse, we propose Dual-Track Geometry Online Learning (DT-GOL), a novel framework that shifts from temporal compensation to spatial reasoning to bridge the supervised latency gap. By modeling the delay challenge as a semi-supervised task, we leverage real-time topological evolution of features as a reliable geometric surrogate for unobservable conceptual changes to achieve proactive supervised adaptation within the delay window. Unlike rigid self-training, we introduce a dynamic evidence calibration mechanism that distills geometric information into soft labels that perceive uncertainty, effectively mitigating the confirmation bias inherent in hard pseudo-labels. Furthermore, to resolve the stability-plasticity dilemma, we design a decoupled dual-track architecture in which a master learner serves as a stable anchor, updated strictly from delayed ground truth, while a transient branch leverages soft geometric knowledge for low-risk forward adaptation. Extensive experiments on real and synthetic datasets demonstrate that DT-GOL significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art baseline methods, especially in scenarios with concept drift.

AI.tldr: DT-GOL is a novel online learning framework that addresses label latency in non-stationary environments by using spatial geometric reasoning and a dual-track architecture with dynamic evidence calibration to bridge supervised latency gaps without confirmation bias.
AI.motivation: Current online learning methods struggle with dynamic non-stationary environments where label latency occurs - new data often doesn't receive timely labels due to slow/expensive labeling processes, which hinders rapid adaptation to concept drifts and changing environments.
AI.method: 1) Models label delay as a semi-supervised task, using real-time topological feature evolution as geometric surrogate for unobservable conceptual changes; 2) Introduces dynamic evidence calibration mechanism that distills geometric information into soft labels perceiving uncertainty (avoiding hard pseudo-labels); 3) Decoupled dual-track architecture: master learner (stable anchor updated from delayed ground truth) + transient branch (low-risk forward adaptation using soft geometric knowledge).
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real and synthetic datasets show DT-GOL significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art baseline methods, especially in scenarios with concept drift and label latency challenges.
AI.conclusion: DT-GOL successfully addresses the supervised latency gap in online learning for non-stationary environments by shifting from temporal compensation to spatial geometric reasoning, avoiding confirmation bias through soft labels, and resolving stability-plasticity trade-off via dual-track architecture, enabling proactive adaptation within delay windows.

title: PG-MAP: Joint MAP Optimization for Inference-Time Alignment of Diffusion and Flow-Matching Models
summary: Inference-time alignment of pretrained text-to-image models is typically performed along a single control axis, such as classifier-free guidance, attention editing, or reward-based latent perturbations. This limitation prevents modeling joint dependencies between conditioning and latent variables and hinders transfer across generative transports. We propose PG-MAP, a training-free framework that formulates inference-time alignment as a trajectory-level Gibbs-MAP / proximal energy optimization over the conditioning $c$ and latent state $z_t$ via a forward-consistency coupling, optionally guided by a frozen preference reward. This joint formulation enables coordinated updates across modalities while remaining compatible with both diffusion and flow-matching models through transport-specific adaptations. Across diffusion backbones (SD~1.5, SDXL), PG-MAP consistently improves alignment metrics such as PickScore and Aesthetic, and can be effectively combined with tuned classifier-free guidance to achieve the strongest overall performance. On flow-matching models (SD3.5-medium), the framework reduces to a latent-only variant, achieving $\mathbf{91.9\%}$ PickScore and $75.7\%$ HPS win rates against a static baseline, with controlled experiments ruling out noise-related artifacts. Human evaluations further confirm consistent preference over strong baselines, including tuned CFG and compute-matched universal guidance. Finally, an oracle-routing analysis shows that the relative importance of conditioning and latent optimization depends on prompt types, surfacing further headroom that a per-prompt selector could exploit.

AI.tldr: PG-MAP is a training-free framework that formulates inference-time alignment of text-to-image models as joint optimization over conditioning and latent variables using Gibbs-MAP/proximal energy optimization with forward-consistency coupling, enabling coordinated updates across modalities for improved alignment.
AI.motivation: Current inference-time alignment methods typically operate along single control axes (e.g., classifier-free guidance, attention editing, reward-based latent perturbations), which prevents modeling joint dependencies between conditioning and latent variables and hinders transfer across different generative transports.
AI.method: PG-MAP formulates inference-time alignment as a trajectory-level Gibbs-MAP/proximal energy optimization over conditioning (c) and latent state (z_t) via forward-consistency coupling, optionally guided by frozen preference rewards. It supports both diffusion and flow-matching models with transport-specific adaptations, enabling coordinated updates across modalities.
AI.result: PG-MAP consistently improves alignment metrics (PickScore and Aesthetic scores) across diffusion backbones (SD1.5, SDXL). On flow-matching models (SD3.5-medium), it achieves 91.9% PickScore and 75.7% HPS win rates against static baselines. Human evaluations confirm preference over strong baselines including tuned CFG and compute-matched universal guidance.
AI.conclusion: PG-MAP provides an effective training-free framework for joint optimization of conditioning and latent variables during inference, demonstrating improved alignment across different model architectures. The method shows that relative importance of conditioning vs. latent optimization depends on prompt types, suggesting further improvements could be achieved with per-prompt routing strategies.

title: Attacking the Trusted Imagination: Oracle-Level Integrity Attacks on Imagine-then-Act World Models
summary: Many recent vision-language-action (VLA) policies adopt an imagine-then-act design. A world-action model (WAM) first imagines a short future as a latent trajectory z~, on which the action is then conditioned. We identify this trusted imagination, rather than the reactive policy, as the exposed attack surface.
