Our results provide practical guidance for developing compact machine learning (ML) models for real-time GNSS interference monitoring on embedded platforms (iMXRT1062 MCU, Raspberry Pi Zero 2W, and Raspberry Pi 5).

AI.tldr: The paper investigates efficient deep learning methods for GNSS interference monitoring on resource-constrained embedded hardware by combining model compression techniques with hardware-aware neural architecture search.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental tension between needing increasingly expressive deep neural networks for robust GNSS interference classification/characterization and the requirement for fast, memory-efficient inference on embedded hardware that must process large volumes of raw IQ samples in real time.
AI.method: The approach uses iterative structured pruning, post-training static quantization, and hardware-aware zero-shot neural architecture search (NAS) applied to MCUNet as a compact baseline. These techniques are evaluated for their impact on model size, computational complexity, and memory usage.
AI.result: Experiments on a GNSS interference dataset show that combining model compression with hardware-aware design optimizes models for embedded deployment while maintaining task performance. The approach provides practical guidance for real-time GNSS interference monitoring.
AI.conclusion: The combination of compression techniques and hardware-aware neural architecture search enables the development of compact, efficient ML models suitable for real-time GNSS interference monitoring on embedded platforms like iMXRT1062 MCU and Raspberry Pi devices.

title: Deep learning-based detection of cessation of breathing in pre-term infants
comment: 14 pages main text, 8 figures. Submitted to IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics (JBHI)
summary:
Apnoea of prematurity is characterised by recurrent episodes of cessation of breathing and remains difficult to detect reliably using routinely monitored physiological signals in the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU). Existing bedside monitors rely primarily on respiratory rate and oxygen saturation thresholds, often generating high false-positive alarm rates and missing short or irregular events. Improving automated detection using routinely acquired clinical signals could enhance identification of clinically meaningful events without additional sensing hardware.
  We evaluated deep learning-based detection of apnoea-related Cessation Of BrEathing (COBE) events using impedance pneumography (IP), electrocardiography (ECG), and photoplethysmography (PPG) signals from approximately 430 hours of NICU recordings collected from 24 pre-term infants. Three independent reviewers annotated COBE events, producing a dataset of 346 COBE and 608 non-COBE events. We compared a shallow convolutional neural network (CNN), residual networks (ResNets), and a ConvNeXt architecture using an independent held-out test set.
  Across all architectures, detection performance was influenced more strongly by signal modality than by architectural complexity. Unimodal IP-based models achieved balanced accuracies of 86.8-88.0%, outperforming ECG-derived (62.6-69.7%) and PPG-derived (65.1-66.4%) respiratory surrogates. Multimodal fusion yielded modest improvements over IP alone. The best-performing model, a ConvNeXt architecture combining IP and PPG inputs, achieved 88.7% balanced accuracy and an F1 score of 0.75 on the independent test set.
  These findings demonstrate that deep learning models applied to routinely monitored NICU signals can reliably detect COBE events and highlight the importance of signal modality in data-constrained neonatal monitoring settings.

AI.tldr: Deep learning models using routine NICU signals can detect apnea-related breathing cessation events in preterm infants, with impedance pneumography (IP) showing the best performance compared to ECG and PPG signals.
AI.motivation: Current apnea detection in NICUs relies on respiratory rate and oxygen saturation thresholds, leading to high false-positive rates and missed events. Automated detection using routine clinical signals could improve identification of clinically meaningful apnea events without additional hardware.
AI.method: Evaluated deep learning models (shallow CNN, ResNets, ConvNeXt) using 430 hours of NICU recordings from 24 preterm infants. Used impedance pneumography (IP), electrocardiography (ECG), and photoplethysmography (PPG) signals with 346 COBE and 608 non-COBE events annotated by three reviewers.
AI.result: Signal modality influenced performance more than architectural complexity. IP-based models achieved 86.8-88.0% balanced accuracy, outperforming ECG (62.6-69.7%) and PPG (65.1-66.4%). Best model (ConvNeXt with IP+PPG) achieved 88.7% balanced accuracy and 0.75 F1 score.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning with routine NICU signals reliably detects COBE events, highlighting the importance of signal modality in data-constrained neonatal monitoring. IP is most effective, with multimodal fusion providing modest improvements.

title: Unlocking In-Context Learning in Audio-Language Models from Decentralized Medical Audio
summary: Clinical audio diagnosis in low-resource settings requires models that identify conditions from minimal examples without large annotated corpora. We propose Federated Self-Contextualization (FSC), a multimodal language model framework for in-context clinical audio diagnosis across federated hospital clients. FSC constructs pseudo-label episodes via unsupervised clustering of audio representations, bypassing scarce real diagnostic labels, and enables contextual reasoning from support-query pairs. Our progressive three-stage pipeline first aligns audio embeddings with the language model via caption-based pretraining, then adapts it for episodic in-context inference through federated optimization. At test time, given a small labeled support set, the model diagnoses an unseen query through multimodal reasoning. On held-out respiratory and cardiac conditions, FSC achieves 71.6% accuracy in 2-way 2-shot evaluation, outperforming audio-language baselines by over 9%.

AI.tldr: FSC: Federated Self-Contextualization framework for clinical audio diagnosis in low-resource settings using multimodal language models and in-context learning without extensive labeled data.
AI.motivation: Clinical audio diagnosis in low-resource settings requires models that can identify medical conditions from minimal examples without access to large annotated datasets. There's a need for federated approaches that work across hospital clients with scarce diagnostic labels.
AI.method: Proposes Federated Self-Contextualization (FSC) framework: 1) Constructs pseudo-label episodes via unsupervised clustering of audio representations (bypassing scarce real labels), 2) Progressive three-stage pipeline: audio embedding alignment with language model via caption-based pretraining, followed by adaptation for episodic in-context inference through federated optimization, 3) At test time, model diagnoses unseen queries from small labeled support sets through multimodal reasoning.
AI.result: Achieves 71.6% accuracy in 2-way 2-shot evaluation on held-out respiratory and cardiac conditions, outperforming audio-language baselines by over 9%.
AI.conclusion: FSC enables effective clinical audio diagnosis in low-resource federated settings through self-contextualization and in-context learning, demonstrating promising performance on respiratory and cardiac conditions with minimal labeled examples.

title: Dynamic multi-agent deep reinforcement learning-based pricing and incentivization approach in multimodal transportation networks
summary: In multimodal transportation systems, shared mobility services (SMSs) are promoted for their potential to enhance flexibility and reduce congestion. However, SMS demand is often concentrated in high-density areas, which can limit the effectiveness and accessibility for various commuter groups. This uneven integration challenges transportation system efficiency, especially in terms of emissions and spatial equity. Addressing these issues requires coordination among multiple stakeholders whose objectives frequently conflict. Whereas authorities aim to ensure sustainable and equitable mobility, SMS providers focus on revenue maximization, and travelers seek to minimize personal travel costs. This paper proposes a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning framework that captures these interactions through dynamic pricing and incentivization strategies for SMSs and public transport. The framework integrates two reinforcement learning (RL) agents: (i) a public authority that allocates spatio-temporal public transport incentives to improve equity, emissions, and efficiency, and (ii) an SMS provider that dynamically adjusts fares to optimize revenue. The agents interact with the transportation system and adapt strategies in response to evolving demand, congestion, and network conditions. Numerical experiments conducted over a three-hour morning peak period show that dynamic incentivization effectively reduces congestion peaks, lowers commuters' costs by around 20% and emissions by approximately 10%, while nearly doubling public transport profit and supporting a more equitable distribution of benefits. When combined with dynamic SMS pricing, the two RL agents demonstrate the ability to balance conflicting objectives between private providers and public authorities. The proposed approach provides a decision-support tool for sustainable and equitable multimodal mobility planning.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent deep reinforcement learning framework for coordinating shared mobility services and public transport through dynamic pricing and incentives to balance stakeholder conflicts.
AI.motivation: Uneven integration of shared mobility services (SMSs) in multimodal systems limits effectiveness and accessibility, creating challenges for transportation efficiency, emissions, and spatial equity due to conflicting stakeholder objectives between public authorities, SMS providers, and travelers.
AI.method: A multi-agent deep reinforcement learning framework with two RL agents: (1) public authority that allocates spatio-temporal public transport incentives for equity, emissions, and efficiency, and (2) SMS provider that dynamically adjusts fares for revenue optimization, both interacting with the transportation system and adapting to evolving demand and network conditions.
AI.result: Dynamic incentivization reduces congestion peaks, lowers commuter costs by ~20% and emissions by ~10%, nearly doubles public transport profit, and supports more equitable benefit distribution. Combined with dynamic SMS pricing, the two RL agents effectively balance conflicting objectives between private providers and public authorities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-agent RL framework provides an effective decision-support tool for sustainable and equitable multimodal mobility planning by enabling coordination among conflicting stakeholders through dynamic pricing and incentive strategies.

title: Exposing the Illusion of Erasure in Knowledge Editing for LLMs
summary: Knowledge Editing (KE) has emerged as a frontier for updating specific facts in LLMs without costly retraining, but its reliability and underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. In this work, we examine KE from an adversarial elicitation perspective, revealing that edited knowledge is often not fully erased and continues to surface, with consistent failures observed across diverse model architectures. To explain this behavior, we conduct a mechanistic analysis of popular KE methods. We show that low-rank updates do not overwrite existing knowledge but instead redistribute it within the model's representation space. Furthermore, we find that these methods act as targeted suppression mechanisms that reduce the likelihood of expressing original facts, rather than removing them from the model. Analysis of the loss landscape reveals that edited knowledge lies in narrow, anisotropic regions that are highly sensitive to perturbations, making them highly vulnerable to indirect prompting and adversarial attacks. By exposing these profound architectural vulnerabilities, our work proves that KE algorithms are inherently bypassable and motivates a fundamental reevaluation of how we deploy post-hoc updates in several LLM applications.

AI.tldr: Knowledge editing in LLMs doesn't fully erase facts but redistributes them, making edited knowledge vulnerable to adversarial recovery through indirect prompting.
AI.motivation: To understand the reliability and underlying mechanisms of Knowledge Editing (KE) in LLMs, which updates specific facts without retraining but has poorly understood vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Examine KE from an adversarial elicitation perspective, conduct mechanistic analysis of popular KE methods, analyze loss landscapes, and test vulnerability to indirect prompting and adversarial attacks across diverse model architectures.
AI.result: Edited knowledge is not fully erased and continues to surface; low-rank updates redistribute rather than overwrite knowledge; KE methods suppress original facts rather than remove them; edited knowledge lies in narrow, anisotropic regions highly sensitive to perturbations.
AI.conclusion: KE algorithms are inherently bypassable due to profound architectural vulnerabilities, requiring fundamental reevaluation of post-hoc update deployment in LLM applications.

title: Non-asymptotic estimates of the minimal risk in statistical learning
summary: In this paper we prove some concentration inequalities for two types of error probabilities in the Empirical Risk Principle (ERP) in statistical learning, which provide a lower bound and an upper bound for the minimal risk (in terms of the minimal empirical risk) with non-asymptotic high confidence. The usual boundedness condition of the empirical risk function is relaxed to the Gaussian or exponential integrability condition. The confidence of the lower bound of the minimal risk is shown to be independent of the number of training parameters and the dimension of the input vectors, allowing one to detect the deficiency of a learning machine efficiently; and the confidence of the upper bound of the minimal risk is proved to be high provided that the sample size $n$ is much greater than the box dimension of the parameter set $Θ$ in the Orlicz metric $d_{ψ_1}$ associated with the risk functions. Our work is based on Talagrand's concentration inequalities (the sharp versions by Bousquet and Klein-Rio), transport-entropy inequalities and the recent progress in the theory of empirical processes and statistical learning.

AI.tldr: The paper develops concentration inequalities for error probabilities in Empirical Risk Minimization, providing non-asymptotic confidence bounds for minimal risk under relaxed boundedness conditions.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous non-asymptotic confidence bounds for the minimal risk in statistical learning, relaxing the usual boundedness condition of empirical risk functions to more realistic Gaussian or exponential integrability conditions.
AI.method: Uses Talagrand's concentration inequalities (sharp versions by Bousquet and Klein-Rio), transport-entropy inequalities, and tools from empirical process theory to derive concentration inequalities for error probabilities in Empirical Risk Minimization.
AI.result: Obtains lower and upper bounds for minimal risk with high confidence: lower bound confidence is independent of parameter count/dimension for efficient deficiency detection; upper bound requires sample size to exceed the box dimension of parameter set in Orlicz metric.
AI.conclusion: The developed concentration inequalities provide practical non-asymptotic confidence bounds for empirical risk minimization under realistic integrability conditions, enhancing the theoretical foundation for evaluating learning machine performance.

title: GRIMIP: A General Framework for Instance-Specific Configuration of MIP Solvers Using LLMs
summary: Configuring the hyperparameters of Mixed-integer programming (MIP) solvers is a high-dimensional, instance-dependent optimization problem where suboptimal settings can degrade solving time by orders of magnitude. Default configurations are often suboptimal, while traditional tuning methods either suffer from the ``cold-start'' problem and inefficient search or heavily rely on expert experience. This paper introduces \textbf{GRIMIP} (\textbf{\underline{G}}eneral \textbf{\underline{R}}easoning for \textbf{\underline{I}}nstance-specific \textbf{\underline{MIP}} configuration), a novel hybrid intelligence framework that synergistically integrates the semantic reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) with the sample-efficient search of Bayesian Optimization (BO). GRIMIP enables the LLM to function as a complete probabilistic surrogate within the BO loop, significantly improving performance and reducing sampling and evaluation costs. On seven benchmarks including MIPLIB, GRIMIP achieves over 40\% reduction in Primal-Dual Integral on hard instances, outperforming SMAC and other LLM-assisted BO methods. By granting LLMs sufficient autonomy, GRIMIP combines the expert-level reasoning of LLMs with the efficient search of BO, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: GRIMIP combines LLMs with Bayesian Optimization for automated MIP solver hyperparameter tuning, achieving over 40% reduction in Primal-Dual Integral on hard instances.
AI.motivation: MIP solver hyperparameter configuration is instance-dependent and high-dimensional; default settings are often suboptimal, while traditional tuning methods suffer from cold-start problems, inefficient search, or heavy reliance on expert experience.
AI.method: Hybrid intelligence framework that integrates LLMs' semantic reasoning capabilities with Bayesian Optimization's sample-efficient search, allowing LLM to function as a complete probabilistic surrogate within BO loop.
AI.result: On seven benchmarks including MIPLIB, GRIMIP achieves over 40% reduction in Primal-Dual Integral on hard instances, outperforming SMAC and other LLM-assisted BO methods.
AI.conclusion: GRIMIP successfully combines expert-level reasoning of LLMs with efficient search of BO to achieve state-of-the-art performance in MIP solver hyperparameter tuning by granting LLMs sufficient autonomy.

title: Superhuman AI for Generals.io Using Self-Play Reinforcement Learning
summary: We present a superhuman AI agent for Generals.io, a real-time strategy game that requires both long-horizon planning and short-term tactics under strong imperfect information. Trained for four days on 4x NVIDIA H200 GPUs, our agent reaches #1 on the public 1v1 leaderboard of over 5,000 human players, leading the second-ranked player by the same margin that separates second place from 25th, and beats the two top-ranked humans head-to-head with a combined 199-70 record across 269 ladder matches. A key enabler is a JAX-native simulator that reaches tens of millions of frames per second on a single GPU, roughly a 10,000x speedup over the prior simulator. On top of this, we train a vision transformer policy end-to-end by self-play with a policy-gradient loop and sparse win/loss reward, using top-advantage sample filtering and an exponential moving average of the policy parameters. Taken together, our findings highlight what matters, and what does not, once a fast simulator removes the data bottleneck.

AI.tldr: A superhuman AI agent achieves #1 rank on Generals.io leaderboard by combining a 10,000x faster simulator with end-to-end vision transformer training via self-play.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of creating AI agents for real-time strategy games like Generals.io that require both long-horizon planning and short-term tactics under strong imperfect information. Previous approaches were bottlenecked by slow simulation speeds.
AI.method: The key innovation is a JAX-native simulator that achieves tens of millions of frames per second (10,000x speedup). On this foundation, they train a vision transformer policy end-to-end using self-play with policy-gradient loop and sparse win/loss reward, employing techniques like top-advantage sample filtering and exponential moving average of policy parameters.
AI.result: The agent achieves #1 rank on the public 1v1 leaderboard (over 5,000 human players), with a margin that separates it from second place equivalent to the gap between second place and 25th place. It beats the two top-ranked humans 199-70 across 269 ladder matches, trained in just four days on 4x NVIDIA H200 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: The research demonstrates that once the data bottleneck is removed through fast simulation, what matters most is the combination of efficient simulator design and end-to-end learning methods, highlighting the diminishing importance of complex reward engineering when sufficient data throughput is available.

title: SOAP-Bubbles: Structured Weight Uncertainty for Neural Networks
summary: Structured weight-uncertainty can improve many aspects of deep learning, but it remains costly to estimate and difficult to implement. Here, we show that these issues can be addressed by adapting the SOAP optimizer. Our key idea is to run IVON, an existing diagonal-covariance variational method, in the eigenspace of SOAP's preconditioner and then use the preconditioner to transform the diagonal estimate into a non-diagonal covariance. The resulting method has costs similar to those of SOAP and requires no drastic changes to training pipelines. We call the posteriors obtained in this way SOAP-Bubbles and our new optimizer Eigenspace-VON (EVON). We show that, for logistic regression, EVON recovers the exact Gaussian covariance and that, for language model pretraining, it yields significantly better results than existing diagonal-covariance methods. Our work makes it easier to estimate more expressive posterior distributions for deep learning at scale.

AI.tldr: SOAP-Bubbles: A method that uses SOAP optimizer's preconditioner to transform diagonal variational inference into non-diagonal covariance estimates, achieving expressive posterior distributions at low computational cost.
AI.motivation: Structured weight-uncertainty in deep learning is valuable but typically costly to estimate and difficult to implement in practice, creating barriers to wider adoption of uncertainty-aware methods.
AI.method: Adapt the SOAP optimizer to run IVON (diagonal-covariance variational method) in the eigenspace of SOAP's preconditioner, then use the preconditioner to transform the diagonal covariance estimate into a non-diagonal covariance. This creates SOAP-Bubbles posteriors and the new EVON optimizer.
AI.result: EVON recovers exact Gaussian covariance for logistic regression and yields significantly better results than existing diagonal-covariance methods for language model pretraining, while maintaining computational costs similar to SOAP and requiring minimal changes to training pipelines.
AI.conclusion: The work enables easier estimation of more expressive posterior distributions for deep learning at scale by making structured uncertainty estimation more practical and computationally efficient.

title: Adaptive Hard-Soft Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Robust Boundary-Constrained PDE Solving
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) provide an effective way to solve partial differential equations (PDEs) by embedding physical principles into the learning process. However, the conventional PINN formulation, in which all constraints are imposed as soft penalty terms within a composite loss, often exhibits slow convergence, sensitivity to loss weight scaling, and inaccurate boundary enforcement due to poor conditioning of the optimization landscape. To address these limitations, this study proposes a unified hard--soft physics--informed neural network (HSPINN) with adaptive loss weighting. In this framework, Dirichlet and periodic boundary conditions are enforced exactly by construction through analytical or polynomial lifting, masking functions, and periodic feature mappings, while the governing PDE residuals, Neumann fluxes, and initial conditions are treated as soft constraints. An inverse-share softmax strategy dynamically balances the relative importance of individual loss components during training, eliminating manual penalty tuning and improving gradient stability. This formulation ensures boundary admissibility throughout optimization and enhances convergence efficiency and numerical robustness. Applications to representative elliptic (Poisson), parabolic (Burgers), and hyperbolic (convection with periodic boundaries) problems demonstrate that HSPINN consistently achieves faster convergence, higher accuracy, and greater stability than conventional PINNs, establishing a general and scalable foundation for physics-constrained deep learning across science and technology.

AI.tldr: A novel PINN framework (HSPINN) uses exact hard constraints for boundaries with adaptive soft weighting for PDE residuals, achieving faster convergence and better accuracy than conventional PINNs.
AI.motivation: Conventional PINNs suffer from slow convergence, sensitivity to loss weight scaling, and poor boundary enforcement due to soft penalty formulation and ill-conditioned optimization landscapes.
AI.method: Proposes HSPINN: Dirichlet/periodic BCs enforced exactly via analytical/polynomial lifting, masking functions, and periodic mappings; PDE residuals, Neumann fluxes, and ICs as soft constraints with inverse-share softmax adaptive weighting.
AI.result: HSPINN achieves faster convergence, higher accuracy, and greater stability on elliptic (Poisson), parabolic (Burgers), and hyperbolic (convection with periodic boundaries) problems compared to conventional PINNs.
AI.conclusion: HSPINN establishes a general, scalable foundation for physics-constrained deep learning with guaranteed boundary admissibility and improved optimization efficiency.

title: Rethinking Molecular Graph Backdoors under Chemistry-aware Admission
summary: Backdoor attacks on molecular graph neural networks (GNNs) are typically evaluated as abstract graph edits, but real molecular learning pipelines do not train on arbitrary graphs. Molecular records must first survive parsing, sanitization, canonicalization, and graph-string consistency checks. We formalize this overlooked admission stage as ChemGuard, an operational protocol for testing whether a submitted molecular record can enter a realistic learning pipeline, while complementing existing defenses. ChemGuard admits a record only when its molecular string is sanitizable and the graph reconstructed from that string matches the submitted molecular graph. Under this operational view, many existing graph-based backdoors lose much of their apparent efficacy because their poisons are chemically invalid or representation-inconsistent. We then show that admission checks alone are insufficient to rule out molecular backdoors. We propose ChemBack, an admission-aware molecular backdoor attack that constructs chemically feasible motif-anchor attachments and ranks admitted candidates by fingerprint-based Tanimoto similarity to clean target-class molecules. ChemBack is model-free during trigger selection, using molecular structures, target labels, fingerprints, and public validity checks, but no victim model, surrogate GNN, learned embedding, gradient, logit, or training-code access. Across molecular benchmarks, validators, architectures, and defenses, \textbf{ChemBack} achieves high attack success with fully admitted poisons while preserving clean accuracy. Our results reveal a two-sided lesson, chemistry-aware admission suppresses many graph-only backdoors, yet chemically valid and target-aligned molecular backdoors remain a practical threat.

AI.tldr: ChemGuard formalizes molecular pipeline admission checks that filter out many graph-based backdoors, but ChemBack demonstrates chemically valid admission-aware attacks that remain effective.
AI.motivation: Existing backdoor attacks on molecular GNNs ignore real-world molecular learning pipelines that have validation stages (parsing, sanitization, canonicalization, consistency checks) that filter out many chemically invalid attacks, creating a false sense of security.
AI.method: 1) Formalize ChemGuard - admission protocol requiring sanitizable molecular strings and graph-string consistency. 2) Develop ChemBack - model-free attack that constructs chemically feasible motif-anchor attachments and ranks admitted candidates by Tanimoto similarity to target molecules using fingerprints, without needing victim model access.
AI.result: ChemGuard effectively suppresses many existing graph-only backdoors by filtering chemically invalid/representation-inconsistent poisons. ChemBack achieves high attack success with fully admitted poisons while preserving clean accuracy across benchmarks, validators, architectures, and defenses.
AI.conclusion: There's a two-sided lesson: chemistry-aware admission suppresses many graph-only backdoors, yet chemically valid and target-aligned molecular backdoors like ChemBack remain practical threats requiring continued defense research.

title: Convergence of Gradient Descent for General Neural Network Architectures Beyond the NTK Regime
comment: arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2506.24120
summary: Training dynamics is central to understanding neural networks, yet its theoretical analysis remains difficult even for simple architectures and becomes substantially more challenging for general modern architectures. In this paper, we propose a convergence framework for analyzing gradient descent (GD) dynamics under a broad family of neural network architectures and datasets beyond the neural tangent kernel (NTK) regime. The framework is formulated at the level of network blocks and covers architectures including pre-normalized multi-layer transformers. More precisely, under mild assumptions, we prove that for almost all initializations, GD with regular learning rates converges to the neighbourhood of a stationary point. This is mainly proved by establishing an iterate-dependent PL-type inequality through analyticity and measure-zero arguments, and by proving Lipschitz smoothness along the GD trajectory through polynomial generalized smoothness and a local relaxed dissipative condition. We further interpret the theorem under Xavier initialization and practical architectural scaling, showing that the learning rate scale depends on the depth and effective bottleneck dimensions rather than the largest width. Finally, we derive structural nondegeneracy implications for residual connections and function composition, and provide a generic characterization of global minimizers within our framework.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework proving gradient descent convergence for diverse neural architectures (including transformers) under mild assumptions, with learning rates depending on depth and bottleneck dimensions rather than width.
AI.motivation: Training dynamics analysis is crucial but difficult, especially for modern architectures beyond NTK regime; existing theoretical approaches struggle with complex architectures like transformers.
AI.method: Propose convergence framework at block level for broad architectures; prove GD convergence using iterate-dependent PL inequalities via analyticity/measure-zero arguments, polynomial generalized smoothness, and local relaxed dissipative conditions.
AI.result: For almost all initializations, GD with regular learning rates converges to stationary point neighborhoods; learning rate scales with depth and bottleneck dimensions (not largest width); structural implications for residual connections and composition.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides theoretical guarantees for GD convergence across diverse modern architectures, offering insights into architectural scaling and initialization effects, advancing beyond NTK limitations.

title: Distribution-Aware Diffusion-LLM for Robust Ultra-Long-Term Time Series Forecasting
comment: 18 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables. Accepted at 35th International Conference on Artificial Neural Networks (ICANN 2026)
summary: Time series forecasting is a fundamental machine learning task. Recent work has explored Large Language Models (LLMs) for this purpose due to their strong generalization, pattern recognition, and zero-shot or few-shot capabilities. Despite their suitability for long-context learning, LLMs face challenges in multimodal settings: they lack calibrated probabilistic modeling for non-text data and struggle to align heterogeneous representations. To address these issues, we propose a new framework Diffusion-LLM that integrates a conditional diffusion model into an LLM-based forecasting pipeline. This joint design enables learning the conditional distribution of future data while improving semantic alignment in a shared latent space. We evaluate Diffusion-LLM on six long-term forecasting benchmarks, including ETT, Weather, and ECL. Our method consistently outperforms existing LLM-based baseline, achieving notable gains in ultra-long-term and few-shot forecasting and demonstrating the value of distribution-aware regularization for enhancing robustness and generalization in time series LLMs.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-LLM: A framework that integrates conditional diffusion models with LLMs for time series forecasting to address LLMs' limitations in probabilistic modeling and multimodal representation alignment.
AI.motivation: While LLMs show promise for time series forecasting due to their generalization and pattern recognition capabilities, they face challenges in multimodal settings: they lack calibrated probabilistic modeling for non-text data and struggle to align heterogeneous representations.
AI.method: Proposes Diffusion-LLM, a framework that integrates a conditional diffusion model into an LLM-based forecasting pipeline. This joint design enables learning the conditional distribution of future data while improving semantic alignment in a shared latent space.
AI.result: Evaluated on six long-term forecasting benchmarks (ETT, Weather, ECL) and consistently outperformed existing LLM-based baselines. Achieved notable gains in ultra-long-term and few-shot forecasting, demonstrating the value of distribution-aware regularization for enhancing robustness and generalization in time series LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The integration of diffusion models with LLMs successfully addresses LLMs' limitations in multimodal time series forecasting, providing better probabilistic modeling and representation alignment, leading to improved performance in long-term and few-shot forecasting scenarios.

title: Physics-Informed Modeling for Wood Thermal Analysis and Prediction
summary: Wood materials exhibit complex, spatially varying thermal properties that challenge traditional architectural assumptions of material homogeneity. Although data-driven approaches can directly map wood RGB images to their corresponding thermal responses, they operate as uninterpretable black boxes that prioritize statistical correlation and may absorb experimental noise rather than thermodynamic plausibility. To address these limitations, we present physics-informed deep learning frameworks that integrate partial differential equations (PDEs) to predict pixel-level thermal responses of spatially heterogeneous wood materials using wood RGB images and testbed temperature maps. Specifically, we investigate two distinct approaches to enforcing a normalized 2D steady-state heat transfer equation derived from the general heat transfer equation: Physics-Informed Convolutional Neural Networks (PICNNs), which embed physics as a soft penalty term in the loss function, and Physics-Integrated Convolutional Neural Networks (PInteCNNs), which hard-code an analytical approximator-predictor-corrector solver directly into convolutional neural networks. To validate our proposed approaches, we collect three real-world multimodal datasets of Poplar, Grandis Cross-Cut (Grandis-CC), and Grandis Radial-Cut (Grandis-RC) wood samples. We further demonstrate that embedding physical inductive biases successfully balances predictive accuracy, physical interpretability, and intra-species diversity, outperforming data-driven approaches in handling complex wood material heterogeneity and enabling the extraction of interpretable physical parameters. Project: https://zekifayes.github.io/pim

AI.tldr: Physics-informed deep learning frameworks integrate heat transfer PDEs with convolutional neural networks to predict thermal responses of heterogeneous wood materials from RGB images, outperforming purely data-driven approaches by balancing accuracy with physical interpretability.
AI.motivation: Traditional architectural assumptions treat wood as homogeneous, while data-driven approaches for mapping wood RGB images to thermal responses are uninterpretable black boxes that may learn experimental noise rather than true thermodynamic relationships.
AI.method: Two physics-informed approaches: 1) Physics-Informed CNNs (PICNNs) with physics as soft penalty in loss function, and 2) Physics-Integrated CNNs (PInteCNNs) that hard-code an analytical approximator-predictor-corrector solver directly into the network architecture, both based on normalized 2D steady-state heat transfer equations.
AI.result: The frameworks were validated on three real-world multimodal datasets (Poplar, Grandis-CC, Grandis-RC) and successfully balanced predictive accuracy, physical interpretability, and intra-species diversity while handling complex wood heterogeneity better than data-driven approaches.
AI.conclusion: Embedding physical inductive biases into deep learning models enables accurate prediction of wood thermal responses while maintaining interpretability, allowing extraction of meaningful physical parameters that address limitations of black-box data-driven approaches.

title: HyperQuant: A Rate-Distortion-Optimal Quantization Pipeline for Large Language and Diffusion Models
summary:
We present HyperQuant (Hadamard, optimallY Packing, Entropy Rice-coding), a unified post-training quantization pipeline for the weights and the KV cache of large language and diffusion transformers. Across a suite of self-contained experiments (Table 1), HyperQuant outperforms the recent HIGGS scheme at every operating point from 3 to 5 bits per scalar (bps) on weights, and beats both TurboQuant and OCTOPUS on KV quantization down to 1.7 bps. Beyond the LLM setting, HyperQuant quantizes the 19B-parameter LTX-2 DiT video model with no observable per-frame artifacts. End-to-end on an H100 at 4 bps, HyperQuant compresses the linear weights ~3.9x and the KV cache ~3.79x at near-lossless quality.
  HyperQuant combines four known ideas into a single construction: (i) a per-tile Randomized Hadamard Transform that makes the per-coordinate distribution of weights and activations approximately Gaussian; (ii) quantization to a low-dimensional optimal lattice (E8, D4, A2, or Z); (iii) lossless bit-stripping and near-entropy-optimal variable-length Rice coding of the lattice indices; and (iv) bias-correction methods for the KV cache that keep the reconstruction unbiased under inner products, preserving attention semantics. We further integrate the pipeline with 8-bit and 4-bit Tensor-Core MMA paths (fp8-e4m3, int8, nvfp4, mxfp4), and find that int8 beats fp8 on the post-RHT lattice output. Project page: https://moonmath.ai/hyperquant/

AI.tldr: HyperQuant is a unified post-training quantization pipeline for LLM and diffusion transformer weights and KV cache, combining Hadamard transform, optimal lattice quantization, Rice coding, and bias correction to achieve superior compression with near-lossless quality.
AI.motivation: There's a need for efficient post-training quantization methods that can compress both weights and KV cache in large language and diffusion transformers while maintaining near-lossless quality and preserving attention semantics, outperforming existing schemes like HIGGS, TurboQuant, and OCTOPUS.
AI.method: Combines four techniques: (1) per-tile Randomized Hadamard Transform to make weight/activation distributions approximately Gaussian; (2) quantization to low-dimensional optimal lattices (E8, D4, A2, or Z); (3) lossless bit-stripping and near-entropy-optimal Rice coding; (4) bias-correction for KV cache to keep reconstruction unbiased under inner products and preserve attention semantics.
AI.result: Outperforms HIGGS at 3-5 bps on weights, beats TurboQuant and OCTOPUS on KV quantization down to 1.7 bps, quantizes 19B-parameter LTX-2 DiT video model without observable artifacts, achieves ~3.9x weight compression and ~3.79x KV cache compression at 4 bps with near-lossless quality on H100.
AI.conclusion: HyperQuant presents an effective unified quantization pipeline that successfully combines multiple known techniques to achieve superior compression performance for both weights and KV cache in transformer models, with integration into modern tensor core compute paths demonstrating practical deployment viability.

title: Differential Spectral Damping Gap Adaptive Regularization for Ill-Conditioned Kernel Methods
comment: 11 pages, 3 tables, 1 figure. Complete source code and experiments are available at https://github.com/Praveg432/dsd-regularization
summary: Kernel methods requiring matrix inversion -- particularly Least-Squares Twin Support Vector Machines (LSTSVM) -- suffer from exponential eigenvalue decay in their system matrices, producing severely ill-conditioned problems where standard Tikhonov regularization applies uniform damping regardless of eigenvector reliability. We propose Differential Spectral Damping (DSD), a regularization formula that adapts its penalty to localized eigengap structure: preserving eigenvectors with large spectral gaps (reliable per Davis-Kahan perturbation theory) while aggressively suppressing those with small gaps (directionally corrupted beyond recovery). We motivate DSD through a principled design procedure grounded in the Davis-Kahan $\sin(Θ)$ theorem, systematically deriving the requirements for a reliability-aware damping function and selecting the exponential form for its smoothness, differentiability, and natural saturation properties. Through rigorous paired testing with fairly optimized baselines (including gradient-optimized Tikhonov receiving equal optimization opportunity), we demonstrate that DSD improves LSTSVM classification accuracy by +4.8 percentage points on real-world GINA ($d=970$, Cohen's $d = 4.49$, $p < 0.0001$), +10.4 percentage points at $d=200$, and +2.6 percentage points on Madelon ($d=500$) -- all using only principled spectral initialization while Tikhonov receives grid search. For pre-image reconstruction on manifold data, DSD ties Tikhonov at high perturbation noise ($p=0.99$) but slightly underperforms at lower noise levels; both reduce naive inversion error by $66\times$. We characterize the precise operating regime ($d \geq 100$, condition number $> 10^3$) and document where simpler methods suffice, providing practitioners with clear deployment guidance.

AI.tldr: Differential Spectral Damping (DSD) adapts regularization penalties based on eigenvector reliability determined by eigengap size, outperforming uniform Tikhonov regularization for high-dimensional kernel methods and delivering significant accuracy improvements on real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Kernel methods like LSTSVM suffer from exponential eigenvalue decay leading to severely ill-conditioned problems. Standard Tikhonov regularization applies uniform damping regardless of eigenvector reliability, treating all eigenvectors equally despite varying directional corruption levels.
AI.method: Differential Spectral Damping (DSD) - a regularization formula that adapts penalties based on eigengap structure. It preserves eigenvectors with large spectral gaps (reliable according to Davis-Kahan theory) while aggressively suppressing those with small gaps. The method uses an exponential form for its damping function due to smoothness, differentiability, and saturation properties.
AI.result: DSD improves LSTSVM classification accuracy by +4.8 percentage points on GINA dataset (d=970), +10.4 percentage points at d=200, and +2.6 percentage points on Madelon dataset (d=500). For pre-image reconstruction, DSD ties Tikhonov at high noise levels but slightly underperforms at lower noise; both reduce naive inversion error by 66x. The method works best when d ≥ 100 and condition number > 10³.
AI.conclusion: DSD provides a superior regularization approach for kernel methods like LSTSVM by adaptively damping eigenvectors based on their reliability through spectral gap analysis, delivering significant accuracy improvements in high-dimensional, ill-conditioned settings while providing clear practical guidance on when to use it.

title: Leveraging Similarities in Multi-Armed Bandits
summary: In many online learning and bandit problems, the actions we consider possess inherent similarities--for instance because they share latent traits, tags, or hierarchical structure. We study online learning with a similarity-structured action set, encoded by a rooted tree whose leaves are the actions and whose levels quantify how closely two actions are related. The loss sequence is assumed tree-compatible: losses of similar actions are constrained to be close. We establish an impossibility result showing that usual one-point bandit feedback cannot, in general, leverage range or tree-induced similarity, even under very strong similarity constraints. We then provide a unified set of algorithms which adapt to a wide range of richer feedback models, from semi-bandit feedback down to multi-point bandit protocols, including the minimal two-point feedback setting. We show these algorithms exhibit best-of-both-worlds guarantees and provably exploit action similarities by replacing the number of actions $K$ by a similarity-aware effective number of actions $K_{\mathrm{eff}}$ in the regret bounds. As an application, we show that under two-point feedback, it is possible to achieve $\sqrt{T}$ regret in Lipschitz bandits when $d \leq 2$.

AI.tldr: The paper studies online learning with similarity-structured action sets (encoded as trees) where similar actions have similar losses. It shows one-point bandit feedback cannot exploit these similarities, then provides algorithms for richer feedback models that adapt to action structure by replacing K with K_eff in regret bounds.
AI.motivation: Many online learning/bandit problems involve actions with inherent similarities (shared traits, tags, hierarchical structure), but existing algorithms may not effectively leverage these structural relationships to improve performance.
AI.method: Models action similarities using rooted trees (leaves as actions, levels quantify similarity). Studies tree-compatible losses (similar actions have similar losses). Proves impossibility for one-point feedback, then develops unified algorithms for richer feedback models (semi-bandit to multi-point including two-point feedback).
AI.result: Shows one-point bandit feedback cannot leverage tree-induced similarities even under strong constraints. Provides algorithms with best-of-both-worlds guarantees that replace K with effective number K_eff in regret bounds. As application: achieves √T regret in Lipschitz bandits with d≤2 under two-point feedback.
AI.conclusion: Richer feedback beyond one-point is necessary to exploit action similarities in structured bandits. The proposed algorithms can effectively leverage hierarchical action structures across various feedback models, providing substantial improvements over standard approaches that ignore action relationships.

title: GRINQH: Graded Input-based Quantization Hierarchy for Efficient LLM Generation
summary: Autoregressive decoding with LLMs is primarily bottlenecked by GPU memory bandwidth, especially in edge-computing settings. While quantization is essential for mitigating this bottleneck, most existing methods treat inference as a uniform process and fail to account for the asymmetry between the compute-bound prefill stage and the memory-bound decoding stage. We propose GRINQH (GRaded INput-based Quantization Hierarchy), a weight-only post-training quantization framework that accelerates decoding by unifying quantization and sparsification. GRINQH leverages activation magnitudes as a proxy for computational importance to dynamically assign weight channels to different precision levels, enabling flexible average bit widths during decoding. Evaluated on Llama3 and Qwen3 models, GRINQH outperforms state-of-the-art fixed- and mixed-precision baselines at comparable 3- and 4-bit settings, even enabling effective 2-bit generation. We experimentally verify theoretical speedups by leveraging a hierarchical nested memory layout for multi-precision storage in a custom GPU kernel. Ultimately, GRINQH establishes a new state-of-the-art Pareto frontier for LLM generation, enabling a dynamic trade-off between generation quality and inference speed.

AI.tldr: GRINQH is a novel quantization framework that uses activation magnitudes to dynamically assign weight channels to different precision levels, achieving better performance than existing quantization methods for LLM inference.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive decoding with LLMs is bottlenecked by GPU memory bandwidth, especially in edge computing. Existing quantization methods treat inference uniformly and fail to account for the asymmetry between compute-bound prefill (initial processing) and memory-bound decoding (token generation).
AI.method: GRINQH (GRaded INput-based Quantization Hierarchy) is a weight-only post-training quantization framework that unifies quantization and sparsification. It leverages activation magnitudes as a proxy for computational importance to dynamically assign weight channels to different precision levels, enabling flexible average bit widths during decoding.
AI.result: Evaluated on Llama3 and Qwen3 models, GRINQH outperforms state-of-the-art fixed- and mixed-precision baselines at comparable 3- and 4-bit settings, even enabling effective 2-bit generation. Experimental verification shows theoretical speedups using a hierarchical nested memory layout in a custom GPU kernel.
AI.conclusion: GRINQH establishes a new state-of-the-art Pareto frontier for LLM generation, enabling a dynamic trade-off between generation quality and inference speed through its graded input-based quantization hierarchy approach.

title: Interpretable Kolmogorov-Arnold Network with Feature-Isolated Temporal Attention Mechanism for Electricity Load Forecasting
summary: Accurate electricity load forecasting is a crucial prerequisite for stable power system operations. While prevalent deep learning models present competitive performance, they often operate as black boxes and lack interpretability. While the Kolmogorov-Arnold network (KAN) has emerged as a promising alternative because of its learnable activation function design, its direct application to time-series forecasting faces challenges in modeling complex temporal data patterns. Also, simple integration into existing architectures, such as serving as replacement of neural modules, cannot fully leverage KAN's interpretability strengths. To address these gaps, this study develops LoadKAN, a novel hybrid and interpretable framework for load forecasting that synergistically combines a specifically-designed feature-isolated temporal attention mechanism with a KAN module. The attention stage aims to extract temporal dynamics from each input feature independently, such as historical load and human mobility, providing distilled feature representations to the KAN module for interpretable predictions. When evaluated on datasets from three representative U.S. electricity markets, our LoadKAN remains highly competitive when compared to extensively-tuned, state-of-the-art, black-box deep learning benchmarks. More importantly, LoadKAN's interpretability enables a granular analysis of the learned non-linear relationships between six distinct mobility patterns and electricity load. Through KAN-learned activation functions, our quantitative sensitivity analyses on mobility features reveal complex and market-specific dependencies. These findings further demonstrate the ability of our LoadKAN to generate insights often obscured by opaque black-box neural forecasting models.

AI.tldr: LoadKAN is a novel hybrid interpretable framework for electricity load forecasting that combines feature-isolated temporal attention with Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KAN) to provide both competitive accuracy and interpretability, enabling granular analysis of relationships between mobility patterns and electricity demand.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning models for electricity load forecasting lack interpretability (operate as black boxes), while KAN networks face challenges in modeling complex temporal patterns. Simple integration of KAN into existing architectures doesn't fully leverage its interpretability strengths for time-series forecasting.
AI.method: LoadKAN combines a specially-designed feature-isolated temporal attention mechanism with a KAN module. The attention stage extracts temporal dynamics from each input feature (historical load, human mobility) independently, providing distilled feature representations to the KAN module for interpretable predictions.
AI.result: When evaluated on three U.S. electricity market datasets, LoadKAN remains highly competitive against state-of-the-art black-box deep learning benchmarks. More importantly, it enables granular analysis of learned non-linear relationships between six distinct mobility patterns and electricity load through KAN-learned activation functions and quantitative sensitivity analyses.
AI.conclusion: LoadKAN demonstrates that interpretable forecasting models can achieve competitive accuracy while providing valuable insights into complex market-specific dependencies between mobility patterns and electricity demand - insights often obscured by opaque black-box neural forecasting models.

title: What Does a Chemical Language Model Know About Molecules?
summary: Chemical language models (cLMs) are widely assumed to learn surface-level syntactic patterns rather than learning meaningful molecular semantics. Here, we apply sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to MolFormer, an encoder-only cLM, to mechanistically examine how molecular representations are built across layers. We discover that early layers rely on position-tracking latents to parse molecular grammar, while later layers encode atom-in-substructure and pharmacologically relevant features. Additionally, we show that non-canonical SMILES produce more disruptive representation shifts than invalid SMILES, driven by position-latent disruption propagating across layers. To support further exploration, we develop InterMol, an interactive visualizer for SAE activations on molecular strings and structures.

AI.tldr: SAEs applied to MolFormer reveal early layers use position-tracking latents for molecular grammar, while later layers encode chemical/pharmacological features; non-canonical SMILES cause more representation disruption than invalid ones; InterMol visualizer created for SAE activations.
AI.motivation: Chemical language models (cLMs) are assumed to learn only surface-level syntactic patterns rather than meaningful molecular semantics. There's a need to mechanistically examine how molecular representations are actually built across layers to understand what these models truly learn.
AI.method: Applied sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to MolFormer (an encoder-only chemical language model) to mechanistically examine molecular representation building across layers. Analyzed how different types of SMILES (non-canonical vs invalid) affect representation shifts. Developed InterMol, an interactive visualizer for SAE activations on molecular strings and structures.
AI.result: Early layers rely on position-tracking latents to parse molecular grammar. Later layers encode atom-in-substructure and pharmacologically relevant features. Non-canonical SMILES produce more disruptive representation shifts than invalid SMILES, driven by position-latent disruption propagating across layers.
AI.conclusion: Chemical language models learn meaningful molecular semantics beyond surface syntax, with systematic progression from positional grammar parsing to chemical/pharmacological feature encoding. The developed InterMol tool enables further mechanistic exploration of molecular representations in cLMs.

title: Selective Time Series Forecasting via Metalearning
summary: Deep learning methods have achieved state-of-the-art in time series forecasting, yet their accuracy varies considerably across samples, as some instances remain inherently difficult to predict. Reject option mechanisms, which allow models to abstain from high-risk predictions, are well established in classification and regression but underexplored in forecasting. Existing abstention strategies typically rely on proxies, such as the width of the prediction interval or learned confidence scores derived from forecasts. However, these approaches are inherently tied to the training domain, limiting their ability to generalize. We propose a selective forecasting framework that addresses this limitation by modeling the empirical percentile of forecasting errors, that is, a scale-invariant statistic, based on structural characteristics extracted from recent lags via metalearning. By decoupling the rejection decision from the forecast itself and grounding it in domain-agnostic features, the framework enables effective abstention transfer across heterogeneous time series. Experiments in both in-domain and transfer learning settings show that rejecting samples predicted as challenging consistently improves forecasting accuracy across coverage levels.

AI.tldr: A selective forecasting framework that improves prediction accuracy by identifying and rejecting inherently difficult-to-predict samples using metalearning on structural features from recent lags.
AI.motivation: Deep learning methods for time series forecasting have inconsistent accuracy across samples, with some instances remaining inherently difficult to predict. While reject option mechanisms exist for classification and regression, they're underexplored in forecasting and current approaches rely on domain-dependent proxies that don't generalize well across different time series.
AI.method: Proposes a selective forecasting framework that models empirical percentiles of forecasting errors using structural characteristics extracted from recent lags via metalearning. Decouples rejection decisions from forecasts by using domain-agnostic features, enabling transfer across heterogeneous time series.
AI.result: Experiments in both in-domain and transfer learning settings demonstrate that rejecting samples predicted as challenging consistently improves forecasting accuracy across different coverage levels.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses generalization limitations of existing abstention strategies by using metalearning on structural features, enabling practical implementation of selective forecasting across diverse time series domains.

title: Do Location Encoders Capture Spatial Effects? A GeoShapley Benchmark Across Scales
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables, Submitted to SIGSPATIAL '26 short papers
summary: Location encoders transform geographic coordinates into high dimensional embeddings for downstream machine learning, but it is unclear how well these representations capture interpretable spatial effects. We benchmark whether GeoShapley, a game-theoretic explainer that treats all location features as a single joint player, can recover spatially varying coefficients from models built on location-encoder embeddings. Eleven encoders from the TorchSpatial framework are evaluated against a synthetic process with known coefficients, across three scales (grid, county, global), with and without raw coordinates alongside the embedding, and under untrained and contrastively trained conditions. Measuring recovery as the correlation between estimated and true coefficients, we report how it varies with scale and encoder architecture and compare the embeddings against a raw-coordinate baseline. Recovery of the primary coefficient is consistently high across encoders, whereas recovery of a secondary coefficient is more scale-dependent, differing most at the global scale; the raw-coordinate baseline remains competitive throughout.

AI.tldr: GeoShapley game-theoretic explainer is benchmarked to assess how well location encoder embeddings capture interpretable spatial effects by recovering known coefficients from synthetic processes.
AI.motivation: Location encoders create embeddings from geographic coordinates, but it's unclear how well these representations capture interpretable spatial effects that can be explained using methods like GeoShapley.
AI.method: Used GeoShapley, a game-theoretic explainer that treats location features as a single joint player, to benchmark recovery of spatially varying coefficients from synthetic processes with known coefficients. Evaluated 11 encoders from TorchSpatial across three scales (grid, county, global), with/without raw coordinates alongside embeddings, under untrained and contrastively trained conditions.
AI.result: Recovery of primary coefficients was consistently high across encoders, while recovery of secondary coefficients was more scale-dependent (most different at global scale). Raw-coordinate baseline remained competitive throughout all evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Location encoder embeddings show varying ability to capture interpretable spatial effects, with primary coefficients well-recovered but secondary coefficients showing scale-dependent performance, suggesting embeddings have different strengths depending on spatial scale and task complexity.

title: TROPT: An Open Framework for Unifying and Advancing Discrete Text Optimization
summary: Discrete text-trigger optimization -- searching for text sequences that, when ingested by a model, steer it toward a specified objective -- underpins model red-teaming (e.g., LLM jailbreaks), as well as auditing and interpretability. However, the current state of discrete optimizers hinders their adoption and progress. First, existing optimizers, when open-sourced at all, are scattered across research codebases tied to specific models, objectives, and problem domains. Second, optimizer variants proliferate, each requiring engineering overhead to use or extend, and remaining hard to compare head-to-head. Together, these raise the bar for adopting optimizers in existing or new domains, and for advancing them via new strategies. We address these gaps with TROPT, the first open-source framework that unifies discrete optimizers' execution and standardizes their development under a single interface. TROPT makes it easy to customize end-to-end optimization recipes by swapping any component -- models, objectives, and optimizers -- extending its reach across domains and new applications. TROPT currently ships with 30+ optimization recipes -- covering applications such as jailbreaking and probing model internals -- built from 15+ optimizers (spanning white-box to black-box access) and 15+ losses, from foundational to state-of-the-art methods. Demonstrating its utility, we leverage TROPT in several studies: (i) controlled, large-scale experiments comparing and enhancing optimization strategies for LLM jailbreaks, revealing potent-yet-underadopted techniques; and (ii) porting optimizers from one domain (e.g., LLM jailbreak) to new domains (e.g., corpus-poisoning embedding model). In all, TROPT significantly lowers the barrier to adopting and advancing discrete text optimization.

AI.tldr: TROPT is an open-source framework that unifies discrete text-trigger optimization, providing standardized interfaces for model red-teaming, auditing, and interpretability tasks with 30+ recipes and 15+ optimizers.
AI.motivation: Existing discrete text optimizers are scattered across research codebases, tied to specific models/objectives, and difficult to extend or compare. There's a need for a unified framework to lower adoption barriers and enable systematic advancement of optimization techniques.
AI.method: TROPT provides a single interface for executing and developing discrete optimizers, allowing easy customization by swapping components like models, objectives, and optimizers. It includes 30+ optimization recipes built from 15+ optimizers (white-box to black-box) and 15+ losses.
AI.result: TROPT enables: (i) large-scale comparisons of optimization strategies for LLM jailbreaks, revealing under-adopted techniques; (ii) porting optimizers across domains (e.g., from jailbreaking to corpus-poisoning); and demonstrates utility across applications including jailbreaking and model probing.
AI.conclusion: TROPT significantly lowers the barrier to adopting and advancing discrete text optimization by providing a unified framework that standardizes development, enables component swapping, and supports cross-domain applications with extensive recipe and optimizer coverage.

title: Collapsed Effective Operators for Higher-order Structures
summary: Higher-order structures are powerful relational modeling tools, yet existing spectral operators decompose the topology into separate ranks, leaving practitioners to fuse the information back to vertices through ad hoc choices. We introduce Collapsed Effective Operators, which condense higher-order degrees of freedom into a single vertex-level operator via Schur complementation of a graded Laplacian. This yields a (generally dense) operator that encodes long-range interactions mediated by topology and is applicable to arbitrary higher-order constructs. We show it preserves positive semi-definiteness with a spectral upper bound relative to the rank-0 Hodge Laplacian, effectively lowering system energy under higher-order connectivity. Empirically, our operator improves spectral clustering, signal smoothing, and enables the inclusion of topological features in neural network architectures via positional encoding. The project page can be found http://circle-group.github.io/research/CollapsedEffectiveOperators

AI.tldr: Collapsed Effective Operators condense higher-order structures into single vertex-level operators via Schur complementation, enabling unified handling of multi-scale topological interactions in spectral methods.
AI.motivation: Existing spectral operators decompose topology into separate ranks, requiring practitioners to fuse information back to vertices through ad hoc choices, which is inefficient and arbitrary.
AI.method: Introduces Collapsed Effective Operators using Schur complementation of a graded Laplacian to condense higher-order degrees of freedom into a single vertex-level operator, creating a generally dense operator that encodes long-range interactions mediated by topology.
AI.result: The operator preserves positive semi-definiteness with spectral upper bound relative to rank-0 Hodge Laplacian, effectively lowering system energy under higher-order connectivity. Empirically improves spectral clustering, signal smoothing, and enables topological features in neural networks via positional encoding.
AI.conclusion: Collapsed Effective Operators provide a principled, unified approach to incorporate higher-order topological structures into vertex-level analysis, overcoming limitations of ad hoc fusion methods and enhancing various spectral applications.

title: Simulation-Free Estimation of Traffic Flows from Sparse Count Data
summary:
We propose a method for estimating time-varying traffic flow patterns from sparse aggregated vehicle counts. The method partitions the study area into spatial regions, constructs a set of feasible region-to-region routes, and solves a weighted least-squares optimization problem to determine the number of vehicles to allocate on each route. A weighted contribution matrix encodes sensor coverage, steering the optimizer toward flow configurations that are directly observable by sensors. Edge-level trajectories are then derived by scoring candidate routes against the temporal and volumetric profiles of aggregated regional sensor counts.
  The method is evaluated on the Brussels road network using real and synthetic traffic data. Results show that the proposed approach reproduces the daily traffic profile in the input data and outperforms the baseline methods at a fraction of the computational cost.

AI.tldr: A method for estimating time-varying traffic flow patterns from sparse aggregated vehicle counts using spatial region partitioning, feasible route construction, and weighted least-squares optimization with sensor coverage weighting.
AI.motivation: The need to estimate detailed traffic flow patterns from limited, aggregated vehicle count data collected by sparse sensor networks in urban areas like Brussels.
AI.method: 1) Partition study area into spatial regions, 2) Construct feasible region-to-region routes, 3) Solve weighted least-squares optimization to allocate vehicles to routes, 4) Use weighted contribution matrix encoding sensor coverage to steer optimization toward observable flows, 5) Derive edge-level trajectories by scoring candidate routes against temporal and volumetric profiles of aggregated regional sensor counts.
AI.result: Evaluation on Brussels road network with real and synthetic traffic data shows the method reproduces daily traffic profiles from input data and outperforms baseline methods at a fraction of the computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully estimates time-varying traffic flow patterns from sparse aggregated counts with good accuracy and computational efficiency, making it suitable for real-world urban traffic monitoring applications.

title: The Energy Consumption of Transformer Fine-Tuning: A Roofline-Inspired Scaling Model
summary: Transformer-based models underpin modern natural language processing but incur rapidly growing computational and energy costs. As training scales in both model size and parallelism, accurately predicting energy consumption has become critical for sustainable and cost-aware system design. We present a framework for modeling the energy consumption of Transformer training on multiple GPUs. Using controlled architectural sweeps of BERT models, we relate measured energy to lightweight proxies for compute, memory traffic, and hardware efficiency. Inspired by roofline models, our approach incorporates a speedup-based hardware-efficiency factor that captures the effects of tensor parallelism and fully sharded data parallelism. We derive a scaling law model that accurately predicts training energy across heterogeneous configurations.

AI.tldr: A framework for predicting energy consumption in multi-GPU Transformer training using controlled architectural sweeps and scaling laws.
AI.motivation: Transformer models incur rapidly growing computational and energy costs as training scales, making accurate energy prediction critical for sustainable and cost-aware system design.
AI.method: Use controlled architectural sweeps of BERT models to relate measured energy to lightweight proxies for compute, memory traffic, and hardware efficiency. Incorporate a speedup-based hardware-efficiency factor inspired by roofline models to capture effects of tensor parallelism and fully sharded data parallelism.
AI.result: Develop a scaling law model that accurately predicts training energy across heterogeneous configurations.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides an effective method for modeling and predicting energy consumption in multi-GPU Transformer training, addressing the critical need for sustainable and cost-aware system design at scale.

title: Scheduling Thoughts: Learning the Order of Thought in Diffusion Language Models
summary: Masked diffusion language models decode by iteratively unmasking tokens, where the unmasking order defines an "order of thought" that strongly influences generation quality yet is typically chosen heuristically. We derive a tractable upper bound on the sequential decoding mismatch, measured by the Kullback-Leibler divergence and expressed in terms of the model's pathwise log-likelihood, with tightness under sufficient model expressivity. This bound induces a dense self-aware reward over ordered trajectories, casting order selection as a principled policy optimization problem with a frozen denoiser. We instantiate this idea as Self-Aware Scheduling (SAS), which learns a lightweight order policy using Group Relative Policy Optimization and applies seamlessly to both any-order and semi-autoregressive decoding. On Sudoku with 1B MDM, SAS improves puzzle accuracy from 82.0% (best heuristic schedule) to 91.8%, and reaches 97.5% with second-stage fine-tuning along learned trajectories. On mathematical reasoning with LLaDA-8B, SAS improves pass@1 on GSM8K from 64% to 76% and on MBPP from 39.5% to 41%, consistently matching or exceeding heuristic schedules across generation lengths and block sizes. Project page: https://jimmyxu123.github.io/SAS

AI.tldr: SAS (Self-Aware Scheduling) is a method that learns optimal token unmasking orders for masked diffusion language models through policy optimization with a frozen denoiser, significantly improving generation quality on reasoning tasks like Sudoku and math problems compared to heuristic schedules.
AI.motivation: The unmasking order in masked diffusion language models strongly influences generation quality, but current approaches typically use heuristic schedules rather than principled methods to determine the optimal "order of thought" during decoding.
AI.method: Derived a tractable upper bound on sequential decoding mismatch using KL divergence and pathwise log-likelihood, creating a self-aware reward over ordered trajectories. This enables order selection as policy optimization with frozen denoiser. Implemented as SAS using Group Relative Policy Optimization, applicable to any-order and semi-autoregressive decoding.
AI.result: On Sudoku with 1B MDM: SAS improved accuracy from 82.0% (best heuristic) to 91.8%, reaching 97.5% with second-stage fine-tuning. On mathematical reasoning with LLaDA-8B: Improved pass@1 on GSM8K from 64% to 76%, on MBPP from 39.5% to 41%. Consistently matched or exceeded heuristic schedules across generation lengths and block sizes.
AI.conclusion: SAS provides a principled framework for learning optimal token unmasking orders in masked diffusion language models, demonstrating significant performance improvements over heuristic schedules on reasoning tasks, making it a promising approach for improving generation quality in diffusion-based language models.

title: SVD-Surgeon: Optimal Singular-Value Surgery for Large Language Model Compression
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve remarkable performance across a wide range of tasks, but their deployment is constrained by substantial memory and compute requirements. Low-rank compression via singular value decomposition (SVD) is an effective remedy, but existing methods focus on how to factorize and which components to keep. We introduce SVD-Surgeon, a training-free method that brings the Optimal Brain Surgeon (OBS) framework to the singular-value basis. Treating each singular value as a parameter, it computes a closed-form update of the retained singular values that compensates, to second order in the model loss, for those removed by truncation. The same analysis yields a saliency for choosing which values to prune. As it operates directly on the singular-value factorization, SVD-Surgeon can be layered on top of existing SVD compressors. Applied to SVD-LLM, a leading SVD-based method, it improves the perplexity-compression trade-off on the OPT family and LLaMA 2-7B without any retraining.

AI.tldr: SVD-Surgeon is a training-free method that applies Optimal Brain Surgeon framework to SVD compression of LLMs, computing closed-form updates to retained singular values to compensate for truncation losses.
AI.motivation: LLMs have substantial memory and compute requirements that constrain deployment. While SVD compression helps, existing methods focus on factorization and component selection but don't compensate for performance loss from truncation.
AI.method: SVD-Surgeon brings Optimal Brain Surgeon (OBS) framework to singular-value basis by treating each singular value as a parameter. It computes closed-form updates to retained singular values that compensate for truncated ones to second order in model loss. The same analysis yields saliency scores for pruning decisions.
AI.result: Applied to SVD-LLM, it improves the perplexity-compression trade-off on OPT family and LLaMA 2-7B models without any retraining. The method can be layered on top of existing SVD compressors.
AI.conclusion: SVD-Surgeon provides an effective training-free approach to improve SVD compression of LLMs by compensating for truncation losses through closed-form updates based on second-order loss analysis.

title: Patient-Aware Contrastive Learning Preserves Per-Patient Structure in RR-Interval Representations
summary: Contrastive representation learning struggles on physiological signals when each subject contributes a distinct baseline pattern. If class differences overlap with subject differences,class-level objectives such as supervised contrastive learning tend to merge per-subject structure into a single per-class cluster,removing the individual variation that a model needs to generalize to unseen patients. We study this problem in the setting of Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation(PAF) detection from RR-interval(RRI) sequences and propose a patient-aware contrastive objective that forms positive pairs only from same-patient, same-class segments, preserving each patient's own sinus rhythm(SR) baseline while still pushing the two classes apart. Examining the learned embeddings directly, our objective achieves the most consistent per-patient SR structure (cohesion $0.850$ vs. $0.800$ for supervised contrastive loss (SupCon) and $0.772$ for binary cross-entropy (BCE)). We also identify that BCE produces the cleanest global class separation yet the most disordered per-patient structure. This is precisely why a linear probe trained on its features breaks down on unseen patients. On the IRIDIA-AF dataset, the resulting representation reaches a patient-independent Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC) of $0.989 \pm 0.003$ with $2.6\times$ lower seed variance than supervised contrastive baselines.These results highlight that per-subject geometric consistency, rather than global class separability, is key to robust cross-patient generalization.

AI.tldr: A patient-aware contrastive learning method for physiological signals that preserves individual baseline patterns while separating classes, achieving better cross-patient generalization for atrial fibrillation detection.
AI.motivation: Contrastive learning on physiological signals struggles when subject differences overlap with class differences, causing models to merge per-subject structure into single per-class clusters and lose individual variation needed for generalization to unseen patients.
AI.method: Proposed patient-aware contrastive objective that forms positive pairs only from same-patient, same-class segments, preserving each patient's own sinus rhythm baseline while still pushing the two classes apart.
